**Context and Relevance**
Sequential text labeling is a well-established domain within natural language processing. Recently, neural networks have been applied not only to traditional sequential tasks, such as part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) and named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016), but also to reframe non-sequential tasks, such as syntactic parsing, within a labeling framework (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019). In contrast, the direct labeling of human speech audio sequences remains comparatively underexplored. When addressed, speech labeling typically employs a proxy approach: automatically transcribing speech into text and subsequently applying text-only models (Surdeanu et al., 2005; Mollá et al., 2007; Eidelman et al., 2010). The primary challenge in this paradigm shifts from native speech labeling to adapting models to the adverse conditions of speech recognition error rates. Such models are commonly utilized in end-to-end applications, including dialogue state tracking (Henderson et al., 2014; Ram et al., 2018). While recent advances in end-to-end neural learning suggest the potential for directly labeling linguistic categories from speech alone (Ghannay et al., 2018), existing multimodal learning applications in multimedia processing generally focus on classification tasks, such as sentiment analysis, rather than fine-grained sequence labeling (Zadeh et al., 2017), despite the close alignment of modalities like text, speech, and video in those contexts.

Specifically, our model exploits the online temporal alignment between the input audio signal and the raw ASR transcription.

Recently, a few studies have applied editingbased approaches to sentence generation. Guu et al. propose a heuristic deleteretrievegenerate component for a supervised sequencetosequence (Seq2Seq) model. Dong et al. learn the deletion and insertion operations for text simplification in a supervised way, where their groundtruth operations are obtained by some dynamic programming algorithm. Our editing operations (insertion, deletion, and replacement) are the search actions of unsupervised simulated annealing. Regarding discrete optimization/searching, a na ¨ ıve approach is by hill climbing, which is in fact a greedy algorithm. In NLP, beam search is widely applied to sentence generation. BS maintains a k -best list in a partially greedy fashion during lefttoright (or righttoleft) decoding. By contrast, UPSA is local search with distributed edits over the entire sentence. Moreover, UPSA is able to make use of the original sentence as an initial state of searching, whereas BS usually works in the decoder of a Seq2Seq model and is not applicable to unsupervised paraphrasing. 3 Approach In this section, we present our novel UPSA framework that uses simulated annealing (SA) for unsupervised paraphrasing. In particular, we first present the general SA algorithm and then design our searching objective and searching actions (i.e., candidate sentence generator) for paraphrasing. 3.1 The Simulated Annealing Algorithm Simulated Annealing (SA) is an effective and general metaheuristic of searching, especially for a large discrete or continuous space. Let X be a (huge) search space of sentences, and f (x) be an objective function. The goal is to search for a sentence x that maximizes f (x) . At a searching step t , SA keeps a current sentence x t , and proposes a new candidate x by local editing. If the new candidate is better scored by f , i.e., f (x ) > f (x t ) , then SA accepts the proposal.

(2) Function words : pronouns, auxiliaries. (3) Other grammar : common verbs, comparisons. (4) Affect words : positive and negative emotion. (5) Social word : family, friends. (6) Cognitive processes : discrepancies, certainty. (7) Perceptual processes : feeling, seeing. (8) Biological processes : body, health. (9) Core drives and needs : power, reward focus. For each sentence, we use a procedure described by Hosseini et al. to abstract its tokens to the following categories: numbers ( NUM ), entities ( ENT ), containers ( CONT ) and attributes ( ATTR ). In addition, verbs are abstracted to six categories, each corresponding to a different change in the number of entities owned by containers. Thus, each template fully specifies how to update a world state , i.e., the number of entities each container owns. The top part of Figure 3 illustrates the abstraction process. Finally, we count for each extracted template its frequency in the data, and use the top12 templates for passage generation. Details on the abstraction process, categories used, and extracted templates are in A.2 . Passage generation Using the extracted templates, we can generate sentences and maintain a world state of all containers and the number of entities they own. We construct a small vocabulary (<100 words) that maps categories to domainspecific words, and use the following procedure to generate passages. We sample 36 templates with replacement, and instantiate them onebyone (the bottom part of Figure 3 illustrates instantiation). Each template is instantiated by uniformly sampling values from the vocabulary with probability 1 − p and from previously generated sentences with probability p . To avoid a collection of unrelated sentences, we set the probability of using previously used values to p = 0 . 7 . An example passage is shown in Table 3 . Question generation After generating a passage, the world state holds information about all containers in the passage and the number of entities they hold. In Table 3 , the state will include the number of families and rebels of different nationalities in each container (the commander, the householder, and the countries). Based on this world state, numerical reasoning questions can be asked. To create questions, we craft 13 question templates that are instantiated with objects from the world state. The questions teach the model to track events and perform numeric and discrete operations.

**4. Attachment versus Recency**

We commence with a proof-of-concept investigation. Prior research has established that Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) language models possess a strong recency bias (Ravfogel et al., 2019). Consequently, it is plausible that only one attachment type—specifically low attachment—is learnable. To examine this hypothesis, we adopted the methodology outlined by McCoy et al. (2018) and constructed a synthetic language to control the distribution of RC attachment across two distinct experiments.

The first experiment addressed the following question: If all RC attachment instances in the training data are high, what volume of RC observations is required for a high-attachment bias to generalize to unseen data? The second experiment investigated the proportion of high versus low attachment instances necessary in the training set to induce a specific bias.

The synthetic language comprised RC attachment sentences and filler declarative sentences. The filler sentences adhered to the phrase structure template defined in (5a), while the RC attachment sentences followed the template in (5b):

> **(5)**
> a. D N (P D N) (Aux) V (D N) (P D N)
> b. D N Aux V D N 'of' D N 'that' 'was/were' V

Elements enclosed in parentheses were optional and thus not present in all filler stimuli. Specifically, all filler sentences contained a subject (abbreviated as D N) and a verb (abbreviated as V). Second, regarding the extraction of question-relevant rules, the current methodology faces significant challenges in identifying the most pertinent text spans necessary for generating subsequent questions. For instance, the state-of-the-art E³ model (Zhong and Zettlemoyer, 2019) achieves an F1 score of only 60.6% in this specific task. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework for conversational machine reading that incorporates an Explicit Memory Tracker (EMT). This component explicitly monitors individual rule sentences to facilitate decision-making and the generation of follow-up inquiries.

Specifically, the EMT first segments the rule text into discrete sentences and allocates them into dedicated memory modules. Subsequently, the initial query, user scenario, and dialogue history are processed sequentially to update each memory module independently. At every dialogue turn, the EMT predicts the entailment status (satisfied or unsatisfied) for each rule sentence and determines the appropriate action based on the current memory state. When the decision is to inquire, the EMT extracts a relevant rule span to formulate a follow-up question using a coarse-to-fine reasoning strategy; this involves weighting token-level span distributions with sentence-level entailment scores.

Unlike previous approaches that treat entailment-oriented reasoning for decision-making and question generation as separate processes, the EMT leverages its updated memory modules to address both tasks within a unified framework. We evaluated the EMT against existing methods using the ShARC dataset (Saeidi et al., 2018). Our results demonstrate that explicitly tracking rules via external memory significantly enhances both decision accuracy and the quality of generated follow-up questions. Notably, the EMT outperforms the previous best model, E³, by 1.3 points in macro-averaged decision accuracy and by 10.8 points in BLEU-4 score for question generation. Furthermore, beyond performance improvements, the EMT offers enhanced interpretability by explicitly tracking rules, allowing for the visualization of the model's entailment-oriented reasoning process.

Motivated by recent advancements in computer vision (Zheng et al., 2016), Neural Machine Translation (Cheng et al., 2018), and Automatic Speech Recognition (Sperber et al., 2017), this study proposes two Noise-Aware Training (NAT) objectives. These objectives are designed to enhance the accuracy of sequence labeling tasks involving noisy inputs while maintaining efficiency on clean data. Figure 1 provides a visual overview of the problem domain and the proposed methodology. The primary contributions of this work are summarized as follows:

*   **Problem Formulation and Modeling:** We formally define the noisy sequence labeling problem, wherein inputs are subjected to an unknown noise process (Section 2.2). Furthermore, we introduce a model to estimate the underlying error distribution (Section 3.1) and develop a novel noise induction procedure to simulate realistic noisy inputs (Section 3.2).
*   **Data Augmentation Strategy:** We propose a data augmentation algorithm (Section 3.3) that directly injects noise into input data, facilitating the training of neural models on a mixture of noisy and clean samples.
*   **Stability Training Methodology:** We adapt a stability training framework (Zheng et al., 2016) to the sequence labeling context. This method explicitly addresses input noise by encouraging the model to generate noise-invariant latent representations (Section 3.4).
*   **Empirical Evaluation:** We evaluate the proposed methods against state-of-the-art baseline models (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019) using real-world data containing OCR errors and misspellings. The results demonstrate the efficacy of our approach (Section 4).
*   **Reproducibility:** To facilitate future research and ensure experimental reproducibility, the source code and datasets are made publicly available.

#### 2.1 Neural Sequence Labeling

Figure 2 illustrates a standard architecture for the neural sequence labeling task. The sequence labeling system is denoted as $F(x; \theta)$, or simply $F(x)$, where $x = (x_1, \dots, x_N)$ represents a tokenized input sentence of length $N$, and $\theta$ denotes the set of all learnable parameters. The function $F(x)$ accepts $x$ as input and outputs both the probability distribution over class labels $y(x)$ and the final predicted sequence of labels $\hat{y} = (\hat{y}_1, \dots, \hat{y}_N)$.

Semantic relations provide important clues about what contents are questionworthy and what reasoning should be performed; e.g. , in Figure 1 , both the entities Pago Pago International Airport and Hoonah Airport have the located at relation with a city in United States. It is then natural to ask a comparative question: e.g. , Are Pago Pago International Airport and Hoonah Airport both on American territory? . To efficiently leverage the semantic graph for DQG, we introduce three novel mechanisms: (1) proposing a novel graph encoder, which incorporates an attention mechanism into the Gated Graph Neural Network (GGNN), to dynamically model the interactions between different semantic relations; (2) enhancing the wordlevel passage embeddings and the nodelevel semantic graph representations to obtain an unified semanticaware passage representations for question decoding; and (3) introducing an auxiliary content selection task that jointly trains with question decoding, which assists the model in selecting relevant contexts in the semantic graph to form a proper reasoning chain. We evaluate our model on HotpotQA, a challenging dataset in which the questions are generated by reasoning over text from separate Wikipedia pages. Experimental results show that our model — incorporating both the use of the semantic graph and the content selection task — improves performance by a large margin, in terms of both automated metrics (Section 4.3 ) and human evaluation (Section 4.5 ). Error analysis (Section 4.6 ) validates that our use of the semantic graph greatly reduces the amount of semantic errors in generated questions. In summary, our contributions are: (1) the very first work, to the best of our knowledge, to investigate deep question generation, (2) a novel framework which combines a semantic graph with the input passage to generate deep questions, and (3) a novel graph encoder that incorporates attention into a GGNN approach. 2 Related Work Question generation aims to automatically generate questions from textual inputs. Rulebased techniques for QG usually rely on manuallydesigned rules or templates to transform a piece of given text to questions. These methods are confined to a variety of transformation rules or templates, making the approach difficult to generalize. Neuralbased approaches take advantage of the sequencetosequence (Seq2Seq) framework with attention.

First, the encoder is poorly optimized, as shown by the low ∥ ∂ L rec /∂ e ∥ 2 . Second, the decoder degenerates into a powerful language model. Third, h contains less information from e in VAE than in DAE, which is indicated by the lower ∥ ∂ h /∂ e ∥ F / ∥ h ∥ 2 . We call these features as encoderdecoder incompatibility . To bridge the incompatibility and posterior collapse, we start with the manifold hypothesis which states that realworld data concentrates near a manifold with a lower dimensionality than the ambient space. In our case, we denote the manifold of text data as X ⊂ S l ∈ N V l where V is the vocabulary. In the language of differential geometry, the encoded text e ∈E ⊂ R d and the decoding signal h ∈H ⊂ R d can be viewed as the parameterizations (or coordinates ) of x ∈X under two different charts (or coordinate systems ). Formally, we denote the chart maps as ϕ e : X →E and ϕ h : X →H , which satisfy e = ϕ e ( x ) and h = ϕ h ( x ) for any x ∈X . Given the two charts, the map from E to H is called the transition map ϕ h ◦ ϕ − 1 e : E →H between the two charts.

Processes are typically isolated from one another, lacking shared memory, yet they may interact through operating system-provided mechanisms such as the file system. Each process comprises one or more threads, where each thread represents a sequence of instructions capable of concurrent execution. All threads within a process share the resources associated with that process. In this paper, the term "thread" is employed to denote entities executing concurrently; this terminology is adopted because the distinction between processes and threads is often indistinct within the operating system kernel, a context relevant to the coursework under examination.

Concurrent execution on a given platform is generally achieved through two primary mechanisms, which are frequently utilized in conjunction. First, hardware may provide true parallelism via multiple cores or central processing units (CPUs) executing instructions simultaneously. Second, software-implemented multitasking—whether preemptive or non-preemptive—may alternate between multiple threads at high speed to create the illusion of parallel execution.

The second concept addressed in this paper is the critical section. A critical section refers to a segment of a concurrent program that, if executed simultaneously, could result in undesired or unpredictable behavior. This scenario typically arises when a shared resource, such as a shared variable, is accessed. To mitigate issues associated with concurrent execution, critical sections must be protected, commonly through the use of locks. However, protecting a critical section inherently reduces the volume of code capable of concurrent execution. Consequently, it is imperative to precisely define the scope of critical sections to avoid unnecessarily diminishing program concurrency and, by extension, the benefits derived from concurrent design.

This study addresses the following research questions:
*   **RQ1:** We empirically evaluate the reliability of approximating influence functions (Koh and Liang, 2017) for interpreting the decisions of deep transformer-based architectures, such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019).
*   **RQ2:** We examine the degree of consistency between findings derived from influence functions and insights obtained from gradient-based saliency scores across representative natural language processing (NLP) tasks.
*   **RQ3:** We explore the utility of influence functions as a mechanism for identifying artifacts or confounds within training data that models may exploit.

To the best of our knowledge, this work constitutes the first investigation in NLP to systematically compare interpretation methods that generate input-based saliency maps against those that explain predictions via influential training examples. Furthermore, we propose a novel quantitative metric to assess the impact of hypothesized artifacts (Gururangan et al., 2018; McCoy et al., 2019) on model predictions using influence functions.

### 2. Explaining Black-Box Model Predictions

Predictions generated by machine learning models in NLP are contingent upon two primary factors: the input text and the model parameters. While prior efforts to interpret opaque NLP models have predominantly focused on the input text, this work investigates the complementary approach of analyzing the influence of training data examples. Saliency maps aim to enhance interpretability by highlighting specific segments of the input text, whereas influence functions seek explanatory clues within the model parameters, ultimately tracing interpretations back to the specific training examples that shaped these parameter estimates. The following sections provide a detailed exposition of these two interpretation methodologies.

#### 2.1 Gradient-Based Saliency Maps

As a standard and illustrative method for "explanation by input features," we focus on gradient-based saliency maps. In this approach, the gradient of the loss function $L$ is computed with respect to each token $t$ in the input text, where the magnitude of the gradient serves as a feature importance score (Simonyan et al., 2014; Li et al., 2016a). A distinct advantage of gradients is their inherent local "faithfulness": they quantify the extent to which the loss would change were a specific token to be perturbed by a small amount.

Caskurlu et al. conducted a study on faculty views on the alignment between CS programs and industry needs. They found many faculty believe CS programs prepare students well for industry jobs, citing the fact that the majority of their graduates have no problems finding high paying jobs. However, faculty also mentioned that soft skills are undertaught and that some topics such as software maintenance are hard, if not impossible, to teach . A study by Lewis surveyed 13 faculty and 160 undergraduates on their beliefs and attitudes about a variety of CSrelated topics, and then compared how students and faculty differed in their views. Topics included things like the importance of group work and the role of aptitude for success. They found that although students and faculty disagree on many topics in general. In some cases, students later in their CS degree began to better align with faculty views than those earlier in their career, but in other cases the disconnect persisted . One such statement rejected by faculty, but more accepted by the students, was “In the real world, computer scientists spend a lot of time working alone.” This finding is relevant to our study, as it shows how faculty and students may disagree on basic assumptions about applications of CS, even after interacting with these faculty during their undergraduate education. These findings have been replicated and further explored, focusing on areas where faculty and students disagree . While the motivation for our study is primarily the gap between academia and industry, we framed our interviews more broadly. In particular, we included interview questions concerning the goals of an undergraduate CS education in general and the preparation of students for careers in academia. There are two reasons for this choice. The first is that we wanted our interviews to allow us to identify other important aspects of a CS education besides preparation for industry. The second is that we wanted to avoid potentially offending faculty by focusing solely on industryspecific interview questions that could cause them to take a defensive position, compromising our data collection process. Our research questions are: What are faculty views on the goals of an undergraduate CS education? What are faculty views on the role of an undergraduate CS education in preparing students for industry? What are faculty views on better preparing students for careers in industry?

2.  **Inter-rater Reliability Testing:** Each author independently applied the marking scheme to a common set of 15 programs (three programs from five random students). The resulting scores were compared, and discrepancies were discussed. During this deliberation, raters influenced one another's judgments, leading to the adjustment of certain marks to ensure consistency.

Whereas software focuses on the program details for solving problems with computers, theoretical computer science focuses on the mathematical processes behind software. The idea of Turingcompleteness is a foundational concept in theoretical computer science, which considers how computers in general—not just the ENIAC or today’s computers, but even tomorrow’s computers that we haven’t yet invented—can solve problems. This theoretical perspective expands computer science knowledge by contributing ideas about (1) whether a problem can be computed by a Turingcomplete computer at all, (2) how that problem might be computed using an algorithm, and (3) how quickly or efficiently a computer can run such an algorithm. The answers to these questions suggest the limits of what we can achieve with computers from a technical perspective: Using mathematical ideas, is it possible to use a computer to compute all problems? If the answer to a problem is yes, how much of a computing resource is needed to get the answer? Clearly both humans and computers have their strengths and limitations. An example of a problem that humans can solve but computers struggle with is interpreting subtle emotions or making moral judgments in complex social situations. While computers can process data and recognize patterns, they cannot fully understand the nuances of human emotions or ethics, which often involve context, empathy, and experience. On the flip side, there are tasks that neither computers nor humans can perform, such as accurately predicting chaotic systems like longterm weather patterns. Despite advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), computer functions that perform tasks, such as visual perception and decisionmaking processes that usually are performed by human intelligence, these problems remain beyond our collective reach due to the inherent unpredictability and complexity of certain natural systems. Theoretical computer science is often emphasized in undergraduate computer science programs because academic computer science emerged from mathematics, often to the detriment of perspectives that center on the social and technical values embodied by applications of computer technology. These perspectives, however, are gradually changing. Just as the design of ARPANET shaped the design of the Internet, computer scientists are also learning that the physical aspects of computer hardware determine what can be efficiently computed. For example, many of today’s artificial intelligence technologies rely on highly specialized computer hardware that is fundamentally different at the physical level compared to the generalpurpose programmable silicon that has been the traditional focus of computer science.

Taking part in activities, whether with others or alone, plays an important role in personal growth and helps people develop valuable life skills. While it‚Äôs true that solo activities can enhance a range of abilities, I believe that joining group activities offers even greater benefits than doing things alone.

Group activities are especially effective at building social skills. One reason for this is that working in a team requires cooperation among members. As a result, participants not only strive to fulfill their own responsibilities but also support others in reaching their full potential. Achieving a team‚Äôs goal depends on everyone working together, not just individual effort. Additionally, group activities help improve communication skills. To succeed, team members must share their ideas clearly and simply so everyone understands. Because people in group activities are often exposed to such situations, they become more adept at communicating effectively.

Despite these benefits of teamwork, individual activities also develop important skills. For example, they foster self-discipline. In sports, for instance, someone who runs alone must stick to a training schedule to achieve their goals. Another advantage is that people who do activities alone are less likely to compare themselves to others. By setting personal milestones, individuals focus on their own progress and better understand their true abilities, without being influenced by others.

In conclusion, while solo activities can promote self-discipline and help people concentrate on their own abilities, I believe that group activities are more valuable overall. This is because they cultivate teamwork and communication skills, which are essential for success in life.

This study presents a dual analysis aimed at enhancing the understanding of student engagement and the methodological rigor of attitude assessment in computing education.

First, an examination of student reflection data was conducted to identify major themes and subthemes aligned with the MUSIC Model of Online Course Design. The analysis confirms the presence of all five core components—eMpowerment, Usefulness, Success, Interest, and Caring—within the course design framework. Findings indicate that structural elements, including module organization, content scope, project and assessment design, and the integration of instructional resources, are critical determinants in fostering an engaging online learning environment. While this design model demonstrates adaptability for various skill-based or project-based online modules, its efficacy relies on intentional planning and strict alignment with specific learning outcomes.

Second, this paper details the validation of a modified instrument for assessing computer science attitudes. Building upon Dorn and Tew's Computing Attitude Survey, the instrument was expanded to include items addressing gender dynamics and student perceptions regarding the utility of computing. These modifications respond to the widening gender disparity in computer science degree attainment. The revised survey explores attitudes toward women in the field and evaluates the perceived relevance of computer science to students' personal and professional trajectories. Consequently, a revalidation process was undertaken to compare the psychometric properties of the revised instrument against the original.

This work addresses a broader challenge identified by Sahami regarding the need for computing education research to establish itself as a more accepted and rigorous academic discipline. Sahami emphasizes the necessity of consistent, objective benchmarks to facilitate cross-study comparability and track field-wide progress. By validating this modified instrument, this study aims to contribute a foundational tool for measuring student attitudes, thereby supporting the development of such benchmarks. The research underscores the significance of factors such as self-efficacy, confidence, interest, and perceived usefulness, noting that deficits in self-efficacy often correlate with academic struggle. Furthermore, the study highlights how gender differences and prior successful experiences in computing significantly influence student attitudes and success rates, contextualized by the historical decline in female computer science graduates since the 1984 peak of 37.1%.

**Methodology and Model Architecture**
To enhance generation diversity, the approach described by [Author] et al. (2019a) involves injecting random noise into the encoder's hidden states, a technique previously validated by Kurata et al. (2016). For implementation, the current study utilizes the same Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network architecture as the baseline paraphrase model. Specifically, during the paraphrase generation phase, perturbations sampled from a uniform distribution ranging from 0.6 to 1.4 are applied multiplicatively to the encoder's hidden states.

The **SRPara** model incorporates the Semantic Role Paraphrase (SRPB) structure proposed by Wang et al. (2019). Within this framework, the SLING semantic parser (Ringgaard et al., 2017) is employed to analyze the semantic frame of an utterance and identify the semantic role of each constituent token. Subsequently, three distinct sequences—comprising the original tokens, semantic frame labels, and semantic role labels—are processed independently by three parallel encoders. The outputs from these encoders are projected via a linear layer before being transmitted to a decoder for paraphrase generation. The encoder and decoder implementations for SRPara are identical to those used in the NAEPara model.

To ensure a fair comparative analysis, all models were trained using the same datasets (CamRest676 or MultiWOZ). Specifically, the complete set of user utterances from the training corpora of CamRest676 or MultiWOZ was utilized to train the LSTM language model for WordSub and the policy network for TextSub. Furthermore, the paraphrase models (NAEPara and SRPara) were trained using the identical paraphrase dataset constructed as detailed in Section 3.1.

**5. Results and Analysis**
The experimental outcomes for the CamRest676 and MultiWOZ datasets are presented in Table 1 and Table 2, respectively. In both tables, the first row represents the baseline performance without data augmentation. Rows two through six display results derived from various data augmentation techniques (categorized as substitution-based or paraphrase-based), while the final row illustrates the performance of the proposed method. The results are stratified into three columns based on training data volume (20%, 50%, and 100%).

The experimental results across both datasets support several key observations. First, the proposed data augmentation framework consistently and significantly enhances the system's task completion capabilities—measured by Exact Match Rate (EMR), Success F1 (Succ.F1), Information F1 (Info), and Success Rate (Succ)—without compromising language fluency. This suggests that the integration of additional dialog paraphrases facilitates the learning of more informative responses by exposing the model to a broader spectrum of user expressions during training. Second, the proposed framework demonstrates superior performance compared to alternative data augmentation methods across all dialog task-relevant metrics under every tested condition.

**Fine-tuning versus Retraining**

Existing literature on adversarial training indicates that retraining models on augmented datasets enhances robustness (Belinkov & Bisk, 2018; Eger et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2019). However, this approach necessitates substantial computational resources. In contrast, our findings demonstrate that fine-tuning a pre-trained model for a single epoch is sufficient to achieve significant robustness against inflectional perturbations while maintaining high performance on clean evaluation sets (Table 5).

**6.3 Experimental Results**

*SpanBERT*
Adhering to the methodology of Joshi et al. (2019), we fine-tuned the SpanBERT SQuAD 2 model for an additional four epochs on our adversarial training set. As illustrated in Table 5, this approach yields immediate benefits: after just one epoch of fine-tuning, the model exhibits robustness against the majority of initial adversarial examples, with a performance degradation of less than two points on the clean development set. Crucially, subsequent application of the MORPHEUS attack fails to significantly compromise the robust model's performance.

While extending fine-tuning to four epochs restores performance on the clean SQuAD 2.0 development set to levels nearly equivalent to the original SpanBERT SQuAD 2, this comes at the cost of a slight reduction in accuracy on answerable questions. Consequently, if maximizing performance on answerable questions is the primary objective, our results suggest that a single epoch of fine-tuning on the adversarial set represents a more efficient and cost-effective strategy. Notably, full adversarial retraining of SpanBERT did not yield superior performance. Furthermore, we observed that weighting random sampling according to the adversarial distribution improved the robust model's performance on answerable questions (see Table 7 in the Appendix).

*Transformer-big*
Similarly, the Transformer-big model demonstrated a dramatic improvement in robustness following one epoch of fine-tuning on the adversarial training set, with the error rate decreasing from 56.25% to 20.20%. This improvement incurred a minor trade-off, resulting in an approximate 3-point drop in BLEU score on clean data (Table 5). Extending the fine-tuning process for an additional three epochs narrowed the performance gap on clean data but rendered the model less robust against novel adversarial examples. Additionally, experiments utilizing randomly sampled subsets indicated that training on the complete original dataset is essential for preserving performance on clean data (see Table 8 in the Appendix).

To address the primary technological constraint—specifically, the current lack of support for Safe Exam Browser (SEB) browser keys within Blackboard Learn—and to mitigate broader risks of academic misconduct, a comprehensive set of procedural safeguards has been established.

First, students are required to arrive at the examination venue early to initialize the SEB configuration file. Activation of this file is contingent upon a settings password, which is distributed by invigilators only fifteen minutes prior to the commencement of the examination. Access to examination materials on the Learning Management System (LMS) and Vocareum is further secured by a distinct test password. This credential is released only after invigilators have verified that all candidates have successfully launched SEB. To prevent unauthorized remote access or the sharing of credentials by students who exit the venue, the test password is altered fifteen minutes after the exam begins; thereafter, it remains known exclusively to the invigilating staff.

To ensure that examination content cannot be accessed outside the SEB environment, the browser's exit password feature is utilized. This password, required to terminate the SEB session, is communicated only after invigilators have confirmed the submission of all solutions. In the event of an involuntary system restart, SEB is configured to relaunch automatically. Should this automatic recovery fail, the requirement for the updated test password to access the LMS or Vocareum serves as a secondary control, immediately alerting invigilators to the anomaly.

Recognizing the potential for failure across multiple vectors—including SEB, the LMS, Vocareum, network connectivity, and student hardware—systematic contingency procedures have been designed to address all conceivable failure combinations. These protocols encompass the deployment of backup submission platforms, alternative Integrated Development Environments (IDEs), and reserve laptops. In the event of a total network failure, provisions are made for the manual submission of answers via paper and USB storage devices. Specific scenarios and corresponding protocols are detailed in the accompanying documentation.

Finally, the successful execution of this Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) model relies heavily on the personnel involved, particularly when examinations are conducted simultaneously across multiple venues. To ensure operational consistency, all invigilators undergo a unified training session, with specific emphasis on standardized procedures. Furthermore, the institution's Educational Technology team provides robust support through the conduct of mock examinations, software validation, policy development and refinement, and the provision of real-time technical assistance to both staff and students during the examination period.

$$U_{tg} = V_g [e(y_t), C_t^k] + b_g \quad (15)$$

where $V_g$ and $b_g$ represent learnable parameters. Consequently, the final probability distribution for a vocabulary word $w$ is formulated as:

$$P_{\text{final}}(w) = (\lambda u_{\text{gen}} + \mu k_{\text{gen}}) P_v(w) + \lambda (1 - u_{\text{gen}}) \sum_i u_t^i + \mu (1 - k_{\text{gen}}) \sum_i d_t^i \quad (16)$$

The vocabulary probability $P_v(w)$ is computed via a softmax function over a two-layer transformation:

$$P_v(w) = \text{softmax}(V_2 (V_1 [s_t, C_t^u, C_t^k] + b_1) + b_2) \quad (17)$$

In these equations, $V_1$, $V_2$, $b_1$, $b_2$, $\lambda$, and $\mu$ are learnable parameters subject to the constraint $\lambda + \mu = 1$. It is noted that if a word $w$ is an out-of-vocabulary (OOV) term and does not appear in the current utterance, $P_v(w)$ evaluates to zero; in such instances, the model relies on copying words from the knowledge base rather than the dialogue history.

### 3. Experiments

#### 3.1 Datasets

This study utilizes two recently released datasets: Wizard of Wikipedia and DuConv, which feature knowledge representations in the form of sentences and triplets, respectively.

**Wizard of Wikipedia** (Dinan et al., 2018): This is an open-domain chitchat dataset comprising dialogues between a "Wizard" (a knowledge expert) and an "Apprentice" (a curious learner). The Wizard has access to an information retrieval system capable of recalling relevant Wikipedia paragraphs, whereas the Apprentice does not observe this knowledge. The dataset consists of 22,311 dialogues containing 201,999 turns, partitioned into training (166,787), validation (17,715), and test (17,497) sets. The test set is further divided into two subsets: *Test Seen* (8,715 dialogues), which shares 533 topics with the training set, and *Test Unseen* (8,782 dialogues), which contains 58 topics not present in the training or validation sets. Ground-truth knowledge annotations provided in the original dataset are excluded from this study, as the model's capacity for autonomous knowledge selection during generation is a critical evaluation metric.

**DuConv** (Wu et al., 2019b): This is a proactive conversation dataset containing 29,858 dialogues and 270,399 utterances. In this setting, the model assumes the role of a leading agent assigned an explicit goal and a knowledge path consisting of two specific topics. The agent is provided with knowledge relevant to these designated topics to facilitate goal-oriented dialogue.

The dataset construction process began with questions authored by Wikipedia contributors, each paired with a hyperlink to a relevant Wikipedia article. These data were initially utilized to develop a Question Answering (QA) system, which was subsequently evaluated on a set of 1,500 questions. For each query, the top ten system-generated responses were manually annotated to determine correctness. From the 250 questions yielding positive responses, human annotators extracted only the relevant continuous segments. This procedure was extended to the remaining positive examples to derive concise responses, which were then compiled into positive question-answer pairs.

To curate challenging negative examples, the study employed Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) token overlap (Sennrich and Birch, 2016) to measure similarity between questions and potential answers. For each question, the four most similar negative candidates were selected, provided their similarity score exceeded a threshold of $\tau = 0.3$. Given that negative answers were, on average, significantly longer than positive ones—a characteristic that could introduce bias—the lengths were balanced. To identify the most relevant segments within negative examples, a modified BPE-based metric incorporating a penalty for sentence count was applied:

$$d_{\text{sim}}^{\text{BPE}} = \frac{\text{sim}_{\text{BPE}}}{1.2 \times \# \text{sents}}$$

The primary objective is to classify whether a given answer to a specific question is correct. Due to the high class imbalance inherent in the dataset, the F1-score was selected as the evaluation metric.

Additionally, the Polish Summaries Corpus (PSC) (Ogrodniczuk and Kopeć, 2014) was utilized, comprising summaries for 569 news articles. For each article, five extractive summaries were generated by distinct human annotators, each selecting approximately 5% of the original text. A subset of 154 articles was further augmented with five abstractive summaries per article, which were not derived directly from the source text fragments. Based on the PSC, a text similarity task was formulated. Positive pairs, representing summaries of the same article, were constructed using only articles containing both extractive and abstractive versions. Each extractive summary was paired with the two least similar abstractive summaries from the same article. The same BPE token overlap metric used in the preparation of the "Czy wiesz?" dataset was applied to calculate similarity between these extractive and abstractive summaries.

Existing scholarship regarding professional development through internships in the Computer Science (CS) industry remains limited. Current literature primarily addresses the role of internships in professional identity formation, identifies barriers to securing such positions, or explores student experiences during participation. However, a significant gap persists in research concerning the specific characteristics of CS students who successfully obtain internships, as well as the attributes that facilitate this success.

To address this gap, this study investigates the distinguishing characteristics of students who secure internships versus those who do not, utilizing Albert Bandura's Social Cognitive Theory as a theoretical framework. This theory delineates the properties of agentic behavior and their influence on an individual's capacity to establish and pursue goals. Bandura posits that human agency comprises four core properties: intentionality, forethought, self-reactiveness, and self-reflectiveness. Intentionality refers to the deliberate planning and strategic formulation required to achieve specific outcomes. Forethought involves the temporal extension of agency, enabling individuals to visualize future scenarios through cognitive representations that guide prospective actions. Beyond planning and foresight, agency encompasses self-reactiveness, which empowers agents to construct appropriate courses of action and regulate their behaviors. Finally, self-reflectiveness allows for the metacognitive examination of one's functioning, facilitating necessary corrections for future endeavors. Bandura asserts that individuals who cultivate competencies, self-regulatory skills, and efficacy beliefs are more likely to realize desired futures than those with underdeveloped agentic resources.

This theoretical framework informs the coding and interpretation of both qualitative and quantitative data regarding students' internship-seeking behaviors and outcomes. The study posits that securing an internship—a desired future outcome—necessitates the exercise of agency, which in turn fosters the cognitive development of requisite skills. Manifestations of agency can be observed through behavioral proxies, including the application for positions, preparation for interviews, and the acquisition of technical and professional skills valued by employers.

Methodologically, this research employs a cross-sectional, multi-institutional study utilizing a Concurrent Triangulation Design. This approach facilitates an understanding of CS student participation in internships and broader professional development activities through the concurrent collection of survey data and semi-structured interviews. In this design, qualitative and quantitative data are gathered simultaneously, analyzed independently, and subsequently integrated to provide a comprehensive analysis.

Development environments serve as a critical interface for novice programmers, functioning not only as platforms for code entry and execution but also as mechanisms for error reporting, input/output management, and access to the underlying notional machine. While several educational programming environments for Python have emerged in recent years, the critical issue of error reporting remains largely unaddressed. Evaluations frequently indicate that the primary obstacle for students is the poor quality of standard Python error messages. Consequently, an educational Python environment featuring enhanced error reporting has been developed. This paper outlines the design rationale and three core features of this environment: modifications to the Python language, improved error messaging, and an integrated visual debugger.

Ideally, the design of a programming environment should foster a comprehensive understanding of the interrelationships between the user, the program code, the executing program, and the underlying notional machine. Although Python distributions include IDLE—a simple environment based on an interactive shell with an integrated editor—initial transitions from Java to Python in introductory high school curricula revealed significant limitations. Beyond general usability concerns, students encountered substantial difficulties due to the ambiguity of Python's default error messages, necessitating the development of a more robust educational tool.

The development of the marine chronometer, a device essential for the precise determination of longitude, required a century of effort before John Harrison of England successfully engineered the first accurate model. While the underlying scientific and mathematical principles for geolocation were already established, the practical resolution of the problem depended on technological innovation. For a detailed account of this development and the challenges faced by Harrison, Dava Sobel's *Longitude* is recommended.

Similarly, the evolution of the steam engine illustrates the distinction between invention and commercial viability. Although the initial designs were attributed to Thomas Savery and Thomas Newcomen, James Watt introduced two critical improvements: an external condenser, which prevented the cooling of the steam cylinder, and a slide valve mechanism that facilitated the automatic admission of steam regardless of piston direction. Despite these technical advancements, Watt faced significant barriers to market success in mid-18th-century England. The prevailing business model required the inventor to finance the manufacturing, installation, and maintenance of the engines, a capital-intensive endeavor beyond Watt's individual means. Consequently, he sold a fifty percent interest in his patent to John Roebuck. While this partnership enabled the construction of several engines, limited financial resources and the impending expiration of the patent hindered further expansion.

The situation was resolved through the intervention of Matthew Boulton, who possessed substantial financial capital and influential connections. Boulton acquired Roebuck's share and successfully secured a seventeen-year extension of the patent, thereby protecting his investment. Leveraging his financial strength, the Boulton & Watt partnership introduced an innovative commercial model: engines were provided to purchasers at no upfront cost in exchange for a royalty of ten percent of the annual fuel savings generated by the engine's superior efficiency. Given that competing engines, which often exhausted steam directly to the atmosphere, were markedly inefficient, the Watt engine delivered significant cost reductions, ensuring a steady revenue stream for the inventors.

This historical narrative underscores a fundamental principle of industrial innovation: while technical ingenuity is a prerequisite for invention, the successful commercialization of a product typically requires a multidisciplinary approach. Rarely does a single individual possess the requisite combination of technical expertise, marketing acumen, and financial resources; thus, collaborative effort is essential for achieving economic success.

**Abstract**

This study employs a specific analytical methodology to evaluate BERT and RoBERTa architectures under the hypothesis that specific attention heads consistently establish connections from tokens within a negation scope to their corresponding negation cues. Our findings indicate that following fine-tuning on a negation scope task, both BERT and RoBERTa exhibit enhanced sensitivity to negation and demonstrate greater attentional consistency across diverse negation datasets compared to their pre-trained counterparts. However, this improvement relative to a control task is observed exclusively in base model variants, rather than large-scale models. This disparity suggests that evidence for the shallow encoding of negation is present only within the base architectures.

**1. Introduction**

The advent of large-scale pre-trained language models, such as BERT and ELMo, has yielded significant performance gains across a broad spectrum of natural language processing tasks (Peters et al., 2018a; Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). Consequently, a substantial body of research has emerged dedicated to elucidating the linguistic properties acquired by these models. Recent scholarship frequently utilizes "probes"—supervised models trained to predict specific linguistic features, including morphology (Belinkov et al., 2017), syntax (Hewitt and Manning, 2019), and semantics (Peters et al., 2018b)—as a means of assessment (see Belinkov and Glass, 2019, for a comprehensive survey). High performance in probing tasks is generally interpreted as evidence that the underlying language models have successfully internalized the requisite linguistic knowledge.

However, the extent to which this encoded linguistic knowledge evolves when a pre-trained model is fine-tuned for a downstream task remains insufficiently understood. Peters et al. (2019) applied supervised probes to BERT both prior to and following fine-tuning, positing that fine-tuning renders internal representations task-specific. Nevertheless, the use of supervised probes presents challenges in disentangling knowledge acquired by the probe itself from knowledge inherent in the model's internal representations (Hewitt and Liang, 2019). In response, recent studies have shifted toward unsupervised probing methods that require no additional model training. Instead, these approaches analyze the attention mechanisms directly, examining the degree to which the model weights other tokens during the computation of a target token's representation. For instance, Clark et al. (2019) inspected pre-trained transformers and identified several syntactic properties encoded intuitively, noting that maximum attention from a dependent often aligns with its syntactic head. However, such investigations have predominantly focused on pre-trained models, leaving a gap in understanding how these intuitive encodings are affected by fine-tuning for downstream applications.

**Methodology and Data Collection**

Data collection was conducted through a multi-method approach comprising observational studies, semi-structured interviews, and a quantitative survey. Observations were carried out across three distinct teams, with each team monitored for a duration of one week. This timeframe aligns with the organization's standard one-week Extreme Programming (XP) iteration cycle, thereby enabling the comprehensive observation of all processes typically executed within a single iteration. Additionally, the research team participated in select meetings of other teams to broaden the scope of inquiry.

The observational protocol began with a broad examination of general workflows, subsequently narrowing the focus to specific behaviors and meetings most pertinent to the established taxonomy. To triangulate these findings, five semi-structured interviews were conducted with four employees, comprising one product manager and three engineers. Furthermore, a twelve-item survey was developed to assess stakeholder perceptions regarding the importance of various processes within the Pivotal 2 environment. This survey was distributed internally to the entire developer population via an email list by a designated organizational contact, yielding a total of 18 responses.

**Analytical Framework**

The analysis utilized Marks et al.'s taxonomy of team processes, which categorizes team activities into transition, action, and interpersonal processes. Transition processes are defined as activities where teams primarily engage in evaluation and planning to guide the achievement of objectives. Action processes refer to periods where teams execute activities that directly contribute to goal accomplishment. Interpersonal processes are characterized as foundational activities that underpin the effectiveness of other team processes.

A closed coding method was employed to analyze the data, utilizing codes derived directly from the aforementioned taxonomy. Observation notes, interview notes, and transcripts were subjected to line-by-line coding by a single researcher. Within the subsequent analysis, processes highlighted in bold denote those selected for integration into the course curriculum and are described in greater detail herein.

**Key Observations: Iteration Planning Meetings (IPM)**

A primary focus of the observation was the Iteration Planning Meeting (IPM), which occurs at the commencement of each iteration. A central objective of the IPM is to establish a shared understanding of the value, constraints, and acceptance criteria for each user story—the fundamental unit of work within an iteration. As noted in survey responses, this ensures that all team members possess a common understanding of the stories in the backlog.

In the observed IPMs, the typical procedure involved a designated individual presenting each user story. The team then engaged in clarification discussions to align on expectations. The meeting protocol dictated that progression would not occur until all developers confirmed that the expectations were clear. Selected details arising from these discussions were subsequently documented within the user story descriptions.

For each training instance $(x, y)$, constraint feature extractors are applied to derive positive examples in the form $(\psi(x, y), +1)$. Negative examples are generated through several straightforward yet effective methodologies. One approach involves applying minor perturbations to the structure $y$ within a training pair $(x, y)$ to produce a modified structure $y'$, which is presumed invalid. Applying the constraint feature extractor to this perturbed structure yields a negative example $(\psi(x, y'), -1)$. It is imperative to ensure that $\psi(x, y')$ remains distinct from any positive examples. Alternatively, the feature vector $\psi(x, y)$ may be perturbed directly, bypassing structural modification. In the empirical evaluations presented in subsequent sections, both methodologies are employed to generate negative examples; comprehensive details are provided in the supplementary material. Despite their simplicity, these approaches yielded observable performance improvements. The investigation of more sophisticated perturbation techniques for structures or features—such as those proposed by Smith and Eisner (2005) or adversarial learning frameworks (Goodfellow et al., 2014)—constitutes a direction for future research.

### 4. Entity and Relation Extraction Experiments

In the domain of entity and relation extraction, the input consists of a sentence accompanied by a set of entity candidates. The objective is to classify the type of each candidate, as illustrated in the following example (where labels are underlined):

> [Organization Google LLC] is headquartered in [Location Mountain View, California].

Furthermore, the task requires the determination of directed relations between entities. In the aforementioned example, the relation from "Google LLC" to "Mountain View, California" is classified as *OrgBasedIn*, whereas the reverse direction is labeled *NoRel*, indicating the absence of a relation. This task necessitates the prediction of a directed graph and exemplifies a typical structured prediction problem, wherein isolated predictions for entities and relations are insufficient.

### The Language

As noted by Habash (2010), Arabic varieties are typically categorized into three distinct groups: (i) Classical Arabic, as preserved in the Qur'an and related canonical texts; (ii) Modern Standard Arabic, which serves as the official language for the vast majority of Arabic-speaking nations; and (iii) Dialectal Arabic, characterized by substantial variation that often precludes mutual intelligibility across geographically distant regions. Given the constraints of space, an exhaustive description of Arabic linguistic variation is beyond the scope of this work. Readers are therefore referred to Habash (2010), Samih (2017), and, most notably, Saadane and Habash (2015) for a comprehensive account of Algerian dialectal Arabic, which is the primary focus of this study.

Similar to conventional ranking frameworks, discriminative reranking models may be trained utilizing a margin-based hinge loss. This objective function ensures that the score assigned to the correct parse tree exceeds that of any incorrect candidate by a margin of at least $m$. The loss function is defined as:

$$L(y, t) = \max(0, s(x, t, \Theta) + m - s(x, y, \Theta)), \quad \forall t \in \text{gen}(x) \setminus \{y\} \quad (5)$$

Zhu et al. (2015) employ a structured margin defined as $m = \kappa \Delta(y, t)$, where $\Delta(y, t)$ represents the count of erroneous edges in candidate $t$ relative to the gold standard $y$. The parameter $\kappa$ serves as a discount hyperparameter that modulates the influence of the structural difference $\Delta$ on the loss calculation. Furthermore, the final loss is typically computed using the highest-scoring tree predicted by the model, denoted as $\hat{y}$. Alternatively, the loss associated with the single predicted tree may be substituted with the average loss computed across all trees within the candidate list.

### 3.3 Mixture Reranking Models

The aforementioned models do not incorporate scores derived from the base parser during the ranking process. Consequently, it is reasonable to hypothesize that integrating the strengths of both the base parser and the reranker could yield a superior final model. The predominant approach to achieve this integration is to treat the base parser and the reranker as a mixture model. The score of a reranking model, $s_r$, can be combined with the score of the base parser, $s_b$, via a linear combination:

$$s(x, y) = \alpha s_r(x, y, \Theta) + (1 - \alpha) s_b(x, y) \quad (6)$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is a weighting parameter.

### 4. Evaluation of Neural Rerankers for Dependency Parsing

This section presents a systematic evaluation of various neural reranking models applied to $k$-best lists generated by diverse parsers. Initial experiments aim to replicate the reported results for existing rerankers (specifically IORNN and RCNN) on English data. Subsequently, the performance of these rerankers is assessed on German and Czech datasets. Unless otherwise specified, all comparative results are based on Unlabeled Attachment Score (UAS) and Labeled Attachment Score (LAS), inclusive of punctuation.

**English Data:** Following the methodology of Zhu et al. (2015), the Penn Treebank (PTB) is utilized with standard data splits: sections 2–21 for training, section 22 for development, and section 23 for testing. The reranking models are applied to unlabeled parse trees.

The initial state of the guider network is established by encoding the true input sentence using a convolutional neural network (CNN). Formally, this is expressed as $s^G_0 = \text{Enc}(X)$, where $\text{Enc}(\cdot)$ represents the encoder transformation implemented via a CNN (Zhang et al., 2017). Crucially, at each time step, the input to the guider network comprises features derived from the entire sequence generated up to that point. This mechanism provides the LSTM decoder with a global contextual guide, thereby accounting for the holistic properties of the generated text.

In the context of text generation with planning, the guider network facilitates next-word prediction for the generator (the LSTM decoder). This framework, inspired by Model Predictive Control (MPC) methods (Nagabandi et al., 2017), functions as a form of plan-ahead attention. Given the feature $f_t$ at time $t$ from the current input sentence, the guider network generates a future feature representation, $G_\psi(s^G_{t-1}, f_t)$, by processing $f_t$ through the LSTM guider. Since the guider network is trained on real data, the resulting predicted feature encapsulates global structural information inherent to the training corpus.

To leverage this information for next-word prediction, the predicted feature is integrated with the decoder's output through an attention-like mechanism. Specifically, a linear transformation $\phi$ is applied to the predicted feature $G_\psi(s^G_{t-1}, f_t)$ to produce a weight vector $w_t \triangleq \phi(G_\psi(s^G_{t-1}, f_t))$. This weight vector is then applied to the LSTM decoder's output $O_t$ via element-wise multiplication. The resulting vector is subsequently passed through a softmax layer to generate the next token $y_t$.

The contributions of our work can be summarized as follows. First, our results demonstrate that the relationship between LM perplexity and lexical frequency is consistent with the phenomenology of DAT and its deleterious effects on patients’ vocabulary. We show that the “two perplexities” approach is successful at distinguishing between cases and controls in the DementiaBank corpus because of its ability to capture specifically linguistic manifestations of the disease. Second, we observe that interpolating between dementia and control LMs mitigates the tendency of dementiabased LMs to be “surprised” by transcripts indicating severe dementia, which is detrimental to performance when the difference between these LMs is used as a basis for classification. In addition, we find a similar smoothing effect when using pretrained word embeddings in place of a randomly instantiated word embedding layer. Finally, we develop a modification of Fritsch et al’s “two perplexity” approach that is consistent with these observations - replacing the dementia model with an interpolated variant, and introducing pretrained word embeddings at the embedding layer. Both modifications exhibit significant improvements in performance, with best results obtained by using them in tandem. Though not strictly comparable on account of differences in segmentation of the corpus amongst others, we note the performance obtained also exceeds that reported with models trained on text alone in prior research. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel edit operation, **DeleteKeepBefore**, which establishes a unique span by searching for context preceding the edit location. This structure mirrors that of *ReplaceKeepBefore*: the tokens associated with `DeleteOldKeepBefore` are replaced by those in `DeleteNewKeepBefore`, with an overlap occurring at the beginning of the respective spans. In the specific example under consideration, the corresponding edit representation is as follows:

`<DeleteOldKeepBefore> AB <DeleteNewKeepBefore> A <DeleteEnd>`

The span linked to `DeleteOldKeepBefore` is unique, thereby explicitly identifying the first instance of "B" for deletion. Furthermore, this notation indicates that the token "A," which precedes the edit location, is retained.

Conversely, the **DeleteKeepAfter** operation is defined as:

`<DeleteOldKeepAfter>[old span] <DeleteNewKeepAfter>[new span] <DeleteEnd>`

This structure is employed when the deletion span is not unique and a preceding unique sequence of unchanged tokens cannot be identified to utilize *DeleteKeepBefore*. For instance, consider the transformation of $C_{old} = \text{ABCAB}$ to $C_{new} = \text{ACAB}$, where the first "B" is to be deleted. A search preceding the edit location yields only "AB," which is not unique within $C_{old}$; consequently, it remains ambiguous which "B" should be removed. To resolve this, we apply **DeleteKeepAfter**, which constructs a unique span by searching for context following the edit location. Similar to *ReplaceKeepAfter*, the tokens in `DeleteOldKeepAfter` are replaced by those in `DeleteNewKeepAfter`, with an overlap at the end of the spans. For the aforementioned example, the edit representation is:

`<DeleteOldKeepAfter> BC <DeleteNewKeepAfter> C <DeleteEnd>`

Here, the span attached to `DeleteOldKeepAfter` is unique, clearly designating the first "B" for deletion while indicating that the token "C," which follows the edit location, is preserved.

### Data Filtering

Following the methodology of Panthaplackel et al. (2020), we apply heuristics to minimize instances where code modifications and comment updates are unrelated. Specifically, given our focus on `@return` comments pertaining to the return values of a method, we exclude any examples where the code change does not involve either a modification to the return type or at least one return statement.

**Abstract**

This paper introduces the Text-Based Ideal Point (TBIP) model, an unsupervised probabilistic topic modeling framework designed to quantify the political positions of authors through the analysis of textual data. The efficacy of the TBIP is demonstrated using two distinct datasets comprising politicized text: speeches from the U.S. Senate and tweets from senators. Although the model operates independently of voting records or explicit party affiliations, it successfully differentiates lawmakers by party, generates interpretable politicized topics, and infers ideal points that closely align with classical vote-based estimates. A primary advantage of this text-centric approach over traditional vote-based analysis is its capacity to estimate the ideal points of any entity producing political discourse, including non-voting actors. Consequently, the model is applied to the 2020 Democratic presidential primary, where it successfully maps candidates along a coherent spectrum ranging from progressive to moderate based solely on their tweet content.

**1. Introduction**

Ideal point models have long served as a standard methodology for characterizing modern democracies by analyzing legislative voting records to estimate the positions of lawmakers on a political spectrum (Poole and Rosenthal, 1985). However, voting behavior represents only one dimension of political expression; press releases, social media posts, and speeches also convey significant political preferences. Like voting records, these textual signals are systematically recorded and readily accessible. This study develops the Text-Based Ideal Point (TBIP) model, a probabilistic topic model engineered to analyze unstructured political texts and quantify the underlying preferences of their authors.

While classical ideal point models examine how individuals vote on a shared set of legislation, the TBIP analyzes how different authors articulate a shared set of latent topics. The model is grounded in the theory of political framing, which posits that the specific lexical choices employed when discussing a topic can convey distinct political messages (Entman, 1993). Given a corpus of political texts, the TBIP estimates the latent topics under discussion, the latent political positions of the authors, and the variation in word choice per topic as a function of the author's political stance.

A defining characteristic of the TBIP is its unsupervised nature. It can be applied to any political text corpus regardless of whether the authors belong to established political parties. Furthermore, it facilitates the analysis of non-voting actors, such as political candidates. Figure 1 illustrates an application of the TBIP to the speeches of the 114th U.S. Senate.

The lexicon contains examples such as the word for "good" tagged as an adjective with a positive polarity score of +0.7, and the word for "bad" tagged as an adjective with a negative polarity score of –0.69. Most of the previous research on sentiment used adjectives to identify the polarity of sentences. Some researchers used adverbs and adjectives together to build a lexicon; some used adjectives, adverbs, and verbs. For our Persian sentiment lexicon, we used adjectives, adverbs, verbs, and nouns, because all these words and phrases are useful to determine the polarity of the sentence. A lexicon can be developed in different ways, such as manually or using existing lexicons such as SentiWordNet or General Inquirer. The words and phrases used in our lexicon were taken from different resources such as movie review websites, weblogs, and Facebook. There were four different categories of sources, namely, websites related to movies, news, mobile phones, and computers. We manually assigned polarity between –1 and +1 to each word and phrase. The degree of intensity was indicated: for example, the Persian words for "happy", "cheerful", and "delighted" have different positive values. In order to assign polarity manually to some words and phrases, we used the TextBlob Python package, used to assign polarity to words, phrases, and sentences in English. For this, we translated Persian words into English. We also manually assigned a part of speech (POS) tag to each word or phrase. The lexicon contains 556 adjectives, 51 adverbs, 371 nouns, and 522 verbs. In order to evaluate the performance of our lexicon, we used two classification algorithms; we used our lexicon to assign polarity to the features extracted from the dataset. The preprocessing step consisted of four parts: tokenisation, normalisation, stopword removal, and stemming. Normalization was used to remove noise from the text. Stemming was used to remove inflection of infected forms: the lexicon only provides the base form of the words. The purpose of the feature selection was to remove unnecessary features, which improved the performance and efficiency of the classification. The features we used were based on word polarity, POS tag, and presence and frequency of sentiment words. Presence and frequency of sentiment words. The sentiment words identify the overall polarity for sentiment classification.

Open Information Extraction (OIE) systems, which primarily rely on syntactic features, demonstrate significant adaptability across diverse domains with minimal fine-tuning and are capable of extracting a wide variety of relation types (Mausam, 2016). These characteristics are particularly advantageous in the context of scientific literature, where the extraction of knowledge regarding unfamiliar concepts can facilitate comprehension for non-specialist audiences. For instance, the OIE extractions associated with the term "xylem," as illustrated in the upper portion of Figure 1, exemplify this utility.

However, empirical evidence indicates that existing OIE systems exhibit a marked decline in performance when processing the longer, more complex sentences characteristic of scientific texts, compared to their performance on Wikipedia content (Groth et al., 2018). Common challenges encountered by OIE systems when applied to web, news, and Wikipedia corpora include the accurate delineation of argument boundaries, the management of latent n-ary relations, the handling of negation, and the generation of uninformative extractions (Schneider et al., 2017). In their evaluation of two state-of-the-art OIE systems, Groth et al. (2018) prioritized correctness over metrics such as recall, positing that the fundamental challenge of Information Extraction (IE) lies in ensuring that extractions accurately reflect the semantic consequences of the source sentence. Illustrating the issue of uninformative output, Fader et al. (2011) observe that while the extraction "(Faust, made, a deal)" captures the general consequence of the sentence "Faust made a deal with the devil," it fails to convey the critical information regarding the counterparty involved. Consequently, this study investigates the filtering of both incorrect and uninformative OIE extractions from scientific texts by leveraging central concepts identified through narrow IE (see Section 5.3).

### 2.2 Narrow Relation Extraction from Scientific Text

Narrow Relation Extraction (RE) involves the identification of two or more related entities within a text and the subsequent classification of the specific relation holding between them. Early research addressing the combined tasks of Named Entity Recognition (NER) and relation labeling predominantly utilized precomputed dependency features (Liu et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2016), word position embeddings (Zeng et al., 2014), or restricted input to the Shortest Dependency Path between entity pairs (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Santos et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2015).

For example in Figure 2 , if code “464.00 ( acute laryngitis without mention of obstruction )” is assigned to a patient, it is unlikely to assign the code “464.01 ( acute laryngitis with obstruction )” to the patient at the same time. If automatic ICD coding models ignore such a characteristic, they are prone to giving inconsistent predictions. Thus, a challenging problem is how to model the code hierarchy and use it to capture the mutual exclusion of codes. Code Cooccurrence : Since some diseases are concurrent or have a causal relationship with each other, their codes usually cooccur in the clinical text, such as “997.91 ( hypertension )” and “429.9 ( heart disease )”. In this paper, we call such characteristic code cooccurrence which can capture the correlations of codes. The code cooccurrence can be utilized to correctly predict some codes which are difficult to predict by only using the clinical text itself. For example in Figure 1 , the code of “ acute respiratory failure ” can be easily inferred via capturing apparent clues (i.e., the green bold words) from the text. Although there are also a few clues to infer the code of “ acidosis ”, they are very obscure, let alone predict the code of “ acidosis ” by only using these obscure clues. Fortunately, there is a strong association between these two diseases: one of the main causes of “ acidosis ” is “ acute respiratory failure ”. This prior knowledge can be captured via the fact that the codes of the two diseases usually cooccur in clinical texts. By considering the correlation, the automatic ICD coding model can better exploit obscure clues to predict the code of “ acidosis ”. Therefore, another problem is how to leverage code cooccurrence for ICD coding. In this paper, we propose a novel method termed as Hyper bolic and Co -graph Re presentation method (HyperCore) to address above problems. Since the treelikeness properties of the hyperbolic space make it more suitable for representing symbolic data with hierarchical structures than the Euclidean space, we propose a hyperbolic representation learning method to learn the Code Hierarchy. Meanwhile, the graph has been proved effective in modeling data correlation and the graph convolutional network (GCN) enables to efficiently learn node representation.
