**Analysis of Attention Head Patterns**

Notably, the ELECTRA-LARGE model exhibits distinct patterns across the majority of probing tasks. Specifically, high-performing attention heads are predominantly situated in the lower layers, with the exception of the OddManOut task. Conversely, attention heads in the lower layers of other configurations appear to process minimal information relevant to these probing tasks. While ELECTRA-SMALL and ELECTRA-BASE demonstrate similar heat map distributions (see Appendix B), the ELECTRA-LARGE model diverges significantly from these counterparts. These observations suggest that learning behaviors associated with attention heads are not strictly uniform across models, even when they share identical architectures and pretraining objectives.

**4.4 Selection of Influential Attention Heads**

Given the observation that specific attention heads across various layers contribute differently to distinct probing tasks, we developed a method to generate novel embeddings, as detailed in Section 3.3. We subsequently conducted an experiment comparing this approach against two baselines across ten probing tasks. Table 3 presents a comparative analysis of three embedding types constructed using the BERT family: the final layer output ($z_i = L$), the optimal layer output ($z_{best}$), and the reconstructed sentence embedding ($h_n = H$) for each task and pretrained encoder (comprehensive results are provided in Appendix B).

A comparison of accuracy between the final layer and the optimal layer reveals that the final layer does not outperform the optimal layer for any of the probing tasks. This indicates that certain linguistic features are primarily processed in earlier layers and are not further refined in subsequent layers. Furthermore, the performance comparison between the optimal layer output and the proposed reconstructed sentence embedding demonstrates a significant increase in classification accuracy, with a median improvement of 19.22% across nearly all tasks. These findings strongly support the efficacy of the proposed method in identifying superior attention heads to construct final representations tailored for specific linguistic information. It is important to note that these newly constructed sentence embeddings consist exclusively of attention head outputs. Our results imply that these embeddings retain substantial information comparable to the hidden states generated by the full multi-head attention and feed-forward network layers.

**5. Enhancement of Downstream Tasks**

**5.1 Downstream Tasks from GLUE**

To evaluate whether the proposed embedding construction method extracts not only simple linguistic features but also rich sentence-level representations suitable for complex applications, we assessed its performance on downstream tasks derived from the GLUE benchmark.

A counselor who leans too much on advancing forwards could rush the conversation at the expense of establishing an empathetic connection; a counselor who leans too much backwards , on addressing what was already said, may fail to make any progress. 1 These examples are derived from material used to train counselors in our particular setting, detailed in Section 2 . In this work, we develop a method to examine counselor behaviors as they relate to this balancing challenge. We quantify the relative extent to which an utterance is aimed at advancing the conversation, versus addressing existing content. We thus map each utterance onto a continuous backwardsforwards axis which models the balance of these objectives, and refer to an utterance’s position on this axis as its orientation . At an intuitive level, our approach considers the range of content that is expected to follow or precede a particular utterance. For an utterance like c 1 that aims to advance the conversation towards an intended target, we would expect a narrow range of appropriate replies, concentrated around that target (e.g., suggestions of possible confidants). We would likewise expect an utterance like c 2 that aims to address a previouslydiscussed situation to only be an appropriate reply for a narrow range of possible utterances, concentrated around that specific type of situation (e.g., disclosures of negative feelings). Starting from this intuition, we develop an unsupervised method to quantify and compare these expected forwards and backwards ranges for any utterance, yielding our orientation measure. Using this measure, we characterize counselor behaviors in a large collection of textmessage conversations from a crisis counseling service, which we accessed in collaboration with the service and with the participants’ consent. We show how orientation meaningfully distinguishes between key conversational strategies that counselors are taught during their training. We also show that our measure tracks a conversation’s progress and can signal its effectiveness, highlighting the importance of balancing the advancing and addressing objectives, and laying the basis for future inquiries in establishing potential causal effects. In summary, we develop an unsupervised methodology that captures how counselors balance the conversational objectives of advancing and addressing (Section 4 ), apply and validate it in a large dataset of counseling conversations (Section 5 ), and use it to investigate the relation between a counselor’s conversational behavior and their effectiveness (Section 5.4 ).

The curriculum comprised eight distinct modules. Module 1 addressed surfaces and solids of revolution, defined as geometric forms generated by rotating two-dimensional curves around a coordinate axis. Module 2 focused on isometric drawings, which represent three-dimensional objects on a two-dimensional plane; specifically, an isometric view is oriented along the diagonal of a constituent cube. Module 3 covered coded plans, presenting a top-down projection of isometric shapes wherein each unit cube is annotated according to its vertical height. Modules 4 and 5 examined orthographic drawings, which depict object faces parallel to the viewing plane, encompassing front, side, and top elevations. Module 6 introduced rotations about a single axis, involving the rotation of an object around a fixed straight line. Module 7 extended this concept to rotations about two or more axes. Finally, Module 8 explored reflections and symmetry; reflection is defined as the transformation of an object across a plane, while symmetry exists when a plane bisects an object into two mirror-image halves.

To evaluate the correlation between students' spatial skills and programming proficiency, Pearson's correlation coefficient ($r$) was employed. This coefficient ranges from +1 to -1, where an absolute value approaching 1 signifies a strong association, and values nearing 0 indicate a weak relationship. To assess the efficacy of the intervention in enhancing both spatial skills and programming ability, a $t$-test was applied to data exhibiting a normal distribution. For non-normally distributed data, non-parametric alternatives, specifically the Kruskal-Wallis test or a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), were utilized to determine statistical significance via associated $p$-values. Normality was verified using the D'Agostino and Pearson omnibus test, which integrates measures of skewness and kurtosis. Throughout this study, a significance threshold of $p < 0.01$ was adopted to denote statistically significant results.

We posit normal hierarchical priors defined as follows:

$$
p(H_u | \tau_{h_u}) = \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} \mathcal{N}(h_i^u; 0, \tau_{h_u}^{-1} I),
$$
$$
p(U | H_u, \tau_u) = \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} \mathcal{N}(u_i; s_i^u, \tau_u^{-1} I),
$$

where $\tau_{h_u}$ and $\tau_u$ denote the precision hyperparameters. Analogously, we assume normal hierarchical priors for $p(V | H_v, \tau_v)$ and $p(H_v | \tau_{h_v})$. Consequently, the joint density of the data $\mathcal{D}$ and the model parameters $\theta = \{U, V, H_u, H_v\}$, conditioned on the precision hyperparameters $\mathcal{T} = \{\tau_u, \tau_v, \tau_{h_u}, \tau_{h_v}\}$, is expressed as:

$$
p(\mathcal{D}, \theta | \mathcal{T}) = p(\mathcal{D} | \theta)p(\theta | \mathcal{T}), \quad (1)
$$

where the likelihood is given by $p(\mathcal{D} | \theta) = p(\mathcal{D} | U, V) = \prod_{(i, j) \in \mathcal{O}} \sigma(d_{ij} u_i^\top v_j)$, and the prior term expands to $p(U | H_u, \tau_u)p(H_u | \tau_{h_u})p(V | H_v, \tau_v)p(H_v | \tau_{h_v})$. Figure 1 illustrates the graphical model of the BHWR framework.

The objective is to compute the posterior predictive distribution for an arbitrary entry $d_{ij}^*$ given $\mathcal{D}$ (where $d_{ij}^*$ is not necessarily contained within $\mathcal{D}$). The probability of co-occurrence between words $w_i$ and $w_j$ is defined as:

$$
p(d_{ij}^* = 1 | \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{T}) = \int \sigma(u_i^\top v_j) p(\theta | \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{T}) \, d\theta. \quad (2)
$$

### 3.2 Posterior Approximation

Given that the posterior distribution $p(\theta | \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{T})$ in Equation (2) is analytically intractable, we employ a Variational Bayes (VB) approximation (Bishop, 2006). This approach approximates the posterior via a fully factorized distribution $q(\theta)$:

$$
q(\theta) = q(U)q(V)q(H_u)q(H_v) = \left( \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} q(u_i) \right) \left( \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} q(v_i) \right) \left( \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} q(h_i^u) \right) \left( \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}} q(h_i^v) \right).
$$

Generally, if the government takes action to address these issues‚Äîby enforcing effective laws to boost public safety and happiness, and by properly funding sanitation boards to reduce water stagnation during the rainy season‚Äîconditions could improve. Investing in research and development could also help clear up air and water pollution, leading to better health for city residents.

Alternatively, the government could focus on developing nearby towns by providing essential facilities like modern housing, better education, good roads, and proper water and sanitation systems. These improvements could encourage people to move to smaller towns, easing the pressure on major cities.

For example, Delhi, the capital of India, is grappling with severe air pollution and safety concerns due to its high population. Research indicates that these problems could be mitigated by reducing population density. Meanwhile, some suggest that adopting successful technologies or urban planning models from other cities around the world could also help. I personally believe this could be an effective solution if implemented by the government.

Considering both the positive and negative aspects, it‚Äôs difficult to say which approach would work best to solve these urban issues.

In educational research, data are frequently drawn from naturally clustered systems, such as students nested within courses, which are themselves nested within institutions. Such clustering can introduce bias into statistical estimates due to the increased similarity of individuals within the same cluster compared to those in different clusters, regarding both measured and unmeasured variables. Multilevel modeling (MLM) addresses this issue by accounting for multiple levels of data hierarchy. This approach is compatible with various traditional single-level statistical techniques, including regression, random-coefficient models, and structural equation modeling (SEM). In constructing a multilevel model, relationships are specified among variables at the within-level (micro-level or Level 1), the between-levels (macro-levels, e.g., Level 2, Level 3), and potentially across these levels.

I disagree with the statement that successful people focus on trying new things and taking risks rather than sticking to what they already do well, for three main reasons.

First, successful individuals often choose to refine and deepen their existing skills and knowledge, even if they are already proficient. While learning new things and taking risks can help build confidence and expand abilities, constantly switching focus might actually weaken the skills they have worked hard to develop. By concentrating on what they already excel at, they can maintain and further strengthen their expertise.

Second, successful people aim to use their skills and knowledge in the future. Those who have a passion and talent for a particular field often dream of pursuing careers that require their specific abilities. Unlike average individuals who may have a range of interests‚Äîsuch as tennis, piano, or karate‚Äîsuccessful people tend to focus intensely on what truly interests them and what they want to improve.

Lastly, competition is a key reason why successful people continue to hone the skills they already possess. Having rivals pushes them to work harder and strive for improvement in their chosen area. If someone in this competitive environment shifts focus to something new, it might be seen as them admitting defeat or giving up on their original goal.

In conclusion, successful people generally avoid taking on new skills and risks because they have rivals, want to make the most of their existing knowledge, and are driven to improve what they already do well. While learning new things isn‚Äôt necessarily bad, if their goal is to master their current skills, they should focus on refining those rather than branching out into unfamiliar areas.

The goal of the explanation here is to increase the degree of trust between the user and the system, giving the user more nuance towards whether the system’s decision is likely correct, or not. In the general case, the final evaluation metric is the performance of the user at their task. For example, Feng and BoydGraber evaluate various explanations of a model in a setting of trivia question answering. However, in the context of faithfulness, we must warn against HCIinspired evaluation, as well: increased performance in this setting is not in5 As Kaur et al. concretely show, even experts are prone to overly trust the faithfulness of explanations, despite no guarantee. dicative of faithfulness; rather, it is indicative of correlation between the plausibility of the explanations and the model’s performance. To illustrate, consider the following fictional case of a nonfaithful explanation system, in an HCI evaluation setting: the explanation given is a heatmap of the textual input, attributing scores to various tokens. Assume the system explanations behave in the following way: when the output is correct , the explanation consists of random content words; and when the output is incorrect , it consists of random punctuation marks. In other words, the explanation is more likely to appear plausible when the model is correct, while at the same time not reflecting the true decision process of the model. The user, convinced by the nicerlooking explanations, performs better using this system. However, the explanation consistently claimed random tokens to be highly relevant to the model’s reasoning process. While the system is concretely useful, the claims given by the explanation do not reflect the model’s decisions whatsoever (by design).

In the literature on formal syntax, a conventional explanation for constraints on adjective ordering posits that adjectives are categorized into distinct semantic classes (e.g., *Color* or *Size*). According to this view, a universal, total ordering of these classes exists across all languages (e.g., *Color* < *Size*), thereby dictating the linear arrangement of adjectives in any specific instance (Cinque, 1994; Scott, 2002). However, such discrete theoretical frameworks encounter increasing complexity as the number of requisite semantic classes expands—potentially exceeding twelve (Scontras et al., 2017)—and as the granularity of these classifications intensifies (see Bar-Sever et al., 2018, regarding the associated learning challenges).

In contrast, quantitative syntactic theories typically rely on a singular construct grounded in real-valued numerical scores assigned to adjectives, which determine their ordering preferences. These scores may be derived from large-scale corpus data or human ratings. The present study evaluates the predictions of four such quantitative theories: the Subjectivity Hypothesis (Scontras et al., 2017; Simonič, 2018; Hahn et al., 2018; Franke et al., 2019; Scontras et al., 2019); the Information Locality Hypothesis (Futrell & Levy, 2017; Futrell et al., 2017; Hahn et al., 2018; Futrell, 2019); the Integration Cost Hypothesis (Dyer, 2017); and the Information Gain Hypothesis, which is introduced herein.

The paper proceeds by first detailing the theoretical underpinnings of each model, followed by their implementation and empirical testing against large-scale naturalistic English data. Beyond comparing the predictive accuracy of these models, we conduct additional analyses to elucidate the significant similarities and divergences among their predictions. The study concludes with a discussion of the implications of these findings for the understanding of adjective ordering and related linguistic phenomena, alongside an outlook on future research directions.

### 2. Theories of Adjective Order

#### 2.1 Subjectivity
Scontras et al. (2017) demonstrate that adjective ordering is strongly predicted by subjectivity scores. These scores represent the mean ratings obtained from human participants who evaluate adjectives on a numerical scale regarding their degree of subjectivity. Empirical evidence indicates that adjectives rated as more subjective tend to appear further from the head noun than those rated as less subjective. Furthermore, the strength of the ordering preference correlates with the differential in subjectivity scores between two adjectives.

The proposed algorithm is applicable to both speech-to-text and speech-to-speech simultaneous translation tasks (Oda et al., 2014; Bangalore et al., 2012; Yarmohammadi et al., 2013). In the speech-to-text scenario, the impact of modifications on the end-user is minimized, as the system restricts revisions to the most recent output tokens at a relatively low frequency. Conversely, in the speech-to-speech context, the capacity to revise extra words facilitates their integration into the lookahead window of incremental Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems (Ma et al., 2019b). In contrast, alternative retranslation strategies (Arivazhagan et al., 2020) necessitate non-local revisions, rendering them incompatible with incremental TTS architectures. Furthermore, this work introduces, for the first time, two evaluation metrics specifically designed for revision-enabled simultaneous translation: Revision-aware Average Lagging (RAL), a generalized latency metric, and the revision rate. The efficacy of the proposed technique is empirically validated using both fixed (Ma et al., 2019a) and adaptive (Zheng et al., 2019a) policies across Chinese-English and English-Chinese translation directions.

### 2. Preliminaries

**Full-Sentence Neural Machine Translation (NMT).** Conventional full-sentence NMT processes a source sentence $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ via an encoder, where $x_i$ denotes an input token. On the target side, the decoder greedily selects the highest-scoring word $y_t$ conditioned on the source representation $h$ and the previously generated target tokens $y_{<t} = (y_1, \dots, y_{t-1})$. The final hypothesis $y = (y_1, \dots, y_T)$, where $y_T = \langle \text{eos} \rangle$, is selected to maximize the joint probability:

$$ p(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{|y|} p(y_t \mid x, y_{<t}) \quad (1) $$

**Simultaneous Translation.** Without loss of generality, and irrespective of the specific policy design, simultaneous translation is formulated as:

$$ p_g(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{|y|} p(y_t \mid x_{\leq g(t)}, y_{<t}) \quad (2) $$

where $g(t)$ represents an arbitrary fixed or adaptive read policy. For the sake of clarity, this work assumes the policy is predefined and does not explicitly distinguish between fixed and adaptive policy types in the subsequent formulation.

### 3. Opportunistic Decoding with Timely Correction and Beam Search

**Opportunistic Decoding.** To facilitate exposition, this method is initially applied within the context of fixed policies.

The following tasks represent a diverse set of benchmarks designed to evaluate natural language understanding and reasoning capabilities:

**Choice of Plausible Alternatives (COPA)** (Roemmele et al., 2011) is a classification task requiring models to identify the correct cause or effect of a given premise by selecting between two plausible alternatives.

**Winograd Schema Challenge (WSC)** (Levesque et al., 2012) is a sentence-level commonsense reasoning task. It presents a text containing a pronoun and a set of candidate noun phrases, requiring world knowledge to resolve the correct referent. In this study, we utilize the SuperGLUE binary classification formulation, where models must determine whether a specific noun phrase is coreferent with the target pronoun.

**Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE)** (Dagan et al., 2005, et seq.) is a task focused on determining whether a hypothesis logically follows from a given premise.

**Multi-Sentence Reading Comprehension (MultiRC)** (Khashabi et al., 2018) is a multi-hop question-answering task. It provides paragraphs accompanied by questions and a list of potential answers, requiring models to distinguish between true and false statements.

**Words in Context (WiC)** (Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados, 2019) is a binary classification task for word sense disambiguation. Given two text snippets containing the same polysemous word, models must determine if the word is used with the same meaning in both contexts.

**BoolQ** (Clark et al., 2019a) is a question-answering task consisting of passages paired with binary (yes/no) questions.

**Reading Comprehension with Commonsense Reasoning (ReCoRD)** (Zhang et al., 2018) is a multiple-choice question-answering task based on news articles. For each article, a question is presented with a masked entity; models must identify the correct entity from a provided list of candidates drawn from the text.

**Policy Definitions**

Let $s_t$ denote the state representation. The action policy for selecting a topic vertex, denoted as $\mu_{\text{what}}$, is defined as:

$$
\mu_{\text{what}}(s_t, v_j) = \frac{\exp(s_t^\top v_j)}{\sum_{l=1}^{N_{\text{act}}} \exp(s_t^\top v_l)} \quad (3)
$$

where $v_j$ represents the embedding of the $j$-th candidate topic vertex (distinct from parameters $w_i$ and $e^*$), and $N_{\text{act}}$ is the total number of candidate topic vertices.

Similarly, the policy for selecting a response mechanism, denoted as $\mu_{\text{how}}$, is defined as:

$$
\mu_{\text{how}}(s_t, r_i) = \frac{\lambda_i \exp(s_t^\top r_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_r} \lambda_j \exp(s_t^\top r_j)} \quad (4)
$$

where $r_i$ is the representation of the $i$-th candidate response within the state module, and $\lambda_i$ serves as a mechanism mask. Specifically, $\lambda_i$ is assigned a value of 1 if the $i$-th response mechanism is a neighbor of the selected topic vertex; otherwise, it is set to 0.

**3.6 Reward Functions**

In alignment with prior research, we incorporate the following utterance-level rewards:

*   **Local Relevance:** This metric is computed using DualEncoder (Lowe et al., 2015), a state-of-the-art multi-turn response selection model.
*   **Repetition Penalty:** A penalty of 1 is applied if the generated response shares more than 60% of its words with any contextual utterance; otherwise, the penalty is 0.
*   **Target Similarity:** For target-guided conversations, this is calculated as the cosine similarity between the selected keyword and the target word within a pre-trained word embedding space.

To leverage the global structural information of the Conversation Graph (CG) and facilitate policy learning, we propose the following additional rewards:

*   **Global Coherence:** This reward is defined as the average cosine distance between the selected topic vertex and historical topic vertices (either previously selected or mentioned) within the TransE-based embedding space (consistent with Equation 2).
*   **Sustainability:** To encourage the generation of sustainable, coherent, and diverse dialogues, we promote topic vertices with a high degree of connectivity. This is quantified by calculating the PageRank score of the selected topic vertex over the full CG.
*   **Shortest Path Distance to Target:** In target-guided scenarios, this reward is assigned a value of 1 if the selected topic vertex is closer to the target vertex (in terms of shortest path distance) than the previously selected vertex. If the distance remains unchanged, the reward is 0; if the distance increases, the reward is -1.

Given the fundamental role of computational thinking to nearly all elements of 21stcentury life, there is a growing need to develop systems that enable P12 teachers to fully integrate CT within existing curricula and disciplinary teaching. It’s essential that teachers come to understand the value of computational thinking--not just as an isolated concept that relates to computer science, but also as a way to enhance and support more complex disciplinespecific and interdisciplinary understandings. Our findings offer important implications for increasing access to computational thinking through supporting teachers in integrating CT in middle and high school content classrooms. Analysis of data from the implementation of the 3C model indicates that it effectively supported shifts in teacher beliefs and selfefficacy regarding the role of CT in P12 classrooms. Further, findings suggest that teachers found great value in learning to code, thinking critically about connections between disciplinary content and computational thinking principles, and collaborating with colleagues to support disciplinespecific and interdisciplinary learning. Overall, we believe that the combination of Code, Connect, and Create sessions helped to shift teacher beliefs about the role of CT in disciplinary content, while simultaneously addressing their concerns that they might be unequipped with the programming experience and knowledge to support students in learning to code. While the 3C model proposes a new framework that has promise for supporting teachers in learning to integrate computational thinking into their classrooms, there are still many areas that need further exploration and analysis. First, there is a pressing need to develop ongoing, flexible, and differentiated scaffolds to support teachers’ computational thinking infusion. A key next step is for researchers to collaborate with practitioners to study the impact of tiered supports that can prevent teachers’ knowledge from becoming inert . Further, in order to support sustained integration into disciplinary content and curricula, studies must identify explore the forms and functions of collaborative, school and districtbased communities for teachers to reflect on pedagogical innovations and failures.

Because education is so important, there is ongoing debate about the best ways to teach. One of the main topics is homework. While homework can sometimes put too much pressure on students, it helps them understand the material better and prepares them for adult life.

A major downside of homework is the significant amount of time and effort it often demands. In many schools, homework can take several hours to complete, which can be overwhelming after a full day of classes. Another problem is the grades students receive for their homework, as these can greatly impact the mental health of growing children.

On the other hand, homework allows students to practice their skills and gain a deeper understanding of the subjects they are studying. It is especially useful when a student is sick or misses class, as it gives them a chance to catch up at home. There are also many cases where students who struggle in class improve their grades over time by consistently doing their homework.

Personally, I believe homework is a vital part of education. It teaches responsibility, such as meeting deadlines, and helps reinforce what you learn in class. Additionally, homework teaches teenagers how to learn independently, which is one of the most important skills to develop at school.

In conclusion, even though homework can sometimes be time-consuming and may not always lead to the desired grades, it helps prepare students for adult responsibilities like work projects and meeting deadlines.

Life experiences in all circumstances play a significant role in shaping a person‚Äôs life and personality. Some people believe that experiences from early childhood, before starting school, have the greatest influence on a person‚Äôs future, while others argue that experiences during teenage years, especially in school or college, are more impactful. In reality, both childhood and teenage experiences affect a person‚Äôs future in different ways.

Several risk factors can influence these opinions, such as whether a person grows up in a big city or a village, the personalities and number of family members, whether they live with their entire family or only part of it, and whether they live with grandparents. These factors can greatly impact a person‚Äôs experiences. Additionally, a person‚Äôs own personality as a child or teenager, the schools they attend, the neighborhood they live in, their friends‚Äô ages and personalities, their hobbies and activities, and even genetic factors can all play a role in shaping their future.

In my view, all of these experiences, along with the various risk factors, have both positive and negative effects on a person‚Äôs future. The positive influences should be nurtured and used to benefit one‚Äôs life, while the negative impacts should be addressed and managed through further experience and learning.

I believe that successful people are those who are willing to try new things and take risks, rather than just sticking to what they already know how to do well.

For example, a successful businessperson needs to keep innovating and taking risks to maintain and grow their success.

To start with, a successful person never settles and always strives to improve on their previous achievements. Such individuals are usually ambitious, with clear goals in life. This ambition drives them to push their limits, explore new opportunities, and take risks‚Äîeven when it‚Äôs challenging or could lead to setbacks.

Take a successful builder or entrepreneur, for instance. They often look for ways to expand their business, such as constructing new houses to sell for profit. This involves risking their own money, since there‚Äôs always a chance things might not go as planned or they might not make a profit. These individuals may also take on different projects with various partners, always facing some level of risk, but their desire to keep succeeding and take pride in their work motivates them to keep going.

On the other hand, some people prefer to stay within their comfort zones and avoid taking risks.

These individuals tend to stick with what they know and are hesitant about the future. They may lack confidence or ambition, which makes them unwilling to venture into new territory.

In conclusion, I agree with the statement that successful people are those who embrace new challenges and take risks, for all the reasons mentioned above.

Another recent study by a research group at Aalto University using their own platform also found that an interactive ebook improved student motivation and learning compared to a static ebook . Brad Miller started creating the Runestone ebook platform in 2011. He wanted to have ebooks with embedded code that the learner could run and edit. Brad also wanted ebooks to be free so that everyone has the chance to learn computer science, even if they can not afford a $200 textbook. Brad’s goal was “democratizing textbooks for the 21st century". Authors write Runestone ebooks using restructuredText (rst) , which is a minimal markup language that is similar to Markdown . The source for the ebook is converted from restructuredText into HTML5 by the "runestone build" command as shown in The build process uses Sphinx ; a tool written in Python that creates documentation. Sphinx contains many builtin directives for things like figures and notes, but is also extensible. Runestone developers have added new directives to Sphinx to create new interactive features for the ebooks. For example, the activecode directive shown in The displayed code is shown in If the code is Python, it is run as JavaScript on the client using Skulpt . If the code is JavaScript or HTML it is run directly in the browser. If the code is Java, Processing, C, C++ or SQL the code is executed on the Runestone server, which is built on top of the web2py application framework. It provides web services and supports the instructor tools. The build process produces either a static website, or a set of HTML5 templates that can be used to serve hundreds of custom courses as shown in The Runestone ebook server combines the templates for each page with information from the database about the student and the course, and then serves each student a custom page. As the student interacts with the various components on the page, an API is used to gather this data and store it in the database. The first major version of Runestone supported text, images, videos, executable and editable Python code, and a Python code visualizer/stepper (Python Tutor) .

We see that the average Fscore of the bagofconcepts model is 0.92 more than the bagofwords model for the 2013 dataset, and 0.17 more than the bagofwords model for the 2014 dataset. So, even though the effect of moving to conceptlevel sentiment analysis from wordlevel sentiment analysis will vary from one dataset to another, conceptlevel sentiment features will almost always perform better since they already include wordlevel sentiment features. On comparing the results obtained by the modified ngrams and emoticon rules model with the modified ngrams, emoticon rules and conceptlevel unsupervised rules model, we see that tweaking the SVM's predictions using rules and sentic computing increases the average Fscore by 3.63 and 2.6 in the two datasets respectively. Hence, this shows that the linguistic rules and sentic computing based secondary classifier proposed by us substantially improve the result and is thus very beneficial for sentiment analysis. Overall, our final sentiment analysis system achieves an average Fscore that is 4.47 units and 3.12 units higher than the standard ngrams model. In this paper, we describe the pipeline of a Twitter sentiment analysis system that enhances supervised learning, by using modified features for supervised learning as well as applying rules based on linguistics and sentic computing. Based on our results, we can conclude that unsupervised emoticon rules and modified ngrams for supervised learning help improve sentiment analysis. They do so by handling peculiar linguistic characteristics introduced by special partsofspeech such as emoticons, conjunctions and conditionals. Moreover, we have shown that verifying or changing the lowconfidence predictions of a supervised classifier using a secondary rulebased (highconfidence, unsupervised) classifier is also immensely beneficial. In the future, we plan to further improve performance of our classifier. We will do this by further analysing the linguistics of tweets to take into account other conjunctions such as "or", conditionals such as "assuming", or modal verbs such as "can", "could", "should", "will" and "would". We also plan to develop more sophisticated rules to improve the classification of tweets that the supervised classifier assigns a low decision score to. Apart from deeper linguistic analysis and better rules, expanding commonsense knowledge bases such as SenticNet and the use of conceptbased text analysis can also help to boost the predictions of the unsupervised classifier, thereby improving the predictions of the whole system. The proposed approach can also be fed to a multimodal sentiment analysis framework.

In many countries, only a small percentage of people earn very high incomes. While some believe that these large salaries are excessive and that governments should set limits on how much people can earn, others argue that high earnings are actually beneficial for the nation.

Considering the widespread poverty around the world, I can see why some people think it‚Äôs unfair to allow extremely high wages. In my view, many high earners tend to spend their money on unnecessary luxuries just to display their wealth. Meanwhile, there are families working multiple jobs just to make ends meet, which makes it seem reasonable for governments to consider putting caps on income. Moreover, even if some people truly deserve their high salaries, if the government could access the billions sitting in private bank accounts, it could help millions of people by addressing various issues. This could serve the greater good by reducing hunger and poverty, or even funding research to cure diseases like cancer.

On the other hand, it‚Äôs also true that people often earn their high salaries because of their contributions. For example, CEOs and other top executives often create jobs through their innovation and leadership, building companies that employ thousands. It‚Äôs important to remember that everyone pays taxes, so those with higher incomes contribute more to public funds. Additionally, differences in salary can be justified by varying levels of education, experience, or responsibility.

In conclusion, although there is a significant wage gap in every country and both perspectives are valid, imposing limits on salaries could potentially slow down national development.

Similar to the Diversity LSTM, the conicity values are much lower than in the vanilla model. We observe that both Diversity LSTM and Orthogonal LSTM perform significantly better than the MLP model, especially in difficult tasks such as Question Answering with an average relative increase in accuracy of 73.73%. Having established that the performance of Diversity and Orthogonal LSTMs is comparable to the vanilla LSTM and significantly better than a Multilayer Perceptron model, we now show that these two models give more faithful explanations for its predictions. 5.3 Importance of Hidden Representation We examine whether attention weights provide a useful importance ranking of hidden representations. We use the intermediate representation erasure by Serrano and Smith to evaluate an importance ranking over hidden representations. Specifically, we erase the hidden representations in the descending order of the importance (highest to lowest) until the model’s decision changes. In Figure 3 , we report the box plots of the fraction of hidden representations erased for a decision flip when following the ranking provided by attention weights. For reference, we also show the same plots when a random ranking is followed. In several datasets, we observe that a large fraction of the representations have to be erased to obtain a decision flip in the vanilla LSTM model, similar to the observation by Serrano and Smith. This suggests that the hidden representations in the lower end of the attention ranking do play a significant role in the vanilla LSTM model’s decisionmaking process. Hence the usefulness of attention ranking in such models is questionable. In contrast, there is a much quicker decision flip in our Diversity and Orthogonal LSTM models. Thus, in our proposed models, the top elements of the attention ranking are able to concisely describe the model’s decisions.

I disagree with the statement, "Successful people try new things and take risks rather than only doing what they already know how to do well," because it is based on general statistics rather than universal truth. This idea is just a broad assumption and doesn‚Äôt necessarily apply to everyone. In my view, people achieve success mainly by working hard and focusing on what they excel at.

Those who are knowledgeable and skilled in their field are more likely to succeed than those who aren‚Äôt. For example, individuals with a university or college degree in a specific area have a better chance of succeeding than those without that specialized education. While some people may want to experiment and try new things, simply doing so doesn‚Äôt guarantee they‚Äôll outperform those with established expertise. Taking risks and exploring new areas doesn‚Äôt always lead to better outcomes than sticking with what you already do well. Imagine an art major who decides to pursue business. Since their background is in art, they would have to start from scratch in the business world, making it much harder for them to thrive. Although they might eventually find some success, they probably wouldn‚Äôt reach their full potential in business compared to art, where their strengths lie. Therefore, they‚Äôre more likely to build a successful career in art rather than in business.

Trying new things can be worthwhile, and if you can handle the workload and grasp the material, you might become successful. However, the odds are higher that you‚Äôll succeed in the area where you already have talent and experience. Exploring new paths isn‚Äôt a bad idea, but it doesn‚Äôt replace the value of focusing on what you do best.

BRIGHTCS (Building Student Retention through Individuated Guided coHort Training in Computer Science) is a research and development project that 1) creates a computer science learning ecosystem for middle school Black girls and other girls of color and 2) researches the merits of the ecosystem in supporting persistence in CS to determine best practices for broadening participation to other marginalized student groups in computing. First, this paper describes the BRIGHTCS program, from the structural, instructional, and curricular designs of the program to partnerships with local and community organizations that make up the ecosystem. Second, it presents the initial findings of research on the program and its impacts on student outcomes such as socialemotional attributes associated with persistence. The study employs a multimethod descriptive design. Data includes student surveys, interviews (from students, parents, instructors, teachers, and mentors), artifact reviews, and student observations. The study includes 46 students across four middle schools in two states. At the start of the program, 37% of the students reported being very interested in CS, and 72% reported being very confident in learning CS. This is much higher than a national benchmark of students. After four months of program implementation, the qualitative results show a more nuanced picture of the value of a learning ecosystem. First, the ecosystem offers implicit messaging about equity and success. Second, the ecosystem offers explicit messaging about personal challenges and improvement. Third, following the implicit and explicit messaging to students, students went from naïve confidence to authentic selfefficacy in CS. Black college freshman interest in computer science (CS) has been declining for almost 20 years—to an alltime low of 9% in 2014 . There is clearly a need to start integrating CS into the K12 public education system earlier, when Black students and parents are highly interested in learning about CS . To understand the decline, this paper considers two areas within the K12 context: 1) the importance of the middle school setting; and 2) the structural, instructional, and curricular barriers for students of color to access and excel at CS. First, the middle school grades (often grades 68 or 78) are a critical transition point academically, developmentally, and socially for students .

As such, it can be compared to existing dialogue datasets and summarization datasets. 2.1 Dialogue Datasets There are currently many existing dialogue datasets (disregarding machinetomachine) that can be roughly grouped into taskoriented, conversational, scripted, constrained, and spontaneous dialogues. Taskoriented datasets address specific tasks and are constrained by an ontology. If the task is sufficiently constrained, even a humantohuman taskoriented dialogue can lack spontaneity and noise of open domain conversation,,. Agents trained on such datasets cannot be expected to model spontaneous conversational dialogue. Scripted dialogue datasets are closer to conversational dialogue. Popular scripted dialogues come from TV shows, movies, and novels; sometimes featuring further annotations,,. Though the lack of noise can be helpful in training a dialogue system, they do contain artificialities in their linguistic properties.

There are many successful people in the world, and these individuals often become successful by taking risks and trying new things. That‚Äôs why I agree with the idea that risk-taking is crucial for success.

In our everyday lives, we see many successful people who are always open to new experiences and willing to take risks to maintain their achievements. I completely agree that successful people aren‚Äôt afraid to try new things and face challenges. Taking risks is not easy‚Äîsometimes, if a risk doesn‚Äôt pay off, they could even face bankruptcy. That‚Äôs why many successful people surround themselves with teams to help make the right decisions.

For example, if a successful person owns a restaurant, they need to come up with new ideas, like creating new dishes. These new dishes might turn out to be delicious and attract more customers, which means the risk paid off and the restaurant becomes even more successful. However, if the new dishes aren‚Äôt well received, the owner could lose customers and their business could suffer. But in this case, the risk worked out and the owner became even more successful.

On the other hand, if someone takes a risk and it doesn‚Äôt work, they might lose customers and profits, and their business could eventually fail if nobody comes anymore.

In conclusion, making the right choices is very important for keeping your business running and for achieving success. The most important qualities in a successful person are honesty and kindness. Believe me, if you take risks and they work out, you will succeed.

Recruiting: This student started to attend our group meetings immediately after joining Yale (a freshman at the time of recruitment) in order to learn more about research. Ideation: Due the opportunity of getting this student involved early, we gave the student an especially challenging project of developing a symbolic execution engine for Haskell programming language. The student was directly supervised by a PhD student from the group, but also worked independently, developing their own research agenda. Research: A symbolic execution engine takes as input a program, and instead of executing the program on some concrete values, it runs the program using symbolic values. The result is a mathematical formula that describes the program’s behavior. Although symbolic execution engines are well studied, languages like Haskell, based on lazy semantics, had no efficient symbolic execution engine. The problem required a deep understanding of Haskell’s semantics: the research combined some foundational theoretical problems, but was also implementationintensive. Outcomes: While the student kept working with our group throughout their undergraduate program, they also developed other research interests at the intersection of mathematical reasoning and computer science. Nevertheless, the symbolic execution engine work resulted in papers accepted and presented at two top ACM sponsored venues . In addition, we helped the student explore other research directions, and the student did three research internships: one at a different university, one at an international research institute, and one at a research lab of a large company. The student presented work at various international meetings and volunteered at conferences, developing a strong sense of helping the community. The student also received the 2019 NSF Graduate Research Fellowship, was accepted to several PhD programs, and is currently attending one of them. Recruiting: The student took a course (during their 3rd year) from our research group and asked directly about a possibility to conduct an independent research study. The student worked directly with a professor, as the group had no graduate students at the time. Ideation: The student was doing a double major in math and computer science, and was interested in finding a way to leverage both these backgrounds. Historically, the identification of thematic frames has relied on manual annotation by human coders (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Card et al., 2015) or on supervised prediction models trained on annotated datasets (Johnson et al., 2017; Baumer et al., 2015). In contrast, the Topic-Based Ideal Point (TBIP) model operates in a fully unsupervised manner. It derives ideological topics without requiring adherence to predefined frames and eliminates dependence on coder subjectivity.

Existing methodologies, such as Wordfish (Slapin and Proksch, 2008), model authored political texts concerning a single issue, functioning effectively as a single-topic variant of the TBIP. Wordfish has been applied to party manifestos (Proksch and Slapin, 2009; Lo et al., 2016) and single-issue dialogues (Schwarz et al., 2017). Wordshoal (Lauderdale and Herzog, 2016a) extends this framework to multiple issues by analyzing collections of texts pre-labeled by topic, such as Senate speeches categorized by debate subject. This approach fits separate Wordfish models to texts within each label and combines them via one-dimensional factor analysis to generate ideal points.

Unlike these models, the TBIP does not necessitate the prior grouping of texts into single-issue categories. It is inherently capable of processing unstructured data, such as social media posts, while simultaneously estimating author ideal points and ideology-adjusted topics for latent issues. Furthermore, by utilizing stochastic optimization, the TBIP algorithm is designed to scale efficiently to large datasets. Section 5 presents an empirical comparison of TBIP-derived ideal points against those generated by the aforementioned models.

### 4. Inference

The TBIP framework incorporates several categories of latent variables: neutral topics ($\tilde{\theta}_k$), ideological topics ($\phi_k$), topic intensities ($\theta_d$), and ideal points ($x_s$). Conditional on the observed text, inference of these latent variables is conducted via the posterior distribution $p(\theta, \tilde{\theta}, \phi, x \mid y)$. However, the exact calculation of this distribution is computationally intractable. Consequently, the model employs approximate inference techniques. Specifically, mean-field variational inference is utilized to fit an approximate posterior distribution (Jordan et al., 1999; Wainwright et al., 2008; Blei et al., 2017). This approach reframes the inference problem as an optimization task. Let $q(\theta, \tilde{\theta}, \phi, x)$ denote a variational family of approximate posterior distributions, parameterized by variational parameters $\lambda$.

The proposed architecture builds upon the framework established by Amodei et al. (2015) with the integration of dense bottleneck layers. This modification yields an approximate 4% reduction in Word Error Rate (WER). Furthermore, the MultiQT model demonstrates a significant advantage for the multimodal approach, achieving an absolute improvement of 6.15 points in F1-score compared to the text-only baseline. We posit that in real-world scenarios characterized by high WER and limited in-domain training data, the performance gains justify the fusion of textual and acoustic features for question tracking. Alternatively, the Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system could be extended into a multitask learning framework to jointly perform question tracking and speech transcription; however, this avenue is reserved for future investigation. From a practical standpoint, implementing such a multitask approach necessitates full human transcription of the audio data in addition to question annotations. This requirement generally entails greater time and financial costs compared to annotation focused solely on questions.

**Qualitative Analysis**
An analysis of model predictions on a subset of 21 calls was conducted to identify primary sources of labeling errors. The results indicate that in over 50% of the erroneous cases, incorrect predictions were triggered either by the presence of question-related keywords within non-interrogative sentences or by background questions posed by callers that lacked corresponding labels. Additionally, undetected questions were observed in instances involving highly noisy ASR transcripts or atypical phrasing.

**Symptom Labeling**
Experiments utilizing silver-standard symptom data reveal trends analogous to those observed in question tracking. The dual-modality MultiQT model achieved an instance F1-score of 76.9, representing an absolute improvement of 1.8 points over the best single-modality baseline. The text-only approach served as the runner-up (−1.8 F1), while the audio-only model exhibited a substantial decline of 23.6 points in F1-score. Notably, a simple text-only keyword matching baseline achieved a score of 73.7. We argue that symptom tracking is predominantly driven by textual information rather than acoustic features, as the distinctive prosodic markers associated with questions, such as intonation shifts, are typically absent when speakers communicate symptoms.

**6. Related Work**
The broader context of this research involves tracking dialogue states within emergency medical services calls. These interactions typically consist of sequential question-and-answer exchanges pertaining to various medical symptoms.

Smith , 2011 ) involves assigning values to multiple interdependent variables. Certain joint assignments may be prohibited by constraints designed by domain experts. As a simple example, in the problem of extracting entities and relations from text, a constraint could disallow the relation “married to” between two entities if one of the entity is not a “person”. It has been shown that carefully designed constraints can substantially improve model performance in various applications, especially when the number of training examples is limited. Designing constraints often requires taskspecific manual effort. In this paper, we ask the question: can we use neural network methods to automatically discover constraints from data, and use them to predict structured outputs? We provide a general framework for discovering constraints in the form of a system of linear inequalities over the output variables in a problem. These constraints can improve an already trained model, or be integrated into the learning process for global training. A system of linear inequalities represents a bounded or unbounded convex polytope. We observe that such a system can be expressed as a twolayer threshold network, i.e., a network with one hidden layer of linear threshold units and an output layer with a single threshold unit. This twolayer threshold network will predict 1 or − 1 depending on whether the system of linear inequalities is satisfied or not. In principle, we could try to train such a threshold network to discover constraints. However, the zerogradient nature of the threshold activation function prohibits using backpropagation for gradientbased learning. Instead, in this paper, we show that a construction of a specific twolayer rectifier network represents linear inequality constraints. This network also contains a single linear threshold output unit, but in the hidden layer, it contains rectified linear units (ReLUs). Pan and Srikumar showed that a twolayer rectifier network constructed in such a way is equivalent to a threshold network, and represents the same set of linear inequalities as the threshold network with far fewer hidden units. The linear constraints thus obtained can augment existing models in multiple ways.

Recently, I received an anonymous but truly impressive PowerPoint presentation via email that made me reflect and ponder on different philosophies of life. One idea, in particular, stood out to me: ‚ÄúTo achieve something you‚Äôve never accomplished before, you have to do something you‚Äôve never done before.‚Äù Successful people often earn recognition because they venture out into new opportunities instead of staying safely where they are.

Literature has long supported the idea that success comes after overcoming unique challenges. In the epic of Gilgamesh, the hero sets out on a journey to gain immortality by traveling to the ends of the earth. Although he ultimately loses the flower of youth, Gilgamesh gains a newfound sense of fulfillment and energy to improve his kingdom. His quest for immortality is achieved in another sense‚Äîhis name is remembered throughout history, as the poem concludes.

However, the most powerful examples of risk and success can be found in history. Several names instantly come to mind: Gandhi, Mother Teresa, Martin Luther King Jr., Betty Williams, Nelson Mandela‚Ä¶ Each of these individuals led courageous actions against societal norms and expectations, giving future generations the gifts of freedom, justice, and hope. If that doesn‚Äôt define ‚Äúsuccess,‚Äù I‚Äôm not sure what does.

My own experiences have also shown me the connection between risk and achievement. Two years ago, I decided to try out for my school‚Äôs soccer team. Although I usually played midfield, I volunteered to be the goalie because the team needed one. The position intimidated me at first, but I kept practicing to develop my skills. Amazingly, I became an all-star goalie and was even invited to compete in the Junior Olympics as a keeper.

In conclusion, success almost always involves taking risks. Progress, innovation, and recognition all stem from bold attempts to create change. In this sense, ‚Äúchange‚Äù is crucial‚Äîa shift from safety to challenge often leads to a transformation from defeat to victory.

We begin by examining raw attention mechanisms, as illustrated in Figure 1a (cf. Vig, 2019), using a correctly classified example. A critical observation is that while distinct attention patterns across different positions are evident in the initial layers, attention weights in deeper layers tend to become uniform. Similarly, Figure 2 (left), which plots raw attention scores of the [CLS] token against input tokens across various layers, reveals a lack of interpretable structure. These findings suggest that as representations propagate through the network, embeddings become increasingly contextualized, potentially distributing information uniformly across tokens.

This phenomenon underscores the necessity of tracing attention weights back to the input layer and aligns with the conclusions of Serrano and Smith (2019), who demonstrated that attention weights do not necessarily correlate with the relative importance of input tokens. To quantify the utility of raw attention weights—and to evaluate the alternative methods discussed in the subsequent section, including input gradients—we employ an input ablation technique known as *blankout*. This method estimates the importance score of each input token by sequentially replacing it with an unknown token (UNK) and measuring the resulting impact on the predicted probability of the correct class.

We subsequently calculate the Spearman's rank correlation coefficient between the final-layer attention weights of the [CLS] embedding and the importance scores derived from *blankout*. As presented in the first row of Table 1, the correlation between raw attention weights and *blankout* scores is generally low, with the exception of the first layer. Table 2 further indicates that this low correlation persists when comparing attention weights against input gradients.

FOBIE contains annotations of keyphrases found in fulltext scientific papers, similar to S CIENCE IE. Our framework is based on the classification task for textdriven stock price movement, which has been studied intensely as follows. Early research on exploiting financial news articles for better stock price prediction dates back to Ou and Penman, in which financial indicators were extracted manually from financial statements. Later, in Fung et al., NLP methods were adopted for automatic text feature extraction. Since the 2000s, Twitter and other textcentered social media platforms have become essential sources of financial signals. Bollen et al. found evidence for causality between the public mood extracted from tweets and the Dow Jones Industrial Average index. In Nguyen et al., post texts collected from the Yahoo! Finance Message Board were used to predict whether the prices of 18 US stocks would rise or drop on the next trading day. As deep learning methods for NLP have become more common, many papers have reported the use of neural networks for textdriven stock classification (or prediction) tasks. Ding et al. proposed an event embedding to represent a news headline with a vector and used a convolutional neural network for classification. In that work, all the event embeddings of news articles published on the same day were simply averaged to summarize that day’s market information. Hu et al. was among the first works that applied the attention mechanism to the task of newsdriven stock price movement classification. They developed a duallevel attention framework, in which news articles were assigned different weights depending on the output of a logistic regression component with a bias term, so that the most informative news articles were “highlighted.” The method of weighting news articles in this paper is similar to that previous work. The stockspecific information in Hu et al. was encoded in the neural network, however, making it focused on the price prediction task. In contrast, we represent such stockspecific information by the stock embedding, i.e., a vector, which is easy to interpret geometrically and extract for other applications. For one such application, we evaluated our stock embedding in terms of portfolio optimization. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper applying NLP techniques to modern portfolio theory.

While the above scenario is extreme, this misunderstanding is not entirely unlikely, since any degree of correlation between plausibility and model performance will result in increased user performance, regardless of any notion of faithfulness. 5 Guidelines for Evaluating Faithfulness We propose the following guidelines for evaluating the faithfulness of explanations. These guidelines address common pitfalls and suboptimal practices we observed in the literature. Be explicit in what you evaluate. Conflating plausability and faithfulness is harmful. You should be explicit on which one of them you evaluate, and use suitable methodologies for each one. Of course, the same applies when designing interpretation techniques—be clear about which properties are being prioritized. Faithfulness evaluation should not involve humanjudgement on the quality of interpretation. We note that: (1) humans cannot judge if an interpretation is faithful or not: if they understood the model, interpretation would be unnecessary; (2) for similar reasons, we cannot obtain supervision for this problem, either. Therefore, human judgement should not be involved in evaluation for faithfulness, as human judgement measures plausability. Faithfulness evaluation should not involve humanprovided gold labels. We should be able to interpret incorrect model predictions, just the same as correct ones. Evaluation methods that rely on gold labels are influenced by human priors on what should the model do, and again push the evaluation in the direction of plausability. Do not trust “inherent interpretability” claims. Inherent interpretability is a claim until proven otherwise. Explanations provided by “inherently interpretable” models must be held to the same standards as posthoc interpretation methods, and be evaluated for faithfulness using the same set of evaluation techniques. Faithfulness evaluation of IUI systems should not rely on user performance. Endtask user performance in HCI settings is merely indicative of correlation between plausibility and model performance, however small this correlation is. While important to evaluate the utility of the interpretations for some usecases, it is unrelated to faithfulness. 6 Defining Faithfulness What does it mean for an interpretation method to be faithful? Intuitively, we would like the provided interpretation to reflect the true reasoning process of the model when making a decision. But what is a reasoning process of a model, and how can reasoning processes be compared to each other? Lacking a standard definition, different works evaluate their methods by introducing tests to measure properties that they believe good interpretations should satisfy. Some of these tests measure aspects of faithfulness.

The experimental evaluation compares the proposed approach against six baselines categorized into three groups: standard Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) models, knowledge-enhanced architectures, and fine-tuned GPT-2 systems. The standard Seq2Seq model (Sutskever et al., 2014) serves as the fundamental encoder-decoder baseline for language generation. The knowledge-enhanced group comprises MemNet (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), which utilizes a memory mechanism to store and retrieve concepts; CopyNet (Zhu et al., 2017), which incorporates a copying mechanism for concept generation; and CCM (Zhou et al., 2018a), which employs a graph attention mechanism to model central concepts. Notably, these models focus primarily on grounded concepts and do not explicitly model conversational structures via multi-hop concepts. Additionally, GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), a pre-trained model achieving state-of-the-art performance across various language generation tasks, was included. The 124M parameter GPT-2 model was fine-tuned in two configurations: (1) *GPT2-lang*, where all conversations are concatenated and trained as a standard language model; and (2) *GPT2-conv*, where the architecture is extended with an encoder-decoder structure and supervised using response data.

Regarding implementation details, zero-hop concepts were initialized by matching keywords in the input posts to concepts within ConceptNet, following the methodology of CCM (Zhou et al., 2018a). These zero-hop concepts were subsequently expanded to their neighbors to construct the central concept graph. Given that the outer layer contains a substantial volume of two-hop concepts accompanied by significant noise, a pruning strategy was adopted to mitigate computational costs. Specifically, a preliminary version of ConceptFlow (select) was trained on 10% of the random training data to learn graph attention weights. These weights were then applied to the entire dataset to select the top 100 two-hop concepts. Standard training and testing were subsequently conducted using this pruned graph; further details regarding this filtering process are available in Appendix A.4. Finally, concept and word representations were initialized using TransE embeddings (Bordes et al., 2013) and GloVe embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014), respectively.

First, if there is only one correct translation at each time step, the output probabilities will have “peakier” distributions with low entropy and a single word receiving a large portion of the probability mass. In this case, there is very little variation in the hypotheses space. Second, if there are various correct translation options at a given generation step, the output probability distribution will have higher entropy, with multiple target words receiving similar probabilities. In this case, generating hypotheses from the model will result in similar sentences containing synonyms or paraphrases. Finally, if the NMT model has not seen enough data during training for a given combination of words, we would expect output probabilities to exhibit high entropy, approximating a uniform probability distribution. In this case, generating MT hypotheses from the model should result in a highly diverse set with lower quality translations. Below we explore various approaches to uncertainty quantification in neural networks in order to generate a set of additional hypotheses for MT evaluation. Monte Carlo Dropout. It has been shown that softmax function used in neural networks to generate output probability distribution does not properly capture uncertainty as it produces overconfident predictions. Most of the work on uncertainty quantification in deep learning relies on Bayesian formalism. Representing uncertainty through Bayesian neural networks usually comes with prohibitive computational costs and various approximations have been developed to alleviate this issue. One such approximation by Gal and Ghahramani is called Monte Carlo (MC) dropout. Dropout is a method developed to reduce overfitting when training neural models Srivastava et al.. It consists in randomly masking neurons to zero based on Bernoulli distribution. Gal and Ghahramani use dropout at test time before every weight layer. They perform N forward passes through the network and collect posterior probabilities generated by the model with parameters perturbed by dropout: { ˆ p ( ⃗y | ⃗x, ˆ θ ) N i =1 } where ˆ θ represents the perturbed parameters. They show that this is equivalent to an approximation to the probabilistic deep Gaussian process.

Coherent document has an underlying structure and there is a causal relationship between the two clauses of an emotioncause pair, which distinguishes it from other nonemotioncause pairs in the document. Thus, knowledge about the interrelations between the clauses in a document is beneficial for extracting potential emotioncause pairs. Further, according to the cohesion and coherence of discourse, the probability of two distant clauses containing causal relationship is relatively small. Thus, relative position information between two clauses of a clause pair can be considered as an effective feature for emotioncause pair extraction. Based on the above two considerations, in this paper, we tackle emotioncause pair extraction from a ranking perspective, i.e., ranking clause pair candidates in a given document, and propose a onestep approach which emphasizes interclause modeling to perform endtoend extraction. Our approach first models the interclause relationships via exploiting graph attention to learn clause representations, facilitating pair extraction through capturing the latent relationship between two clauses. It then learns clause pair representations and rank these pairs to extract emotioncause pairs. A kernelbased relative position embedding scheme is proposed to model the mutual impact among relative positions and enhance clause pair representations for effective ranking. We integrate the two components into a unified neural network, which is optimized endtoend. Unlike the previous twostep solution, our approach can directly extract emotioncause pairs from documents.

This study advances the field of disaster-centric emotion detection through three primary contributions: (1) the introduction of a sufficiently large dataset to facilitate the training of supervised classifiers; (2) an investigation into various pretraining strategies designed to instill robust inductive biases; and (3) the establishment of domain adaptation baselines by leveraging emotive samples acquired through distant supervision.

### 3. Dataset Construction

This section details the construction of **HURRICANE EMO**, an annotated corpus comprising 15,000 English tweets collected during Hurricanes Harvey, Irma, and Maria. The following subsections outline the dataset's components, including the initial preprocessing pipeline (§3.1), the annotation methodology (§3.2), and the formulation and calculation of inter-annotator agreement (§3.3).

#### 3.1 Preprocessing

The foundational data for this study was derived from the large-scale Twitter repository released by Chowdhury et al. (2019), herein referred to as **HURRICANE EXT** (Extended). This repository contains tweets streamed during the aforementioned hurricane events and served as the starting point for our dataset construction. The preprocessing phase involved two distinct stages.

First, to ensure data privacy and consistency, all usernames and URLs were replaced with the tokens `<USER>` and `<URL>`, respectively, followed by the removal of duplicate entries. Second, filtering techniques were applied to ensure the retention of tweets containing emotive content. This process relied on a lexical prior, requiring that each candidate tweet contain at least one word derived from **EMOLEX** (Mohammad and Turney, 2013). EMOLEX comprises 14,182 crowdsourced words associated with various emotion categories. It is important to note that while these words frequently appear in emotional contexts, they are not exclusively emotion terms; for instance, the word "payback" is associated with "anger" but is also prevalent in financial discourse.

The utilization of this lexicon for bootstrapping emotion datasets is well-established in prior literature (Bravo-Marquez et al., 2014; Majumder et al., 2017; Giatsoglou et al., 2017), primarily because alternative approaches—such as utilizing raw, unlabeled tweets or employing a pre-trained model for initial classification—present significant challenges. Initial attempts to construct the dataset without emotion-specific filtering (i.e., using raw tweets) resulted in a high volume of spurious content, including news snippets unrelated to emotional expression. The resulting noise level rendered the annotation process prohibitively costly and inefficient, necessitating the adoption of the lexical filtering strategy described above.

Namysl and Konya (2019) evaluated the efficacy of contemporary Optical Character Recognition (OCR) engines. Their findings indicate that, despite significant technological advancements since the digitization of many historical archives (Kim and Cassidy, 2015; Neudecker, 2016), leading OCR systems continue to encounter challenges when processing non-standard or low-quality inputs.

A conventional strategy for managing such textual errors involves post-OCR correction prior to downstream processing. Prominent techniques in this domain include candidate ranking (Fivez et al., 2017; Flor et al., 2019), noisy channel modeling (Brill and Moore, 2000; Duan and Hsu, 2011), voting mechanisms (Wemhoener et al., 2013), sequence-to-sequence models (Afli et al., 2016; Schmaltz et al., 2017), and hybrid systems (Schulz and Kuhn, 2017). In contrast to these approaches, the present study adopts an alternative methodology aimed at enhancing model robustness without reliance on prior error correction, a problem that remains unresolved in the context of OCR errors (Chiron et al., 2017; Rigaud et al., 2019).

**6. Conclusions**

This study investigated the disparity in accuracy between sequence labeling performed on clean versus noisy text (§2.3). We formally defined the noisy sequence labeling problem (§2.2) and introduced a model capable of estimating the true noise distribution (§3.1). Furthermore, we developed a noise induction procedure designed to simulate realistic noisy inputs (§3.2). Two noise-aware training methods were proposed to enhance sequence labeling accuracy on perturbed data: (i) a data augmentation approach utilizing a mixture of clean and noisy examples during training to foster resistance to erroneous input (§3.3); and (ii) a stability training algorithm that enforces output similarity between original and perturbed inputs, thereby facilitating the construction of noise-invariant latent representations (§3.4).

Experimental results demonstrate that Noise-Aware Training (NAT) consistently improves the performance of established sequence labeling models across various error distributions while maintaining accuracy on original inputs (§4). Additionally, the proposed method avoids the computational overhead of retraining embeddings on noisy data sources by leveraging existing text representations. We conclude that NAT extends the applicability of current models beyond idealized scenarios.
