The proposed methodology employs a unified encoder, denoted as $g_\theta$, ensuring that all textual inputs are processed using a shared set of weights. The resulting encoded vectors are designated as $t_{i+k}$, representing the target representations the model aims to predict relative to an anchor vector $c_i$ at a specific distance $k$. As defined in Equation 1, both $t_{i+k}$ and $c_i$ are derived via the function $g_\theta$ applied to the input sentences. It is important to distinguish the CONPONO encoder $g_\theta$ from the architecture utilized in Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC); whereas CPC typically employs separate encoders for anchors and targets, CONPONO processes both the anchor and the target jointly within a single encoder.

$$ t_{i+k} = g_\theta(s_i, s_{i+k}), \quad c_i = g_\theta(s_i) \quad (1) $$

Subsequently, a log-bilinear model is defined in Equation 2 to evaluate the plausibility of a target $t_{i+k}$ occupying position $k$ relative to the anchor $c_i$. The complete parameter set for the model comprises the encoder weights $\theta$ and a distinct weight matrix $W_k$ for each distance $k$. While the bilinear formulation in Equation 2 mirrors that of CPC, the underlying encoder architecture differs fundamentally.

$$ f_k(s_{i+k}, s_i) = \exp(t_{i+k}^T W_k c_i) \quad (2) $$

The loss function for each distance $k$ is specified in Equation 3. This formulation contrasts the score of the correct target against the scores of negative samples $s_j$, which are drawn from both the same document and the broader corpus $S$.

$$ L_k = -\mathbb{E}_S \left[ \log \frac{f_k(s_{i+k}, s_i)}{\sum_{s_j \in S} f_k(s_j, s_i)} \right] \quad (3) $$

To train CONPONO, negative examples are sampled randomly from the corpus. Additionally, "hard negatives" are generated by selecting sentences from the same paragraph but at different distances $k$. Notably, when the absolute distance $|k|$ exceeds one, the sentences situated between the anchor and the target are intentionally omitted from the input. This exclusion of intermediate context is designed to establish a rigorous learning objective, preventing the model from relying on trivial signals typically present in contiguous sentence pairs.

A person becomes successful when they set clear goals and put in the effort needed to achieve them. Once they have a goal, they get organized and may discuss their plans with others to get advice and support. This helps them succeed, as they put in a lot of hard work and dedication.

This approach can also earn them respect in society, increase their popularity, and bring them rewards and recognition.

Successful people never forget the steps they took to reach their goals. This experience helps them tackle new challenges and encourages them to take risks. Introduction  
Throughout human history, there have been many 'successful' individuals in various fields, such as business or sports‚Äîfor example, Bill Gates and Zinedine Zidane. But the real question is: What motivates these people to take risks and try new things? In this essay, I will analyze the different factors behind their decisions. I will also discuss whether successful people prefer to stick with what they know or are more inclined to explore new opportunities.

I. Reasons They Might Avoid Trying New Things  
a. Fear of Threats  
Once someone has reached the top, they may hesitate to try new things out of fear of failure and the risk of losing everything they have achieved.  
b. Nothing Left to Prove  
After reaching their goals, some people might feel they have nothing more to prove, making them less motivated to take further risks.

II. Reasons They Are Willing to Try New Things  
a. Wanting More  
Sometimes, what they have accomplished is simply not enough, and they crave more. For instance, an aging boxer might seek one more challenge by fighting a fresh opponent.  
b. Because They Now Have the Means  
At first, they might not have had the resources to pursue all their dreams, but after achieving fame and success, they can invest money and effort into new ventures that make them happy.

III. Thrill or Calm?  
I believe whether a successful person chooses to try something new depends on their mindset. There is no rule dictating their actions. Without pressure from family, friends, or their environment, they will do what feels right for them.

Conclusion  
Do what you believe is best for yourself.

In the previous example, stimulus salience is associated with the prominence of the country; for text comparison we associate word salience with tfidf weights. At the text level, we associate salience with a combination of wordsalience, interword similarity, and text length provided by soft cardinality. Experimentally, we observed that this effect also occurs when comparing texts, but not necessarily in the same direction suggested by Tversky. We used this effect to improve the performance of our similarity model. In addition, we proposed a parameter that biases the function to generate greater or lower similarity scores. Finally, in our model we used a soft cardinality function instead of the classical set cardinality. Just as classical cardinality counts the number of elements which are not identical in a set, soft cardinality uses an auxiliary interelement similarity function to make a soft count. For instance, the soft cardinality of a set with two very similar (but not identical) elements should be a real number closer to 1.0 instead of 2.0. Let A and B be two sets being compared. When each element of A or B has an associated weight, the problem of comparing those sets becomes a weighted similarity problem. This means that such model has to take into account not only the commonalities and differences, but also their weights. Also, if a similarity matrix S is available, the problem becomes a weighted soft similarity problem because the commonality between A and B has to be computed not only with identical elements, but also with elements with a degree of similarity. The values of S can be obtained from an auxiliary similarity function that satisfies at least nonnegativity and reflexivity. Other postulates such as symmetry and triangle inequality are not strictly necessary. A setbased weighted softsimilarity model using resemblance coefficients and the soft cardinality function instead of classical set cardinality was proposed. The idea of calculating the soft cardinality is to treat elements in set A as sets themselves and to treat interelement similarities as the intersections between the elements.

These two selected vertices are then utilized to direct the response generation process. By leveraging prior dialog transition information encoded as graph edges, this approach narrows the candidate response space to facilitate more effective policy decisions, rather than treating the entire set of keywords as potential actions.

Moreover, to facilitate the modeling of the long-term impact of policy decisions within an ongoing dialogue, we propose novel CG-based reward functions to more accurately quantify the long-term influence of selected actions. We further employ a graph attention mechanism alongside graph embeddings to encode the global structural information of the CG into dialog state representations, thereby enabling decisions that are informed by global context.

The debate over whether students should focus on a few specialized subjects or study a broad range of topics until they finish school is ongoing worldwide, including in Japan. While there are advantages to an education system that allows students to specialize early, I believe it is better for students to study a wide variety of subjects until they graduate.

In Japan, for example, there are courses designed for specific industries, such as those for aspiring hair stylists, IT programmers, and entertainers. Learning specialized skills early can be beneficial for mastering these professions. In particular, in the entertainment industry, younger individuals with talent are often valued more highly than older ones, so starting early can be an advantage.

However, there are also clear benefits to studying a diverse range of subjects until the end of high school. For instance, learning mathematics helps develop logical thinking and problem-solving skills, while studying English is essential for communicating with people around the world. Additionally, having broad knowledge is often useful in daily life and the workplace, where a variety of educational backgrounds can be valuable in conversations and tasks.

Moreover, it can be challenging for teenagers, especially those around fifteen, to decide what career or industry they want to pursue. Young people frequently change their minds about their future professions, even if they have specific dreams early on. Therefore, learning a wide range of subjects helps keep their options open and gives them more time to make informed decisions about their future careers. By studying broadly until high school, students can consider their choices carefully before selecting a specific field of study at university.

Environmental issues are a frequent topic of discussion in today‚Äôs society. Some people argue that individuals should adjust their habits and lifestyles to help improve the ecological situation, while others believe that this problem should be addressed primarily by governments. This essay will examine both perspectives and share my own viewpoint.

It is undeniable that humanity is largely responsible for the current state of the environment, and meaningful progress is unlikely without the active participation of the majority of individuals. For example, simple changes like sorting household waste can make recycling efforts more effective and convenient. Additionally, by reducing their use of plastic products, consumers can help decrease plastic pollution in the environment.

On the other hand, many believe that governments have more power to bring about environmental change. Authorities can, for instance, discourage the use of private cars by imposing higher taxes and additional fees. Governments also have the resources to educate the public about environmental issues and to promote key ecological initiatives, from introducing special courses in schools to creating TV programs and websites focused on these topics. Furthermore, it is a fundamental duty of governments to protect the environment by investing public funds in research and advanced technology. Countries with strong environmental laws, such as Norway, have achieved significant progress and have managed to solve many ecological problems.

In conclusion, I think the most effective way to address environmental challenges is through a combined approach. Both individual efforts and government action are essential to resolving current issues and ensuring a healthy planet for future generations.

Given the critical role of skill prediction within the Job Profile Generation (JPG) framework, the conditional probability $P(Y_i \mid X_i, B_i)$ is decomposed into a two-stage generative process comprising skill prediction and job requirement paragraph generation:

$$P(Y_i \mid X_i, B_i) = P(Y_i \mid X_i, S_i, B_i) \cdot P(S_i \mid X_i, B_i) \quad (1)$$

Here, $S_i = (s_{i,1}, s_{i,2}, \dots, s_{i,l})$ denotes the sequence of skills corresponding to the job requirement, where $l$ represents the sequence length. Under the assumption that $S_i$ and $B_i$ are conditionally independent given $X_i$, the term $P(S_i \mid X_i, B_i)$ simplifies to $P(S_i \mid X_i)$.

Subsequently, to refine the skill representation, global prior information is incorporated via a skill knowledge graph $G_s = (E_1, R, E_2)$, where $E_1$ and $E_2$ represent the sets of head and tail entities, respectively, and $R$ denotes the set of relations. Utilizing the basic information $B_i$ and the knowledge graph $G_s$, a refined skill set $O_i = (o_{i,1}, o_{i,2}, \dots, o_{i,k})$ is derived as follows:

$$O_i = f(B_i, G_s) \quad (2)$$

In this formulation, $f$ represents an invertible query function designed to ensure a one-to-one mapping between $B_i$ and $O_i$.

Wilson et al. posit that aligning student and instructor expectations is essential for enhancing learning outcomes and performance; consequently, curriculum designers must possess a clear understanding of student motivations and programmatic expectations.

Research into student motivations across disciplines, including geography and computer science, indicates a strong preference for curricula that emphasize career guidance and vocational training. In the field of computer science, students often value career-relevant instruction but may fail to recognize the professional applicability of certain theoretical courses. Concurrently, industry practitioners report a persistent gap between the competencies of university graduates and the expectations of the workforce, encompassing both technical and non-technical domains.

Specific deficiencies identified among recent computer science graduates include an undervaluation of software testing, difficulties with configuration management systems and other professional tools, a lack of relevant project experience, and insufficient exposure to maintaining or extending large, poorly documented legacy codebases. Furthermore, numerous studies highlight a deficit in the social and "soft" skills necessary for professional success. An analysis of course syllabi revealed a notable absence of soft skills in curricular documentation. Graduates have been found to struggle with effective communication with colleagues and clients, as well as with seeking assistance from peers or senior engineers when required.

Additionally, students often hold fundamental misconceptions regarding software engineering practices in industry, particularly concerning the importance of team collaboration, although internship experiences have been shown to mitigate these misunderstandings to some degree. Craig et al. synthesized prior research and conducted further student interviews, concluding that computer science students are ill-prepared for customer-centered projects with evolving scopes, long-term development cycles, collaboration within large teams on complex systems, and the application of professional tools and formal methodologies. Notably, a recent study indicated that approximately half of computer science students felt their academic programs did not adequately prepare them for their professional careers.

Assuming that the latent variable $z$ follows an isotropic Gaussian distribution, both the prior $p_{\theta}(z \mid q, u)$ and the posterior $q_{\phi}(z \mid q, r)$ are modeled as normal distributions defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\theta}(z \mid q, u) &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_p, \sigma_p^2 I) \\
q_{\phi}(z \mid q, r) &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_q, \sigma_q^2 I)
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}
$$

The parameters for the means and variances are computed via the following transformations:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mu_p \\ \log(\sigma_p^2) \end{bmatrix} = W_p \cdot \mathbf{x}_p + b_p \tag{2}
$$
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mu_q \\ \log(\sigma_q^2) \end{bmatrix} = W_q \cdot \mathbf{x}_q + b_q \tag{3}
$$

where $W_p, W_q, b_p,$ and $b_q$ denote the trainable parameters. A sample of $z$ is generated using the reparameterization trick (Kingma and Welling, 2013) and subsequently incorporated into the decoder as an input component at each time step. Furthermore, to mitigate the issue of latent variable collapse, a Bag-of-Words (BOW) loss (Zhao et al., 2017) is integrated. The PAGenerator is trained to maximize the variational lower bound (Chung et al., 2015; Serban et al., 2017), formulated as:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; q, r, u) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid q,r)} [\log p_{\theta}(r \mid z, q, u)] - \text{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid q, r) \parallel p_{\theta}(z \mid q, u)) + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid q,r)} [\log p(r_{\text{bow}} \mid z, q, u)]
\tag{4}
$$

### 2.2 User Information Enhancing Regularization

Ideally, the user embedding should be fully leveraged throughout the model training process. However, the PAGenerator is susceptible to the KL vanishing problem, wherein the rapid reduction of the loss function $\mathcal{L}$ in Equation (4) may result from the decoder's strong fitting capacity on the training data rather than the effective utilization of user embeddings. To address this, a regularization term is introduced to encourage the incorporation of user-specific latent information. Initially, as depicted in Figure 1, a generic unknown user vector $u_{\text{unk}}$ is defined to represent scenarios where user information is unspecified.

We interpret this result as a dual observation: it demonstrates the viability of a unified model capable of competent performance across diverse tasks—a capability absent in isolated fine-tuned models—while simultaneously highlighting an open challenge for the research community to develop architectures that more effectively leverage multitask synergies.

To further enhance single-task performance, we adopted a "Multitask followed by Fine-tuning" (MT+FT) strategy, consistent with methodologies employed by Liu et al. (2015) and Raffel et al. (2019). This approach involves initial training on all tasks in a multitask setting, followed by fine-tuning on individual tasks to generate specialized models. This pipeline permits distinct hyperparameter optimization for each target task. Our experiments revealed that applying relative task upweighting during the initial multitask phase significantly influenced the final quality of the fine-tuned models (see Table 5). Specifically, allocating the majority of the multitask training weight to the target task yielded superior results. Although this method achieves marginally better performance than fine-tuning alone, the improvements are generally modest. The proposed methodology constructs a feature vector comprising $N$ dimensions, where $N$ represents the total number of concepts in the vocabulary (specifically 2,729 in this study). Each dimension corresponds to the similarity score between a target concept and every other concept within the set. This approach is grounded in the geometric intuition that if two data points exhibit similar distances to a common set of reference points in Euclidean space, they are likely to be proximal to one another.

To operationalize these similarity-based features for classification, thirteen distinct measures were employed: ten derived from lexical resources and three based on co-occurrence statistics. The lexical measures include one SenticNet-based metric and nine WordNet-based metrics. The co-occurrence measures consist of text distance, pointwise mutual information, and emotional affinity.

Specifically, the SenticNet similarity is defined as the inverse of the distance, where distance is calculated as the absolute difference between the polarity scores of two concepts. As all concepts utilized in this study are sourced from SenticNet, valid polarity scores are available for the entire set. For WordNet-based calculations, the open-source `WordNet::Similarity` package (utilizing English WordNet 3.0) was employed. This package provides nine distinct similarity measures derived from the analysis of the WordNet hierarchy, glosses, and associated metadata; all nine were utilized as independent information sources.

A challenge arose due to the incomplete overlap between SenticNet 1.0 and WordNet 3.0. Of the 2,729 concepts identified in the ISEAR database, 1,436 were directly mapped to WordNet. The remaining concepts underwent manual review and rephrasing to facilitate mapping. For instance, multi-word expressions were reduced to their primary lexical head (e.g., "make mistake" was reduced to "mistake"). Through this process, an additional 169 concepts were successfully mapped, resulting in a total of 1,605 concepts with valid WordNet similarity scores.

For the remaining 1,124 concepts that could not be mapped to WordNet, similarity scores were assigned random values within the interval $[0, 1]$. This stochastic assignment was chosen over a fixed value (such as zero) to prevent unmapped concepts from being artificially clustered together or uniformly distanced from the rest of the concept space, thereby preserving the structural integrity of the feature representation.

**Section 3.2: The Impact of Capitalization on Model Performance**

As demonstrated by Hao (2020), capitalizing the initial character of test instances significantly enhances the ability of language models to distinguish between grammatical and ungrammatical English sentences. To determine whether this finding generalizes across languages, we applied initial capitalization to all test examples in applicable languages. Hebrew was excluded from this analysis due to the absence of a case distinction in its orthography.

Table 4 presents the results, detailing the relative performance gains or losses of our LSTM language models when processing capitalized stimuli compared to lowercase inputs. For all languages except German, capitalization yielded a notable improvement in syntactic performance. In the case of German, a marginal decline in overall performance was observed; however, this is likely attributable to the language's already exceptional baseline performance in lowercase conditions, potentially driven by its mandatory capitalization of all nouns.

A significant observation is that the correlation between morphological complexity and overall syntactic performance across languages has diminished (ρ = 0.2). It is hypothesized that capitalization functions as an explicit syntactic cue by clearly delineating sentence boundaries, thereby reducing the model's reliance on morphological cues for grammatical discrimination. Consequently, capitalizing test sentences prior to model inference appears to be a beneficial strategy for improving syntactic accuracy. This aligns with the explanation provided by Hao (2020), which posits that determiners such as "the" typically appear only at the sentence onset, thereby providing the model with critical cues regarding the subject noun (usually the token immediately following the determiner).

This is a strong and problematic assumption that can lead to false negatives. 3.2 Limitations of Manual labelling The main limitation of manual labelling is the absence of evidence on the intention of the author of the texts that are being labelled. Annotator perception may be different to author intention, in light of studies that point out how sarcasm perception varies across sociocultural contexts. Joshi et al. provide more insight into this problem on the Riloff dataset. They present the dataset, initially labelled by Americans, to be labelled by Indians who are trained linguists. They find higher disagreement between Indian and American annotators, than between annotators of the same nationality. Furthermore, they find higher disagreement between pairs of Indian annotators, indicating higher uncertainty, than between pairs of American annotators. They attribute these results to sociocultural differences between India and the United States. They conclude that sarcasm annotation expands beyond linguistic expertise and is dependent on considering such factors. Labels provided by thirdparty annotators might therefore not reflect the sarcastic intention of the authors of the texts that are being labelled, making this labelling method suboptimal for capturing intended sarcasm. To investigate this further, we looked at the Riloff dataset, which is published as a list of labelled tweet IDs. We could only retrieve 1,832 tweets, the others being removed from Twitter. We looked at the agreement between the presence of tags and manual annotation. Table 2 shows the results. We notice that 58% of the tweets that contained the predefined hashtags were labeled nonsarcastic. This disagreement between distant supervision and manual annotation provides further evidence to doubt the ability of the latter to capture intended sarcasm, at least not the flavor that distant supervision might capture. We could not perform the same analysis on the SemEval2018 dataset because only the text of the tweets is provided, hashtags are filtered out, and tweet IDs are not available. As we have shown, both labelling methods use a proxy for labelling sarcasm, in the form of predefined tags, predefined sources, or thirdparty annotators. As such, they are unable to capture the sarcastic intention of the authors of the texts they label, generating both false positives and false negatives.

It should be noted that this representation explicitly supports the modeling of multi-actor conversations, encompassing both active speakers and non-speaking participants. Reference history sequences exceeding 512 tokens are truncated from the end to maintain a maximum length of 512 tokens. Conversation turns are similarly constructed by concatenating them in chronological order, prefixed by a special token to denote the commencement of the dialogue. For practical implementation, all turns occurring after the final turn associated with the target speaker are excluded for a given iteration.

Within the conversation sequence, each token is assigned a specific token type embedding if it corresponds to the speaker of interest, while a distinct embedding is applied to tokens associated with other participants. Furthermore, the conversation and reference history utilize disjoint sets of token type embeddings to clearly differentiate between these content types. The maximum length for a conversation sequence is capped at 512 tokens; to encourage a diverse range of conversation lengths, any excess tokens are truncated from the beginning of the sequence. Consequently, models incorporating reference history operate with a total sequence length of 1024 tokens, whereas those without reference history are limited to 512 tokens.

### 4.2 Architectures

The modeling of conversations with reference histories offers significant flexibility, given that conversation turns and reference comments are only indirectly related in terms of both content and style. Accordingly, the design choices evaluated herein involve either encoding the references separately from the conversation or jointly.

**Decoder-Only (GCCD-EC):** The simplest of the three configurations consists solely of a transformer decoder, adhering to the original GPT-2 architecture. The input comprises reference history tokens concatenated with conversation turn tokens and their corresponding token types. A left-to-right (LR) causal mask is applied across the entire sequence (see Figure 1). Despite its architectural simplicity, this model demonstrates superior performance.

**Seq2Seq Baseline (GCCS2S):** In this configuration, reference material and associated token types are encoded via a separate transformer utilizing a bidirectional mask. Subsequently, conversation turns are decoded using a left-to-right mask, employing both self-attention mechanisms and cross-attention against the final hidden states of the encoded reference. This approach aligns with the standard formulation of attention-based Seq2Seq models (Vaswani et al., 2017).

**Variational Autoencoder Baseline (GCCVAE):** This configuration similarly encodes the reference history and corresponding token types within a separate transformer using a bidirectional mask.

The data collection methodology employed in this study integrates multiple sources to construct a comprehensive legislative dataset. Initially, the LegiScan API was utilized to aggregate legislative data for all bills within the target states, encompassing three primary categories: (1) bill metadata, including bill type, title, description, sponsors, and links to full texts; (2) vote metadata, detailing individual legislator voting records (Yea, Nay, Absent, or Not Voting); and (3) legislator metadata, comprising party affiliation and district information.

To ensure textual accessibility, proprietary PDF documents were systematically converted into machine-readable text files using open-source libraries. Furthermore, to capture the granular progression of legislation, the system scraped the "History" sections from the LegiScan website. This process extracted a chronological series of legislative events—such as committee reports and roll call votes—that are not accessible via the standard API. In total, 34,443 bills introduced between 2011 and 2018 were collected. While 58% of these bills possessed both full texts and individual voting records, the analysis focused specifically on bills reaching the second or third reading stages, where full chamber voting occurs. Consequently, a subset representing 32% of the total corpus was selected to construct the legislative graph (see Table 2).

Supplementary data regarding legislator biographies, ideology, and geography were also aggregated. Biographical details, political interests, and committee assignments were sourced from Ballotpedia (2019). For the minority of legislators (less than 10%) lacking Ballotpedia profiles, alternative public textual sources, such as social media platforms, were utilized. Ideological positioning was derived from the scores established by Shor and McCarty (2011), categorizing legislators as conservative, moderate, or liberal. Additionally, district identifiers were cross-referenced with GIS census data (Census, 2019) to classify districts as either urban or rural. Table 3 provides a demographic breakdown of legislators by party, gender, ideology, and district type.

Finally, campaign finance data were obtained via the FollowTheMoney API (FollowTheMoney, 2019). This source was used to compile comprehensive records of financial contributions to legislators and candidates, specifically identifying donors associated with the legislators and cosponsors of the bills under study.

This study documents the deployment of Crescendo within two distinct educational contexts: a middle school programming camp and an introductory university course for non-majors. Preliminary findings derived from field observations and log data indicate that Crescendo's support mechanisms effectively sustained student engagement and facilitated rapid progression through programming concepts. Nevertheless, a subset of students continued to encounter difficulties despite the highly structured nature of the tasks, necessitating additional instructor intervention. These observations suggest that even robust scaffolding may be insufficient to guarantee independent task completion for all learners.

Over the past four decades, the design and implementation of introductory programming curricula have evolved significantly, with particular emphasis placed on the development of specialized programming environments. Two primary categories of such environments have been widely adopted: Novice Programming Environments (NPEs), exemplified by Scratch, Alice, and MIT App Inventor; and Drill-and-Practice Systems (D&PSs), including CodeWorkout, CloudCoder, and Problets.

NPEs have demonstrated efficacy in enhancing student engagement and academic performance relative to traditional instructional methods. Characterized by block-based interfaces, these environments enable the creation of interactive applications, such as games, and are typically integrated with open-ended curricula (e.g., Big Ideas in Computer Science). While such exploratory approaches can yield profound learning experiences, they may also impose excessive cognitive loads on novices, thereby increasing the demand for instructor support. For instance, Lee and Ko's comparative study of instructional approaches within the Gidget programming game revealed that students achieved significantly higher learning outcomes in a highly structured, level-based curriculum compared to an open-ended, creation-oriented approach.

Conversely, Drill-and-Practice Systems (D&PSs), such as CodingBat, CloudCoder, CodeWorkout, Infandango, and Problets, offer distinct pedagogical advantages. These systems provide automated assessment and immediate feedback, which are critical components of effective learning. By isolating single programming concepts within discrete assignments, D&PSs allow students to focus on mastering specific strategies sequentially. Furthermore, the structured architecture of these environments facilitates the integration of Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS) principles, including mastery learning, adaptivity, and automated formative assessment, all of which have been shown to substantially improve learning outcomes.

This paper presents Crescendo, a self-paced programming practice environment designed to synthesize the pedagogical strengths of both NPEs and D&PSs.

Transductive Support Vector Machines (TSVM) (Joachims, 1999) represent a widely adopted semisupervised approach that extends standard Support Vector Machines by minimizing the number of unlabeled data points located near the decision margin. Similarly, the Semisupervised Variational Autoencoder (SemiVAE) (Kingma et al., 2014) leverages deep generative models to effectively utilize unlabeled data; this architecture serves as a core component of the proposed SemiORC model. Furthermore, the Semisupervised Sequential Variational Autoencoder (SSVAE) (Xu et al., 2017) employs a modified Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network as its decoder and currently stands as the state-of-the-art semisupervised model for text classification. However, a common limitation among these baselines is their lack of interpretability; as black-box semisupervised learning models, they are unable to highlight keywords that are informative to prediction outcomes. Additionally, we include an ablation baseline, ORC, which constitutes the supervised variant of SemiORC. Unlike the proposed method, ORC excludes unlabeled data from the document representation modeling process.

Regarding evaluation, we adhere to standard metrics for multilabel classification, specifically Hamming loss, subset accuracy, and the Micro-F1 score. Hamming loss (Schapire and Singer, 1999) quantifies the average Hamming distance between true and predicted label sets. Accuracy is defined as the subset accuracy, measuring the proportion of instances where the predicted label set exactly matches the true label set. The Micro-F1 score (Manning et al., 2008) provides a weighted average of precision and recall, derived from the aggregate counts of true positives, false negatives, and false positives.

The experimental setup involves implementing the SemiORC model using TensorFlow on a workstation equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080Ti GPU. Model training is optimized via the Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2015) and regularized using dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). Hyperparameters are configured as follows: the number of projection matrices ($h$) is set to 4, and the word embedding dimension ($d$) is set to 64. The learning rate and the weight parameter $\alpha$ are empirically tuned to 0.001 and 2, respectively, while the dropout rate is varied between 0.3 and 0.7. For the Logistic Regression and TSVM baselines, document representations are learned using Doc2Vec (Le and Mikolov, 2014), with risk label prediction performed via classifiers built using the scikit-learn library (Pedregosa et al., 2011). For the SemiVAE and SSVAE baselines, the encoders, decoders, and classifiers are all constructed using LSTM networks. Finally, to ensure statistical robustness, all methods are evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, with each experiment repeated 10 times.

I agree with the statement. In my opinion, people who are willing to take risks and try new things tend to be more successful than those who stick only to what they already know.

The first reason for my view is quite simple. When I think about babies, it becomes clear to me. If a baby never tries new things, like learning to walk instead of crawling, or never takes risks, such as tasting warm food, they would never learn to walk or understand the danger of hot food. Without taking these steps, a baby would never grow into an adult.

Next, I consider the importance of innovation. Take, for example, the person who invented electricity. When he experimented with his machines to generate the first watts of power, he faced serious risks, including the chance of electrocution or even threats from oil companies at the time. Similarly, Pierre and Marie Curie faced high levels of radioactivity when they discovered elements like radium and polonium. It‚Äôs impossible to say these individuals were not successful.

Finally, I think about the world of art. Artistic progress would not have happened if some artists‚Äîoften seen as crazy in their time‚Äîhadn‚Äôt dared to paint in new styles or choose different subjects. Picasso, for example, invented Cubism simply by trying new things. Today, he is world-famous, and his paintings are among the most valuable in the art market.

For these three reasons, I believe that successful people are those who try new things and take risks, rather than just sticking to what they already know how to do well.

These days, there is frequent discussion about education and how it relates to differences in people‚Äôs abilities and opportunities. Some argue that students should be separated according to their academic skills, while others believe that quality education can be provided without dividing students based on ability.

Supporters of separating students think that homogeneous groups learn more effectively and quickly. If someone in the group has lower skills than the rest, teachers face two choices: either lower the standard of lessons so everyone can keep up, or leave the less skilled student behind. Neither option is ideal. Therefore, grouping students with similar abilities can make the learning process more efficient for everyone.

On the other hand, some believe that educating all students together is better. This approach can motivate students with lower skills to work harder to keep up with their more advanced peers. Additionally, separating students might cause those with lower abilities to develop self-esteem issues, making them feel unworthy of being in the "better" group. Another advantage of mixed-ability classes is that higher-achieving students can help their classmates, though the overall level of the class may be lower.

In conclusion, I think the best approach depends on the educator‚Äôs goals and the students‚Äô ages. Teaching children is very different from teaching teenagers. For instance, if the aim is to train future engineers, dividing students by academic ability might be appropriate. However, if the goal is to educate well-rounded individuals, it‚Äôs better not to separate the class.

Demographic data were collected, including student gender and self-reported prior familiarity with Python (categorized as yes or no). In total, pre- and post-survey responses were obtained from 180 students (87 male, 93 female; 29 with prior Python experience), representing a 71% completion rate.

Prior to hypothesis testing, Cronbach's alpha reliability analysis was conducted to assess the internal consistency of the survey constructs. All constructs, with the exception of "Usefulness," demonstrated acceptable reliability ($\alpha \geq 0.7$) and were aggregated into single composite scores. Items related to "Usefulness" were analyzed individually.

Data analysis was performed using repeated-measures ANOVA with Type II sums of squares. The within-subjects factor was time of survey (pre- vs. post-survey), while between-subjects factors included gender, prior Python familiarity, and the trimester of course enrollment. To address the familywise error rate, post hoc comparisons were conducted using paired t-tests (for the time factor) with Bonferroni corrections. Participants who failed to respond to all items in both the pre- and post-surveys were excluded from the analysis for each respective factor. Results reported herein include only statistically significant main effects and two-factor interaction effects ($p < 0.05$).

**Confidence**
A significant main effect of time of survey was observed on student confidence, $F(1, 162) = 5.61, p = 0.019$, indicating higher confidence levels in the post-survey compared to the pre-survey (paired difference $M = 0.13, SD = 0.73$). Additionally, prior familiarity with Python significantly influenced confidence, $F(1, 162) = 25.26, p < 0.001$. Students with prior experience reported higher confidence across both surveys ($M = 0.88, SD = 0.76$) compared to those without ($M = 0.14, SD = 0.81$).

A significant interaction effect was found between gender and time of survey, $F(1, 162) = 6.83, p = 0.009$. While male confidence remained statistically unchanged, female confidence increased significantly ($p = 0.0003$; paired difference $M = 0.26, SD = 0.68$). Notably, females began with significantly lower confidence than males in the pre-survey ($p = 0.005$; female $M = 0.02, SD = 0.89$; male $M = 0.38, SD = 0.91$). Consequently, although confidence levels did not equalize across genders by the end of the course, the intervention significantly reduced the initial gender gap.

**Interest**
Time of survey exerted a significant effect on student interest, $F(1, 163) = 7.53, p = 0.007$. Interest levels were significantly higher in the post-survey compared to the pre-survey (paired difference $M = 0.12, SD = 0.60$).

**Comparative Baseline Results**

For the purpose of comparative analysis, we report results derived from the original publications. Specifically, for the SNLIHard benchmark, we utilize the hierarchical tensor-based Densely Interactive Inference Network (DIIN; Gong et al., 2018) as implemented by Gururangan et al. on the MNLI dataset. Regarding the NLI stress tests, we employ the InferSent model (Conneau et al., 2017) as implemented by Naik et al., trained on the SNLI dataset.

**Linguistic Analysis**

To characterize the composition of the ANLI dataset, we conducted a comparative analysis of linguistic phenomena against established NLI corpora. Our findings indicate that the FEVER and SNLI datasets exhibit a significantly lower prevalence of complex linguistic features compared to MultiNLI and ANLI (see Table 8).

Both ANLI and MultiNLI demonstrate comparable proportions of hypotheses exceeding twenty words in length, as well as similar frequencies of negation (e.g., "never," "no"), disjunctions (e.g., "or"), and modal verbs (e.g., "must," "can"). However, MultiNLI hypotheses generally contain a higher frequency of pronouns, quantifiers (e.g., "many," "every"), interrogative words (e.g., "who," "why"), and conjunctions (e.g., "and") than their ANLI counterparts. Notably, the ANLI Round 3 (A3) subset approaches the MultiNLI levels regarding negation and modal usage. Conversely, ANLI exhibits a higher incidence of cardinal numerals and temporal expressions (e.g., "before," "month," "tomorrow") than MultiNLI.

These disparities likely stem from the distinct textual genres utilized in the construction of each dataset. Since the contexts for ANLI Rounds 1 and 2 (A1 and A2) are derived exclusively from a single Wikipedia source (specifically, the HotPotQA dataset), and given that Wikipedia articles frequently include dates in their opening lines, annotators appear to have prioritized the construction of hypotheses emphasizing numerals and temporal terms.

Within the ANLI dataset itself, A1 and A2 display nearly identical distributions across most linguistic tags (varying by less than 2%), consistent with their shared data source. In contrast, A3 exhibits the highest incidence of every linguistic tag (with the exception of numerals and temporal terms) across the entire ANLI corpus. This trend is likely attributable to the broader range of genres sampled for A3 contexts, which influenced annotator hypothesis construction. This hypothesis is further supported by the observed divergence in tag percentages between A3 contexts and those of A1 and A2. Furthermore, the elevated frequency of linguistic complexity markers in A3 serves as additional evidence that the HAMLET data collection procedure successfully generates progressively more challenging data as the iterative rounds advance.

Within the framework of the Hybrid Value Network (HVN), the objective is to minimize the aggregate loss function defined as the sum of individual value estimation errors for the system, user, and global components. These loss functions are formulated as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{SV}(\theta) &= \left( r_S + \gamma V_{S}^{\theta^-}(s'_S) - V_{S}^{\theta}(s_S) \right)^2, \\
\mathcal{L}_{UV}(\theta) &= \left( r_U + \gamma V_{U}^{\theta^-}(s'_U) - V_{U}^{\theta}(s_U) \right)^2, \\
\mathcal{L}_{GV}(\theta) &= \left( r_G + \gamma V_{G}^{\theta^-}(s') - V_{G}^{\theta}(s) \right)^2,
\end{aligned}
$$

where the total loss $\mathcal{L}_V$ is given by:
$$
\mathcal{L}_V = \mathcal{L}_{SV} + \mathcal{L}_{UV} + \mathcal{L}_{GV}. \quad (2)
$$

In the above equations, $V^{\theta}$ denotes the hybrid value network parameterized by $\theta$, while $\theta^-$ represents the parameters of the target network. The overall loss $\mathcal{L}_V$ constitutes the summation of value estimation errors across each component reward.

The proposed methodology for Multi-Agent Dialog Policy Learning is outlined in **Algorithm 1**.

**Algorithm 1: Multi-Agent Dialog Policy Learning**

*Input:* Dialog corpus $\mathcal{D}$ annotated with dialog acts $\{a\}$.

1.  **Initialization:** Initialize weights $\phi$ and $\omega$ for the system policy $\pi$ and user policy $\mu$, respectively.
2.  **Pre-training:** Pre-train policies $\pi$ and $\mu$ on human conversational data $\mathcal{D}$ utilizing Equation (1).
3.  **Network Setup:** Initialize weights $\theta$ for the hybrid value network $V = (V_S, V_U, V_G)$ and set the target network parameters $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$.
4.  **Training Loop:** For each training iteration:
    *   Initialize the user goal and dialog states $s_U$ and $s_S$.
    *   Sample actions $a_U$, $a_S$, and the terminal signal $T$ based on the current policies $\pi$ and $\mu$.
    *   Execute the sampled actions to observe rewards $r_U$, $r_S$, $r_G$ and the subsequent states $s'_U$, $s'_S$.
    *   Update the hybrid value network (critic) using Equation (2).
    *   Compute the advantages $A_U$, $A_S$, and $A_G$ using the current value network.
    *   Update the dialog policies (actors) using Equation (3).
    *   Update the states: $s_U \leftarrow s'_U$ and $s_S \leftarrow s'_S$.
    *   Synchronize the target network parameters $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$ every $C$ steps.
    *   Terminate the loop when the session ends according to signal $T$.

Each dialog policy is designed to maximize the cumulative returns associated with its respective objectives. Specifically, the system policy $\pi$ aims to maximize the expected cumulative sum of system and global rewards, expressed as $\mathbb{E}[\sum_t \gamma^t (r_{S,t} + r_{G,t})]$.

The components of a course–lecture, recitation, homework, and exams–do not exist in isolation. Rather, these components are designed to complement and supplement each other as students interact with them in various ways and at various times over a semester. The interactions (and potential interactions) of these course components can add to the cognitive challenges already present in coding tasks and programming assignments. As part of a larger investigation related to the broad research question, “What are the experiences of students in the CS1 course as they complete homework assignments and submit them through an autograding system?”, a more specific research question emerged as we started interviewing students: What are the sources of information that students use when they get “stuck” while working on homework assignments in a CS1 course and how do they navigate multiple sources of information? Homework is typically viewed as a tool to help students gain a better understanding of the course material and how to use it to succeed in the class . It remains an essential component of beginning programming courses because “goaldirected practice coupled with targeted feedback are critical to learning” , and programming assignments are the tools of choice for providing this handson practice. From the students’ perspective, time spent doing homework represents a large portion of their exposure to the material of the class, and, ideally, it would be the largest portion of time spent on programming tasks. Traditionally, homework is an aspect of the course that instructors have very little opportunity to observe, much less direct the way in which students engage with the assigned problems. Consequently, instructors are left wondering whether students have a clear understanding of their information needs when doing homework or not. When students do not know the next step for a homework assignment, they must work out how to find that information . How do students understand their information needs as they are working to solve programming problems? This is a question that has received attention in the field of information science as information seeking behavior and numerous theoretical frameworks have been used to explain these search patterns in lowguidance settings including Dervin’s theory of sensemaking and Bates’ “berrypicking” model .

**System Design and Data Methodology**

**Interactive Visualization and User Input**
The system incorporates an interactive avatar interface wherein relevant anatomical regions are dynamically highlighted in response to textual descriptions of medical conditions. This functionality is triggered by prompts such as, "This medical condition affects the following body parts." Furthermore, the interface supports direct user interaction, allowing individuals to indicate specific areas of discomfort by clicking on the avatar, accompanied by input such as, "I am not feeling well here."

**SocialBot Architecture and Conversational Modeling**
To facilitate engaging discourse on diverse subjects, including current events, the SocialBot module utilizes a curated knowledge vault. This repository enables the system to initiate and sustain conversations on topics of interest, exemplified by responses such as, "Sure, we can discuss German beer," or "I see you wish to discuss Afghan hounds." Topic extraction is performed through the aggregation of data from Wikipedia, news outlets, and social media platforms, including Reddit. The conversational logic is designed to emulate effective human interaction principles: providing substantive content and demonstrating active interest in the interlocutor's contributions.

**Prototype Development and Language Processing**
Following the establishment of high-level design objectives, the current prototype employs simplified language understanding capabilities. These serve as functional placeholders to expedite development and will be superseded by advanced natural language processing methodologies in subsequent iterations.

**Data Sources and Preprocessing**
The prototype's training and operational data are derived from the following sources:

1.  **Medical Topic Data:** A dataset comprising 1,031 high-level medical topics was extracted from Medline. Summaries were processed to generate question-answer pairs, resulting in three distinct data files: question-topic pairs, answer-topic pairs, and question-answer pairs. Detailed statistics regarding data volume and partitioning are provided in the subsequent sections.
2.  **Clinical Dialogue Data:** The MedDialog dataset, containing 260,000 patient-doctor dialogues sourced from healthcaremagic.com and icliniq.com, was utilized to model clinical interactions.
3.  **Medical Question-Answering Data:** The MedQuAD dataset, consisting of 47,457 question-answer pairs derived from 12 National Institutes of Health (NIH) websites, was integrated to enhance domain-specific query resolution.
4.  **General News Data:** The News category dataset from Kaggle was employed, focusing on 39 of its 41 available topics. Categories related to "Healthy Living" and "Wellness" were excluded to maintain a clear distinction from the medical domain. Short descriptions were extracted for processing.
5.  **Social Media Data:** Questions and comments were aggregated from 30 distinct subreddits, with each subreddit treated as a discrete topic. The distribution of queries per topic is detailed in the accompanying analysis.

Although the existing encoderdecoder architecture yields reasonable generation results, it relies heavily on the decoder to extract thorough semantic components of the word, leading to underspecific definition generation results, i.e. missing some semantic components. As illustrated in Table 1 , to generate a precise definition of the word “captain”, one needs to know that “captain” refers to a person , “captain” is related to ship , and “captain” manages or is in charge of the ship , where person , ship , manage are three semantic components of word “captain”. However, due to the lack of explicitly modeling of these semantic components, the model misses the semantic component “manage” for the word “captain”. Linguists and lexicographers define a word by decomposing its meaning into its semantic components and expressing them in natural language sentences. Inspired by this, Yang et al. incorporate sememes, i.e. minimum units of semantic meanings of human languages, in the task of generating definition in Chinese. However, it is just as, if not more, timeconsuming and expensive to label the components of words than to write definitions manually. In this paper, we propose to explicitly decompose the meaning of words into semantic components for definition generation. We introduce a group of discrete latent variables to model the underlying semantic components.Extending the established training technique for discrete latent variable used in representation learning and machine translation tasks, we further propose two auxiliary losses to ensure that the introduced latent variables capture the word semantics. Experimental results show that our method achieves significant improvements over previous methods on two definition generation datasets. We also show that our model indeed learns meaningful and informative latent codes, and generates more precise and specific definitions. 2 Background In this section, we introduce the background of the original definition modeling task and two extensive works to original definition modeling. 2.1 Definition Modeling Definition generation was firstly proposed by Noraset et al..

BLEU is a wellknown word overlap metric that computes ngram precision between the generated sequence and the reference. Because precision favors shorter sentences, BLEU also adds a brevity penalty that punishes shorter sentences. BLEU has been found to correlate poorly with human judgment. METEOR was designed as an improvement on BLEU using a harmonic mean of precision and recall, as well as stemming and synonyms. ROUGEL identifies the longest common subsequence between the generated and reference sequence to better account for sentencelevel structure when computing word overlap. Greedy Matching is an embeddingbased metric that greedily matches each word in the generated sequence to a reference word based on the cosine similarity of their embeddings. The final score is then an average over all the words in the generated sequence. Embedding Average computes a sentence embedding for both the generated sequence and the groundtruth response by taking an average of word embeddings. The score is then a cosine similarity of the average embedding for both the generated and reference sequence. Vector Extrema follows a similar setup to Embedding Average, where the score is the cosine similarity between sentence embeddings. Rather than taking an average over word embeddings, this method identifies the maximum value for each dimension of the word embedding. Taking the maximum is motivated by the idea that common words will be deemphasized as they will be closer to the origin. Vector Extrema has been shown to perform better on dialog tasks than other metrics. SkipThought uses a recurrent neural network to produce a sentencelevel embedding for the generated and reference sequences.

If a certain linkage is important in the design, though not the only way to accomplish a task, you should make a persuasive case for it, or it may not be accepted. Embedded within the design process is the selection of the optimum solution among several possibilities, and it is important to articulate well your justification for this solution. In business and industry, a competing view may be asserted by another department asking for funding, recognizing that not all projects can be funded. The marketing department may want assistance to expand sales; manufacturing may request funds for new equipment or, in the case of a design, argue against the design because it requires new labor skills. The reports that you write describing your project, as well as the memos and letters that you generate, should persuade others that you are correct, your reasoning is sound, and your conclusions are convincing. Of course, the work you generate must be good, it must stand on its own merits; and you will ensure this standing if those merits are effectively communicated. It is not redundant to ask yourself before writing whether you have a good idea of precisely what you want to communicate. Frequently, this will relate to presenting information or persuading people to act or think in a certain way. Questions to ask yourself at this stage include: Why do they want to read this document? Does the technical level of detail intrigue or bore them? Is their initial attitude likely to be positive, negative, or neutral? Will the document give them the information they desire? Answers to these questions will help you write more effective term papers, letters, and engineering reports. Get to the point. Readers appreciate going from the general to the specific, from conclusions to details. For instance, if you perform a series of tests on a piece of equipment to determine its desirability in a system, your supervisor will want to know what you found out and your recommendation. The supporting detail of the testing procedure is important and will be further analyzed by others, but as the recommendation moves upward in management, your conclusion is the focus. Please note, this assumes that the testing procedure and the analysis are sound and accurate; if they are not, then no amount of clever language will suffice. Engineering competence is essential.

Coreference resolution is a relationoriented task that links an expression to its mentions within or beyond a single sentence. Dependency parsing is also a relationoriented task that aims to relate a word (singleword span) to its syntactic parent word with the corresponding dependency type. Detailed explanations of all tasks can be found in Appendix A . While the tasks above represent a remarkably broad swath of NLP, it is worth mentioning what we have not covered, to properly scope this work. Notably, sentencelevel tasks such as text classification and natural language inference are not covered, although they can also be formulated using this spanrelation representation by treating the entire sentence as a span. We chose to omit these tasks because they are already wellrepresented by previous work on generalized architectures and multitask learning, and thus we mainly focus on tasks using phraselike spans. In addition, the spanrelation representations described here are designed for natural language analysis , and cannot handle tasks that require generation of text, such as machine translation, dialog response generation, and summarization.

**3.2 Comparative Baselines**

Our proposed model was implemented and evaluated on two datasets: Wizard of Wikipedia and DuConv. In both instances, our approach was benchmarked against a range of state-of-the-art baselines.

*   **Wizard of Wikipedia:** The following baseline models were selected for comparison:
    *   **Seq2Seq:** An attention-based sequence-to-sequence architecture that operates without access to external knowledge, a standard approach in open-domain dialogue systems (Vinyals and Le, 2015).
    *   **MemNet (Hard/Soft):** A knowledge-grounded generation model. The "Hard" variant selects knowledge candidates based on semantic similarity, whereas the "Soft" variant stores candidates within memory units to facilitate generation (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018).
    *   **PostKS (Concat/Fusion):** A knowledge-grounded model utilizing a GRU decoder. The "Concat" variant employs hard knowledge grounding via concatenation, while the "Fusion" variant utilizes a Hierarchical Graph Fusion Unit (HGFU) for soft knowledge integration (Lian et al., 2019).
    *   **KIC:** Our proposed joint neural conversation model, which integrates knowledge-aware pointer networks with a recurrent knowledge interaction hybrid generator.

*   **DuConv:** For this Chinese dialogue dataset featuring structured knowledge, comparisons were conducted against the baselines cited in Wu et al. (2019b). These baselines include a comprehensive set of both retrieval-based and generation-based models.

**3.3 Evaluation Metrics**

We employed an automatic evaluation framework utilizing standard metrics proposed by Wu et al. (2019b) and Lian et al. (2019). To ensure consistency, the official evaluation tools provided by the respective authors were used to compute experimental results. The selected metrics include BLEU-1/2/3, F1, and DISTINCT-1/2, which collectively assess fluency, coherence, relevance, and diversity. Specifically, the F1 metric evaluates performance at the character level and is primarily applied to the Chinese DuConv dataset.

Given that our method incorporates knowledge via soft fusion without explicit knowledge selection, evaluation focuses on the overall dialogue quality rather than the independent performance of knowledge selection mechanisms.

In addition to automatic metrics, a human evaluation was conducted involving three annotators. These annotators assessed the generated responses based on fluency, informativeness, and coherence. Scores were assigned on a scale from 0 to 2, where 0 indicates poor quality and 2 indicates high quality across the specified dimensions.

**Evaluation of Constructed Sentences**

When evaluating models on naturally occurring sentences, high performance may stem from statistical regularities within the data rather than a genuine mastery of syntax. For instance, in a context such as "The players *MASK* the ball," a model might correctly predict the verb form by exploiting the heuristic that animate nouns typically function as subjects, whereas inanimate nouns do not. As noted by Gulordava et al. (2018), such reliance allows a model to achieve correct predictions for incorrect reasons. To determine whether our models were leveraging this specific statistical heuristic, we constructed a 400-sentence test set designed to render such heuristics ineffective.

This dataset was generated using a probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG) in which all words of a given part of speech are equally likely to appear in any position. Each sentence adheres to a Subject-Verb-Object structure, with noun phrases optionally modified by adjectives and/or prepositional phrases (see Appendix F), as illustrated in Example (4):

> (4) The fern near the sad teachers hates the singer.

The Dependency LSTM is particularly susceptible to word co-occurrence heuristics due to its architectural limitation: it lacks the capacity for a parent node to account for the sequential position of its children. This deficiency becomes critical when determining subject-verb agreement, as the model cannot robustly distinguish between a verb's subject and its direct object. Consistent with this hypothesis, the dependency model performed at chance levels (see Figure 1a). This result suggests that the model's high accuracy on natural data is partially attributable to lexical heuristics rather than syntactic processing. In contrast, the other models maintained strong performance, indicating a lower susceptibility to reliance on word co-occurrence patterns.

### 5.1 Experimental Setup

To evaluate the proposed methodology for the automatic verification of synset-image associations, a rigorous procedure for generating negative instances—defined as irrelevant $\langle \text{synset}, \text{image} \rangle$ pairs—was established. Specifically, a negative instance $\langle s, i \rangle$ is constructed by selecting two distinct synsets, $s$ and $s'$, and an image $i$ associated with $s'$ from the gold-standard dataset. These negative instances are categorized based on the semantic relationship between $s$ and $s'$:

*   **Sibling:** A relationship exists where both $s$ and $s'$ are connected to a common synset $s''$ via the hypernymy relation within BabelNet (e.g., *FUN RUN* and *MARATHON*).
*   **Polysemy:** Both $s$ and $s'$ share the same lemma (e.g., the synsets for *swim.v.01* and *swim.v.02*).
*   **Unrelated:** No semantic relation connects $s$ and $s'$ within BabelNet (e.g., *RACING* and *GLADFULNESS*).

Leveraging WordNet relations as described above effectively mitigates potential issues arising from images that instantiate multiple concepts. For instance, images depicting specific athletic events (such as *HIGH JUMP* or *POLE VAULT*) are excluded from serving as negative examples for the broader concept of *COMPETITION* due to the existing hyponymy relationship. Furthermore, a manual validation of a representative sample of negative examples was conducted to ensure the reliability of the generation methodology.

The resulting dataset is perfectly balanced across the two output classes. It was partitioned into training, validation, and test sets adhering to an 80%/10%/10% distribution. Both the class labels and the semantic relations defining the negative instances were proportionally distributed across these splits. To assess the system's generalization capability regarding previously unseen concepts, the validation and test sets were explicitly constructed to include instances referencing synsets absent from the training set. This specific subset of the test data is designated as the *zero-shot* test set. Detailed statistics are provided in Table 1.

### 4.2.2 Clique Extraction

The Bron–Kerbosch (BK) algorithm, a recursive backtracking procedure designed to identify all maximal cliques within a given graph $G$, is employed for efficient clique extraction. The pruning operation detailed in Section 3.1 results in a sparse word graph; for such graphs, the BK algorithm achieves a time complexity of $O(dn^{3d/3})$ (Eppstein and Strash, 2011), where $n$ denotes the number of vertices in $G$ and $d$ represents the graph's degeneracy. An optimized public C implementation of the BK algorithm is utilized, with the extraction process restricted to cliques containing a minimum of three words. Empirical observations indicate that clique extraction is completed within seconds using this implementation.

### 4.2.3 Mapping of Clique and Word Embeddings

In the experimental phase, the mapping of clique embeddings across two languages is performed using the methodology proposed by Artetxe et al. (2018b), implemented via their publicly available codebase. The transformation matrix $W$ is initialized as a random orthogonal matrix. Following the construction of the seed dictionary, the Procrustes problem (Equation 1) is solved, subsequently followed by a refinement process.

While analogous methodologies have been established within the domain of computer vision for image processing, their application to Natural Language Processing (NLP), specifically for sentiment polarity classification, remains unexplored. This study introduces two distinct frameworks for handling multimodal data: feature-level fusion and decision-level fusion. Both approaches involve the independent extraction of features from each modality. In the feature-level fusion strategy, the resulting feature vectors are concatenated to form a unified representation, which is subsequently processed by a supervised classifier. Conversely, the decision-level fusion approach entails feeding modality-specific features into separate classifiers, with the final prediction derived from the aggregation of their individual decisions.

Experimental results indicate that both proposed methods significantly outperform current state-of-the-art benchmarks. For textual feature extraction, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was employed. Although the original dataset consists of Spanish utterances, and direct processing in the source language is generally preferred, this study utilized Google Translate to convert the data into English. Preliminary analysis revealed that processing the data without translation yielded an accuracy of 68.56%.

The selection of CNNs as trainable feature extractors is predicated on several key considerations. The convolutional layers of a CNN function effectively as feature extractors, generating local representations for individual words that are aggregated into a global feature vector for the entire text. While CNNs are traditionally utilized as end-to-end supervised classifiers—often relying on simplistic internal classifiers that may not be optimal for final classification—their internal feature representations can be isolated and repurposed. This adaptation transforms the CNN from a standalone classifier into an automatic, trainable feature extractor that does not rely on handcrafted features. Instead, it adapts to the specific characteristics of the dataset in a supervised manner, producing hierarchical features that effectively capture contextual nuances.

A recognized limitation of CNNs when used strictly as classifiers is their tendency to converge to local optima, a consequence of employing backpropagation techniques similar to those used in Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs). However, drawing inspiration from computer vision paradigms, this work pioneers the extraction of internal CNN features within the NLP domain. These features are then fed into a more sophisticated classifier, thereby mitigating the limitations of the CNN's native classification capability while leveraging its robust feature learning potential.

As previously mentioned, these arguments may be collected from a large audience by conducting a survey, or mined automatically from text. Ajjour et al. addressed the task of splitting a set of arguments into a set of nonoverlapping frames such as Economics , Environment and Politics . Reimers et al. classified argument pairs as similar/dissimilar. Misra et al. aimed to detect argument pairs that are assumed to share the same argument facet , which is similar to our notion of key points . However, they did not attempt to explicitly identify or generate these facets, which remained implicit, but rather focused on detecting similarity between argument pairs. In contrast to these works, we directly map arguments to key points.

Numerous studies have investigated the development and application of automatic grading platforms [1, 3, 4, 7–9, 12]. Historically, the majority of these systems have relied on binary evaluation criteria, such as verifying program execution or confirming correct outputs for specific test cases. Consequently, the feedback provided to learners through such mechanisms has often been limited in scope. While recent initiatives have sought to enhance feedback quality through advanced autograding techniques—for instance, by integrating knowledge maps to classify common errors and generate targeted hints [citation needed]—the existing literature predominantly addresses introductory computer science curricula.

In contrast, the present study focuses on intermediate-level (300-level) coursework, characterized by more complex programming assignments and advanced conceptual requirements. Beyond assessing technical proficiency in a specific programming language, this work prioritizes the evaluation of students' comprehension of algorithmic principles and adherence to established coding standards [citation needed]. Furthermore, it was observed that current autograding solutions lack the necessary granularity to deliver the detailed feedback required for this level of instruction. Given the course's emphasis on the implementation of artificial intelligence algorithms, the objective was to establish a comprehensive checklist of essential steps and concepts for each algorithm, utilizing this framework to guide student improvement.

To address these needs, the teaching team developed an in-house grading tool tailored for intermediate classes, with the intention of disseminating this resource to the broader computer science community. Additionally, while prevailing literature often validates grading approaches based on the alignment of automated scores and feedback with human evaluations [citation needed], there is a notable scarcity of research assessing these systems based on actual student outcomes. As automatic grading becomes increasingly ubiquitous, it is imperative to examine the potential implications of substituting human assessment with automated feedback in real-world educational contexts. A primary objective of this research is to address this critical gap.

**Methodological Overview**

The proposed SeqGFMN framework shares conceptual similarities with SeqGAN, specifically regarding the utilization of a per-token reward mechanism derived from feature matching loss. However, SeqGFMN mitigates the necessity for generator pretraining and the computational complexity associated with training a discriminator. Instead, it leverages a fixed, state-of-the-art text feature extractor, such as BERT. Given the inherent challenges in training implicit models for discrete data generation (de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019), this approach employs reinforcement learning techniques, including REINFORCE and actor-critic methods (Fedus et al., 2018), alongside the Gumbel-Softmax trick (Kusner & Hernández-Lobato, 2016).

(2018) utilized adversarial classifiers to facilitate decoding into distinct styles or languages. Melnyk et al. (2017) and Nogueira dos Santos et al. (2018) proposed a methodology integrating a collaborative classifier with back-translation loss. Furthermore, Prabhumoye et al. (2018) presented an approach involving the training of style-specific encoders by combining a pretrained neural machine translation (NMT) encoder with style classifiers. The primary distinction of the current approach lies in its reliance on feature matching loss to achieve distribution alignment, differing from the aforementioned strategies.

**4. Experiments and Results**

**Datasets**
The proposed methodology was evaluated across three distinct English datasets: MSCOCO (Lin et al., 2014), the EMNLP 2017 WMT News dataset (Bojar et al., 2017), and the Yelp Reviews Dataset (Shen et al., 2017). The MSCOCO and WMT News datasets were utilized for unconditional generation tasks, whereas the Yelp Reviews dataset served to evaluate class-conditional generation and unsupervised text style transfer.

**Feature Extractors for Textual Data**
Experiments were conducted using various feature extractors capable of generating token-level representations. To represent shallow, computationally efficient architectures, word embeddings from GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) and FastText (Bojanowski et al., 2017) were employed. As a representative of large-scale, deep feature extraction, BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) was utilized. Building upon Devlin et al.'s (2018) demonstration that BERT-derived features significantly enhance performance across diverse NLP tasks, this study hypothesizes that BERT features possess sufficient informativeness to facilitate the training of cross-domain text generators via feature matching.

However, these are not hard constraints, because the model may learn to ignore such information. 3 Proposed Model Given a source sentence x = ( x 1 , x 2 , . . . , x n ) as input, our goal is to generate a shorter sentence y = ( y 1 , y 2 , . . . , y m ) as a summary of x . We perform wordlevel extraction, in addition keeping the original word order intact. Thus, y is a subsequence of x . Our wordlevel extraction optimizes a manually defined objective function f ( y ; x , s ) , where the summary length s is predefined ( s < n ) and not subject to optimization. In the remainder of this section, we will describe the objective function, search space, and the search algorithm in detail. 3.1 Search Objective We define an objective function f ( y ; x , s ) , which our algorithm maximizes. It evaluates the fitness of a candidate sentence y as the summary of an input x , involving three aspects, namely, language fluency f ←→ LM ( y ) , semantic similarity f SIM ( y ; x ) , and a length constraint f LEN ( y , s ) .

For each memory cell, the memory vector is updated according to three distinct operational modes: ignoring the current token, establishing coreference with the token, or overwriting the cell with a new entity representation. This update mechanism is formalized as follows:

$$
\mathbf{m}_{i}^{t} = \underbrace{(1 - (o_{i}^{t} + c_{i}^{t})) \mathbf{m}_{i}^{t-1}}_{\text{IGNORE}} + \underbrace{o_{i}^{t} \cdot \mathbf{h}_{t}}_{\text{OVERWRITE}} + \underbrace{c_{i}^{t} \cdot \text{MLP}_{3}([\mathbf{h}_{t}; \mathbf{m}_{i}^{t-1}])}_{\text{COREF}}
$$

In this formulation, the coreference term integrates both the preceding cell vector ($\mathbf{m}_{i}^{t-1}$) and the current token representation ($\mathbf{h}_{t}$), whereas the overwrite term relies exclusively on $\mathbf{h}_{t}$. Distinct from the Referential Reader architecture, which utilizes a pair of Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) specific to each memory cell, the proposed approach employs a single, cell-agnostic MLP ($\text{MLP}_{3}$) for the update parameters.

Subsequently, the memory usage scalar is updated via the following equation:

$$
u_{i}^{t} = \min(1, o_{i}^{t} + c_{i}^{t} + \gamma \cdot u_{i}^{t-1}) \quad (8)
$$

where $\gamma \in (0, 1)$ denotes the decay rate. Consequently, the usage scalar $u_{i}^{t}$ decays over time unless the memory cell is actively updated through an OVERWRITE or COREF operation, in which case the value is incremented to reflect recent utilization.
