To address this, we employ model confidence as the decision criterion. Specifically, a probability threshold, denoted as $\rho_k$, is established for each $wait_k$ policy. At any given step, if the Neural Machine Translation (NMT) model adheres to a $wait_{k'}$ policy and the probability of the most likely predicted token exceeds the corresponding threshold $\rho_{k'}$, the model is deemed confident, and the WRITE action is selected. Conversely, if the probability falls below this threshold, the READ action is chosen. Figure 2 illustrates this decision-making process.

We formally define the application of a $wait_k$ model, $M_k$, utilizing a $wait_k$ policy on a given sequence pair $(x, y)$ as follows:

$$y_{\text{top}}, p_{\text{top}} \leftarrow P_k(M_k, x, y)$$

This operation constrains model $M_k$ to predict the target sequence $y$, returning the top-ranked token $y_{\text{top}}$ at the final step along with its associated probability $p_{\text{top}}$. The operation of reading and retrieving a new source token is denoted by $\text{READ}(\cdot)$, while the expression $x \circ x'$ represents the concatenation of an element $x'$ to the end of sequence $x$. Furthermore, the symbols $\langle s \rangle$ and $\langle /s \rangle$ denote the start and end tokens of a sequence, respectively. The Computer Attitudes Survey (CAS) is a psychometric instrument designed to assess student attitudes toward knowledge acquisition and problem-solving within the discipline of computer science. The instrument is characterized by four primary design principles: (1) it elicits attitudes regarding the CS discipline broadly, rather than focusing on specific courses, pedagogical approaches, or programming languages; (2) its items are formulated to ensure clarity, conciseness, and unambiguous interpretation; and (3) its categorical structure and subscales are derived empirically from student response data rather than being predetermined by researcher interests.

The development of the CAS involved four iterative revisions between 2011 and 2015. The initial version adapted the Classroom Survey of Student Engagement (CLASS) to a computing context. CAS Version 2 (CASv2), developed following think-aloud interviews with faculty and students and piloted in Fall 2011 with 447 introductory students, comprised 53 Likert-scale items. Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) of this version identified eight factors: problem-solving transfer, problem-solving strategies, problem-solving fixed mindset, real-world connections, problem-solving confidence, problem-solving sense-making, abstraction, and personal interest/enjoyment.

CAS Version 3 (CASv3) was subsequently administered in a pre-post format across three semesters at two universities, yielding 2,873 responses. EFA on this dataset revealed seven latent factors. Following Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) on these factors, the instrument was further refined to produce the final iteration, CAS Version 4 (CASv4). Developed by Dorn and Tew, CASv4 consists of 26 items utilizing a 5-point Likert scale and measures five distinct factors: problem-solving transfer, personal interest, problem-solving strategies, real-world connections, and problem-solving fixed mindset.

In the present study, the CASv4 was augmented with two additional sets of items. First, a subset of nine questions from Wiebe et al. was incorporated to assess attitudes related to gender issues; it is noted that these items may introduce the potential for stereotype threat. Second, five additional questions were added to probe student attitudes regarding the utility of computing. These modifications were informed by findings from Cooper et al., who observed that standard attitude surveys often failed to fully capture insights from individual interviews—particularly among Latina students—regarding the significance of gender equality and the perceived utility of computing.

**Comparison with Prior Work**

Relative to existing literature, the "Delete, Retrieve, and Generate" (DRG) framework (Li et al., 2018) and its subsequent extension (Sudhakar et al., 2019) have demonstrated efficacy in generating outputs adhering to a target style while maintaining a high degree of source content preservation. Nevertheless, the DRG approach is subject to several significant limitations: (1) the deletion module frequently misidentifies content words as stylistic markers, resulting in their unnecessary removal; (2) the retrieval phase presupposes the existence of semantically similar content across both source and target styles; (3) the retrieval process incurs substantial computational overhead, particularly when applied to large-scale datasets; (4) the pipeline operates on the assumption that style transfer can be achieved solely by excising stylistic markers and substituting them with target-style phrases; and (5) the method depends on a fixed corpus of style attribute markers, thereby constraining its generalizability to unseen data during inference.

In contrast, the proposed methodology diverges from these approaches by eliminating the retrieval stage and refraining from assumptions regarding the presence of overlapping content phrases between styles. Consequently, this architecture offers enhanced computational efficiency and demonstrates greater robustness to noise. Furthermore, while Wu et al. (2019) conceptualize style transfer as a conditional language modeling task focused exclusively on sentiment modification—specifically as a cloze-style exercise of inserting appropriate words—the current work is capable of generating complete sentences in the target style. Additionally, the proposed approach exhibits superior generalizability, as evidenced by experimental results across five distinct style transfer tasks.

**3.1 Politeness Transfer Task**

The politeness transfer task focuses on sentences wherein the speaker communicates a requirement necessitating fulfillment by the listener. Typical instances include imperatives (e.g., "Let's stay in touch") and interrogatives expressing proposals (e.g., "Can you call me when you get back?"). Adhering to the taxonomy established by Jurafsky et al. (1997), such utterances are collectively termed "action-directives." The objective of this task is to transform action-directives into polite requests. While multiple strategies exist for enhancing politeness, effective modifications for the aforementioned examples include the addition of expressions of gratitude (e.g., "Thanks, and let's stay in touch") or the utilization of counterfactual constructions (e.g., "Could you please call me when you get back?") **Limitations of Traditional and Pretrained Architectures**

Conventional word embeddings alone lack the representational capacity required to adequately model complex emotional contexts. While Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) demonstrate strong performance on the EMO-NET dataset (AbdulMageed and Ungar, 2017), we posit that this success stems primarily from the memorization of emotion lexicons (Section 4.1) rather than the effective capture of implicit emotional nuances. Furthermore, BERT achieves an average accuracy of approximately 64%, indicating significant potential for future improvement. To address this, we conduct a comprehensive error analysis as a foundational step toward enhancing model performance (Section 5.3).

Contrary to expectations, the adoption of more advanced pretrained models, such as RoBERTa, does not guarantee performance gains. Unlike standard benchmarks like GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), where increased pretraining correlates monotonically with improved results, our experiments reveal a divergent trend. Although RoBERTa outperforms GRUs by approximately 5%, it underperforms relative to BERT by roughly 6%. We hypothesize that this degradation is attributable to domain discrepancies between the pretraining and fine-tuning phases. Specifically, the additional pretraining data utilized by RoBERTa (e.g., CCNews) may be insufficiently aligned with the characteristics of Twitter data, which is distinguished by its brevity and unique vernacular (Ritter et al., 2011). Consequently, we advise practitioners against the direct application of state-of-the-art models without incorporating task-specific pretraining objectives, a strategy we explore in Section 6.

**5.3 Error Analysis**

To evaluate model limitations, we sampled 25 erroneous predictions from each of the eight emotion tasks using our BERT model, resulting in a total of 200 errors. These errors were categorized into five groups: lexical and syntactic cues (45%), insufficient context (24%), entity mentions (15%), subjective labeling (10%), and unknown causes (6%). The three predominant categories are discussed below:

*   **Lexical and Syntactic Cues:** Similar to most emotion prediction models, BERT exhibits a reliance on surface-level lexical features. This bias extends to specific syntactic markers, such as punctuation. For instance, in the utterance "pls be safe everyone!!!!", the model incorrectly associates the exclamation marks with a positive emotion, whereas the context suggests the speaker is expressing concern.
*   **Insufficient Context:** A significant portion of errors arises from user comments referencing external events, public policies, or linked content that lack sufficient internal features for supervised learning. This limitation is not necessarily inherent to the BERT architecture but rather reflects constraints within the dataset. For example, in the statement "for [tracy mcgrady] [hall induction] muted by effects of [hurricane harvey] at home," accurate emotion detection requires external knowledge to infer relationships between noun phrases and discern latent sentiments.

This prefix may be genuinely ambiguous making the parser have two derivations in the beam, one in state A and the other in state B , both equally good up until that point in the sentence. After processing some more words, the parser might find a word that resolves the ambiguity and provides evidence that the state A was correct. ### 3.4 Stability Training Method

Zheng et al. (2016) identified output instability as a significant challenge in deep neural networks. To address this, they introduced a training methodology designed to stabilize network outputs against minor input perturbations, successfully applying it to tasks such as near-duplicate image detection, similar-image ranking, and image classification. Drawing inspiration from this approach, we adapt the stability training framework to the domain of natural language processing.

The primary objective is to stabilize the outputs $y(x)$ of a sequence labeling system against small input perturbations. Conceptually, this involves flattening the output function $y(x)$ within the local neighborhood of any given input sentence $x$. Specifically, if a perturbed input $\tilde{x}$ is sufficiently close to the original input $x$, the corresponding output $y(\tilde{x})$ should remain proximate to $y(x)$.

Given the standard training objective $L_0(x, y; \theta)$, the original input sentence $x$, its perturbed counterpart $\tilde{x}$, and the ground-truth label sequence $y$, the stability training objective $L_{\text{stabil}}$ is defined as:

$$
L_{\text{stabil}}(x, \tilde{x}, y; \theta) = L_0(x, y; \theta) + \alpha L_{\text{sim}}(x, \tilde{x}; \theta)
$$

where the similarity loss term is defined as:

$$
L_{\text{sim}}(x, \tilde{x}; \theta) = D\big(y(x), y(\tilde{x})\big)
$$

In this formulation, $L_{\text{sim}}$ enforces similarity between the model outputs for $x$ and $\tilde{x}$; $D$ represents a task-specific feature distance metric; and $\alpha$ serves as a hyperparameter balancing the influence of the similarity objective.

Let $R(x)$ and $Q(\tilde{x})$ denote the discrete probability distributions derived by applying the softmax function to the logits $l(x)$ for $x$ and $\tilde{x}$, respectively:

$$
R(x) = P(y|x) = \text{softmax}\big(l(x)\big)
$$

$$
Q(\tilde{x}) = P(y|\tilde{x}) = \text{softmax}\big(l(\tilde{x})\big)
$$

Similarly, GitHub was selected due to its cost-free access for educational institutions and its user-friendly web interface. However, the core functionality of Infrastructor relies only on the version control system's ability to atomically create repositories and to atomically add, update, replace, and verify the status of files.

I partially agree with the statement. People who succeed in achieving their goals usually go through various stages in life. For instance, those who are persistent and stick to their ideas are more likely to succeed than those who give up easily and don‚Äôt put in much effort after failing the first time.

Trying new things and taking risks definitely increase your chances of getting what you want or reaching your goals. Taking risks requires a lot of courage, and it‚Äôs at this point that people decide whether to aim for better results or risk ending up with worse ones. On the other hand, staying in your comfort zone‚Äîdoing what you‚Äôre good at and enjoy‚Äîcan also lead to either failure or success. Doing things the right way can boost your self-confidence, which is essential for achieving your goals.

For example, imagine a successful income tax CPA with a good reputation who decides to study law to deepen his understanding of tax regulations, making him even more helpful to his clients. Even though this decision might mean less time with his family or at the office, taking this risk could ultimately lead to greater success for his firm.

Nowadays, extreme sports like skydiving and skiing are becoming increasingly popular. These activities give people an adrenaline rush and allow them to push past their usual boundaries. In my view, these sports should not be banned simply because they involve some risk.

Firstly, extreme sports are still sports, just like any other. Even well-known games such as cricket can be dangerous‚Äîfor example, a solid leather ball can cause serious injury. Extreme sports are designed for enjoyment and can also help participants relieve stress.

Secondly, if these sports are organized properly, the risks can be minimized. There should be clear guidelines ensuring that all equipment is in top condition and that thorough safety checks are carried out.

Finally, despite the risks, many people are drawn to extreme sports because they help reduce stress from work or home life. Physical activity causes our bodies to release oxytocin, a hormone that helps us feel calmer and more relaxed, unlike the stress we experience from constant emails or phone calls.

In conclusion, I do not support banning extreme sports. They offer excitement and help people build confidence by challenging their limits. People everywhere enjoy these activities for the thrill and the rush of adrenaline they provide.

Discovering Success

Who is considered successful these days? What does success mean to me?

Is someone who takes risks more successful than someone who doesn‚Äôt?

In my view, being successful in your job, career, or social life comes from developing certain skills and mastering situations you already understand and know will lead to positive outcomes. Speaking from my own experience as a general dental practitioner‚Äîa regular dentist‚ÄîI know how to do my work well to achieve good results. I would never attempt something new or risky just to appear brave if I wasn't certain it would turn out well.

People who take risks in search of success often do so because they feel they have nothing to lose.

For example, imagine I owned a business that failed for some reason. I would lose all my investment and go bankrupt‚Äîeverything would be gone. In that situation, if someone offered me an exceptional opportunity to start a new business from scratch, I would be willing to take the risk because I‚Äôd have nothing left to lose.

Let me give another example from my professional life. Suppose a patient comes to my clinic with pain in a central incisor. I take an x-ray and see a large infection at the root. I‚Äôve never performed an apicoectomy (which involves opening the gum and cutting the root), and I wouldn‚Äôt try it without proper training, as it could be dangerous for the patient. In this scenario, I would never take that risk just to be successful.

In conclusion, I completely disagree with the idea that you need to take risks to find success. I believe you can achieve success in your everyday life without taking unnecessary risks. Go out and find it!

The economic value added to a country hosting an international sports event has been said to benefit the community as a whole. However, many would argue that such events are a waste of useful resources, which could otherwise be streamlined to more needed areas. Although I am of the opinion that the advantages outweigh the disadvantages, in this essay I will discuss both viewpoints with the help of relevant examples.
To support my view, I would like to bring to attention the 2009 Olympics held in China, which brought individuals from all over the world. The funds collected from the event provided a massive boost to the economy of the country, a significant portion of which was utilised for the welfare of underprivileged children in the nation.
On the other hand, it is imperative to note that a majority of profits gained from these shows go to the upper classes. For example, the IPL tournaments held in India have seemingly benefitted the uber rich participating in the auctions, while simultaneously draining tax-payers of their money.
However, coming from a community where sports serves as a bonding activity, I would also like to elaborate on the non-economic benefits of these games. They act as a nidus for soothing political tensions and improve diplomatic relations in times of unrest.
While many may object to the violent nature of some sports such as boxing, and the impact they have on young minds, these activities also serve as a conversation starter and are likely to ignite a spark of interest for sports in many.

**Course Evaluation and Iterative Improvements**

Student feedback indicated that while the implementation of algorithms from scratch or based on research literature presented significant challenges, the allocated time for laboratory completion was deemed adequate. Furthermore, students noted a strong alignment between lecture content and recent job interview inquiries, highlighting the practical relevance of the curriculum.

Regarding laboratory sessions, feedback from the initial course offering suggested that certain sessions lacked sufficient instructional value due to limited formative feedback prior to progression. To address this, the second offering restructured the three-hour laboratory format. The first hour was dedicated to team demonstrations of previous and current results, facilitating a collaborative environment where peers and instructors provided guidance on progress and identified roadblocks.

The ethics component of the course received overwhelmingly positive responses. Students expressed appreciation for the integration of moral philosophy frameworks, which enabled them to translate intuitive reactions into structured analyses. Several participants indicated a desire for similar ethical discourse in other courses. Notably, one student shared a personal experience regarding ethical dilemmas encountered while interning at a military laboratory, underscoring the real-world applicability of these discussions.

Analysis of student response essays revealed a tendency toward generalizations rather than deep engagement with the assigned readings. Consequently, the second offering reduced the reading load from six texts to a maximum of two, allowing for more focused textual analysis. In the third offering, the curriculum was further enhanced by the introduction of the F1/10 simulator. Data indicated that teams allocated over 80% of their development time to simulation, with only 20% dedicated to physical platform tuning and map capture. This shift demonstrated a marked improvement in usability, reducing frustration during development iterations and testing. Given the high fidelity of the vehicle model within the simulator, the transition from simulation to physical reality required minimal effort.

Finally, it is acknowledged that the course scope limits the depth of exploration regarding individual autonomous driving modules. While the curriculum addresses the theoretical class of algorithms for mapping unknown environments, the practical application relies on existing software packages to achieve the primary objective of autonomous racing.

For keyphrase extraction , we use training, validation, and test data from SemEval 2017 Task 10, consisting of IO tags (we use one “I” tag for all three keyphrase types). In all tasks, we implement both the policy and difference classifier by finetuning the last layer of a BERT embedding representation. More specifically, for a sentence of length T , w 1 , . . . , w T , we first compute BERT embeddings for each word, x 1 , . . . , x T using the appropriate BERT model: English BERT and MBERT 5 for named entity and partofspeech, respectively, and SciBERT for keyphrase extraction. We then represent the state at position t by concatenating the word embedding at that position with a onehot representation of the previous action: s t = [ w t ; onehot ( a t − 1 )] . This feature representation is used both for learning the labeling policy and also learning the difference classifier. 4.3 Expert Policy and Heuristics In all experiments, the expert π ⋆ is a simulated human annotator who annotates one word at a time. The expert returns the optimal action for the relevant evaluation metric (Fscore for named entity recognition and keyphrase extraction, and accuracy for partofspeech tagging). We take the annotation cost to be the total number of words labeled. The heuristic we implement for named entity recognition is a highprecision gazeteerbased string matching approach. We construct this by taking a gazeteer from Wikipedia using the CogComp framework, and use 4 ADJ, ADP, ADV, AUX, CCONJ, DET, INTJ, NOUN, NUM, PART, PRON, PROPN, PUNCT, SCONJ, SYM, VERB, X. 5 Multilingual BERT FlashText to label the dataset. This heuristic achieves a precision of 0 . 88 , recall of 0 . 27 and Fscore of 0 . 41 on the training data. The keyphrase extraction heuristic is the output of an “unsupervised keyphrase extraction” approach.

It starts with searching for evidence, and feed it to a textual entailment model f to decide the claim’s veracity. claim verification pipeline starts with searching for existing evidence for the given query claim , and then leverages textual entailment models to determine the veracity of the claim relative to the evidence. However, sites such as snopes.com and other factchecking websites will not only provide their conclusion about the veracity of the claim relative to the evidence, but would also seek additional information that explains why people may think it fake. For example, Snopes details how the claim originated from nationalreport.net . The original version of the claim is related to the query claim, as well as other relevant claims, but carries a different meaning. It says: “Facebook could cease to exist, if they don’t do something about their rising costs” . Subsequently, the inaccurate claim, triggered by the original one, has been repeated by other websites and retweeted on social media, as shown in Figure 2 , possibly increasing the level of credibility some readers assign to it. The origins and causal derivations of data, as described above, are explicitly modeled in the context of databases and scientific workflow systems, where they are termed “data provenance.” We argue that modeling and understanding the provenance of a claim made in natural language is also very important since, beyond attribution, it helps people understand the background and the context in which a claim was generated, how different aspects of the claim are combined, and how a claim has been changed over time by different agents. At the same time, provenance provides us with an explanation for why people think a claim is real or fake, by looking at its history. Even if all one wants is to determine a stance relative to a claim, this may involve considering more than just its current incarnation, but rather its evolution over time and all of the sources that contributed to this evolution. Similarly, one may want to consider who influenced a claim, or who influences a specific author of multiple claims, and this can be accomplished by considering the origin and evolution of these claims.

A central objective of Project-Based Learning (PjBL) is the design of projects that are realistic, authentic, and grounded in real-world contexts. Such projects are inherently complex and often lack a single definitive solution; however, this ambiguity enhances the authenticity of the learning experience. Research indicates that student motivation, engagement, and academic achievement are significantly higher when learners originate their own project ideas compared to scenarios where instructors predetermine or suggest them.

PjBL offers distinct advantages for student learning, primarily through increased engagement and motivation. Student engagement is defined as the degree of active involvement in learning activities, while motivation serves as the driving force behind such involvement. Krause and Coates posit that the relationship between student motivation and engagement is contingent upon the dynamic between the learner and the learning environment. Furthermore, Coates et al. suggest that engagement levels may fluctuate based on the course modality and design. Consequently, instructors bear the initial responsibility of intentionally designing online learning environments that incorporate engaging activities to foster student motivation.

Scholarly consensus affirms that interaction with content, peers, and faculty promotes student engagement and active learning in online settings. While all three forms of interaction are vital for effective online education, students place particular value on interaction with faculty. Critical factors for success in online modalities include the faculty's ability to: (a) communicate effectively through both online and interpersonal channels; (b) utilize visual, auditory, and other multimedia tools; (c) exercise patience with students; and (d) maintain flexibility regarding course requirements, schedules, and communication methods.

It is important to acknowledge that student perceptions are subjective, varying across different courses and individual learners. Conversely, faculty members often maintain that the ultimate responsibility for online learning rests with the students. To address this, Jones developed the MUSIC Model of Academic Motivation, which outlines five strategies to effectively motivate student engagement: eMpowerment, Usefulness, Success, Interest, and Caring. To implement this model, faculty should assist students in: (1) feeling empowered by granting them decision-making authority over certain aspects of their learning; (2) understanding the utility of the material in relation to their short- and long-term goals; (3) believing in their capacity to succeed through requisite effort; (4) developing an interest in the content and instructional activities; and (5) perceiving that the learning community, including instructors and peers, genuinely cares about their academic progress and personal well-being.

This power can, of course, be used to only try to build better, more diverse NRS. But with this promise of user empowerment also comes an inevitable risk of user manipulation. There is a growing literature which addresses the manipulative potential of datadriven digital environments which try to nudge users towards certain ends or outcomes . When digital environments use user data to learn about (patterns of behavior of) their users and run experiments which, through feedback loops, can inform subtle (personalized) tweaks to the digital environment, one is dealing with a subtle but important line between user empowerment and user manipulation. It is important to ask whose interests are being served by nudging strategies. This question is as relevant as ever in the (online) news sector. The commercialization of the news has been discussed elaborately for decades and will remain important as private platforms such as Google and Facebook try (and succeed) to capture the news industry. In such a commercialized news context, one cannot simply assume that (an increased) collection of user data and user profiling tools for purposes of personalized nudging strategies will only be used to benefit the news consumer. The very same data and profiling tools that can be used for increasing exposure to news diversity can, at the very same time, can also be misused in pursuit of commercial or political ends, without the knowledge of the user and/or their ability to object. The difficult line between empowerment and manipulation is underlined by the challenges news organization face in navigating the digital news economy. As a study by Bodo´ makes clear, different actors within one and the same news organization have to engage in a difficult process of mutual sensemaking and negotiation to decide how a NRS should be implemented and what the NRS should aim to optimize. The second potential risk is to reduce the news readers’ autonomy. In general, it is important to note that Thaler and Sunstein’s suggestion that nudging is a policy and design principle without any serious drawbacks has been met with a wide range of criticisms. Many authors point out that nudging strategies can in fact fail to respect the autonomy of citizens .

In recent years, it has been argued that many people have lost sight of the true meaning behind traditional and religious celebrations, viewing them simply as opportunities for a holiday break. This essay fully supports that viewpoint and will explore the reasons behind this shift.

To begin with, there are several factors contributing to the loss of meaning in these celebrations. One major reason is globalization. As people become more connected through social media, they are often distracted by current trends and updates. According to a Yale University study, 60% of the global population does not seek out stories about their cultural roots or heritage. As a result, they do not celebrate traditional or cultural events with genuine understanding or significance. Another key factor is the rise of broken families. Individuals who grew up without one or both parents may struggle to grasp their own traditions and values, making it difficult for them to appreciate the importance of cultural celebrations.

On the other hand, some argue that people simply want to enjoy a holiday break. While it is important to remember the original purpose of various celebrations, many people use these occasions for personal enjoyment. For instance, they might host parties and invite friends over just to have fun. Additionally, work-related stress is another reason people look forward to festive breaks. Many middle-class workers are dedicated and hardworking, but the limited time off means they often use these holidays to rest and recharge at home. Although this may mean neglecting the responsibility to pass on the meaning of these celebrations to younger generations, it is also important for individuals to take care of their own mental and physical well-being.

In conclusion, there are valid arguments on both sides regarding whether people have forgotten the meaning behind celebrations. However, I believe that individuals should strive to understand the significance of these events, as it can help them gain a deeper understanding of themselves.

They first utilized a simple Caesar’s cipher, then defended against brute force and overheard key attacks with secure key exchange and stronger encryption methods, such as the Vigenère cipher. The tasks were designed for students to not only plan and implement methods for protecting their robots, but also to test their algorithms with their robots. On Day 5, students worked on a comprehensive project to defend their robot against replay attacks, which allowed other student groups to capture and reuse an encrypted command being sent from a student to the robot. To do so, students were introduced to sequence numbering, and tasked with adding this measure together with their existing defensive strategy to create a much stronger defense against attackers. Our exploratory study (no control group) primarily focused on evaluating students learning gains in CT, networking, and cybersecurity using a summative, prepost-test and formative evaluations of student projects based on predefined rubrics. We implemented an ECD approach to assessment development to map key CT, networking, and cybersecurity constructs addressed in the curriculum to the evaluation measures. Our prepost-tests consisted of 8 questions. The two networking questions were free response, while the four cybersecurity questions and the two CT pretest questions were multiplechoice. For the posttest, we changed the CT format to one multiple choice and one fillin-theblank question. In the CT section, the question about loops tasked students with choosing the correct output for two sections of pseudocode containing repeat loops; partial credit was awarded for correctly choosing the correct output for one of the two cases. The CT question on conditionals consisted of an "ifthen, ifthen" structure on the pretest but an "ifthen-else" structure on the posttest. This question will be standardized from prepost in the future to remove any possibility of inconsistent results. The first networking question presented a message passing scenario with identifier tags, and asked students to explain the consequences of removing those identifiers (it would cause a network overload). The second networking question dealt with cooperating armies attempting to send attack and confirmation messages back and forth through enemy territory. The key idea was that it was impossible to guarantee messages were successfully received from both sides no matter how many messages were sent. The cybersecurity questions were: What makes cryptographic algorithms secure? (After describing a denial of service attack) Which of the following is the name for this kind of attack?

This study examines student survey responses regarding classroom attributes that influence the learning experience, aiming to identify whether student perceptions reveal significant distinctions between different classroom typologies. The data indicate that the integration of technology was perceived equally favorably across both room settings. However, qualitative feedback specific to Active Learning Classrooms (ALCs) highlighted their capacity to foster interactivity and facilitate the exchange of student ideas. No statistically significant differences were observed regarding students' ability to hear the instructor or their ease of accessing assistance from instructors and teaching assistants. Conversely, a significant divergence was found in perceptions of how conducive the furniture arrangements are for collaborative work; students in ALCs reported a markedly superior ability to interact with peers. Furthermore, while the sense of social isolation was reported to be lower in ALCs, it was not entirely absent.

Future research should prioritize a deeper investigation into the synergy between active learning pedagogies and learning environments within Computer Science (CS), as well as an analysis of student participation dynamics that influence the optimal utilization of these spaces. The authors extend their gratitude to Professor Andrew Petersen for his insightful discussions and to the reviewers for their constructive feedback.

Additionally, this research investigates the impact of the Story Programming approach on student performance in a subsequent C++ course. Specifically, it compares the outcomes of students who acquired CS concepts with minimal or no coding practice against those who learned these concepts exclusively within a coding context. Although prior literature suggests that prior programming exposure is not a predictor of success in such courses, those studies often relied on disparate course durations (15 weeks versus 10 weeks) and failed to control for the specific CS concepts and programming exposure involved. Consequently, the initial hypothesis posited that students trained via the Story Programming approach would underperform in the subsequent C++ class. Contrary to this expectation, the results demonstrate that students from the Story Programming cohort, despite their limited coding experience, did not exhibit a significant performance deficit compared to their peers who underwent a traditional, code-focused curriculum.

However, because the distribution over predicates in (4) only depends on relative probabilities of truth, the model might learn to keep them all close to 0, which would damage the logical interpretation of the model. To avoid this, I have modified the second term of (5) and second term of (8) , using not only the probability of generating a predicate for a pixie, P ( r | x ) , but also the probability of the truth of a predicate, P ( t r,X | x ) . This technique of constraining latent variables to improve interpretability is similar to how Rei and Søgaard constrain attention weights. Finally, as with other autoencoder models, there is a danger of learning an identity function that generalises poorly. Here, the problem is that the pixie distribution for a node might be predicted based purely on the observed predicate for that node, ignoring the wider context. To avoid this problem, we can use dropout on the input, a technique which has been effective for other NLP models, and which is closely related to denoising autoencoders. More precisely, we can keep the graph topology intact, but randomly mask out the predicates for some nodes. For a masked node X , I have initialised the encoder with an embedding as shown in (10) , which depends on the node’s dependencies (only on the label of each dependency, not on the predicate of the other node). e ( X ) = e ( drop ) + X e ( drop ,l ) + X e ( drop ,l − 1 ) (10) 3.4 Approximating the Prior Expectation The previous section explains the inference network and how it is trained. While higher-order n-grams have demonstrated efficacy in writing style detection across various languages, the inclusion of n-gram features ranging from size 4 to 6 resulted in a decline in classifier performance in the present study, whether applied independently or in combination with other feature sets. This degradation is likely attributable to the limited size of the dataset. Notably, the AdaBoost algorithm achieved an accuracy of 87% in distinguishing between authentic and fabricated news articles when utilizing a combined feature set of character bigrams and function word unigrams.

This research posits that the automatic detection of fake news represents a promising avenue for scholarly inquiry. To this end, we introduce a novel resource for low-resource languages: a manually annotated Urdu corpus comprising real and fake news articles extracted from the internet. To the best of our knowledge, this constitutes the first dedicated corpus for fake news detection in the Urdu language, marking a significant contribution to the development of Urdu linguistic resources. Furthermore, we provide a comprehensive statistical analysis of the corpus, elucidating vocabulary size, lexical overlap, and other key findings.

The experimental results validate the application of machine learning classifiers using lexical features, including Bag-of-Words (BoW) and n-grams (with $n$ varying from 1 to 6), both individually and in combination. Collectively, these findings underscore the broad implications of this work. The primary conclusion is that the newly developed Urdu corpus serves as a robust and reliable resource for advancing fake news detection models. Future research will focus on adapting the system to additional languages, investigating region-specific biases, and exploring novel feature sets to enhance detection capabilities.

As society becomes more and more saturated with technology, traditional cultures are losing their roles in society. The compatibility of technology and traditions are often debated where some suggest it is possible, whereas, others argue it is not. In this essay, I will discuss both perspectives and conclude by saying how and why it is almost impossible.
Since people usually appreciate limited ways of living, it is challenging for both conventions and technology to co-exist. They will usually choose one method or another but not both at the same time. For instance, a company would have to focus on using human labour or advanced machineries to produce a product. The incompatibility may not be obvious at first but as technology develops futher, use of machines will increase but employment will decrease. Furthermore, technology is often considered to be more convenient and faster by many people. Work can be done more efficiently so workers will start to abandon the old ways of living or working.
In contrast, it might be possible for two values to work along in society due to different tastes of various people. Traditional cultures are usually appreciated by old generation such as grandparents, meanwhile, new generation prefers technology in their life. For example, grandparents can read newspaper while grandchildren watch TV or play games during family time. Moreover, although it is hard for a company to use both at the same time, it is not totally impossible. If the number of machines is controlled during production, human labour and machines can work together to maximise productivity by covering up each other's disadvantages.
All in all, even though traditional values and technology can be compatible theoratically, it is indeed difficult to compromise the use between two in real life. Ergo, people would have to beware of the risk of losing traditional cultures, if at all.

The following analysis presents a comparative evaluation of various language models across three distinct query-response scenarios. The assessment focuses on four key metrics: factual correctness, sentence completeness, grammatical accuracy, and alignment with the majority consensus.

**Case Study 1: Population Inquiry**
*   **Query:** "Which location is home to over two million [inhabitants]?" (Inferred from context)
*   **Model Performance:**
    *   **PGNPre with SS+** and **DGPT with SS+** generated responses containing significant grammatical errors, specifically the inversion of subject and verb ("it to is").
    *   **GPT2 with SS+**, **GPT2Pre with SS+**, and **DGPT with SS+** produced grammatically correct and complete sentences ("it is home to over two million").
    *   **Conclusion:** The GPT2 variants and the standard DGPT model demonstrated superior grammatical proficiency compared to their pre-trained or specific configuration counterparts in this instance.

**Case Study 2: Oil Discovery Location**
*   **Query:** "In which sea was oil discovered?"
*   **Model Performance:**
    *   **CoQA (Baseline):** Provided a concise answer ("North Sea").
    *   **QuAC (Baseline):** Offered a verbose response regarding the Scottish National Party's campaign, which, while contextually related, was less direct.
    *   **LGRs+BERT** and **DGPT with SS+ (o)** omitted the definite article ("north sea"), resulting in minor grammatical imperfections.
    *   **PGNPre with SS+**, **GPT2 with SS+**, **GPT2Pre with SS+**, and **DGPT with SS+** correctly formulated the response as "oil was discovered in the north sea," ensuring both grammatical correctness and factual precision.
    *   **Conclusion:** The majority of advanced models (GPT2 variants and DGPT) successfully generated complete, grammatically sound sentences that accurately identified the North Sea.

**Case Study 3: Geographic Location of Jersey and Guernsey**
*   **Query:** "Where are Jersey and Guernsey?"
*   **Model Performance:**
    *   **CoQA (Baseline):** Correctly identified the "Channel Islands."
    *   **QuAC (Baseline):** Provided an extensive historical explanation regarding Norman customary law, which, while informative, exceeded the scope of a direct location query.
    *   **LGRs+BERT** and **DGPT with SS+ (o)** failed to include the definite article ("channel islands").
    *   **PGNPre with SS+**, **GPT2 with SS+**, **GPT2Pre with SS+**, and **DGPT with SS+** correctly utilized the phrase "in the channel islands" (with GPT2 variants occasionally using "on," which is also acceptable in this context).
    *   **Conclusion:** Most models accurately identified the location, though consistency in article usage varied. The GPT2 and DGPT models generally produced the most natural and grammatically complete responses.

**Case Study 4: Rhine River Direction**

$$e_{s,t} = \tanh(W_c [c_t; s_{l,t}] + b_c),$$

where $W_c$ and $b_c$ denote trainable parameters. The term $c_t$ represents the context vector, computed via attention-weighted encoding of hidden states and the source attention distribution $a^{\text{src}}_t$, in accordance with the methodology proposed by Bahdanau et al. (2015). The resulting attention vector $e_s$ serves as the basis for generating the vocabulary distribution:

$$P_{\text{vocab}} = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{vocab}} e_{s,t} + b_{\text{vocab}}),$$

as well as the target attention distribution:

$$e^{\text{tgt}}_t = v^{\top}_{\text{tgt}} \tanh(W_{\text{tgt}} e_{s,1:t-1} + U_{\text{tgt}} e_{s,t} + b_{\text{tgt}}),$$
$$a^{\text{tgt}}_t = \text{softmax}(e^{\text{tgt}}_t).$$

The probabilities associated with source-side copying ($p_{\text{src}}$), target-side copying ($p_{\text{tgt}}$), and generation ($p_{\text{gen}}$) are computed from $e_s$. These probabilities function as generation switches and are determined as follows:

$$[p_{\text{src}}, p_{\text{tgt}}, p_{\text{gen}}] = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{switch}} e_{s,t} + b_{\text{switch}}).$$

The final node distribution is defined conditionally. If the node $v_t$ is copied from existing nodes, the probability is given by:

$$P_{(\text{node})}(u_t) = p_{\text{tgt}} \sum_{i: u_i = u_t} a^{\text{tgt}}_t[i].$$

Otherwise, the distribution is calculated as:

$$P_{(\text{node})}(u_t) = p_{\text{gen}} P_{\text{vocab}}(u_t) + p_{\text{src}} \sum_{i: w_i = u_t} a^{\text{src}}_t[i],$$

where $a_t[i]$ denotes the $i$-th element of the attention vector $a_t$. Subsequently, existing indices are deterministically assigned to the identified nodes based on whether the node originates from the target-side distribution.

Existing research indicates that introductory computer science courses centered on programming may be ill-suited for students lacking confidence in or interest in coding. Furthermore, prior exposure to programming is not a reliable predictor of success, as self-reported experience often fails to capture the quantity, quality, and retention of that experience. This study extends prior work by evaluating student performance in a subsequent, rigorous ten-week C++ course (CS 1), while controlling for specific prior exposure to computer science concepts and the extent of coding utilized to learn and apply them.

Traditionally, students at the host institution engage with CS 1 concepts—including algorithms, data representation, control flow (conditional and iterative), functions, and lists—within the context of Python programming during their introductory course (CS 0). In this conventional model, students begin writing Python programs as early as the second week of a ten-week term. Instruction emphasizes basic Python syntax, while problem-solving skills are cultivated through the creation of design documents and testing plans prior to code implementation.

To investigate whether increased coding exposure in CS 0 confers an advantage in CS 1, we developed an alternative pedagogical framework termed "Story Programming." This approach teaches computational concepts outside the context of coding, utilizing narrative structures derived from *Once Upon an Algorithm*. For instance, pathfinding algorithms are introduced through the narrative of Hansel and Gretel, with students physically enacting these algorithms using pebbles during laboratory sessions. The curriculum requires students to read relevant chapters prior to class and complete weekly online assessments. Topics are generally addressed sequentially, with occasional reordering to group related concepts.

The integration of this narrative-based methodology shifts the pedagogical emphasis, enabling greater breadth and depth in concept coverage compared to the traditional model. Specifically, the Story Programming approach provides a more rigorous treatment of data structures and decidability. In contrast, the traditional approach offers only a cursory examination of data structures via lists and merely alludes to the concept of unsolvable problems.

Regarding evaluation metrics, BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) is a widely recognized measure of word overlap that calculates n-gram precision between generated and reference sequences. To mitigate the metric's inherent bias toward shorter sentences, a brevity penalty is applied. However, empirical studies have indicated that BLEU correlates poorly with human judgment (Liu et al., 2016; Lowe et al., 2017; Gupta et al., 2019).

METEOR (Denkowski and Lavie, 2014) was developed to address these limitations by employing a harmonic mean of precision and recall, alongside the incorporation of stemming and synonym matching. Similarly, ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004) enhances the assessment of sentence-level structure by identifying the longest common subsequence between the generated and reference sequences.

In the domain of embedding-based metrics, Greedy Matching (Rus and Lintean, 2012) aligns each word in the generated sequence with a reference word based on the cosine similarity of their embeddings, with the final score derived from the average of these matches. Embedding Average (Wieting et al., 2015) computes sentence embeddings for both the generated and ground-truth responses by averaging word embeddings, subsequently calculating the cosine similarity between these aggregate vectors.

Vector Extrema (Forgues et al., 2014) adopts a similar framework but determines the sentence embedding by selecting the maximum value for each dimension of the word embeddings rather than averaging them. This approach is motivated by the hypothesis that common words, which tend to be closer to the origin, will be naturally de-emphasized. Research suggests that Vector Extrema outperforms other metrics in dialogue tasks (Gupta et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2016). Finally, SkipThought (Kiros et al., 2015) utilizes a recurrent neural network to generate sentence-level embeddings for both the generated and reference sequences.

However, textual data present distinct characteristics compared to other high-dimensional data types. Specifically, intermediate confounding adjustments in text analysis can be directly inspected and evaluated by human reviewers (§ 6), whereas the construction of semantically meaningful textual representations remains an unresolved research challenge.¹ Even when employing straightforward adjustment methodologies, practitioners must first transform raw text into lower-dimensional representations through techniques such as filtered word counts, lexicon-based indicators, topic modeling, or vector embeddings (§ 4).

A further complication arises from the fact that empirical evaluation within causal inference remains an active area of inquiry (Dorie et al., 2019; Gentzel et al., 2019), a task that is further complicated by the inclusion of textual data (§ 7). Consequently, this paper narrows its scope to review methods and applications wherein text data function specifically as causal confounders.

In the broader intersection of text analysis and causal inference, existing literature has explored text as a mediator (Veitch et al., 2019), as a treatment (Fong and Grimmer, 2016; Egami et al.; Wood-Doughty et al., 2018; Tan et al., 2014), as an outcome (Egami et al.), as a basis for causal discovery (Mani and Cooper, 2000), and in the context of predictive (Granger) causality (Balashankar et al., 2019; del Prado Martin and Brendel, 2016; Tabari et al., 2018). Beyond these specific contributions, there has been limited interaction between Natural Language Processing (NLP) research and causal inference. Nevertheless, NLP possesses a robust history of applied modeling and diagnostic pipelines that could significantly inform and enhance causal inference methodologies.

A comprehensive understanding of computing principles and practices is imperative for preparing students for the demands of the twenty-first century. This educational objective extends beyond cultivating information technology consumers; it necessitates the development of innovative creators capable of designing computer systems that enhance quality of life. Consequently, the integration of computing education within K–12 curricula is essential.

A prevalent pedagogical strategy involves teaching computing through mobile application development, leveraging students' familiarity with smartphones. In this context, App Inventor serves as a block-based programming environment that facilitates the creation of Android applications. While existing tutorials and courses predominantly focus on core programming concepts—such as conditionals, operators, and event handling—application development encompasses a broader scope, including User Interface (UI) design. UI design is a cross-cutting concept in computing aimed at maximizing usability and user experience by ensuring interactions are effective, efficient, and satisfying. In the realm of mobile applications, UI design is a critical determinant of success.

Within UI design, visual design plays a pivotal role in shaping the user experience by optimizing the impact of visual elements on both usability and aesthetic appeal. This domain addresses meta-principles such as consistency, hierarchy, and personality. Established frameworks, including Material Design for Android and the W3C Mobile Accessibility Guidelines, provide essential recommendations for creating visually compelling applications that enable users to achieve their objectives effectively within specific contexts.

Given the significance of visual design in application development, it is necessary to incorporate design competencies into K–12 computing education. Aligned with curriculum standards and visual design theory, this integration entails the instruction of fundamental concepts regarding color, typography, imagery, and composition.

There are several things to note about L EA QI: ⋄ If the current policy is already very certain, a expert annotator is never queried. ⋄ If a label is queried, the expert is queried only if the difference classifier predicts disagreement with the heuristic, or the apple tasting procedure flips the difference classifier prediction. ⋄ Due to apple tasting, most errors the difference classifier makes will cause it to query the expert unnecessarily; this is the “safe” type of error (increasing sample complexity but not harming accuracy), versus a Type II error (which leads to biased labels). ⋄ The difference classifier is only trained on states where the policy is uncertain, which is exactly the distribution on which it is run. 3.2 Apple Tasting for OneSided Learning The difference classifier h ∈H must be trained (line 27 ) based on onesided feedback (it only observes errors when it predicts “disagree“) to minimize Type II errors (it should only very rarely predict “agree” when the truth is “disagree”). This helps keep the labeled data for the learned policies unbiased. The main challenge here is that the feedback to the difference classifier is onesided : that is, if it predicts “disagree” then it gets to see the truth, but if it predicts “agree” it never finds out if it was wrong. We use one of’s algorithms, STAP (see Alg 3 ), which works by random sampling from apples that are predicted to not be tasted and tasting them anyway (line 12 ). Formally, STAP tastes apples that are predicted to be bad with probability p ( m + 1) /t , where m is the number of mistakes, and t is the number of apples tasted so far. We adapt Apple Tasting algorithm STAP to our setting for controlling the number of Type II errors made by the difference classifier as follows. First, because some heuristic actions are much more common than others, we run a separate apple tasting scheme per heuristic action (in the sense that we count the number of error on this heuristic action rather than globally). Second, when there is significant action imbalance 2 we find it necessary to skew the distribution from STAP more in favor of querying.

Let the state at time $t$ be denoted by $e_t$. The metric known as Ely surprise is formally defined as:

$$ S^{\text{Ely}}_t = (e_t - e_{t-1})^2 \quad (3) $$

The framework proposed by Ely et al. can be adapted to model narrative suspense under the assumption that each sentence in a story induces a state transition regarding the narrative elements (e.g., characters, settings, and events). In this context, the states $e_t$ correspond to sentence embeddings rather than beliefs regarding terminal states. Consequently, Ely surprise quantifies the distance between the current embedding $e_t$ and the preceding embedding $e_{t-1}$. This study employs both L1 and L2 distance metrics. While other researchers (e.g., Li et al., 2018) have explored information gain and Kullback-Leibler divergence, they reported suboptimal performance when applying these methods to suspense modeling in games.

Similar to Hale surprise, Ely surprise models backward-looking prediction; however, it operates over state representations rather than probability distributions. Ely et al. further introduce a forward-looking prediction measure, defined as the expected difference between the current state $e_t$ and the subsequent state $e_{t+1}$:

$$ U^{\text{Ely}}_t = \mathbb{E}\left[ (e_t - e_{t+1}^i)^2 \right] = \sum_i P(e_{t+1}^i) (e_t - e_{t+1}^i)^2 \quad (4) $$

This metric is closely analogous to Hale entropy reduction, with the distinction that entropy is computed over states (specifically, sentence embeddings in this application) rather than over probability distributions. Intuitively, this measure captures the extent to which the current sentence reduces uncertainty regarding the remainder of the narrative. The forward-looking measures presented in Equations (2) and (4) are herein referred to as Hale uncertainty reduction and Ely uncertainty reduction, respectively.

Furthermore, Ely et al. propose weighted variants of these metrics, wherein each state is scaled by a factor $\alpha_t$. This adjustment accounts for the possibility that certain states possess inherently higher suspense potential:

$$ S^{\alpha, \text{Ely}}_t = \alpha_t (e_t - e_{t-1})^2 $$
$$ U^{\alpha, \text{Ely}}_t = \mathbb{E}\left[ \alpha_{t+1} (e_t - e_{t+1}^i)^2 \right] \quad (5) $$

In this work, we posit that sentences exhibiting high emotional valence contribute more significantly to suspense, as emotional engagement intensifies the reader's experience of suspense.

Data collection for this study encompassed examination scores, demographic information, perception survey responses, and system usage logs. Examination scores were retrieved from the university's Learning Management System (LMS). Across both semesters, assessments adhered to a consistent, text-based framework modeled on previous terms. The assessment structure comprised two non-cumulative midterm examinations and one cumulative final examination. Each midterm was divided into two sections: a multiple-choice and short-answer component focusing on definitions and code reading, and a free-response section requiring pseudocode generation. In alignment with standard course protocols, the cumulative final examination was restricted to definitional and code-reading questions. This exclusion of code-writing tasks was necessitated by logistical constraints, specifically the two-hour examination window and the grading timeline required for final grade submission.

At the conclusion of the semester, the Software Competency Survey 1 (SCS1) was administered in a proctored setting to both participant groups. Concurrently, demographic data were collected via a computer-based survey. Participants completed these assessments in a dedicated facility over a duration of one hour and fifty-five minutes. Participation in the SCS1 was voluntary, resulting in a final sample size of 395 students. Additionally, Likert-scale perception surveys were administered weekly, with comprehensive data points captured at the beginning, midpoint, and end of the semester. Download logs for online resources, such as lecture slides, were also aggregated. A secure server was utilized to record student interactions with the course plugin throughout the term.

To ensure methodological rigor and prevent bias, the first author (who served as the course instructor) and co-authors were blinded to SCS1 participation status during both semesters. Access to this information was restricted to the teaching assistants until after the final grades were officially submitted. Only following grade submission were the assessment and participation data shared with the research team. The primary analysis focused on student learning outcomes, evaluated through course examination scores and SCS1 results. These data were stratified by question type, which corresponds to Neo-Piagetian stages of cognitive development. The present study concentrates on the assessment data to elucidate differences in programming learning outcomes between the two experimental conditions. Future research will address the analysis of survey responses and their correlation with assessment performance.

**Experimental Configuration and Training Protocol**

The model was configured with a batch size of 64 and a discrete latent variable dimension ($K$) of 20. Optimization was performed using the Adam algorithm (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with a learning rate of $5 \times 10^{-5}$. Pretraining for the dialogue generation task was conducted on a cluster comprising eight NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs (32GB memory each). The training process spanned 3.5 million steps, requiring approximately two weeks to achieve convergence.

**2.5 Fine-Tuning and Inference**

The pretrained model demonstrates sufficient flexibility to support diverse dialogue modalities, including chitchat, knowledge-grounded conversation, and conversational question answering. Fine-tuning on smaller conversational datasets is executed by adhering to the training objectives defined in Equation (8). Upon convergence, the inference procedure for generating responses to a given context involves two primary stages:

1.  **Candidate Response Generation:** For each latent value $z \in [1, K]$, a corresponding candidate response $r$ is generated.
2.  **Response Selection:** The probability $p(l_r = 1 \mid c, r)$ is calculated for each candidate. The response exhibiting the highest coherence score is selected as the final output.

It should be noted that the aforementioned fine-tuning and inference protocols are designed for general dialogue generation without specific task-oriented objectives. In scenarios involving specific conversational goals—such as mutual information exchange (Bao et al., 2019)—the fine-tuning process may be adapted to maximize predefined rewards via Reinforcement Learning (RL). Under such conditions, the discrete latent variable is naturally interpreted as an action within the RL framework, allowing response selection to be resolved by identifying the action that yields the maximum reward.

**3. Experiments**

**3.1 Experimental Settings**

**3.1.1 Datasets**
To rigorously evaluate the proposed method, comprehensive experiments were conducted across three publicly available datasets:

*   **PersonaChat (Zhang et al., 2018):** A knowledge-grounded conversation dataset comprising manually annotated dialogues and associated persona profiles (background knowledge). The data features natural interactions where participants attempt to establish mutual understanding.
*   **DailyDialog (Li et al., 2017):** A chitchat dataset containing high-quality human conversations centered on daily life topics.
*   **DSTC7-AVSD (Alamri et al., 2019):** Short for "Audio Visual Scene-aware Dialog," this dataset from the DSTC7 challenge is utilized for conversational question answering tasks.

Power, money, and reputation are all highly sought after, and if you‚Äôre successful in your career, chances are you have all three. That‚Äôs why success holds such significance in our lives. This leads to the question: how does one achieve success?

You might choose to take risks and try new things, or you could concentrate on what you already do best. Personally, I believe you can find success with either approach.

For example, many companies have achieved great results by improving existing products. Take Apple, for instance. They took the standard mp3 player, enhanced its storage, usability, and design, and called it the iPod. Thanks to these improvements and a massive marketing push, they sold an astonishing number of iPods worldwide. The product‚Äôs success was remarkable.

On the flip side, consider the origins of Apple itself‚Äîa near-perfect example of creating an entirely new market. By developing a personal computer for everyday households, they introduced a revolutionary product and took a significant risk. As we know, the market embraced it, and it made a huge impact on society.

There are plenty of examples supporting both strategies, so I don‚Äôt agree with the idea that people who try new things are always more successful than those who stick to what they know. Because of the high risks involved, I personally prefer to focus on what I do well and where I have solid expertise.

Prior research on retention in CS has mostly examined factors such as prior exposure to programming and students’ personality characteristics, which are outside the control of undergraduate instructors. This study focuses on factors within the control of instructors, namely, instructional practices that directly impact students’ classroom experiences. Participants were recruited from 25 sections of 14 different courses over 4 semesters. A multilevel model tested the effects of individual and classaverage perceptions of cooperative learning and teacher directedness on the probability of subsequent enrollment in a CS course, while controlling for students’ mastery of CS concepts and status as a CS major. Results indicated that students’ individual perceptions of instructional practices were not associated with retention, but the average rating of cooperative learning within a course section was negatively associated with retention. Consistent with prior research, greater mastery of CS concepts and considering or having declared a CS major were associated with a higher probability of taking a future CS courses. Implications for findings are discussed. In recent years in the United States, there has been an influx of interest and energy in growing computer science (CS) education across all educational levels. From the CSforAll consortium in the United States to the increase in European countries that have added coding to the elementary and secondary school curriculum , there is growing consensus that knowledge of CS concepts, computational thinking skills, and familiarity with coding will play an important role in future workforce readiness. In order for the United States to meet future workforce demands for CS professionals , it is important for tertiary CS programs to attract and retain students. However, at the collegiate level, college STEM disciplines have historically seen large proportions of students not completing the degree they initially pursue , and CS is no exception . A history of low retention and degree completion rates in CS have prompted research into factors contributing to retention because understanding these factors can help educators develop strategies to attract and retain students in CS programs. For example, researchers have found prior exposure to CS and programming , personality characteristics , motivation for a given course , the learning environment , and achievement in introductory CS courses are factors that predict students’ continued enrollment in CS courses.

**Event and Argument Classification**

The proposed framework employs a dual-classification mechanism to identify event triggers and argument roles. Specifically, each word $w$ is classified into an event type $y_e$, while each entity mention $t$ is assigned an argument role $y_a$. To delineate trigger word boundaries, a BIO tagging schema is utilized: the prefix 'B' denotes the beginning of a trigger, 'I' indicates an internal token, and 'O' signifies non-trigger tokens.

The classification probabilities are modeled using a softmax function as follows:

$$
P(y_e | w) = \frac{\exp(W_e w_C + b_e)}{\sum_{e'} \exp(W_{e'} w_C + b_{e'})}
$$

$$
P(y_a | t) = \frac{\exp(W_a [t_C; w_C] + b_a)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(W_{a'} [t_C; w_C] + b_{a'})} \quad (2)
$$

Regarding data preparation, ground truth text entity mentions are utilized as input during the training phase, adhering to the methodology of Ji and Grishman (2008). For the testing phase, entity mentions are extracted using a named entity recognition system (Lin et al., 2019).

**Image Event Extraction**

**Image Structured Representation**
To derive image structures analogous to Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) graphs, and drawing inspiration from situation recognition frameworks (Yatskar et al., 2016), images are represented as "situation graphs." These are structured as star-shaped graphs (see Figure 4), wherein the central node represents a verb $v$ (e.g., *destroying*), and the surrounding neighbor nodes represent arguments labeled as pairs $\{(n, r)\}$. Here, $n$ denotes a noun (e.g., *ship*) derived from WordNet synsets (Miller, 1995) to specify the entity type, while $r$ indicates the semantic role (e.g., *item*) played by the entity within the event, based on FrameNet (Fillmore et al., 2003).

Two distinct methodologies are developed to construct these situation graphs from images, both trained on the imSitu dataset (Yatskar et al., 2016):

1.  **Object-based Graph:** Analogous to the extraction of entities for candidate arguments, this approach employs object detection techniques to identify visual elements.
2.  **Attention-based Graph:** This method utilizes role-driven attention mechanisms to map visual features to semantic roles.

The resulting structured representations facilitate the alignment of visual content with textual event schemas, enabling the extraction of complex event structures such as agents, destinations, and items within a unified multimedia common space.

This paper is structured as follows: First, we present how our dataset is structured and our training objective. Then, in sections 2.1 and 2.2 we present details of our two models, RTNet and RTNetVAE. Section 2.3 presents our input feature representations. In section 2.4 we discuss our training and testing procedures. In sections 3.1 and 3.2 we analyze the performance of both RTNet and RTNetVAE. Finally, in section 4 we present the results of a human listener test. 2 Methodology Dataset Our dataset is extracted from the Switchboard1 Release 2 corpus. Switchboard has 2438 dyadic telephone conversations with a total length of approximately 260 hours. The dataset consists of pairs of adjacent turns by different speakers which we refer to as turn pairs (shown in Fig. 2 ). Turn pairs are automatically extracted from orthographic annotations using the following procedure: We extract framebased speechactivity labels for each speaker using a frame stepsize of 50ms. The framebased representation is used to partition each person’s speech signal into interpausal units (IPUs). We define IPUs as segments of speech by a person that are separated by pauses of 200ms or greater. IPUs are then used to automatically extract turns , which we define as consecutive IPUs by a speaker in which there is no speech by the other speaker in the silence between the IPUs. A turn pair is then defined as being any two adjacent turns by different speakers. The earlier of the two turns in a pair is considered to be the user turn and the second is considered to be the system turn .

The STARS students wrote in their final report: “Our group enjoyed teaching the kids and seeing them being so interested in learning various aspects of Computer Science. We think that we made an impact on some of the kid’s lives and we got some of the kids to want to pursue Computer Science.” The state of North Carolina requires middle school students to complete several endof-grade (EOG) assessment tests. Members of the research team, along with a group of STARS students, assisted in organizing and teaching a test prep day, which was themed around CT. At the event, middle school students were exposed to three different toolsets: Lego Robotics, EarSketch, and Scratch. The middle school students responded well to the lessons. Curriculum Development. To support teachers with CS/CT infused curriculum development and implementation, we had groups of STARS students work with teachers at NRMS. Each team met with an assigned teacher on a weekly basis. In these meetings they learned the needs of the teacher and discussed ways in which they could help. One team worked with a sixth grade math teacher to develop several online games focusing on ratios and percentages, algebraic expressions, and long division. Another team worked with an EC (exceptional children) teacher to develop course materials for teaching about the solar system and about fractions. Teacher Professional Development. In Summer 2019, we hosted two professional development workshops for teachers at NRMS. In June, eight teachers attended a week long workshop and in July six teachers attended a twoday followup. Both workshops focused on several areas of math, science, and language arts, where CS/CT could be incorporated. These workshops adopted a simplified version of the Beauty and Joy of Computing (BJC) curriculum and used Snap!, which is a webbased dragand-drop programming environment. The followup workshop was projectbased and aimed to have the teachers complete a CS/CT infused lesson plan. For example, one language arts teacher developed an interactive game, which simulates a conversation between two characters from a book and uses this to quiz students on the content. The goal is to then show the students how the game was created and ask them to use it as a template to develop similar games based on other books. A central concern of the project is to understand the STEM ecosystem at NRMS.
