The majority of other instructors, however, felt that it was important to implement measures to prevent cheating, in order to reassure the honest students that the exam was being conducted fairly. It was also argued that eliminating the possibility to cheat helps remove the temptation to do it in the first place, especially for students who are struggling under heavy workloads. Still, the discussion led us to reflect that we should always seek to minimise the disruption to students in setting up these safeguards. Allowing students to practice with SEB before the exam is one such way for us to achieve this. A feature of the exam format that instructors particular liked (over, e.g. pen and paper exams) was the fact that Vocareum allowed them to complete grading faster by having the code ready to run, along with the results of some builtin test cases to indicate the likely quality of code. Note, however, that instructors manually inspected all submissions, to ensure that students did not simply hardcode return values for the given test cases. (Some always try!) To gain some viewpoints of the students, we conducted an informal online survey with 13 of them who were reachable over the summer vacation. Using openended questions, the students were asked about the positive and negative aspects of the exams, whether they preferred a BYOD or pen and paper format, and if they had any suggestions for improvement. In terms of the positive aspects, there was a very clear message from the survey responses: Some also mentioned that they can type faster on their own laptops, and need not worry about getting used to the idiosyncrasies of other computers.

In recent years, there has been a lot of discussion about the role of competition. Many people believe that competitiveness is essential in modern society. Personally, I think it offers a valuable chance for individuals to develop their skills, build relationships, expand their knowledge, and become leaders in various fields. In this essay, I will explore how competition affects people and consider whether it is a beneficial trait.

Looking at the effects of competitiveness, I believe it encourages people to do their best and strive not to be outdone by others. For instance, if there were no competition at my school, I wouldn‚Äôt be motivated to take on different projects or improve my abilities to excel as a student. This quality pushes me to keep raising my standards. Additionally, I am aware that many well-known brands like Louis Vuitton and Gucci are constantly competing to maintain leadership in the fashion industry.

When considering whether competition is positive or negative, I think it is generally a positive force for several reasons. Firstly, in the context of sports, competition is a social process influenced by both biological and social changes that everyone experiences. Secondly, in an era dominated by social media and increasing individualism, the time we spend interacting with others is decreasing. Nevertheless, being able to collaborate and work towards shared goals remains a crucial skill, not just in sports but in all areas of life.

In conclusion, competition can support people‚Äôs overall development, although it may sometimes create more pressure than they can handle. Still, I believe that the advantages of competition outweigh its drawbacks.

Figure 5 presents the results for probing the identity of the subject noun. Although this task proves challenging, distinct trends emerge indicating that the encoders successfully capture word identity signals. Consistent with prior observations, GloVe embeddings perform at chance levels for all tokens except the target subject noun, while GPT embeddings perform at chance for tokens preceding the subject noun, thereby satisfying our sanity checks. Regarding the subject noun itself, all encoders demonstrate comparably high recoverability of word identity, with BERT exhibiting superior performance. Notably, GPT and ELMo show a marginal increase in subject identity recoverability at the verb position, and GPT unexpectedly retains the highest amount of subject identity information at the object determiner.

BERT representations maintain consistently strong encoding of subject identity throughout the sentence. Similarly, GPT embeddings preserve this information from the subject noun onward. In contrast, ELMo's encoding of subject identity declines sharply at the determiners and the object noun. These findings suggest that information regarding surrounding word identities is distributed relatively evenly across sentence tokens in BERT and GPT, whereas ELMo tends to localize word identity information to the immediate vicinity of the word position.

Probing for the identity of the verb and the object yields analogous patterns. Specifically, GloVe embeddings remain at chance for all non-target words, while GPT embeddings perform at chance prior to the target word but align with BERT's performance patterns for subsequent tokens in the object identity task. BERT demonstrates robust performance across the board, whereas ELMo exhibits a more pronounced sensitivity to the distance from the target word. Although identity classification performance significantly exceeds chance levels, it remains substantially below 100%.

In contrast, selfdependency, or emotional inertia, deals with the aspect of emotional influence that speakers have on themselves during conversations. Participants in a conversation are likely to stick to their own emotional state due to their emotional inertia, unless the counterparts invoke a change. Thus, there is always a major interplay between the interspeaker dependency and selfdependency with respect to the emotional dynamics in the conversation. We surmise that combining these two distinct yet related contextual information schemes (sequential encoding and speaker level encoding) would create enhanced context representation leading to better understanding of emotional dynamics in conversational systems. Let there be M speakers/parties in a conversation. The task is to predict the emotion labels (happy, sad, neutral, angry, excited, frustrated, disgust, and fear) of the constituent utterances, where each utterance is uttered by a corresponding speaker. A convolutional neural network is used to extract textual features from the transcript of the utterances. We use a single convolutional layer followed by maxpooling and a fully connected layer to obtain the feature representations for the utterances. The input to this network is the 300 dimensional pretrained 840B GloVe vectors. We use filters of size 3, 4 and 5 with 50 feature maps in each. The convoluted features are then maxpooled with a window size of 2 followed by the ReLU activation. These are then concatenated and fed to a 100 dimensional fully connected layer, whose activations form the representation of the utterance. This network is trained at utterance level with the emotion labels. We now present our Dialogue Graph Convolutional Network (DialogueGCN) framework for emotion recognition in conversational setups. DialogueGCN consists of three integral components — Sequential Context Encoder, SpeakerLevel Context Encoder, and Emotion Classifier. Since conversations are sequential by nature, contextual information flows along that sequence. We feed the conversation to a bidirectional gated recurrent unit (GRU) to capture this contextual information. Since the utterances are encoded irrespective of its speaker, this initial encoding scheme is speaker agnostic, as opposed to the state of the art, DialogueRNN. We propose the SpeakerLevel Context Encoder module in the form of a graphical network to capture speaker dependent contextual information in a conversation.

**Abstract**

This paper examines avenues for enhancement and potential applications regarding text-only speech proficiency assessment.

**1. Introduction**

The escalating global demand for English language proficiency has spurred significant academic and commercial initiatives to develop automated tutoring and assessment systems. These platforms address the critical need for accessible online resources that facilitate language acquisition and align learner performance with validated proficiency scales, which are instrumental in securing educational and professional opportunities (British Council, 2013). A novel category of educational technology has emerged in the form of language learning applications integrated with smart speakers, such as Amazon Alexa and Google Home. While these devices offer distinct advantages regarding immediacy, interactivity, and convenience, they present unique challenges for application providers seeking to evaluate language content.

Due to privacy constraints, audio recordings are not transmitted to developers; instead, only text responses generated by Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) systems are provided. This limitation establishes a distinct challenge within educational technology: the automated assessment of speech proficiency based solely on transcriptions. This study reports on efforts to grade learner English transcriptions derived from ASR systems, comparing a feature-rich baseline against neural networks trained on multitask objectives.

Traditionally, automated grading systems for spontaneous speech rely on a hybrid approach, utilizing features extracted from both audio recordings and ASR transcripts. For instance, the SpeechRater™ system developed by the Educational Testing Service employs text-based features—such as word tokens per second, the length of inter-pausal units, and vocabulary size normalized by duration—alongside audio metrics, with score predictions generated via linear regression (Zechner et al., 2007, 2009; Higgins et al., 2011). However, in the absence of audio data, proficiency scoring must be conducted exclusively on textual input. Consequently, the development of robust methods for text-only speech scoring is essential to ensure the reliability and validity of educational applications in smart speaker environments. Currently, relatively few automated speech grading systems utilize neural approaches that incorporate text-based features derived from transcripts.

Since our pseudolabel generation follows the presence of seed words in the document, ideally, the posterior probability of a document belonging to the class C j after observing the presence of word w (i.e., P ( C j | w ) ) should be very close to 100%. All these counts are based on the prediction results on the input unlabeled documents. • Frequent. Ideally, a seed word s of label l appears in the documents belonging to label l with significant frequency. As previously established, the mentors' pedagogical approach was significantly shaped by their personal experiences with stereotype threat as women of color. Given that this shared experience appeared more salient than specific racial categorization, this study defines "same-group" broadly to encompass girls and women of color. Social reinforcement was further facilitated by same-group peers within the cohort. At two of the four study sites, the theme of participants utilizing one another as resources to overcome challenges in computer science projects emerged with increasing frequency over time. This trend suggests that, by the conclusion of the first semester, participants at these locations felt secure in disclosing their difficulties without fear of judgment, thereby fostering a collective reinforcement of the notion that encountering challenges is an inherent aspect of the learning process. As one participant noted, "We support each other." The cultivation of positive interpersonal relationships—a primary outcome reported by participants at these sites—appears strongly correlated with this dynamic. One student explained, "BRIGHTCS helped me build relationships, so whenever I needed help with anything, I would ask, can you help me with this."

Several participants emphasized the significance of their cohort peers also being girls, while others described a sense of "comfort" among the group. Although not explicitly attributed to gender or race, this sentiment mirrored the feelings of affinity expressed regarding same-group mentors. As one student stated, "I feel like I can express myself now [that I'm] closer to the others here." Consequently, at these sites, elements of same-group membership appear to have been instrumental in the development of new relationships, which in turn enabled the social reinforcement of the positive dimensions of learning through challenge. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that participants at these sites were frequently the sole girls of color in their respective classes with an interest in STEM and computer science.

In contrast, at the remaining two sites—both all-girls schools serving predominantly students of color—no significant cohort effect was observed, likely because the group dynamic did not differ substantially from that of a standard classroom environment. While pre-program survey data indicated that nearly three-quarters of participants possessed high confidence in their ability to learn computer science, suggesting a strong initial sense of self-efficacy, subsequent interviews and observations revealed a more nuanced reality. Many students initially exhibited indicators of low self-efficacy upon encountering the first signs of difficulty within the program.

The ability to explore the trade-off between accuracy and inference speed is contingent upon addressing the high computational cost of pretraining. In Transformer-based architectures, the primary computational bottleneck arises from the input-wide self-attention mechanism executed at each layer. In reading comprehension tasks, this necessitates computing self-attention over the concatenated question and context, a process that facilitates the generation of highly effective, question-dependent context representations. However, the processing of the context typically dominates the computational load due to its significantly greater length relative to the question. If the context can be processed independently of the question, this computationally intensive operation can be shifted to an offline phase, thereby substantially reducing runtime latency.

The central inquiry is whether the context can be processed independently of the question in specific layers without incurring a significant loss in model effectiveness. Two empirical observations support the feasibility of this approach. First, prior research indicates that lower Transformer layers predominantly capture local phenomena, such as syntactic structures, whereas higher layers focus on global, long-distance phenomena, including semantic aspects critical to the target task (Tenney et al., 2019; Hao et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019b). Second, as demonstrated in Section 2, standard BERT-based question-answering models exhibit minimal variance in lower-layer text representations when the question is varied. This suggests that question-specific information is less critical for forming text representations in the lower layers. Collectively, these findings imply that restricting lower layers to local context processing while reserving full global context integration for upper layers can yield significant speedups with negligible impact on performance.

Based on these observations, we introduce DeFormer, a decomposition strategy for pretrained Transformer-based models. In this architecture, lower layers process the question and context text independently, while higher layers process them jointly (see Figure 1). Specifically, in an $n$-layer model, the first $k$ layers are designated for independent processing. Under the DeFormer framework, context texts are processed through these $k$ lower layers offline, and the resulting representations from the $k$-th layer are cached. During inference, the question is processed through the same $k$ layers, after which the pre-computed context representation is retrieved from the cache to facilitate joint processing in the subsequent layers.

As illustrated in Figure 2, the proposed methodology comprises four primary modules:

1.  **Encoding Module**: This component utilizes BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) to generate contextualized representations by encoding the concatenated sequence of rule text, the initial query, the user scenario, and the dialogue history.
2.  **Explicit Memory Tracking Module**: This module sequentially processes the initial query, user scenario, and multi-turn dialogue history to dynamically update the entailment status of each rule sentence.
3.  **Decision-Making Module**: Leveraging the updated rule sentence states, this module performs entailment-oriented reasoning to determine the appropriate response category: *Yes*, *No*, *Irrelevant*, or *Inquire*.
4.  **Question Generation Module**: Activated specifically when the decision is *Inquire*, this module reuses the updated rule states to identify underspecified rule sentences. It subsequently extracts the most informative span within these sentences using a coarse-to-fine approach and reformulates the extracted content into a grammatically correct follow-up question.

### 2.1 Encoding

Let $x_R$, $x_Q$, $x_S$, and $[x_{H,1}, x_{H,2}, \dots, x_{H,P}]$ denote the input sequences of tokens corresponding to the rule text, the initial query, the user scenario, and $P$ turns of dialogue history, respectively. The rule text $x_R$ is first segmented into $M$ distinct rule sentences, $[x_{R,1}, x_{R,2}, \dots, x_{R,M}]$, based on sentence boundaries or bullet points. A `[CLS]` token is inserted at the beginning of each segment. These components are then concatenated into a single unified sequence defined as:
$$ [ [\text{CLS}], x_{R,1}; \dots; [\text{CLS}], x_{R,M}; [\text{CLS}], x_Q; [\text{CLS}], x_S; [\text{CLS}], x_{H,1}; \dots; [\text{CLS}], x_{H,P} ] $$

There is a raft of recent largescale, high quality data that can be massaged into this form, from natural language inference (NLI) tasks to commonsense reasoning tasks. Two collections of data can be derived from the labels of such a supervised task: D + = { ( x ( i ) , y ( i )+ ) } , D − = { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) − ) } , where D + is coherent behavior, e.g. neutral or entailing data in NLI, and D − is incoherent behavior, e.g. contradictions. In general, many forms of this type of data can be collected, not just NLI, and it is also not necessary for the contexts x ( i ) to overlap as we have written here. Standard likelihood training can then be performed on coherent data D + , while the unlikelihood objective is applied to D − as we wish to push down the probability of generating the incoherent response y − given a context x . That is, given an incoherent pair ( x , y − ) we use the loss L UL ( p θ , C identity 1: | y | , x , y − ) , where we penalize each token in the target ( C identity t = { y − t } ). Hence, the loss makes generating the contradicting sentences less likely. 3 Related Work Our work provides new applications of unlikelihood training, showing that unlikelihood offers a general framework for improving generative models, and in particular dialogue models. Outside of that work, the use of negative training in dialogue retrieval, rather than generation, has been previously extensively studied, see e.g.. Motivated by the aforementioned challenges, Chu and Liu (2019) investigated an unsupervised learning framework wherein only source documents, such as product or business reviews, are available without corresponding reference summaries. They proposed an end-to-end neural architecture for abstractive summarization comprising two primary components: (a) an autoencoder designed to learn latent representations for individual reviews, and (b) a summarization module that utilizes the aggregate encoding of these reviews to generate a summary semantically aligned with the source material. In the absence of ground-truth summaries, the model is trained to reconstruct the encoding of individual reviews rather than the aggregate representation. Consequently, the model's efficacy may diminish when processing a large volume of reviews. Furthermore, the autoencoder architecture is restricted to simple decoders that lack attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2014) and copy mechanisms (Vinyals et al., 2015), both of which have demonstrated significant utility in supervised settings for producing informative and detailed summaries. However, the integration of a more powerful decoder poses a risk to the reconstruction objective, as it may learn to model arbitrary output distributions while disregarding the encoded input (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Bowman et al., 2016).

In this study, we facilitate the application of supervised learning techniques to the domain of unsupervised summarization. Specifically, we construct a synthetic training dataset derived from a corpus of product reviews to train a more robust neural model. This synthetic data is generated by selecting a review from the corpus to serve as a pseudo-summary, creating multiple noisy variants thereof to function as pseudo-reviews. These variants are produced via two noise generation functions operating at different levels of granularity: segment noising introduces perturbations at the word and phrase levels, while document noising substitutes a review with a semantically similar alternative. The resulting synthetic dataset is employed to train a neural model capable of denoising the pseudo-reviews to recover the summary. This approach is grounded in the observation that human composition of opinion summaries often involves the removal of divergent information, a process analogous to denoising. The proposed architecture consists of a multi-source encoder and a decoder augmented with an attention mechanism.

**1. Introduction**

Attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017) have emerged as a fundamental component of neural sequence processing architectures. Consequently, the visualization of attention weights has become the predominant method for interpreting model decisions and gaining insight into internal representations (Vaswani et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016; Lee et al., 2017; Dehghani et al., 2019; Rocktäschel et al., 2016; Chen and Ji, 2019; Coenen et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019). While it is methodologically unsound to equate attention weights directly with causal explanations (Pruthi et al., 2019; Jain and Wallace, 2019), they frequently yield plausible and meaningful interpretations (Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019; Vashishth et al., 2019; Vig, 2019).

This paper addresses specific challenges arising in the deeper layers of transformer models, particularly the loss of token identifiability within high-level embeddings (Brunner et al., 2020). To mitigate this, we propose two efficient methods for computing attention scores relative to input tokens (hereafter referred to as *token attention*) at any given layer. These methods integrate the raw attention weights of the current layer (i.e., *embedding attention*) with those from preceding layers. Both approaches are grounded in modeling the network's information flow as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), wherein nodes represent input tokens and hidden embeddings, edges denote attentional connections between layers, and edge weights correspond to attention scores.

The first method, **Attention Rollout**, operates on the assumption that input token identities are linearly aggregated across layers according to attention weights. This technique recursively propagates weights to capture the cumulative flow of information from input tokens to intermediate hidden states. The second method, **Attention Flow**, conceptualizes the attention graph as a flow network. By employing a maximum flow algorithm, it calculates the maximum flow values from hidden embeddings (sources) to input tokens (sinks), thereby quantifying the extent of information transmission.

### 2.1.2 Embedding Layer

Prior to processing the multimodal graph through the stacked fusion layers, an embedding layer is introduced to initialize node states. Specifically, for each textual node $v_{xi}$, the initial state $H^{(0)}_{xi}$ is defined as the sum of its word embedding and positional encoding (Vaswani et al., 2017). To derive the initial state $H^{(0)}_{oj}$ for a visual node $v_{oj}$, visual features are first extracted from the fully connected layer succeeding the ROI pooling layer within the Faster R-CNN architecture (Ren et al., 2015). Subsequently, a multilayer perceptron (MLP) employing ReLU activation functions projects these features into the same latent space as the textual representations.

### 2.1.3 Graph-Based Multimodal Fusion Layers

As illustrated in the left portion of Figure 2, $L_e$ graph-based multimodal fusion layers are stacked atop the embedding layer to encode the aforementioned multimodal graph. Within each fusion layer, intra-modal and inter-modal fusion operations are executed sequentially to update all node states. This approach ensures that the final node states simultaneously encapsulate intra-modal contextual information and cross-modal semantic relationships. Given that visual and textual nodes represent distinct semantic units containing modality-specific information, their state update processes are modeled using analogous operations with distinct parameter sets. Specifically, in the $l$-th fusion layer, the updates for textual node states $H^{(l)}_x = \{H^{(l)}_{xi}\}$ and visual node states $H^{(l)}_o = \{H^{(l)}_{oj}\}$ primarily involve the following steps:

**Step 1: Intra-modal Fusion.** In this stage, self-attention mechanisms are employed to generate contextual representations for each node by aggregating messages from its neighbors within the same modality.

The cited work includes explicit coreference guidance; however, since referential information is useful for language modeling, we expect language models to learn referential information even without explicit supervision. Here we analyze to what extent this is the case. We carry out our analysis using probe tasks, or tasks that check whether certain information is encoded in a model. The reasoning is as follows: Even if a linguistic property is encoded in the network, it is not necessarily directly accessible through the model output; therefore, we train a probe model to predict a feature of interest, in this case anaphoric coreference, given the model’s hidden representations as input. We focus on the two main linguistic levels that are relevant for coreference: morphosyntax , with grammatical constraints such as the fact that pronouns agree in number and gender with their antecedents, and semantics – in particular reference, such as the fact that a pronoun refers to the same entity as its antecedent. Our hypothesis is that language models will capture grammatical properties, but not semantic information. This hypothesis is based on the observation that morphosyntax is a formal property of language that is easier to induce from cooccurrence patterns. The fact that language refers to entities is not obvious from language alone, and LMs use only textual input. Instead, what we find is that, while it is true that language models are much better at grammar, they do show evidence of learning semanticoreferential information to some extent. Our explanation for this unexpected, partially positive result is that, because the same entity underlies all its mentions, the contexts in which the mentions appear are coherent and distinct from those of mentions of other entities. For instance, in Figure 1 , the second she mention gives additional information about Yeping Wang that is consistent with the information given in the previous sentence. This paper has two main contributions. The first is an analysis methodology to probe for referential information encoded in language models, on two linguistic levels (morphosyntax, semantics) and two kinds of context: local (around one paragraph of context), and global (document context). This methodology can be applied to any architecture.

In the context of highly complex tasks, a primary objective must be decomposed into subgoals, each necessitating a sophisticated plan. Consequently, the integration of multiple plans becomes essential; this requirement represents a significant source of difficulty in software development. To address this complexity, our marking scheme was designed to account for the diverse typology of plans and goals. While the scheme encompasses all goals and subgoals, it assigns differential weights based on the complexity of the associated plans: goals relying on fundamental knowledge are assigned a unitary weight, whereas the combination of plans is assigned a double weight.

The process of eliciting these goals and plans revealed that a profound comprehension of the task structure is requisite, particularly when assignments are challenging and demand the synthesis of multiple strategies. Although no explicit metric exists to quantify this, it is observed that developing a goal- and plan-based marking scheme entails a greater investment of time and effort compared to a holistic approach. However, this additional burden is marginal and remains comparable to that of other analytic methodologies.

Conversely, other objectives necessitate iterative strategies. Specifically, goals (2) and (3) require a Counting Loop Plan. These plans are highlighted in the analysis: the former iterates over lines, while the latter iterates over words, constituting a Conditional Counting Loop Plan, as the counting occurs only upon the satisfaction of a specific condition. The experimental framework employs the BERTBase architecture, wherein the initial eight of twelve transformer layers are frozen, restricting fine-tuning to the final four layers and the output projection layer. This architectural choice, alongside the utilization of smaller model variants and reduced training set sizes, is necessitated by the substantial computational overhead associated with influence function calculations. Specifically, the stochastic estimation method LiSSA (Agarwal et al., 2017) is adopted; even with these optimizations, computing influence scores for 10,000 training examples relative to a single test instance requires approximately ten minutes on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 Ti GPU.

It is important to acknowledge that the theoretical guarantees of influence functions rely on the model being strictly convex (implying a positive definite and invertible Hessian) and fully converged. Since deep neural networks such as BERT are inherently non-convex and training is typically halted via early stopping, these strict conditions are not met. However, as detailed by Koh and Liang (2017), influence functions remain effective approximations under these circumstances. To address non-convexity, a damping term is added to the Hessian to ensure positive definiteness and invertibility. Regarding non-convergence, the resulting approximations are interpretable as the true influence of training examples plus a constant offset independent of individual instances. Beyond theoretical justification, empirical validation is provided in Section 4 to demonstrate the practical applicability of influence functions to BERT across the following tasks:

1.  **Sentiment Analysis:** A binarized version of the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST-2) (Socher et al., 2013) is utilized. The BERT-based model is trained on 10,000 examples, achieving 89.6% accuracy on the SST-2 development set (872 examples). A random sample of 50 examples from this development set is selected for the extraction of model prediction explanations.
2.  **Natural Language Inference (NLI):** This semantic classification task evaluates the relationship between a premise and a hypothesis, categorized into three classes: entailment, neutral, and contradiction. The model is trained on the Multi-Genre NLI (MNLI) dataset (Williams et al., 2018), which comprises 393,000 premise-hypothesis pairs spanning ten distinct genres.

The neural architecture is trained on a large corpus of generic web texts, constructed on the basis of the CommonCrawl corpus. 6 In order to filter out noise and retain clean, orderly training data, we apply the following filtering steps: • we only keep sentences written in the relevant language; • we only keep sentences of up to 20 words; • we only keep sentences that contain at least one function word from a predefined list—the idea again is to filter out noisy sentences, and only keep wellformed, grammatical ones; we create a list of about 10 highly frequent function words, specific to each language; • of all the sentences that remain after these filtering steps, we only keep the ones that appear successively within a document. Using the filtering steps laid out above, we construct a training corpus of 500 million words for each language. We employ a vocabulary of 15 K words (those with highest frequency throughout the corpus); less frequent words are replaced by an <unk> token, the probability of which is set to zero during generation. Both encoder and decoder are made up of two GRU layers with a hidden state of size 2048, and the word embeddings are of size 512. Encoder, decoder, and output embeddings are all shared. Model parameters are optimized using stochastic gradient descent with an initial learning rate of 0.2, which is divided by 4 when the loss does no longer improve on a heldout validation set. We use a batch size of 64, and we apply gradient clipping. The neural architecture has been implemented using PyTorch, with substantial reliance on the OpenNMT module. For the application of the topical constraint, we use an entropy threshold of 2 . 70 . The n -gram model is a standard KneserNey smoothed trigram model implemented using KenLM, and the NMF model is factorized to 100 dimensions. Both the n -gram 6 commoncrawl.org model and the NMF model are trained on a large, 10 billion word corpus, equally constructed from web texts without any filtering steps except for language identification. For syllable length, we use µ = 12 , σ = 2 .

To generate each video segment $f(m)$ from a corresponding text sentence $e(n)$, we employ a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) framework (Rabiner, 1989; Vogel et al., 1996). Subsequently, emission probabilities derived from the IBM1 model (Brown et al., 1993) are utilized to generate the set of visual features (blobs) $\{f(m)_1, \dots, f(m)_J\}$ within $f(m)$ from the set of nouns $\{e(n)_1, \dots, e(n)_I\}$ in $e(n)$, as defined by the following equation:

$$P(f(m) | e(n)) = \epsilon(I) \prod_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{i=1}^{I} p(f(m)_j | e(n)_i) \quad (1)$$

In this HMM formulation, the hidden state represents the alignment between a video segment and a text sentence, while the state transition probabilities govern transitions between adjacent alignments. To ensure computational tractability, the model constrains each video segment to align with exactly one sentence, although multiple sentences may align to a single video segment.

This algorithm is applied to learn pairwise alignments across text-text, text-video, and video-video recipe pairs. Given two recipes (source and target) for the same dish, the alignment task is defined as mapping each text instruction (or video transcript sentence) in the source recipe to one or more corresponding instructions (or transcript sentences) in the target recipe. Two specific modifications are introduced to the aforementioned alignment algorithm:

First, unlike wet lab protocol data, the recipe pairs do not adhere to a strict temporal sequence. Consequently, the alignment algorithm must accommodate long-range jumps. To address this, the window for jump probabilities is set to $[-2, 2]$.

Second, alignments are learned using transcriptions rather than objects detected within the videos. We hypothesize that the linguistic richness inherent in instructional video transcripts facilitates superior alignment with text transcripts, a finding consistent with prior observations (Malmaud et al., 2015; Sener et al., 2015). Accordingly, when learning IBM1 word-level probabilities, the model utilizes all words in the video transcript sentences (excluding stop words) and all words in the text instructions.

**Data Sources and Model Architecture**

For part-of-speech tagging, training and test datasets were sourced from the Modern Greek subset of the Universal Dependencies (UD) treebanks (Nivre, 2018), utilizing a schema of 17 universal tags. For keyphrase extraction, training, validation, and test data were obtained from SemEval 2017 Task 10 (Augenstein et al., 2017). In this task, Input/Output (IO) tagging was employed, consolidating the three distinct keyphrase types into a single "I" tag.

Across all tasks, both the policy and the difference classifier were implemented by fine-tuning the final layer of a BERT-based embedding representation (Devlin et al., 2019). Specifically, for a sentence of length $T$ comprising words $w_1, \dots, w_T$, BERT embeddings $x_1, \dots, x_T$ were computed for each token using task-specific models: English BERT for named entity recognition, Multilingual BERT (mBERT) for part-of-speech tagging, and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) for keyphrase extraction. The state representation at position $t$, denoted as $s_t$, was constructed by concatenating the word embedding at that position with a one-hot encoding of the preceding action: $s_t = [w_t; \text{onehot}(a_{t-1})]$. This feature representation served as the input for both the labeling policy and the difference classifier.

**Expert Policy and Heuristics**

In all experimental configurations, the expert policy ($\pi^*$) was simulated as a human annotator operating sequentially, one word at a time. The expert action was defined as the optimal choice relative to the specific evaluation metric: F-score for named entity recognition and keyphrase extraction, and accuracy for part-of-speech tagging. The annotation cost was quantified as the total number of words labeled.

For named entity recognition, the heuristic approach employed a high-precision, gazetteer-based string matching method. This was constructed by extracting a gazetteer from Wikipedia via the CogComp framework (Khashabi et al., 2018) and applying the FlashText algorithm (Singh, 2017) for dataset labeling. On the training data, this heuristic achieved a precision of 0.88, a recall of 0.27, and an F-score of 0.41. For keyphrase extraction, the heuristic utilized the output of an unsupervised keyphrase extraction approach (Florescu and Caragea, 2017).

### 3.3 Final Dataset Generation

The ArgKP dataset comprises (argument, key point) pairs annotated with binary labels indicating the presence or absence of a match. The dataset was constructed from the initial labeled data through the following procedure:

The label score for each pair was defined as the proportion of annotations classifying the pair as a match. Pairs with a label score of $\geq 0.6$ were designated as positive (matching), while those with a score of $\leq 0.15$ were designated as negative (non-matching). Pairs with scores falling between these thresholds were excluded to ensure high-confidence labeling.

Subsequent data cleansing involved the removal of key points associated with fewer than three matching arguments. This filtering process resulted in the exclusion of 135 out of 378 key points and 14,679 out of 38,772 pairs generated in the prior step.

The final dataset consists of 24,093 labeled (argument, key point) pairs, of which 4,998 (20.7%) are positive. The dataset encompasses 6,515 unique arguments (averaging 232.67 per topic) and 243 unique key points (averaging 8.67 per topic). For each pair, the dataset explicitly records the associated topic and the argument's stance relative to that topic.

To assess the quality of the final dataset, an independent expert annotator—a professional debater uninvolved in the dataset's development—evaluated a random sample of 100 instances.

The quantitative influence of these factors was evaluated using a linear mixed-effects regression model, with suite-level performance predicted by model architecture and training dataset size (operationalized as the logarithm of word count). Both predictors yielded statistically significant contributions to the Syntactic Generalization (SG) score ($p < 0.001$). However, predictor ablation analysis indicates that model architecture exerts a stronger influence on model fit than dataset size (AIC = –581 when dataset size is ablated; AIC = –574 when architecture is ablated).

Beyond this primary analysis, the results derived from GPT-2 models provide further evidence regarding the relative impact of model architecture versus data scale. Although n-gram models and GPT-2 (due to byte-pair encoding) might theoretically exhibit distinct sensitivities to training data volume, we repeated the analyses excluding these architectures individually and simultaneously. In all instances, the qualitative patterns described in the main text remained consistent.

As illustrated in Figure 2, our controlled GPT-2 models (BLLIP-MD and BLLIP-LG) achieved SG scores comparable to those of the pretrained GPT-2 model, despite being trained on less than 1% of the data utilized by the pretrained counterpart. This finding suggests diminishing returns regarding training data scale for syntactic generalization performance.

### 4.3 Circuit-Level Effects on SG Score

Figure 4 presents a circuit-level breakdown of performance stratified by model architecture (left panel) and training dataset size (right panel). The right panel indicates that dataset size has a negligible effect on SG score deltas across most circuits, with the exception of the Agreement circuit, where models trained on the smallest dataset demonstrated poor performance. Conversely, the left panel reveals substantial inter-circuit variability across different architectures.

Linear mixed-effects analyses corroborate these observations: interactions involving the circuit variable were significant for both training dataset size and model architecture, though the effect was more pronounced for the latter (AIC = –654 and AIC = –623 when size and architecture were respectively ablated). While model inductive biases clearly differentiate performance on specific circuits, they exert minimal influence on the Licensing circuit. This suggests that the Licensing task engages a distinct syntactic process within language models. A plausible explanation is that the interactions tested by Licensing require tracking two covarying tokens where the downstream token is optional (see, e.g., Hu et al., 2020). While the current implementation utilizes a specific set of tools, the system architecture is designed to be modular, allowing for the substitution of alternative technologies without compromising functionality. Central to the assessment framework is a relational database responsible for the organization and storage of critical data. Adhering to established modeling methodologies, the system translates conceptual entities into database relations. Consequently, the CADS system comprises thirteen distinct relations, defined as follows:

*   **ANSWER**: Represents a student's response to a specific RRK question. This relation includes attributes that facilitate direct linkage to course sections, thereby streamlining data aggregation.
*   **COURSE**: An abstraction of a semester-long subject. This entity manages prerequisite information and supports self-referential relationships to define course dependencies.
*   **COURSE SECTION**: Represents a specific, instantiated offering of a course.
*   **ENROLLMENT**: Records a student's participation in a course section and, by extension, their engagement with associated RRK quizzes. This relation is updated upon the availability of final grades.
*   **INSTRUCTION**: Defines the pedagogical role of a course in teaching a specific knowledge topic to a designated level of mastery.
*   **KNOWLEDGE TOPIC**: Denotes a subject area identified by the ACM/IEEE-CS Joint Task Force as essential to computer science education.
*   **KTSLO MAPPING**: Establishes the relationship between a knowledge topic and a student learning objective (SLO). The schema supports a many-to-many relationship, acknowledging that a single topic may encompass multiple skills.
*   **QUESTION-TOPIC MAPPING**: Links an RRK question to a knowledge topic. This structure accommodates multiple topics per question, reflecting the complexity of isolating specific concepts for independent assessment.
*   **RRK QUESTION**: A specific item presented within an RRK quiz, categorized by a taxonomy level of mastery and answered by students.
*   **RRK QUIZ**: An assessment instrument administered to students within specific course sections.
*   **STUDENT**: An individual entity participating in tracked courses.
*   **STUDENT LEARNING OBJECTIVE (SLO)**: A life skill cultivated by the department. The model allows for a single knowledge topic to contribute to multiple learning objectives.
*   **TAXONOMY**: A framework representing the hierarchical levels of mastery corresponding to RRK questions.

Then, we have D generate a sequence of tokens in the hope that the sequence matches s j (padded with special tokens [SOS] at the beginning and [EOS] at the end). To train the autoencoder, we use teacher forcing and minimize the negative loglikelihood loss by (fine-)tuning the parameters of E and D jointly. An autoencoder embeds sentences into vectors in the latent space. We hope that the embedding is smooth in the sense that semantically similar sentences are mapped to vectors that are close to each other. In particular, interpolation between two points in the latent space should correspond to a smooth semantic transition in the text domain. To this end, we use the following two tricks. First, we employ a denoising autoencoder, which is known to yield a smoother embedding. To add noise, we randomly mask each token in s j with probability 15% by replacing the masked tokens with a special token [MASK]. During training, we use the “noisy” s j with masks as input to the encoder, and use the “clean” s j without masks to compute the loss function. Of course, one could try more sophisticated noiseadding strategies. Second, we use early stopping. In our experiments, we observe that as training proceeds, the validation loss of the autoencoder keeps decreasing. In the absence of masks, presumably it would eventually decay to zero so that the autoencoder perfectly reconstructs every sentence. However, this does not necessarily imply that the embedding is smooth. On the contrary, an overtrained autoencoder often tries to remember every individual token and thus fails to achieve smoothness in the latent semantic space. Moreover, it can catastrophically forget some of the information in the initial pretrained model (GPT2) and partially lose the power of generating fluent sentences. In practice, we select a checkpoint by monitoring its validation performance on sentence interpolation. Some examples of sentence interpolation are shown in Table 1 . Sentence Feature Prediction. After encoding sentences into feature vectors, we use a sentencelevel transformer T to predict the feature vector of the missing sentence from those of contextual sentences. This is analogous to the task of predicting masked tokens for (pre-)training BERT, but now it is at the sentence level.

### 5.3 Model Architectures

PPVAE is a model-agnostic framework, allowing the encoders and decoders of both Pretrain-VAE and Plugin-VAE to be adapted for various experimental settings. The specific architectures employed in this study are detailed below.

**Pretrain-VAE**
The encoder consists of a single-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (BiGRU) with 256 hidden units per direction. Two linear fully connected (FC) layers are employed to implement the reparameterization trick (Kingma and Welling, 2014). The decoder utilizes a Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) comprising three layers and eight attention heads. Following each block, additional positional embeddings are incorporated, and the linearly transformed encoded vector is provided as input to each block (Brock et al., 2019). To ensure a fair comparison, the same encoder-decoder architecture is applied to both SVAE and CTRLGen.

**Plugin-VAE**
The encoder is a two-layer fully connected network with hidden dimensions of 64 and 32, respectively, accepting input in $d_g$ dimensions and producing an output via a linear layer of $d_c$ units. The decoder mirrors this structure with a two-layer fully connected network (32 and 64 hidden units), taking the latent variable in $d_c$ dimensions as input and generating an output in $d_g$ dimensions through a linear layer. LeakyReLU activation functions (Maas et al., 2013) are utilized throughout the fully connected networks.

### 5.4 Hyperparameters

**Pretrain-VAE**
The latent space dimension ($d_g$) is set to 128. Word embeddings are initialized randomly with a dimensionality of 256, and the output softmax matrix is tied to the embedding layer. The adversarial classifier comprises two fully connected layers with 128 hidden units and LeakyReLU activation, followed by a single linear output layer without bias. The balance coefficient $\lambda$ is configured to 20 for the Yelp dataset and 15 for the News Titles dataset. Additionally, the WAE-GAN is trained using Wasserstein Divergence (Wu et al., 2018) to stabilize the training process.

Many previous studies lend support to the explanations offered by TPB and TRA about behavior adoption in general and behavior adoption in educational settings specifically . Some studies have suggested the model be expanded by adding “antecedent” subcomponents to perceived control or attitudes , or adding other factors such as past behaviors/habits . We opt for a simplified model for the sake of brevity, and because our purpose is not to validate the theory, but to employ it as a conceptual framework to better understand faculty teaching behavior. This section describes our data collection, measures, and data transformations. This research is couched in the larger BRAID Research project, which is a national study of efforts to broaden participation among women and students of color in undergraduate computing. . The survey instrument used for this paper is modeled on the Higher Education Research Institution’s faculty survey , a prominent nationwide survey of college faculty. Content modifications made to fit the context of CS higher education are informed by evidencebased practices found in the EngageCSEdu collection. The survey instrument was peerreviewed by members of the project’s advisory board and revised using their feedback. The survey was designed to probe instructor behaviors and attitudes related to teaching introductory computing courses, including their use of certain programming languages, the frequency of lecturing and using various studentcentered teaching practices, and attitudes and beliefs toward teaching, students, and their department. It also includes questions about instructors’ background characteristics. Sampling. The survey was fielded online using Qualtrics. Survey data were collected during the 201516 and 201617 academic years from introductory computing course instructors of various ranks, teaching at 15 research colleges and universities (13 public, 2 private) located across the U.S. that participate in the Building, Recruiting, and Inclusion for Diversity (BRAID) Initiative. Institution representatives identified introductory courses being taught and provided the research team with names and contact information for these courses’ instructors. In total, survey invitations were emailed to 257 instructors, which netted 94 respondents (response rate = 36%). Some instructors teach a single introductory course multiple terms and were surveyed multiple times. Because this study investigates the connection between attitudes and behavior, we felt it was inappropriate to treat multiple sets of responses from the same individual as separate “cases,” even though people are not static in their attitudes or behaviors.

Informally, an answer ( i, k, j ) is correct if there is a correct triple ( e 1 , r, e 2 ) , where e 1 and e 2 are entities and r is a relation, such that i , j , and k are mentions of e 1 , e 2 , and r , respectively. 3.1 Evaluation protocol To describe our proposed evaluation protocol, we first revisit the most commonly used methodology to evaluate link prediction methods for KGs, i.e., the entityranking protocol. Then, we discuss its adaptation to OLP, which we call the mentionranking protocol (see Fig. 3 ). KGs and entity ranking. For each triple z = ( i, k, j ) in the evaluation data, a link prediction model ranks the answers for two questions, q t ( z ) = ( i, k, ?) and q h ( z ) = (? , k, j ) . The model is evaluated based on the ranks of the correct entities j and i ; this setting is called raw . When true answers for q t ( z ) and q h ( z ) other than j and i are filtered from the rankings, then the setting is called filtered . OKGs and mention ranking. In OLP, the model predicts a ranked list of mentions. But questions might have multiple equivalent true answers, i.e., answers that refer to the same entity but use different mentions. Our evaluation metrics are based on the highest rank of a correct answer mention in the ranking. For the filtered setting, the mentions of known answer entities other than the evaluated entity are filtered from the ranking. This mentionranking protocol thus uses knowledge of alternative mentions of the entity in the evaluation triple to obtain a suitable ranking. The mentionranking protocol therefore requires (i) ground truth annotations for the entity mentions in the head and tail of the evaluation data, and (ii) a comprehensive set of mentions for these entities. 4 Creating the Open Link Prediction Benchmark O LP B ENCH An OLP benchmark should enable us to evaluate a model’s capability to predict genuinely new facts, i.e., facts can not be trivially derived.

The proposed enhancements aim to mitigate the cognitive and physical burdens associated with Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices, thereby potentially increasing user adherence and motivation. Furthermore, the application of Dialogue Act (DA) classification offers a robust mechanism for the rapid analysis of speech pathology transcripts, facilitating both initial AAC assessments and ongoing intervention strategies.

In the context of initial AAC evaluation, interactions between patients and clinical teams are traditionally coded for specific communicative functions, such as requests, information exchange, and interrogatives. This process typically involves manual recording, transcription, and subsequent annotation of audio or text files by speech-language pathologists (SLPs). By conceptualizing these communicative functions as DA classes, SLPs can annotate representative samples of atypical speech to establish a gold-standard dataset. A DA classification model trained on this data could subsequently automate the annotation process. Such automation would streamline the evaluation workflow for both initial device selection and subsequent device fitting, ultimately accelerating patient access to appropriate AAC solutions and enhancing communicative efficacy.

Post-assessment, DA classification can serve as a longitudinal tool for monitoring patient progress against established evaluation goals. Aligning with current research emphasizing evidence-based intervention for individuals with complex communication needs, this approach enables SLPs and conversational partners to efficiently code interactions and quantify the frequency of target communicative functions. This capability facilitates more frequent feedback loops, allowing the clinical team to adapt intervention strategies with greater precision.

Moreover, advanced models can be developed to distinguish between communication modalities, specifically differentiating between vocalization and device-mediated output. By employing multiclass models to correlate specific communicative functions with their respective delivery methods, clinicians can gain deeper insights into a patient's functional capabilities. This analysis clarifies whether an individual can independently vocalize specific functions or requires device assistance to meet particular conversational demands, thereby informing more targeted therapeutic planning.

Given that natural language frequently serves as an indicator of emotional states, emotion recognition has garnered significant attention within the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), driven by its broad applicability in opinion mining, recommender systems, and healthcare. However, Emotion Recognition in Conversation (ERC) has only recently emerged as a focal point for the NLP community, largely due to the increasing availability of public conversational datasets. ERC offers substantial utility in analyzing social media interactions and enabling real-time dialogue assessment, which is instrumental in domains such as legal proceedings, interviews, and e-health services.

Distinct from standard sentence-level emotion recognition, ERC necessitates the contextual modeling of individual utterances, relying heavily on preceding dialogue turns and the temporal sequence of speaker interactions. Recent studies indicate that both lexicon-based and conventional deep learning approaches, when applied without conversational context, perform suboptimally on ERC datasets. This limitation stems from their failure to account for conversation-specific factors, including contextual cues, the temporality of speaker turns, and speaker-specific information. To address these challenges, this paper introduces **COSMIC**, a commonsense-guided framework designed for robust emotion identification within conversational contexts.

The first research question investigates the persistence patterns discernible within one-semester transaction logs from a self-assessment platform using a probabilistic mixture model. Through the application of this model, four distinct micro-study persistence patterns were identified: "Active" (A), characterized by steady, consistent engagement across all weeks; "Cramming" (C), defined by concentrated activity immediately preceding examinations; "Inactive" (I), reflecting minimal platform engagement; and "Semi-Active" (S), representing a level of effort exceeding that of inactive students but lacking the consistency of active learners.

Building upon these findings, the trained model was applied to subsequent examination periods to identify micro-patterns, which were subsequently aggregated to form macro-persistence patterns spanning the entire semester. Preliminary analysis of these macro-patterns suggests a correlation between shifts in study persistence and examination performance. To further characterize these macro-patterns, the second research question examines the relationship between persistence consistency and academic outcomes.

Adopting an established methodological framework, a consistency scoring scheme was defined and applied to the aggregated macro-patterns. During this analysis, patterns containing the "II" suffix (denoting unobservable data points) were identified as potential sources of ambiguity and were consequently excluded from the correlation analysis to ensure interpretative clarity. The resulting analysis yielded a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.409, indicating a moderate positive association between persistence consistency and exam performance. These findings suggest that short-term study persistence alone is insufficient for predicting academic success; rather, the maintenance of consistent study habits throughout the semester plays a significant role in determining overall academic performance.

The ES program is a bachelor of arts degree with two tracks: Environmental Science (ENVS) and Cultural Resource Management (CRM). Students have to take 60 to 61 programspecific credits. 37 credits are core courses that include courses in environmental sustainability, general biology, anthropology (with focus on human adaptations), urban sociology, environmental psychology and environmental ethics, as well as public policy. 23 to 24 credits are track specific. For the ENVS track they include 8 credits in lab and field courses in environmental science, and urban ecology, as well as 8 credits in two inquiry based laboratory courses, Environmental Chemistry, and Molecular Genetics and Biotechnology. Students are also required to complete a 2credit Research seminar and a 3credit Capstone project. Leveraging the multidisciplinary approach that we had in mind, we decided to create two Special Topics courses, one for each major, that would be tailored to the aspects of the project most relevant to each set of students. The shared experience of students from different disciplines was then the focus of a survey that we posed to all participants, and the results will be discussed in a later section. The ENVS course was offered as a Special Topics course in environmental sciences (ENVS 310). The aim of the course was to literally build an indoors farming system and explore ways to maximize crop yields and minimize energy consumption using technology. While prerequisites were not specified, being a 300 level course, in addition to the lower level introductory science core courses (including environmental sustainability), students should have completed at least one of the track specific lab courses and the urban ecology field course. In these courses students acquire basic skills needed for experimental design, and learn to develop protocols and standard operating procedures. Previous knowledge of programming was explicitly not required. The Special Topics course designed for AIT students (COSC 497) was offered as an alternative to Advanced Database Systems (COSC 456), which is a requirement for all students in the ADP track. In order to ensure a minimum level of experience for all students, we maintained the prerequisites associated with the original course. All students had completed our CS1 (COSC 151, using Python), CS2 (COSC 251, using Java) as well as a Database Systems course (COSC 356, using SQL) or their equivalent at other institutions if they transferred.

To generate these examples, for each groundtruth box, we show a predicted bounding region closest to it and manually group the bounding regions into different categories. We also include regions that the model is actively attending to, even if they are not present in the gold annotations (marked with an asterisk). We then aggregate the attention weights from words to those regions in the same category. We show the best heads of 6 layers that achieve the highest entity grounding accuracy but we find that they also exhibit a certain level of syntactic grounding. We observe the same behaviours as in the quantitative analysis, in that VisualBERT not only performs grounding but also refines its predictions through successive Transformer layers. For example, in the bottom image in Figure 5 , initially the word “husband” and the word “woman” both assign significant attention weight to regions corresponding to the woman. By the end of the computation, VisualBERT has disentangled the woman and man, correctly aligning both. Furthermore, there are many examples of syntactic alignments. In the same image, the word “teased” aligns to both the man and woman while “by” aligns to the man. 4 Related Work There is a long research history of bridging vision and language with the lasted advances being visually grounded language models. However, little analysis has been done on understanding what visionandlanguage models learn. This paper makes the following contributions: • It presents an attempt at evaluating the explanation methods for neural machine translation from a new viewpoint of fidelity. • It proposes a principled metric for evaluation, and to put it into practice it derives a simple yet efficient approach to approximately calculate the metric. • It quantitatively compares several different explanation methods and evaluates their effects in terms of the proposed metric. 2 NMT and Explanation Methods 2.1 NMT Models Suppose x = { x 1 , · · · , x | x | } denotes a source sentence with length | x | and y = { y 1 , · · · , y | y | } is a target sentence. Most NMT literature models the following conditional probability P ( y | x ) in an encoderdecoder fashion: P ( y | x ) = Q t P ( y t | y <t , x ) t P ( y t | s t ) , (1) where y <t = { y 1 , · · · , y t − 1 } denotes a prefix of y with length t − 1 , and s t is the decoding state vector of timestep t . In the encoding stage, the encoder of a NMT model transforms the source sentence x into a sequence of hidden vectors h = { h 1 , · · · , h | x | } . In the decoding stage, the decoder module summarizes the hidden vectors h and the history decoding states s <t = { s 1 , · · · , s t − 1 } into the decoding state vector s t . The result indicates that there is still large room for SoftMasked BERT to make improvement. 3.7 Discussions We observed that SoftMasked BERT is able to make more effective use of global context information than BERTFinetune. With soft masking the likely errors are identified, and as a result the model can better leverage the power of BERT to make sensible reasoning for error correction by referring to not only local context but also global context. For example, there is a typo in the sentence ‘ 我 会 说 一 点 儿 ， 不 过 一个 汉 子 也 看 不 懂 ， 所 以 我 迷 路 了 ’(I can speak a little Chinese, but I don’t understand man. So I got lost.). The word ‘ 汉 子 ’(man) is incorrect and should be written as ‘ 汉 字 ’(Chinese character). BERTFinetune can not rectify the mistake, but SoftMasked BERT can, because the error correction can only be accurately conducted with global context information. We also found that there are two major types of errors in almost all methods including SoftMasked BERT, which affect the performances. For statistics of errors, we sampled 100 errors from test set. We found that 67% of errors require strong reasoning ability, 11% of errors are due to lack of world knowledge, and the remaining 22% of errors have no significant type. The first type of errors is due to lack of inference ability. Accurate correction of such typos requires stronger inference ability. For example, for the sentence ‘ 他主 动 拉 了 姑 娘 的 手 , 心 里 很 高 心 , 嘴 上 故 作 生 气 ’ (He intentionally took the girl’s hand, and was very x, but was pretending to be angry.) where the incorrect word ‘x’ is not comprehensible, there might be two possible corrections, changing ‘ 高 心 ’ to ‘ 寒 心 ’(chilled) and changing ‘ 高 心 ’ to ‘ 高 兴 ’(happy), while the latter is more reasonable for humans. One can see that in order to make more reliable corrections, the models must have stronger inference ability. The second type of errors is due to lack of world knowledge.

Each dataset employed in this study is selected based on one of two criteria: (1) the presence of metadata that facilitates a natural partitioning of samples, or (2) the availability of a counterpart dataset derived from a distinct data-generating process. By partitioning or grouping these datasets, we induce distributional shifts to evaluate out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization capabilities.

The experimental framework utilizes four primary sentiment analysis datasets:

*   **SST-2 and IMDb:** We employ the SST-2 dataset, comprising concise expert movie reviews (Socher et al., 2013), and the IMDb dataset, consisting of full-length layperson reviews (Maas et al., 2011). A cross-dataset evaluation protocol is applied, wherein models are trained on one dataset and evaluated on the other, and vice versa. The task involves predicting binary sentiment, with performance measured by accuracy.
*   **Yelp Review Dataset:** This dataset contains restaurant reviews enriched with detailed metadata, including user identifiers and establishment names. We stratify the data into four distinct groups based on cuisine type: American, Chinese, Italian, and Japanese. Models are tasked with binary sentiment prediction, and accuracy is reported as the primary metric.
*   **Amazon Review Dataset:** Sourced from Amazon product reviews (McAuley et al., 2015; He and McAuley, 2016), this dataset is partitioned into five clothing categories (Clothes, Women's Clothing, Men's Clothing, Baby Clothing, and Shoes) and two entertainment categories (Music and Movies). A sample of 50,000 reviews is drawn for each category. The objective is to predict a 1-to-5 star rating, with accuracy serving as the evaluation metric.

Furthermore, these datasets are utilized to assess performance in semantic similarity, reading comprehension, and textual entailment tasks:

*   **STSB:** This dataset requires the prediction of semantic similarity between sentence pairs (Cer et al., 2017). It encompasses text from diverse genres and sources. We utilize four sources spanning two genres: MSRpar and Headlines (news), and MSRvid and Images (captions). Performance is evaluated using Pearson's correlation coefficient.
*   **ReCoRD:** A reading comprehension dataset comprising paragraphs from CNN and Daily Mail news articles paired with automatically generated questions (Zhang et al., 2018). The dataset is bifurcated into CNN and Daily Mail subsets, with evaluation conducted via exact match scores.
*   **MNLI:** A textual entailment dataset consisting of sentence pairs drawn from various text genres (Williams et al., 2018). We select examples from two transcribed text genres (Telephone and Face-to-Face) and one written text genre (Letters). Classification accuracy is reported as the evaluation metric.

### 2.2 Embedding and Model Architectures

We evaluate natural language processing models utilizing diverse input representations and encoder architectures. The investigation encompasses three distinct model categories, comprising a total of thirteen models.

In the context of named entity recognition, let $s$ denote a predicted entity span, and let $E$ represent the set of all possible sequences in $Y$ that contain $s$. An event is classified as positive if the ground-truth label sequence $y$ includes the predicted span $s$.

When constructing the set $\phi(x)$, it is essential to balance two competing objectives: limiting the number of output entities to a manageable size while maximizing the coverage of positive events within $I(x)$. To illustrate this trade-off, consider a parsing task, such as syntactic or semantic parsing. Two primary schemes for defining $I(x)$ are presented below:

1.  **Scheme 1:** $\phi(x)$ consists solely of the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) label sequence prediction. Consequently, $I(x)$ contains the single event corresponding to the correctness of the sequence $y' = \arg\max_{y} p_{\theta}(y|x)$.
2.  **Scheme 2:** $\phi(x)$ encompasses all possible label sequences. In this case, $I(x)$ includes an event for every $y' \in Y$, indicating whether that specific sequence is correct.

Existing methodologies can be mapped to these schemes. For instance, the model confidence calibration proposed by Dong et al. (2018) aligns with Scheme 1, where the entity of interest is the MAP prediction. Conversely, the application of Platt Scaling in a one-vs-all setting for multiclass classification (Guo et al., 2017) corresponds to Scheme 2, where the entity of interest is the presence of a specific class label.

As previously discussed, Scheme 2 is computationally prohibitive for our purposes due to the large cardinality of $|Y|$. While Scheme 1 is computationally efficient, it suffers from limited coverage of positive events. For example, if a sequence labeling model achieves 60% sentence-level accuracy, only 60% of the positive events are captured by the set of MAP predictions. Effectively, only 60% of the model's correct outputs are utilized to construct the forecaster, thereby constraining the scope of $I(x)$.

To mitigate this limitation, incorporating the top-$k$ predictions into $\phi(x)$ can enhance the coverage of positive events, thereby increasing the volume of positive training data available for the forecaster. However, selecting the optimal value for $k$ requires a careful trade-off between computational cost and event coverage.

**Methodology and Evaluation of Semantic Similarity Measures**

The information content, defined as the weight $w_i$ of a link, is derived from the negative logarithm of the conditional probability, as detailed in Equation (14) and grounded in information theory principles. Specific weighting rules are applied as follows: if no conditional criteria are met, and the phrase is identified as a noun class consisting of proper nouns, no weight is assigned to the link connecting the current node to the proper noun node.

Subsequently, semantic similarity is determined by identifying the minimum distance path while accounting for the maximum link weight. An aggregate similarity score is then computed by summing the weights of all links along the path from the start node ($S$) to the leaf node.

To evaluate the proposed text similarity measure, a dataset comprising 50 sentence pairs from the SemEval 2015 training corpus was utilized. Two distinct experimental runs were conducted:
1.  **Baseline Run:** Utilizing WordNet taxonomy relationships (WordNet version 2.0), this run yielded a similarity score of 0.46.
2.  **Enhanced Run:** By incorporating information content (IC) values calculated through the integration of WordNet taxonomy and a unigram language model, the similarity score was improved to 0.78. This approach demonstrates superior performance compared to other methods reported in the literature.

**Overview of Semantic Analysis Tools**

Several established libraries and toolkits facilitate the computation and analysis of semantic similarities:

1.  **Semantic Measures Library (SML) and Toolkit:**
    SML is a Java-based library distributed under the open-source CeCILL license, specifically designed for semantic measurement. It supports the computation and analysis of semantic similarities between terms and concepts defined within terminologies and ontologies. Additionally, it enables the comparison of entities (e.g., genes) annotated by concepts. The library is compatible with various ontological formats and specifications, including OBO, RDF, and OWL, and supports multi-threaded applications for parallel computation. An accompanying open-source toolkit allows non-developers to access these functionalities via a command-line interface.

2.  **WordNet-Similarity:**
    This Perl module leverages the lexical database WordNet to compute semantic similarities and relatedness measures. It implements a comprehensive suite of algorithms, including Resnik, Jiang-Conrath, Wu-Palmer, Banerjee-Pedersen, Patwardhan-Pedersen, Hirst-St. Onge, Leacock-Chodorow, and Lin. The module includes pre-computed pairwise similarity data for nouns and verbs.

3.  **UMLS-Similarity:**
    Based on the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS), this open-source Perl module is designed to calculate semantic similarity and relatedness measures. It operates specifically on ontologies and terminologies within the medical domain.

This study adopts the concept of emotion mixture, leveraging state-of-the-art Mixture of Experts (MoE) architectures to achieve an optimal equilibrium between positive and negative emotional categories. Drawing inspiration from Serban et al., we incorporate stochasticity at the emotion-group level to enhance response diversity. This mechanism is particularly critical in scenarios where input utterances warrant ambivalent yet contextually appropriate responses, as illustrated in Figure 1.

The primary contributions of this work are twofold. First, we propose a novel framework for empathetic dialogue generation that integrates contextual encoding with emotional modeling. By employing emotion stochastic sampling and emotion mimicry, the model generates responses that are both appropriate and empathetic across varying emotional polarities. Empirical results demonstrate that this approach surpasses current state-of-the-art performance when trained and evaluated on a large-scale empathy dialogue dataset. Second, through extensive ablation studies, we elucidate the specific contributions of emotion mimicry and emotion grouping to the efficacy of empathetic response generation.

While open-domain conversational models have achieved significant advancements in recent years, often producing persona-consistent and diverse outputs, they frequently lack genuine empathy. Generating truly empathetic responses necessitates the precise management of emotional and sentimental nuances. Previous efforts include Zhou et al., who modeled psychological concepts as memory states within Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and utilized emotion-category embeddings during decoding. Similarly, Wang and Wan introduced a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework featuring emotion-specific generators. On a larger scale, other researchers have utilized emojis from Twitter posts as emotion labels, integrating them into an attention-based Sequence-to-Sequence model augmented with a Conditional Variational Autoencoder. However, these existing approaches typically rely on user-provided emotion labels to generate affective responses, a constraint that may limit their ability to produce responses that are genuinely empathetic to the speaker's underlying sentiment.

The debate over whether children should do homework after school or not is a key issue in education. While there are ongoing discussions worldwide and both advantages and disadvantages exist, I believe that doing homework is more important for children.

To begin with, some people argue that children should spend time on activities other than homework. This allows them to exercise and build stronger bodies and immunity. Additionally, in Japan, some parents are concerned that the English education system is not well structured, so they prefer to enroll their children in private English lessons after school from a young age.

On the other hand, children often struggle to keep up with their classes every day, as they are required to learn multiple subjects. Although children have an impressive ability to absorb information, it can be difficult to stay on track with the school curriculum. Therefore, doing homework is essential for children to review what they learned each day. Homework is especially helpful for students who find certain subjects, like math or history, challenging. It gives them a chance to improve in their stronger subjects and also helps them overcome difficulties in weaker ones. Otherwise, children may quickly forget what they learned, and those challenging subjects might continue to be disliked.

In conclusion, while studying and doing homework may seem boring, it is crucial for children to build a strong foundation in essential subjects as early as possible. Many adults who neglected homework as children often regret it later. As children accumulate knowledge over the years, they are better able to imagine and pursue their future dreams. For these reasons, homework is more necessary for children than anything else.
