Human evaluation of the attention mechanisms generated by our proposed model indicates a strong preference for the attention weights produced by the Diversity LSTM over those of the standard Vanilla LSTM. Specifically, human evaluators deemed the Diversity LSTM's explanations superior in 72.3%, 62.2%, 88.4%, and 99.0% of the samples across the Yelp, SNLI, Quora Question Paraphrase (QQP), and Babi 1 datasets, respectively.

**2. Tasks, Datasets, and Models**

The primary objective of this study is to investigate the limitations of existing attention mechanisms coupled with LSTM-based encoders in generating faithful and plausible explanations for model predictions. To this end, we conduct experiments across a diverse range of datasets and tasks. This section outlines the specific tasks and datasets employed, alongside a brief overview of the standard LSTM-attention architecture utilized.

Our experimental framework encompasses four primary tasks: Binary Text Classification, Natural Language Inference (NLI), Paraphrase Detection, and Question Answering. We utilize a total of twelve datasets, the majority of which align with those employed by Jain and Wallace (2019). For organizational clarity, the Text Classification task is subdivided into Sentiment Analysis and Other Text Classification.

*   **Sentiment Analysis:** We employ the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST) (Socher et al., 2013), IMDB Movie Reviews (Maas et al., 2011), Yelp, and Amazon datasets. All datasets within this category utilize a binary target variable (positive/negative).
*   **Other Text Classification:** This category includes the Twitter ADR dataset (Nikfarjam et al., 2015), comprising 8,000 tweets, where the objective is to identify whether a tweet describes an adverse drug reaction. Additionally, we utilize a subset of the 20 Newsgroups dataset (Jain and Wallace, 2019) to classify news articles into sports categories (baseball vs. hockey). From the MIMIC ICD9 corpus (Johnson et al., 2016), we incorporate two datasets: *Anemia*, for distinguishing between chronic and acute anemia diagnoses, and *Diabetes*, for predicting the presence or absence of a diabetes diagnosis.
*   **Natural Language Inference:** We utilize the SNLI dataset (Bowman et al., 2015) to evaluate textual entailment within sentence pairs. This dataset features three classification labels: entailment, contradiction, and neutral.
*   **Paraphrase Detection:** We employ the Quora Question Paraphrase (QQP) dataset, a component of the GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2018), which consists of question pairs labeled as either paraphrased or non-paraphrased.

In order to produce more interpretable rationales, we construct novel variants of OT that have provable and controllable bounds on the sparsity of the alignments. Selecting and aligning text spans can be jointly optimized within this framework, resulting in optimal text matchings. Our model is fully endtoend differentiable using the Sinkhorn algorithm for OT and can be used with any neural network architecture. Model interpretability via selective rationalization has attracted considerable interest recently. Some recent work has focused on overcoming the challenge of learning in the selective rationalization regime, such as by enabling endtoend differentiable training or by regularizing to avoid performance degeneration. Unlike these methods, which perform independent rationale selection on each input document, we extend selective rationalization by jointly learning selection and alignment, as it is better suited for text matching applications. Concurrent to this work, DeYoung et al. introduce the ERASER benchmark datasets with humanannotated rationales along with several rationalization models. **Report on Communication Platform Evaluation and Selection**

It is frequently observed that academic communities, comprising both students and faculty, do not inherently prioritize open collaboration. Furthermore, communications initiated on private channels rarely transition to public domains post-hoc. During the Spring 2019 term, all participating institutions utilized Slack, establishing dedicated channels for individual institutions alongside a general LFP channel. However, inter-institutional engagement remained minimal, and adoption within individual courses was limited.

Several systemic limitations were identified regarding Slack: it is proprietary rather than open source; it lacks full accessibility compliance; it mandates explicit access requests for participation, thereby precluding passive observation (lurking); and it represents an additional platform for students who do not utilize it in their personal or professional lives, increasing cognitive load.

In response, the Coordinating Committee and student researchers conducted an evaluation of alternative communication platforms. The selection criteria mandated that the solution be open source, fully accessible to individuals with visual or auditory impairments, centrally hosted, and capable of allowing unrestricted joining or observation without administrative approval. Following a review of multiple candidates, two platforms were selected for pilot testing: Gitter and Discord. Neither option fully satisfied all established requirements.

Gitter, while open source and hosted (with accessibility via an IRC bridge), presents a significant barrier to entry: registration requires an existing GitHub, GitLab, or Twitter account. Due to the lack of granularity in the authentication systems of these providers, Gitter necessitates broad access to a user's repositories. Although Gitter asserts that this data will not be exploited, the associated privacy warnings are sufficiently concerning that widespread adoption is unlikely.

Conversely, Discord, while proprietary and lacking full accessibility features, offers robust functionality including audio, video, and screen sharing capabilities. Additionally, a significant portion of the student population is already familiar with the platform. The primary concern regarding Discord is its branding as a tool for the gaming community, which incorporates cultural references that may elicit negative reactions from some students. While certain features can be disabled, the platform's core identity cannot be entirely altered.

Consequently, the decision has been made to transition to Discord. This transition is accompanied by a strategic mitigation plan to address potential student apprehensions. This plan includes explicitly acknowledging the platform's reputation, enforcing a strict code of conduct, and actively cultivating a safe and inclusive environment. Furthermore, empirical research will be conducted to assess student perceptions of the tool.

### 3.2 Noise Induction

Ideally, sequence labeling models would be trained on noisy sentences annotated with named entity labels. However, such datasets are scarce. Conversely, labeled clean text corpora are widely available (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Benikova et al., 2014). Consequently, we propose utilizing standard Named Entity Recognition (NER) corpora and synthetically inducing noise into input tokens during the training phase.

Unlike the image domain, which is continuous, the text domain is discrete; therefore, continuous perturbations cannot be directly applied to written language. While prior research has applied distortions at the embedding level (Miyato et al., 2017; Yasunaga et al., 2018; Bekoulis et al., 2018), the semantic impact of such modifications on the underlying textual input remains difficult to interpret. To address this, we apply a noise induction procedure to generate distorted copies of the input.

For each input sentence $x$, every token $x_i = (c_1, \dots, c_K)$, where $K$ denotes the length of $x_i$, is independently perturbed according to the following procedure (see Figure 3):

1.  The symbol $\varepsilon$ is inserted before the first character and after every subsequent character of $x_i$, resulting in an extended token $x'_i = (\varepsilon, c_1, \varepsilon, \dots, \varepsilon, c_K, \varepsilon)$.
2.  For each character $c'_k$ within $x'_i$, a replacement character $\tilde{c}'_k$ is sampled from the corresponding probability distribution $P(\tilde{c}'_k \mid c'_k)$. This distribution is derived from the row of the character confusion matrix associated with $c'_k$.

The participants demonstrated distinct approaches to contextualizing programming concepts. Mandy and Shelly anchored their instruction in familiar, playful environments such as Minecraft, Code.org, and Sphero. For instance, Shelly characterized Scratch as analogous to construction-based games, noting that it allows users to eventually establish specific rules and mechanics. Conversely, Shawn employed a more abstract analogy, comparing Scratch to the functional logic of a calculator program, where specific buttons correspond to distinct commands and operations. Consequently, while Mandy and Shelly utilized relatable, game-based metaphors, Shawn's examples were more complex and technical. This divergence suggests that Shawn's advanced programming expertise may have led to the selection of analogies that were less accessible to the student demographic.

Regarding instructional delivery, Shelly articulated the lesson trajectory—outlining specific activities and student roles—more frequently and with greater clarity than her colleagues. Data indicates that Shelly referenced the lesson trajectory twelve times, accompanied by 24 instances of direct instruction, whereas Shawn referenced the trajectory ten times but provided 40 instances of direct instruction. This disparity implies that Shelly more effectively communicated the overarching goals and the coherence between activities, thereby enhancing students' understanding of the purpose behind their tasks.

Following the implementation of this instructional model across three classes, several areas for improvement were identified for future iterations. First, the temporal gap between the initial board game activity and the subsequent coding phase in Scratch proved detrimental; students often lost retention of core game mechanics by the time they were tasked with authoring their own levels. To address this, the unit will be resequenced to facilitate immediate game redesign following the initial learning phase. Second, instances of redundancy were observed between classroom and library instruction, particularly in Shawn's sessions, rather than the intended complementary relationship. Given the logistical constraints necessitating split instruction between teachers and the librarian, future planning will prioritize enhanced coordination to clearly delineate the specific roles and content delivered in each setting.

Effective presentations require a strategic balance between depth and accessibility, as well as a careful consideration of audience demographics and cognitive processing.

Firstly, presenters often delve too deeply into their subject matter, offering a level of nuance and detail that overwhelms novice participants. This excessive complexity frequently obscures the broader context, preventing the audience from grasping the essential concepts. Secondly, illustrative examples are frequently drawn from the presenter's own discipline, particularly the humanities and social sciences. This approach can alienate participants from technical fields, such as computing, who may perceive the content as inapplicable to their own work. Conversely, presentations that offer a simplified, high-level overview supported by concrete examples from technical disciplines tend to resonate strongly with audiences and receive high evaluations. While steering presenters toward this approach has yielded reasonable success through direct guidance, it remains a critical area for improvement.

Regarding presentations by members of groups underrepresented in computing, those structured around specific challenges and the strategies employed to overcome them are often highly motivating and effective. However, a significant pitfall arises when presenters emphasize atypical personal traits or unique experiences that facilitated their success despite systemic barriers. This can inadvertently portray the speaker as an outlier, rendering their achievements seem unattainable to the audience. This issue is particularly pronounced when presenters respond to skepticism with a defiant "watch me" attitude rather than focusing on relatable, transferable aspects of their journey. Efforts to redirect such presenters toward more universally applicable narratives have met with limited success.

These challenges are compounded by the brain's subconscious pattern-recognition mechanisms. While these mechanisms efficiently distill complex sensory data into actionable information, they also generate automated biases, such as the subconscious association that individuals from underrepresented demographics do not fit the archetype of a computer scientist. Because this processing occurs below the level of conscious awareness, it disproportionately affects busy individuals, who lack the time to scrutinize their initial impressions, and uninformed individuals, who lack the contextual knowledge or alternative frameworks to challenge these biases.

Consequently, these subconscious biases can lead to significant errors in presentation design. A primary lesson is that "less is more." Despite decades of research into the phenomena that impede diversity, many presenters—including experienced ones—continue to attempt to include more material than the audience can effectively absorb. To maximize impact, presenters must prioritize clarity and relevance over comprehensiveness, ensuring that their message is accessible to a diverse and varied audience.

**Abstract**

This paper presents a novel retrieval-and-rewriting framework designed to enhance the initialization of unsupervised machine translation (UMT) models. The proposed methodology involves retrieving semantically analogous sentences from monolingual corpora of two distinct languages, followed by the application of a specialized rewriting model to minimize the semantic divergence between source sentences and their retrieved counterparts. These refined sentence pairs serve as the foundation for initializing Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) models, which subsequently generate pseudo-parallel data to train Neural Machine Translation (NMT) systems. This process is integrated with iterative back-translation. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that the proposed approach yields superior initial UMT models, resulting in a final translation performance improvement exceeding 4 BLEU points.

**1. Introduction**

Recent advancements have established the efficacy of unsupervised machine translation (UMT) (Artetxe et al., 2017; Lample et al., 2017, 2018; Artetxe et al., 2018b; Marie and Fujita, 2018; Ren et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019). The prevailing paradigm entails the construction of two initial translation models (source-to-target and target-to-source), followed by iterative back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2018) utilizing pseudo-data generated reciprocally by these models. The initialization phase is critical; suboptimal initialization may unduly constrain the search space, while excessive noise introduced at this stage can significantly degrade final performance.

Prior UMT methodologies (Lample et al., 2018; Artetxe et al., 2018b; Marie and Fujita, 2018; Ren et al., 2019) typically rely on initialization strategies based on n-gram embeddings. These approaches first construct phrase translation tables leveraging unsupervised cross-lingual n-gram embeddings (Conneau et al., 2017; Artetxe et al., 2018a) to initialize Phrase-Based Statistical Machine Translation (PBSMT) models (Koehn et al., 2003), augmented by language models. However, this conventional approach presents two primary limitations: (1) complex syntactic structures inherent in the original training data are often difficult to reconstruct using n-gram translation tables, and (2) the initial translation tables inevitably contain substantial noise, which tends to be amplified during subsequent processing stages.

To address these challenges, this study proposes a novel retrieval-and-rewriting initialization method for UMT. Specifically, the approach utilizes unsupervised cross-lingual sentence embeddings to retrieve semantically similar sentence pairs from the monolingual corpora of the target languages.

GLUE: A multitask benchmark and analysis platform for natural language understanding . In ASSET: A Dataset for Tuning and Evaluation of Sentence Simplification Models with Multiple Rewriting Transformations Fernando AlvaManchego 1 and Louis Martin 2 , 3 and Antoine Bordes 3 Carolina Scarton 1 and Benoˆıt Sagot 2 and Lucia Specia 1 , 4 1 University of Sheffield, 2 Inria, 3 Facebook AI Research, 4 Imperial College London , , , In order to simplify a sentence, human editors perform multiple rewriting transformations: they split it into several shorter sentences, paraphrase words (i.e. replacing complex words or phrases by simpler synonyms), reorder components, and/or delete information deemed unnecessary. Despite these varied range of possible text alterations, current models for automatic sentence simplification are evaluated using datasets that are focused on a single transformation, such as lexical paraphrasing or splitting. This makes it impossible to understand the ability of simplification models in more realistic settings. To alleviate this limitation, this paper introduces ASSET, a new dataset for assessing sentence simplification in English. ASSET is a crowdsourced multireference corpus where each simplification was produced by executing several rewriting transformations. Through quantitative and qualitative experiments, we show that simplifications in ASSET are better at capturing characteristics of simplicity when compared to other standard evaluation datasets for the task. Furthermore, we motivate the need for developing better methods for automatic evaluation using ASSET, since we show that current popular metrics may not be suitable when multiple simplification transformations are performed. 1 Introduction Sentence Simplification (SS) consists in modifying the content and structure of a sentence to make it easier to understand, while retaining its main idea and most of its original meaning. Simplified texts can benefit nonnative speakers, people suffering from aphasia, dyslexia or autism. They also help language processing tasks, such as parsing, summarisation, and machine translation.

**Contributions**

The primary contributions of this study are delineated as follows:

1.  **Proposal of the Bilingual Attention Language Model (BALM):** We introduce BALM, an attention-based, autoregressive architecture designed to simultaneously learn latent alignments from parallel corpora for cross-lingual word embedding and capture sequential word dependencies.

2.  **Theoretically Grounded Objective Function:** In alignment with the Matrix Language Frame theory (Myers-Scotton, 1997) and the Equivalence Constraint theory (Poplack, 2000; Sankoff, 1998), we formulate an objective function that jointly optimizes cross-entropy loss as a monolingual constraint and quasi-translation loss as a cross-lingual constraint.

3.  **Empirical Validation and Performance:** We demonstrate that BALM effectively learns from bilingual parallel data without requiring codeswitching (CS) data. When fine-tuned on CS data, the model surpasses the state-of-the-art perplexity results on the SEAME dataset. Furthermore, the efficacy of the proposed approach is validated through successful applications in bilingual lexicon induction and language normalization tasks.

**Related Work**

This research is informed by several prior investigations into bilingual language modeling, which can be categorized into two primary domains:

*   **Cross-lingual Correspondence:** A significant body of literature focuses on projecting words from distinct languages into a shared embedding space to establish cross-lingual correspondence. Methodologies include training models on corpora aligned at the sentence level (Zou et al., 2013; Hermann & Blunsom, 2014; Luong et al., 2015) or document level (Vulić & Moens, 2016; Levy et al., 2017). Alternative approaches exploit isomorphic structures (Conneau et al., 2017; Artetxe et al., 2018), dictionaries (Mikolov et al., 2013; Faruqui & Dyer, 2014; Huang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016), shared cognates and vocabularies (Hauer et al., 2017; Smith et al., 2017), or numerals (Artetxe et al., 2017) via ad hoc projection techniques. However, these methods generally fail to explicitly account for sequential word dependencies, resulting in embeddings that do not encode word ordering information. Additionally, while multilingual techniques such as MBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM (Lample & Conneau, 2019) are prominent, they do not explicitly model the syntactic constraints inherent to codeswitching as defined by the Equivalence Constraint theory, thereby underutilizing information that could enhance performance.

*   **Codeswitching Modeling:** A distinct line of inquiry seeks to extend monolingual language modeling techniques to accommodate codeswitched content.

This approach would have also proved challenging in the sense that the instructor had considerable knowledge in creating visually impaired and sighted design teams and no prior knowledge of working with teams involving codesigners with motor, auditory or cognitive disabilities. In future iterations of both courses every effort will be made to leverage relationships with organization for persons with auditory, motor and cognitive disabilities in anticipation of the use of participatory design. Within this report we describe our experience teaching two courses concurrently on accessibility. ### 4. Hierarchy-Aware Global Model

As illustrated in Figure 3, we propose the Hierarchy-Aware Global Model (HiAGM), a framework designed to leverage fine-grained hierarchical information to aggregate label-wise text features. The HiAGM architecture comprises two primary components: a conventional text encoder for processing textual information and a hierarchy-aware structure encoder for capturing hierarchical label correlations. To facilitate hybrid information aggregation, we introduce two variants of the HiAGM: the Multilabel Attention Model (HiAGM-LA) and the Text Feature Propagation Model (HiAGM-TP). 

The HiAGM-LA variant refines label representations via the structure encoder and subsequently generates label-aware text features through a multilabel attention mechanism. Conversely, the HiAGM-TP variant propagates text representations across the entire hierarchical structure, thereby deriving label-wise text features through the fusion of label correlations.

#### 4.1 Prior Hierarchy Information

The taxonomic hierarchy defines the hierarchical relationships among labels. A primary challenge in Hierarchical Text Classification (HTC) lies in effectively utilizing this established structure. While previous studies have employed static methods to incorporate hierarchy paths within pipeline frameworks, hierarchical models, or label assignment models, HiAGM adopts a distinct approach grounded in Bayesian statistical inference. Specifically, HiAGM leverages prior knowledge regarding label correlations derived from the predefined hierarchy and the corpus. We utilize the prior probability of label dependencies as a representation of this hierarchical knowledge.

Consider a hierarchy path $e_{i,j}$ connecting a parent node $v_i$ and a child node $v_j$. The edge feature $f(e_{i,j})$ is characterized by the prior probabilities $P(U_j | U_i)$ and $P(U_i | U_j)$, defined as follows:

$$
P(U_j | U_i) = \frac{P(U_j \cap U_i)}{P(U_i)} = \frac{P(U_j)}{P(U_i)} = \frac{N_j}{P(U_i)}
$$

$$
P(U_i | U_j) = \frac{P(U_i \cap U_j)}{P(U_j)} = \frac{P(U_j)}{P(U_j)} = 1.0 \quad (1)
$$

In these equations, $U_k$ denotes the occurrence of node $v_k$. Consequently, $P(U_j | U_i)$ represents the conditional probability of $v_j$ occurring given the occurrence of $v_i$, while $P(U_j \cap U_i)$ signifies the joint probability of both $v_j$ and $v_i$ occurring simultaneously.

To illustrate the core inquiry, consider the following natural language corpus:

> *cats eat rats rats fear cats mathematicians prove theorems doctors heal wounds*

In accordance with the Chomskyan principle of the autonomy of syntax (Chomsky, 1957), the rules governing syntactic well-formedness can be formulated independently of semantic content and pragmatic context. A canonical example is the sentence *Colorless green ideas sleep furiously*, which is deemed grammatical by native speakers despite being semantically vacuous and pragmatically improbable. This study investigates whether human-like grammaticality judgments can be derived solely from the structural properties of the aforementioned sample. Specifically, we examine whether such a framework can correctly identify sequences such as *cats rats fear* as ungrammatical while accepting sequences like *wounds eat theorems* as grammatical.

We distinguish between two levels of observable structure within a corpus:
1.  **Proximity**: The tendency of words to co-occur within a shared context (e.g., within the same document or sentence).
2.  **Order**: The specific linear sequence in which words appear.

**Definition 1.** Let $L$ be a language defined over a vocabulary $V$. Let $\tilde{L}$ denote the language comprising every possible sequence generated by shuffling the elements of any sequence in $L$. If $V^*$ represents the set of all possible sequences over $V$, and $L$ represents the language instantiated by our corpus, then $L$ is generated by a mixture of contextual and syntactic constraints operating over $V^*$. Our objective is to disentangle syntactic specificities from grammatically irrelevant contextual cues.

The transformation processes mapping $V^*$ to $L$ (via proximity) and $L$ to $\tilde{L}$ (via order) are entirely word-dependent. Consequently, the information utilized by these processes should be encodable within word categories. The following sections provide methodological tools to isolate information pertaining to proximity from that pertaining to order, while relating these categories to established linguistic concepts.

### 3.1 Isolating Syntactic Information

For any given word, the objective is to identify the information associated with each structural type within the corpus and represent it as a partition of the vocabulary into lexical categories:

1.  **Contextual Information**: This encompasses all data unrelated to sentence structure and, by extension, grammaticality. It includes semantic meaning, topical coherence, pragmatic factors, and corpus-specific artifacts. The surface realization of sentence structure constitutes a language-specific combination of word order and morphological markers.
2.  **Syntactic Information**: This refers strictly to information governing sentence structure. Adhering to the requirement of autonomy, this information must be entirely independent of all contextual factors.

## 4. Experiments

The proposed method is initially evaluated on the IWSLT14 English–German (En–De) and IWSLT17 English–French (En–Fr) benchmarks. To further substantiate the efficacy of the approach, experiments are also conducted on the larger WMT14 English–German (En–De) dataset.

### 4.1. Experimental Settings

**Datasets.**  
For the IWSLT14 En–De task, the dataset configuration follows Ranzato et al. (2016), comprising 160,000 sentence pairs for training and 7,584 pairs for validation. The test set is constructed by concatenating the validation subsets (dev2010, dev2012, tst2010, tst2011, and tst2012). For the En–Fr task, the training corpus consists of 236,000 sentence pairs, with 10,263 pairs allocated for validation. The test set is similarly formed by concatenating the validation subsets (dev2010, tst2010, tst2011, tst2012, tst2013, tst2014, and tst2015). Across all IWSLT translation tasks, a joint source–target vocabulary of 10,000 Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) types (Sennrich et al., 2016) is employed.

For the WMT14 En–De task, the training corpus aligns with prior studies (Vaswani et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019a), containing approximately 4.5 million sentence pairs. All data are tokenized using the `tokenizer.pl` script from Moses (Koehn et al., 2007) and segmented into subword units via joint BPE with 32,000 merge operations. The shared source–target vocabulary comprises approximately 37,000 BPE tokens. The newstest2013 corpus serves as the development set, while newstest2014 is utilized as the test set.

**Evaluation Metrics.**  
Consistent with established practices, IWSLT tasks are evaluated using tokenized, case-insensitive BLEU scores. For the WMT14 En–De task, tokenized, case-sensitive BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) are reported.

**Model Configuration.**  
For the IWSLT benchmarks, the `transformer-iwslt` configuration is adopted. This represents a compact model architecture featuring an embedding dimension of 256 and a feed-forward network (FFN) layer dimension of 512.

**4.2 Baseline Methods and Evaluation Metrics**

This section outlines the competing methodologies employed for comparative analysis. The baselines are categorized into unsupervised and supervised approaches.

**Unsupervised Baselines**
*   **Variational Autoencoder (VAE):** We implement a VAE utilizing two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units with a hidden dimensionality of 300. The model is trained on non-parallel corpora by maximizing the variational lower bound of the log-likelihood. During the inference phase, sentences are sampled from the learned variational latent space (Bowman et al., 2016).
*   **Lag VAE:** To address the issue of posterior collapse (Chen et al., 2017), He et al. (2019) propose an aggressive optimization of the VAE inference process through additional update steps. Recognized as a state-of-the-art VAE variant, we utilized the publicly available source code to generate paraphrases for comparison.
*   **Constrained Generation with Metropolis–Hastings (CGMH):** Miao et al. (2019) employ Metropolis–Hastings sampling within the word space for constrained sentence generation. This approach has demonstrated superior performance compared to latent space sampling methods, such as standard VAEs, and is currently considered the state-of-the-art unsupervised paraphrasing technique. We adopted the published implementation to generate comparative paraphrases.

**Supervised Baselines**
We further evaluate our proposed method (UPSA) against established supervised Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) paraphrase generators, including:
*   Residual LSTM (Prakash et al., 2016)
*   VAE-SVGeq (Gupta et al., 2018)
*   Pointer Generator (See et al., 2017)
*   The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017)
*   Decomposable Neural Paraphrase Generator (DNPG) (Li et al., 2019), which is reported as the state-of-the-art supervised paraphrase generator.

To ensure a comprehensive comparison across various paraphrasing settings, we also include domain-adapted supervised models. These models are trained on a source domain and evaluated on a target domain, specifically incorporating:
*   Shallow Fusion (Gulcehre et al., 2015)
*   Multitask Learning (MTL) (Domhan & Hieber, 2017)

**Evaluation Metrics**
Model performance is assessed using standard automatic metrics, specifically BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) and ROUGE (Lin, 2004). However, acknowledging the observation by Sun and Zhou (2012) that BLEU and ROUGE fail to adequately capture the diversity between generated and original sentences, we prioritize the iBLEU metric. iBLEU incorporates a penalty based on the similarity to the original sentence, a methodology also adopted by Li et al. (2019). In addition to automatic evaluation, human assessment is conducted to validate the results, as detailed in subsequent sections.

**4.3 Implementation Details**

The proposed method relies on unsupervised language modeling, encompassing both forward and backward passes. This is realized through a two-layer LSTM architecture with 300 hidden units. The model is trained specifically on non-parallel sentences for each respective dataset.

The survey was conducted in the 1990s across 37 countries and had approximately 3,000 respondents. The respondents were instructed to describe a situation or event in which they felt a particular emotion, in the form of a statement — a short text of a couple of sentences (2.37 on average). Here is an example of a complete statement: I had the window open and the door was shut so that the kitten would not go out. My partner came in and started talking about something and I forgot about the window and suddenly I saw the kitten hanging from the window frame. I was rigid with fright till I got hold of her. The dataset contains 7,666 such statements, which include 18,146 sentences, 449,060 running words. Of the 5,732 concepts contained in SenticNet, 2,729 were found in ISEAR. Since only for those concepts we have important features extracted from ISEAR, only those 2,729 concepts were assigned emotion labels in this work. The statements in the ISEAR dataset are arranged in a table, with 40 additional numeric or categorical columns that give various kinds of information for each statement. Of the 40 data columns, we carried out experiments with just 16 columns, organized in groups covering: background data related to the respondent (age, gender, religion, father's occupation, mother's occupation, country); general data related to the emotion felt (intensity, timing, longevity); physiological data (ergotropic arousals, trophotropic arousals, felt change in temperature); expressive behavior data (movement, nonverbal activity, paralinguistic activity); and the emotion felt in the situation described in the statement. The majority of these parameters are numerical scores with a small number of discrete values expressing different degrees of the parameter. For example, the values for ergotropic arousal are: 1 for change in breathing, 2 for heart beating faster, 3 for muscles tensing/trembling, 4 for perspiring/moist hands; the values for trophotropic arousal are: 1 for lump in throat, 2 for stomach troubles, 3 for crying/sobbing. One of the columns gives the name of the emotion felt. Seven values are used: ANGER, DISGUST, FEAR, GUILT, JOY, SADNESS, and SHAME. This set of seven emotions is different from our target set of six basic emotions used in WNA lists (ANGER, DISGUST, FEAR, JOY, SADNESS, and SURPRISE).

Furthermore, a fundamental distinction exists between the structural constraints of the Essay dataset and the CDCP dataset: while propositions in the former are restricted to at most one outgoing edge, those in the latter exhibit a variable degree of connectivity, with approximately 200 propositions possessing two or more outgoing edges. Consequently, developing models capable of handling these less restrictive, non-tree argument structures is of significant importance. Despite this necessity, the domain remains underexplored, with only a limited number of prior investigations (e.g., Niculae et al., 2017; Galassi et al., 2018).

This paper introduces a novel framework for non-tree argument mining. In contrast to previous approaches, our work emphasizes an effective encoding mechanism for propositions and a graph-based parsing technique designed specifically for non-tree structures. Given sentence or clause spans within an argument, the proposed model jointly predicts proposition types, inter-propositional edges, and edge labels by leveraging two primary architectural components:

1.  **Task-Specific Parameterization (TSP):** This module serves as an effective encoding step for proposition sequences. Built upon a shared encoder, TSP employs two distinct attention-to-encoder layers to maintain task-specific representations: one dedicated to proposition type classification and the other to edge prediction (including edge labels). This design is predicated on the hypothesis that representations for proposition types and edges should be derived independently. This separation is particularly critical in non-tree structures like CDCP, where the correlation between proposition types and edge configurations is weaker than in tree-structured datasets (such as Essay), where every premise proposition is constrained to a single outgoing edge.

2.  **Proposition-Level Biaffine Attention (PLBA):** Following the encoding phase, this component is utilized to predict non-tree edges. While biaffine attention has recently been applied to syntactic and semantic token-to-token dependency parsing (Dozat & Manning, 2017, 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019a, 2019b), we extend this mechanism to model proposition-to-proposition dependencies.

Experimental evaluation on the CDCP dataset demonstrates that the proposed model yields performance improvements over existing baselines. Furthermore, ablation analyses confirm that the TSP module effectively captures task-specific information. The experiments utilize the CDCP corpus (Park & Cardie, 2018; Niculae et al., 2017), which comprises 731 arguments. The dataset includes five proposition categories (32 Reference, 746 Fact, 1,026 Testimony, 2,160 Value, and 815 Policy) and two argumentative edge types (1,307 Reason and 46 Evidence).

**Summary of Principal Component Analysis on Final Examination Data (2013w and 2014w)**

Exploratory analysis utilizing Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was conducted to determine whether specific question types or learning objectives accounted for the majority of variance in the final examinations for the 2013w and 2014w terms. The results indicate that no small subset of questions captures the vast majority of the variance; rather, a significant proportion of the total components is required to explain the data distribution.

Specifically, to account for 80% of the variability, 10 principal components (PCs) were necessary for the 2013w exam and 11 PCs for the 2014w exam, out of a total of 20. A more granular breakdown reveals the following thresholds for cumulative variance:
*   **2013w:** 8 PCs for ≥70%, 10 PCs for ≥80%, and 14 PCs for ≥90%.
*   **2014w:** 9 PCs for ≈70%, 11 PCs for ≈80%, and 15 PCs for 90%.

While the first principal component (PC1) typically accounts for a substantial portion of variation in such analyses, the loadings for PC1 in both terms were distributed relatively evenly among four specific questions: query plans, INL, 2PMMS, and GMEMS. These four items, which all involve computational tasks, contributed most significantly to PC1. The first two questions required decision-making regarding indexes, joins, and metadata, while the latter two involved sorting calculations with optimization elements. However, several unrelated questions also contributed nontrivially to this component. Furthermore, subsequent loadings and higher-order principal components did not reveal any discernible common patterns among the remaining questions.

These findings contrast with literature suggesting that students may identify a narrow subset of topics to maximize grades while minimizing study effort. The data demonstrates that achieving a high cumulative variance requires a broad engagement with the material, as no limited set of topics dominates the examination structure.

This blend allows software developers to find a middle ground by adapting and assembling existing components, minimizing the need for developing entirely new software. A clear example of this cooperation is evident in modern websites, where the ModelView-Controller architectural pattern is widely employed. The ModelView-Controller (MVC) is a software architectural pattern commonly used in the design of interactive applications, providing a systematic way to organize and structure code. The pattern separates an application into three interconnected components: model, view, and controller. The model represents the application’s data structure and business logic, managing data and rules. The view is responsible for displaying the user interface; it shows data to the user and sends user commands to the controller. The controller serves as an intermediary between the model and the view. It processes user input received from the view, interacts with the model to retrieve or update data, and determines the appropriate view for presenting the response. Many practical web application frameworks, such as Django, have already implemented the MVC pattern. In this setup, the application is divided into three parts: the model handles the data structure, the view displays the data on web pages, and the controller manages the business logic, facilitating interaction between the model and the view. Adding a broker pattern to MVC architectures can improve the system’s scalability and flexibility when applicable and/or necessary. The broker acts as a middleman that manages communication between different parts of the application, helping to handle more data and complex operations efficiently. Leveraging these existing frameworks enables developers to concentrate on crafting the specific logic relevant to the website rather than reinventing the wheel. The beauty of this approach lies in the ability to swiftly piece together solutions by extending and adapting the available frameworks. By doing so, developers streamline the development process, enhance efficiency, and capitalize on the collective wisdom embedded in proven frameworks, thereby fostering innovation in a more focused and resourceefficient manner.

Formally, the $n$-th block of the encoder is defined as:

$$ B_n^e = \text{BLOCK}_e(B_{n-1}^e), \quad (5) $$

where $\text{BLOCK}_e(\cdot)$ denotes the block function, which iterates the layer function $F(\cdot)$ $M_n$ times. Specifically, $B_n^e = H_{n, M_n}^e$, where the intermediate representations are computed recursively as:

$$ H_{n,l}^e = F(H_{n,l-1}^e; \Theta_{n,l}^e) + H_{n,l-1}^e, $$
$$ H_{n,0}^e = B_{n-1}^e, $$

for $l \in \{1, 2, \dots, M_n\}$. Here, $H_{n,l}^e$ and $\Theta_{n,l}^e$ represent the hidden representation and parameters of the $l$-th layer within the $n$-th block, respectively.

The decoder operates analogously; however, the layer function $G(\cdot)$ is applied only once per block. The $n$-th decoder block is formulated as:

$$ B_n^d = \text{BLOCK}_d(B_{n-1}^d, B_n^e) = G(B_{n-1}^d, B_n^e; \Theta_n^d) + B_{n-1}^d. \quad (7) $$

In this architecture, each decoder block attends to its corresponding encoder block. Building upon the work of He et al. Unlike approaches that restrict decoder attention to the final representation of the encoder stack, our method facilitates direct attention from the decoder to lower-level encoder layers.

Emotion recognition has long been a focal point of interdisciplinary research, spanning machine learning, signal processing, and social and cognitive psychology. Foundational work by Ekman established the correlation between facial expressions and human emotion, introducing standardized methods for their measurement. Subsequent research by Datcu and Rothkrantz expanded the domain by incorporating acoustic and visual cues. However, conversational emotion recognition has gained significant traction only recently, driven by the availability of large-scale public datasets derived from social media platforms and scripted media, such as films and television programs.

Current methodologies primarily rely on deep learning algorithms to perform contextual modeling in both textual and multimodal settings. Notable contributions include Poria et al., who utilized recurrent neural networks for multimodal recognition, and subsequent studies that employed party and global state modeling to capture emotional dynamics. Additionally, recent approaches have integrated external knowledge bases with transformer architectures to improve recognition accuracy.

In this work, we utilize the RoBERTa model to generate context-independent, utterance-level feature vectors. Specifically, we fine-tune the RoBERTa Large architecture for emotion label prediction based on utterance transcripts. This model adheres to the original BERT Large structure, comprising 24 layers, 16 self-attention heads per layer, and a hidden dimension of 1024, resulting in a total parameter count of approximately 355 million.

**Training and Inference Procedures**

Given an input tuple comprising an image ($I$), dialogue history ($S$), and domain context ($D$), alongside a candidate set $\{c_1, \dots, c_N\}$, the inference process selects the predicted utterance $c_i$ that maximizes the scoring function $s(I, S, D, c_i)$. During the training phase, the model is optimized to maximize the log-likelihood of the ground-truth responses by applying a softmax function over the computed scores. Training is conducted using minibatches of 500 examples; for each instance within a batch, the ground-truth responses of the remaining examples serve as negative samples. In the final human evaluation stage, the model generates responses by considering the entire set of training candidates (356,000 candidates in the reported experiments).

**4.2 Generative Models**

**Dialogue Decoder Architecture**
The output of the image encoder is processed through a final linear projection layer with dimensions $2048 \times 300$, aligning it with the token embedding dimension of the dialogue decoder. This projected representation is appended as an additional token to the end of the Transformer encoder's output sequence. To incorporate stylistic constraints, the style descriptor is prepended to the dialogue history, allowing it to be encoded directly by the dialogue encoder. The system is subsequently treated as a standard sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) Transformer for response generation.

**Training and Inference Configuration**
The generative model is trained with a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of $1 \times 10^{-4}$ using the Adam optimizer. Hyperparameters are selected based on performance on the validation set. During inference, response generation employs beam search with a beam width of 2, augmented by trigram blocking to prevent repetition.

**5. Experimental Evaluation**

The proposed models are evaluated on the Image-Chat and IGC datasets utilizing both automatic metrics and human assessments. The analysis encompasses a comparative study of various module and architectural configurations, as well as ablation studies designed to quantify the contribution of individual model inputs.

**5.1 Automatic Evaluation on Image-Chat**

We first evaluate various configurations of the TransResNetRet model, comparing them against a baseline information retrieval system that ranks candidates based on weighted word overlap with the input message. Performance is measured using Recall at 1 and 5 (R@1/100 and R@5/100). In this retrieval setting, each test sample is ranked against a pool of 100 candidates, consisting of the ground-truth label and 99 randomly selected distractors from the test set. It is noted that, distinct from the automatic evaluation, the human evaluation protocol utilizes the complete set of training candidates.

Therefore, as MoEL, we keep BLEU mostly as reference. Following MoEL and Rashkin et al. , we rely on humanevaluated metrics: Human Ratings. Firstly, we randomly sample four instances of each of the 32 emotion labels from the test set, resulting in a total of 128 instances, compared to the 100 instances used for the evaluation of MoEL. Next, we ask three human annotators to rate each subsampled model response on a scale from 1 to 5 (best score) on three distinct attributes: empathy (How much emotional understanding does the response show?), relevance (How much topical relevance does the response have to the context?), and fluency (How much linguistic clarity does the response have?). Scores across 128 samples and three annotators are averaged to obtain the final rating. Human A/B Test. Given two models A and B — in our case MoEL and MIME (our model), respectively — we ask three human annotators to pick the model with the best response for each of the 128 subsampled test instances. The annotators can select a Tie if the responses from both models are deemed equal. The final verdict on each instance is determined by majority voting. In case no two annotators agree on a selection – that is all three annotators reached three distinct conclusions: MoEL, MIME, and Tie – we bring in a fourth annotator. Sentiment analysis encompasses a diverse range of applications, including the assessment of film popularity based on online reviews and the identification of preferred consumer electronics or musical compositions. Fundamentally, sentiment classification involves assigning textual documents, such as product reviews, to polarity categories—positive, negative, or neutral—based on their subjective content.

Effective sentiment analysis relies heavily on commonsense knowledge, defined as the foundational understanding acquired through human experience. While automatic commonsense reasoning typically employs ontologies and algorithms ranging from predicate logic to machine learning, the integration of such knowledge into sentiment analysis remains underexplored. Commonsense reasoning is particularly critical for resolving context-dependent ambiguities; for instance, the descriptor "small" conveys a negative sentiment when applied to a hotel room but a positive one regarding a queue at a post office. Similarly, the phrase "go read the book" implies a positive recommendation in a literary review but a negative critique in a film review.

To address these challenges, we propose a commonsense knowledge-based approach capable of detecting implicit sentiment. This method analyzes concepts that do not explicitly convey polarity but are implicitly linked to sentiment-bearing concepts. By operating at the concept level, this approach preserves the semantic integrity of multi-word expressions, thereby outperforming existing models. The methodology utilizes a concept parser to decompose text into clauses and subsequently into discrete concepts, which are then mapped onto a vector space of commonsense knowledge.

Furthermore, the efficacy of machine learning models is significantly influenced by feature selection. A feature is deemed relevant if its removal results in a degradation of classification performance; conversely, irrelevant or redundant features can impair model accuracy. In high-dimensional datasets containing thousands of features, it is common to encounter a substantial proportion of non-informative attributes. The inclusion of such irrelevant and noisy features often leads to performance deterioration. Consequently, the identification and elimination of redundant features—detectable through correlation measures—are essential for optimizing classification performance.

The findings from the weakly supervised experiments yield three primary conclusions:
1.  **Metric Significance:** Both action segmentation metrics—overall label accuracy and step label accuracy—are critical for assessing a model's ability to distinguish meaningful actions from background noise.
2.  **Inductive Bias of Ordering:** Step constraints derived from canonical procedural orderings provide a robust inductive bias for unsupervised step induction. Historical approaches necessitate these constraints; without them, models such as the HSMM fail to identify specific steps, instead learning to capture general diversity across video instances.
3.  **Necessity of Narration:** Ordering supervision alone is insufficient to enable models to surpass the performance of a simple baseline that assigns labels based strictly on ordering (ordered uniform). The inclusion of narrative data is essential for achieving superior segmentation results.

Subsequently, we delineate the methodologies employed to compute word centrality within the graph $G$. Initially, a transition probability matrix $T$ is constructed according to the following formulation:

$$ T_{i,j} = \frac{D_{i,j}}{\sum_{j} D_{i,j}} \quad (13) $$

A fundamental indegree centrality metric is defined as:

$$ \text{score}_i = \sum_{j} T_{j,i} \quad (14) $$

Alternatively, the TextRank algorithm (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004), which draws inspiration from the PageRank algorithm (Page et al., 1999), computes the indegree centrality of source words iteratively via a Markov chain process:

$$ \text{score}_i = \sum_{j} T_{j,i} \cdot \text{score}_j \quad (15) $$

In this context, $\text{score}_i$ represents the indegree centrality score for vertex $V_i$, with initial scores uniformly set to $1/|V|$. A stationary indegree centrality distribution is obtained by iteratively computing $\text{score} = T \cdot \text{score}$; in our implementation, this process is limited to a maximum of three iterations. Conversely, outdegree centrality quantifies the extent to which a word $i$ contributes to other nodes within the directed graph:

$$ \text{score}_i = \sum_{j} D_{i,j} \quad (16) $$

Following the computation of these metrics, the source word centrality scores are integrated into the decoding procedure.

### 4.3 Guided Copy Mechanism

The underlying rationale for this mechanism is that word centrality serves as an indicator of source word salience. Consequently, these scores provide prior knowledge to guide the copy module toward focusing on significant source terms. We incorporate the word centrality score as an additional input to derive the copy distribution as follows:

$$ \alpha_{t,i} = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{(W_s s_t)^T (W_h h_i + w_p \text{score}_i)}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) $$

Here, $\text{score}_i$ denotes either the indegree or outdegree centrality score for the $i$-th word in the source text. This formulation may be characterized as centrality-aware dot-product attention. Furthermore, to ensure that salient source words attract sufficient encoder-decoder attention, we introduce a centrality-aware auxiliary loss function. This loss encourages consistency between the aggregate copy distribution and the word centrality distribution, utilizing the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence:

$$ \mathcal{L} = \sum_{t} \log P(y_t) + \lambda \cdot \text{KL}\left( \frac{1}{t} \sum_{t} \alpha_t, \text{score} \right) \quad (18) $$

The application of topic modeling to criminal data, often termed crime topic modeling, facilitates an ecological understanding of crime patterns. Building upon prior scholarship, recent investigations utilizing Brazilian police datasets have employed unsupervised natural language processing (NLP) techniques to cluster criminal incidents. The objective of these studies is to identify policing strategies—specifically traditional, Problem-Oriented Policing (POP), or hotspot approaches—best suited to distinct urban areas. In this methodology, researchers present representative samples of crime clusters to law enforcement officers, who then recommend appropriate tactical responses. However, these studies do not report on the subsequent adoption or efficacy of the recommended strategies.

Concurrently, the complexity of models applied to criminal data has increased, with emerging research focusing on the direct extraction of specific information from unstructured police narratives. For instance, Karystianis et al. utilized rule-based information extraction to examine correlations between mental health and domestic violence typologies. Consequently, despite its effectiveness, the high implementation barrier associated with rule-based systems likely precludes their routine adoption.

To effectively support POP, NLP algorithms must be developed to assist in the characterization of criminal events. This characterization may involve identifying known attributes, such as the presence of alcohol or the nature of victim-offender relationships, or discovering latent characteristics through unsupervised learning. Although existing research establishes a foundation for exploring the utility of NLP in this domain, no current studies appear to focus specifically on characterizing crime events for crime prevention purposes, particularly within the constraints of low-resource environments that do not require high-performance computing infrastructure.

Therefore, future research aimed at enabling POP should prioritize the examination of how existing NLP models can be leveraged against police-generated free text data in low-resource settings to enhance event characterization. Given the diverse array of tasks and responsibilities inherent to policing, it is plausible that NLP methodologies could support a broad spectrum of operational processes associated with these functions.

**3.3 Additional Supportive Contents**

Sentences containing information of the lowest relevance—comprising unverifiable or hypothetical facts, opinionated assertions, future projections, and historical background—are classified as distantly related content. 

*   **Historical Event (D1):** This category temporally precedes the main event by months or years. It comprises past occurrences that may have precipitated the current situation or bear an indirect relationship to the main event or the subjects of the news article.
*   **Anecdotal Event (D2):** This includes events involving specific participants that are difficult to verify. Such content may encompass fictional scenarios or personal accounts from unidentified individuals, often intended to exaggerate the situation.
*   **Evaluation (D3):** This category introduces reactions from immediate participants, experts, or notable figures. These statements are inherently opinionated and may include explicit viewpoints from the author or the news source. They typically aim to elucidate the social or political implications of the main event or to assess the current situation, often utilizing statements from influential figures to selectively emphasize specific perspectives.
*   **Expectation (D4):** This category speculates on the potential consequences of the main or contextual events. While essentially opinion-based, these statements carry significant implications, as the author attempts to evaluate the current situation by projecting possible future developments.

**3.4 Speech vs. Not Speech**

Concurrent with discourse profiling annotations, sentences containing direct quotations or paraphrased comments articulated by human speakers are identified and labeled as "Speech." A binary classification of "Speech" versus "Not Speech" is assigned to each sentence independently of the aforementioned eight schematic discourse roles. It is noted that sentences classified as Speech may be annotated with any of the eight news discourse roles based on their content; however, it is anticipated that Speech sentences will more frequently serve roles such as Evaluation and Expectation.

**3.5 Modifications to the Van Dijk Theory**

Van Dijk's original theory was derived from case studies of specific news reports. To accommodate broader applications across diverse news domains and sources, several minor modifications to the original theoretical framework have been implemented.

This paper presents an experience report evaluating the efficacy of nonmajor peer mentoring as a strategy to enhance student satisfaction and success rates within a Computer Science I (CS1) course at a small liberal arts institution. The study addresses the critical challenge of improving overall student outcomes, with a specific focus on underrepresented populations and nonmajors, for whom the sense of inclusion and belonging is a pivotal determinant of academic achievement.

While CS1 courses serve a diverse cohort of majors, minors, and nonmajors, traditional support mechanisms—such as departmental tutoring and recitation sessions—are predominantly led by CS majors. This paper interrogates whether such an approach inadvertently exacerbates a sense of exclusion among nonmajor students, potentially hindering their retention and performance. In response, we propose and analyze a framework for nonmajor peer mentoring designed to augment, rather than replace, existing support infrastructure.

The report is structured as follows: First, we review relevant literature to contextualize our approach within current pedagogical strategies. Second, we define the operational framework of nonmajor peer mentoring and delineate the necessary infrastructure for its successful implementation, drawing upon direct student feedback that motivated this initiative. Third, we discuss the challenges inherent in establishing such a program and examine how student perceptions can manifest as barriers to success if left unaddressed. Finally, we present an analysis of the program's impact, demonstrating that the integration of nonmajor peer mentors significantly improves both the academic outcomes and the sense of belonging for nonmajor students in the CS1 curriculum.

For SLSTM, it is the simplest approach to segment pooling and alignment: at training time feed the ground truth segments (as opposed to the predicted segments) the segment pooler (step 3 in Figure 1 ). This gives us a onetoone alignment of "predicted" (forced) segments and ground truth segments. This is opposed to only using the predicted segments as the bounds for segment pooler. Exploration. Employing only teacher forcing does not allow the segment labeler to learn how to recover from errors in segmentation. The mechanism for allowing the model to explore incorrect segmentations is to align the predicted segments with overlapping ground truth segments at training time, and treat the all aligned ground truth labels as correct. While many alignments are possible, we use the one presented in Figure 2 . This manytomany alignment ensures that every groundtruth segment is mapped to at least one predicted segment and every predicted segment is mapped to at least one ground truth segment. We can additionally schedule teacher forcing. At the beginning, when the segmentation prediction network performs poorly, the model pools over only ground truth segment bounds, allowing it to learn the cleanest topic representations. However, as training progresses and the segmentation accuracy begins to converge, we switch from pooling over ground truth segments to aligning predicted and ground truth segment. In this way, the segment pooler learns to be robust to segmentation errors. 3.5 Joint Training To jointly train the model, we use a multitask loss, L ( X, y ; θ ) = α · L seg ( X, y seg ; θ seg )+ (1 − α ) · L cls ( X, y cls ; θ cls , aligner ) , where y seg are the labels for the segment prediction LSTM and y cls are segment labels. In addition, we pass in an aligner , which determines how to align the predicted segments with the ground truth segments to compute the loss, and either teacher forces the model or allows it to explore. 4 Experimental Setup We follow the experimental procedure of Arnold et al. to evaluate SLSTM for the tasks of document segmentation and segment labeling. 4.1 Datasets WikiSection . Arnold et al. introduced the WikiSection dataset, which contains Wikipedia articles across two languages (English and German) and domains (Cities and Diseases). Articles are segmented using the Wikipedia section structure.

Model choice, size, and initialization are summarized in Figure A1 . 4 Training Procedure We first outline the training procedure and then detail several guardrail mechanisms used during training to prevent the Summarizer from learning pathological writing strategies. Figure A2 presents training plots of a Summary Loop model and interpretation of the different learning phases. 4.1 Training with Reinforcement Learning We use Reinforcement Learning to train the Summarizer component (agent), such that it achieves high summary score (reward). Note that the Coverage and Fluency models are frozen, and their weights are not trained. We make this choice as allowing Fluency and Coverage models to evolve could enable the models to coordinate and cheat. We use the Selfcritical sequence training (SCST) method, as it has been shown to perform well on similar text generation tasks optimizing BLEU for image captioning or ROUGE scores in summarization. In SCST, the Summarizer is used to produce two summaries of document D : a greedy summary ˆ S , using a decoding strategy that always picks the most likely next word, and a sampled summary S s , picking the next word in the summary by sampling from the word distribution. Summaries are scored using the Summary Loop: ˆ R = SummaryScore ( D, ˆ S ) R s = SummaryScore ( D, S s ) Then we minimize the following loss: L = ( ˆ R − R s ) i =0 log p ( w s i | w s 1 , ..., w s i − 1 , D ) Where p ( w s i | ... ) represent the probability of the i th word conditioned on previously generated word, according to the model. Intuitively, if R s > ˆ R , minimizing L maximizes the likelihood of the sampled sequence — which is desired because it outperformed the greedy summary — and increases expected reward of the model. 4.2 Training guard rails During training, the Summarizer model learns pathological summarization strategies. We build training guard rails to detect the pathological behavior and penalize the model during training. A guard rail has a binary effect: if a pathology is detected in a summary, its Summary Score is reduced by a penalty amount δ . We use δ = 2 for all experiments.

**Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)**
For the CNN architecture, we utilize 500 filters with kernel sizes of [2, 3, 4, 5]. L2 regularization is applied to all filter weights with a coefficient of $10^{-3}$. The hyperbolic tangent (tanh) function serves as the activation function, and dropout is applied to the encoder output with a probability of 0.5. These hyperparameters are maintained for variants incorporating ELMo. For BERT-based variants, the configuration is adjusted to include 200 filters of sizes [2, 3, 4, 5] and an initial learning rate of 0.01; the learning rate is subsequently reduced by a factor of 0.9 upon detection of a plateau in the validation F1 score.

Regarding the SemEval dataset, we adopt the hyperparameters established by Nguyen and Grishman (2015). Training is conducted using the Adadelta optimizer with an initial learning rate of 1.0 over 50 epochs. L2 regularization is applied with a coefficient of $10^{-5}$. The model employs 150 filters of sizes [2, 3, 4, 5], alongside embedding and encoder dropout rates of 0.5 and a word dropout rate of 0.04. For BERT variants on this dataset, the learning rate is similarly reduced by a factor of 0.9 when the validation F1 score plateaus.

**Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM)**
For training on the TACRED dataset, we follow the hyperparameters specified by Zhang et al. (2017). The Adagrad optimizer is employed with an initial learning rate of 0.01. Training proceeds for 30 epochs, with the learning rate gradually decreased by a factor of 0.9 commencing from the 15th epoch. Word dropout is set to 0.04, and recurrent dropout is set to 0.5. The BiLSTM architecture comprises two layers with a hidden dimension of 500 per direction. For variants utilizing ELMo and BERT, the learning rate is reduced by a factor of 0.9 whenever the validation F1 score plateaus.

For the SemEval dataset, the configuration is modified to utilize two BiLSTM layers with a hidden dimension of 300 per direction. Additionally, embedding and encoder dropout rates are set to 0.5.

**Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN)**
For both the TACRED and SemEval datasets, we adopt the hyperparameters defined by Zhang et al. (2018). The Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer is used with an initial learning rate of 0.3, which is reduced by a factor of 0.9 upon each plateau in the validation F1 score. A dropout rate of 0.5 is applied between all GCN layers except the final one. Furthermore, word dropout is set to 0.04, while embedding and encoder dropout rates are set to 0.5. Consistent with the original authors, path-centric pruning is applied with $K=1$.

There are two main ways to understand the importance of historical experience in our daily lives.

The first approach is to disregard history altogether. This perspective is based on the belief that history never truly repeats itself. People in the past made decisions under circumstances very different from those we face today. The world has changed drastically‚Äîtechnology, information, transportation, medicine, and human rights have all evolved since earlier times. Because of these changes, we cannot simply apply old solutions to modern problems.

The second approach is to learn from historical experience and apply its lessons to our lives today. This viewpoint is supported by the fact that similar events and trends have occurred at different times and places throughout history. For example, almost every country in medieval Europe and Asia went through a period of feudalism. Eventually, their leaders realized feudalism was not the best way to govern and abolished it. Thanks to this historical lesson, it‚Äôs unlikely that anyone will try to reestablish feudalism today. Another example is the history of conquest. Many famous conquerors‚Äîsuch as Alexander the Great, Caesar, Genghis Khan, Napoleon, and Hitler‚Äîbuilt vast empires with powerful armies. However, over time, each of these empires eventually fell. Sometimes their enemies united against them; other times, the empires collapsed after the leader‚Äôs death. In the end, none of these empires survived, and today‚Äôs borders are based on nations rather than the old conquests.

I believe the second approach is better. Many of the institutions we value‚Äîlike human rights, property rights, education, and medicine‚Äîexist because we have learned from historical experience. If our ancestors had ignored history, modern society as we know it wouldn‚Äôt exist. We might lack modern healthcare and education, and perhaps even still live in a world with slavery. Therefore, I think it is essential to study history carefully and apply its lessons to our current lives.

Context-based reading comprehension and question answering have garnered significant attention within the natural language processing (NLP) community, particularly following the release of the large-scale SQuAD dataset by Rajpurkar et al. (2016), which comprises over 100,000 questions derived from Wikipedia paragraphs. Prior research has successfully reframed several traditional NLP tasks as question-answering problems, including textual entailment (McCann et al., 2018), entity-relation extraction (Li et al., 2019), and coreference resolution (Wu et al., 2020). However, unlike these domains, bridging anaphora resolution lacks extensive large-scale training datasets. To address this limitation, we formulate questions in a manner that leverages existing question-answering corpora, such as SQuAD, which necessitate commonsense reasoning. Furthermore, we generate a large-scale training dataset of "quasi-bridging" instances and demonstrate its efficacy as a pretraining corpus for bridging anaphora resolution.

Aligning with the perspective proposed by Gardner et al. (2019), which posits question answering as a format rather than a distinct task, our work functions as a specific probing mechanism to evaluate a model's capacity to resolve bridging anaphora within a given context. Bridging anaphora resolution shares conceptual similarities with the Winograd Schema Challenge (WSC), as both require contextual understanding to identify antecedents. Nevertheless, the search space for antecedents in bridging anaphora is substantially larger than in WSC. In WSC, anaphors and their antecedents typically reside within the same sentence, whereas bridging pairs often necessitate cross-sentence inference. For instance, in the ISNotes dataset, only approximately 26% of anaphors have antecedents within the same sentence, while 23% have antecedents located more than two sentences away. Recent work by Kocijan et al. (2019) utilized heuristics to generate a large-scale WSC-like dataset, reporting that models pretrained on this data achieved superior performance on various WSC benchmarks after fine-tuning on small in-domain datasets. We observe analogous performance patterns in the domain of bridging anaphora resolution (see Section 5.3).

### 3. BARQA: A Question-Answering System for Bridging Anaphora Resolution

This section provides a detailed description of our question-answering system, designated as BARQA, specifically designed for bridging anaphora resolution.

Our plans for future years include increased emphasis on collecting postassessments so matched data can be obtained for a larger percentage of the student body, more extensive validation of the CFC test so it can be published for the benefit of the wider computer science education community, and identification (or if no suitable test exists, development) of an assessment that can be used to measure if students’ math skills have changed significantly between the beginning and end of the ICT course. Additionally, as the ICT course continues to be adopted across Louisiana, a growing number of students will have taken ICT in eighth or ninth grade. This large pool of student participants could be tracked longitudinally, and compared with control groups from the same schools, to determine if participation in the ICT course has had an effect on their performance in subsequent math courses. Furthermore, as more math and science teachers are trained to teach ICT, we will be able to develop a bank of coding activities for math and science courses, which could then be made available to teachers via an online portal. The teachers would then be able to adapt and incorporate these coding activities into their regular lessons. Using this population of students and teachers trained in our ICT course, we will be able to study whether the incorporation of functional programming into math and science curricula promotes deeper understanding of those subjects. While the need for teaching computational thinking is already well established, there is still controversy about whether programming should be included or not, or, as Denning calls it, the clash between Traditional CT and New CT. Courses such as ECS or CSP are examples of New CT, but there is not much available in terms of courses that focus on Traditional CT. Due to its capacity for automation and formalization, programming is a natural vehicle for learning computational thinking. While Python and JavaScript courses are relatively available, they do not usually focus on CT. Instead, they follow traditional syntaxoriented approaches to teaching computing, with few connections to math and science. Those courses are more useful for students aspiring to be software developers than for the general student population. We have presented an alternative approach. We have described the design and implementation of a secondary Computational Thinking course based on programming with connections to science and math.

**Proposal for Data-Related Minor Programs**

In response to administrative concerns regarding the rigorous nature of the Data Science major and its potential inaccessibility to the average student, an ad hoc committee was convened to develop alternative pathways. Consequently, the committee designed two distinct minor programs: a Data Analytics Minor and a Data Science Minor. These programs are intended to provide data-related training for students interested in the field who do not intend to pursue a full major.

**Data Analytics Minor**
This program is tailored for students with average quantitative and analytical aptitudes who seek to acquire proficiency in data analysis software. The curriculum comprises the following core components:
*   **STAT 245, DATA 101, and DATA 202:** These courses follow the previously established descriptions.
*   **IS 175 (Productivity Software), recently rebranded as DATA 175:** This hands-on course introduces database concepts via Microsoft Access, spreadsheet management via Microsoft Excel, and programming fundamentals via Microsoft Visual Basic (VB). VB is utilized to process data within Access and Excel and to facilitate data transfer between these applications. For students new to programming, this course offers a more accessible introduction than a standard introductory computer science (CS1) course.
*   **Introductory Computing Options:** Students may select from CS 104, CS 106, or CS 108, all of which utilize Python to teach programming to diverse audiences:
    *   *CS 104:* A two-hour course designed for Engineering majors.
    *   *CS 106:* A four-hour course designed for Science majors.
    *   *CS 108:* A four-hour CS1 course open to all majors.

Within the Data Analytics Minor, the introductory Python computing course and STAT 245 (which includes extensive R programming) represent the most challenging academic requirements.

**Data Science Minor**
This program is designed for students possessing strong quantitative and analytical skills who are majoring in disciplines other than Data Science. The curriculum distinguishes itself through a more rigorous computing sequence:
*   **Computing Sequence:** While Data Analytics minors begin with the Access/Excel/VB course and conclude with a less rigorous CS1 course, Data Science minors follow a standard introductory sequence comprising CS1 and CS2.
*   **Differentiation:** The primary distinction lies in the terminal computing requirement; Data Analytics minors conclude with a foundational CS1 course, whereas Data Science minors complete a more advanced CS2 course.

Collectively, these two minors offer structured, tiered options for students seeking data competency without the full commitment of a Data Science major.

When someone is born, they become part of a family and a social class. Some people manage to succeed in life even if their parents are poor. This leads us to ask: do we have to take risks in order to succeed?

First of all, we become stronger in life when we try new things. In my opinion, it‚Äôs by doing something new‚Äîby taking a risk‚Äîthat we might make mistakes. And it‚Äôs through making mistakes that we learn, grow, and eventually succeed. However, the amount of risk a person can take isn‚Äôt the same for everyone. If your parents are wealthy, I can‚Äôt help but think that you can afford to take more risks, since your family could support you financially if things go wrong. If you have to face the consequences on your own, you‚Äôre likely to take fewer risks. To sum up, I‚Äôd say that having a supportive family background gives you more opportunities to succeed because it allows you to take more chances.

On the other hand, I don‚Äôt think you are required to take risks to succeed in life. Some people are very intelligent and hardworking; they attend prestigious universities and work their way up in companies without taking major risks. These individuals achieve success through dedication and effort rather than risk-taking.

In conclusion, success means something different to everyone. While this essay has mainly focused on financial success, we should ask ourselves if money is the only way to measure success in life. Of course, it isn‚Äôt.
