To identify distinct patterns in student performance, a data-driven grouping strategy was employed using a cluster-based algorithm to label individual student programs. Specifically, Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Models (BGMM) were utilized to determine the optimal number of clusters that best separate the data. This approach employs an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm to minimize the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). Within this framework, clusters deemed statistically insignificant are assigned a weight of zero and effectively excluded from the final model. The analysis was conducted on static representations of students' block-based programs captured at the conclusion of each activity. These representations were derived to ensure compatibility with the clustering algorithm and to facilitate subsequent analytical procedures.

A key idea in our metric is to establish a notion of informational closeness that depends on how items are distributed in the rank of classes. The idea is that two items a and b are informationally close if the probability of finding an item between the two is low. As an example, Figure 1 illustrates the intuition of how item distribution affects informational closeness in the context of paper reviewing. This is similar in spirit to, for instance, comparing the quality of two journals according to their quartiles in the rank of journals of comparable topics. With this notion of informational closeness, proximity between classes adapts to the way in which classes are used in a given dataset. This idea of informational closeness can be implemented using Information Theory: the more unexpected it is to find an item between a and b , the more information such event provides, and the more a and b are informationally closer. Let P ( x ⪯ b ORD a ) be the probability that, sampling an item x from the space of items, x is closer to b than a in the ordinal scale of classes. Then we can define Closeness Information Quantity (CIQ) between a and b as the Information Quantity of the event x ⪯ b ORD a , as follows: CIQ ORD ( a, b ) ≡− log( P ( x ⪯ b ORD a )) . (1) Let us now apply this concept for the evaluation of system outputs. Let D be the item collection, C = { c 1 , . . . , c n } a set of sorted classes such that c 1 < c 2 < . . . < c n , and g, s : D −→C the gold standard and a system output. Given the classes g ( d ) , s ( d ) assigned to an item d ∈D by the gold standard and the system output, CIQ ORD ( s ( d ) , g ( d )) measures the closeness between the assigned class and the gold standard class: CIQ ORD ( s ( d ) , g ( d )) = − log( P ( x ⪯ g ( d ) ORD s ( d ))) .

The remaining instances refer to non-code entities, such as software names and version identifiers. Although markdown tags may facilitate entity segmentation (Section 3.1.3), we underscore the critical necessity of utilizing a human-annotated corpus for the training and evaluation of natural language processing tools within the software domain.

**2.2 Annotation Agreement**

The corpus was annotated by four undergraduate students majoring in computer science. We employed the web-based annotation tool BRAT (Stenetorp et al., 2012) and provided annotators with links to the original StackOverflow posts. In each iteration, every annotator processed 50 question-and-answer threads, 20 of which were double-annotated. An adjudicator subsequently resolved discrepancies through discussion with the annotators, who also cross-verified the 30 single-annotated questions within each batch. The inter-annotator agreement prior to adjudication was 0.62, as measured by span-level Cohen's Kappa (Cohen, 1960).

**2.3 Additional GitHub Data**

To further assess the domain adaptability of our approach, we annotated README files and issue reports from 143 repositories randomly sampled from the GitHub dump (Gousios and Spinellis, 2012), covering the period from October 29, 2007, to December 31, 2017. All code blocks were excluded from the collected issue reports and README files. The resulting GitHub Named Entity Recognition (NER) dataset comprises 6,510 sentences and 10,963 entities across 20 categories, labeled by two in-house annotators. The inter-annotator agreement for this dataset is 0.68, measured by span-level Cohen's Kappa.

**2.4 StackOverflow/GitHub Tokenization**

We developed a specialized tokenizer, SOTOKENIZER, designed specifically for the social computer programming domain. Posts from StackOverflow and GitHub exhibit characteristics common to web-based text, including abbreviations, emoticons, URLs, ungrammatical sentence structures, and spelling errors.

It is commonly argued that the fast pacing technology has helped people do shopping at the present time more easily. However, some people think online shopping is really difficult for them. This essay will consider both the advantages and disadvantages of the use of technology for shopping.
One disadvantage of online shopping can be mentioned is that people cannot familiarize themselves with online applications for ordering essential goods and food. In fact, the elderly are not good at technology enough to go to virtual supermarkets. Besides, those who live in remote areas are not accessible easily to technology to buy online groceries.
Despite its disadvantage, online shopping has brought about advantages for those who are technology natives. Firstly, it can be time-saving. In fact, shoppers can order any things they need with some clicks on the items, payment method, and time of delivery. Especially in a lingering Covid-19, when social distancing measures have been effective in some places in Viet Nam, according to a survey, the number of online shoppers has been increasing day by day, and thus many applications have been into existence, developed, and thrived.
Another advantage of virtual shopping is that it can be life-saving to many people in a pandemic across nations. Going to supermarkets can be detrimental to buyers due to the spread of viruses and the higher risk of infection. For example, in order to buy bread for breakfast, people have to go out for it and thus may be exposed to infectious or incubated victims of Covid-19. Moreover, with preventive measures of Covid-19 containment, such as people not being allowed to go out for their essential goods, if they do not know how to make an online order, it is really difficult for them to survive during a pandemic.
In conclusion, the use of technology for virtual shopping is beneficial to our time-saving and life-saving matters, though it is not familiar to some people. In my opinion, thanks to its advantages, I will continue to go shopping online, though the world has gone through the Covid-19 pandemic successfully.

Questions with higher rates of deferral had lower proportion of correct responses. We interpret these as harder questions. This section shows the importance of encouraging strategic deferrals. In ALG, 227 questions were deferred by 47 students (average of 4.9 per student) while in SYS, 399 questions were deferred by 53 students (average of 7.5 per student). For both courses, we performed a logistic regression to establish the relation between the number of deferred questions by student and their final proportions of correct answers. For ALG, the logistic regression model was statistically significant, χ 2(1) = 10.76, p < .01. The model explained 4% (McFadden’s R2) of the variance in percentage of correct answers. Increased deferrals by a student was associated with a very slight decrease in the total proportion of correct answers. In the case of SYS, the model was not found to be statistically significant, χ 2(1) = 3.32, p = 0.068. Before implementing measures to encourage the deferral of questions, students did not defer much, and performed poorly on the those questions that they deferred (presumably because there was little to gain in getting some of these few questions right). We analyzed SYS to explore if making questions that were deferred by others had an effect on the author’s results. We found no correlation between the number of deferrals a submitted question obtained, and the correctness rate of the students that generated the questions F(1, 51)=0.0005, p = 0.9823. Making harder questions does not necessarily promote higher grades. Combining the above results, we can address the question of how students do when employing deferral (Q4). To determine the effect of having more than one attempt at solving a question, we compared the proportion of questions answered correctly when attempted using one opportunity with those that were deferred and then attempted in the second round. For ALG, a significant difference was found between the proportion of correct answers given a single attempt (74.1%) and given two attempts ( 52.4%), χ 2(1) = 44.989, p < 0.001. For SYS, there was no significant difference in correctness with one attempt (70.1%) and two attempts (65.7%), χ 2(1) = 2.36, p = 0.1242.

Some people prefer sticking to familiar routines and avoid change, while others see change as something positive. For some, the idea of change can be frightening, so they choose to stay in their comfort zone because it feels safe and familiar. On the other hand, there are those who view change as a chance to grow and explore new opportunities.

Change isn‚Äôt for everyone; some people simply dislike it and do their best to avoid it. For certain individuals, spending their whole lives doing the same thing feels less risky than facing the unknown. This is often because change brings uncertainty, and people might choose familiarity even if it‚Äôs not ideal. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, we conducted experiments (Section 5 ) on four benchmark text classification datasets and compared our method with previous stateoftheart semisupervised method, including those built upon models pretrained with large amount of unlabeled data, in terms of accuracy on test sets. We further performed ablation studies to demonstrate each component’s influence on models’ final performance. Results show that our MixText method significantly outperforms baselines especially when the given labeled training data is extremely limited. 2 Related Work 2.1 Pretraining and Finetuning Framework The pretraining and finetuning framework has achieved huge success on NLP applications in recent years, and has been applied to a variety of NLP tasks. Howard and Ruder proposed to pretrain a language model on a large generaldomain corpus and finetune it on the target task using some novel techniques like discriminative finetuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing. In this manner, such pretrained models show excellent performance even with small amounts of labeled data. Pretraining methods are often designed with different objectives such as language modeling and masked language modeling. Their performances are also improved with training larger models on more data. 2.2 SemiSupervised Learning on Text Data Semisupervised learning has received much attention in the NLP community, as unlabeled data is often plentiful compared to labeled data.

Similarly, mathematical proficiency may be associated with visuospatial abilities, which have been empirically linked to success in computing curricula. Regarding grade-level performance, adjacent proficiency groups demonstrated comparable outcomes, with the exception of specific loop-related tasks. Notably, significant performance disparities were observed between the significantly below-grade-level and below-grade-level groups on loop unrolling and most advanced loop questions. The magnitude of these performance gaps increases as the distance between proficiency levels widens, culminating in significant disparities across all question types between the significantly below-grade-level and above-grade-level cohorts. The persistence of significant gaps on loop questions, even among the most proximate proficiency groups, underscores the necessity for instructional refinement.

It is important to recognize that reading and mathematical proficiency may function as intermediate variables influencing success within this curriculum. Given the well-documented correlation between these academic proficiencies and socioeconomic status, they may represent symptoms of broader underlying factors. Regardless of the root cause, elucidating the influence of academic skills on performance in computing curricula is critical. Such understanding will facilitate the development of targeted learning strategies for students facing these challenges.

This study examined the relationships between reading comprehension, mathematical proficiency, and computer science (CS) learning outcomes. The findings indicate that reading comprehension and mathematical proficiency exhibit a stronger association with the acquisition of loop concepts compared to events and sequences. Furthermore, while reading comprehension demonstrated a greater influence on basic loop questions, both skills exerted nearly equivalent effects on advanced loop questions. Finally, the results reveal that while students with reading and mathematical proficiencies at or above grade level are adequately supported by the current curriculum, students with below-grade-level proficiency continue to struggle, particularly with moderate and advanced loop concepts.

These findings highlight the urgent need for curricular enhancements and pedagogical strategies that scaffold the instruction of introductory CS and computational thinking (CT) concepts. The objective is to decouple CS/CT learning from reliance on reading and mathematical skills to the greatest extent possible. As K–8 CS/CT instruction increasingly transitions into the formal educational domain, it is imperative to identify the factors and skills that drive success among younger learners. This enhanced understanding will guide the development of targeted interventions, including curricular improvements, specialized learning strategies, and optimized instructional design, to support students who lack these foundational academic skills.

**Results**

As presented in Table 6, supervised pretraining yields significant performance improvements for 34 emotion categories, though it concurrently degrades overall performance for 24 others. We hypothesize that the SENTIMENT pretraining objective is particularly beneficial for emotions characterized by highly predictive lexical features. For instance, the token "wtf" in the phrase "it's literally the size of texas. wtf" exhibits a strong correlation with AGGRESSIVENESS. Conversely, such explicit lexical cues are absent in samples labeled AWE, such as "not all heros wear capes <3 thank you stanley - homeless #hurricane evacuee grooms lost pets."

The unsupervised pretraining experiments reveal several distinct trends. First, pretraining on EMO NET generally impairs downstream performance, notably reducing SUBMISSION accuracy by 6%. Second, the unlabeled variant of SENTIMENT pretraining provides no discernible benefit. This suggests that while sentiment information is valuable, its utility is contingent upon the strong alignment between the target emotion task and the original sentiment classification task. Third, pretraining on the HURRICANE EXT dataset yields encouraging results. While the most substantial gains are observed in AGGRESSIVENESS and LOVE, this objective also contributes a 12% accuracy improvement on tasks where supervised pretraining previously resulted in performance degradation.

**7. Fine-Grained Unsupervised Domain Adaptation**

As noted in Section 2, the emergence of new disaster events often precludes the immediate availability of emotion annotations. Nevertheless, such annotations remain critical for organizations seeking to analyze the emotional profiles of users during crises (Fraustino et al., 2012). This section investigates methodologies for leveraging supervision from large-scale emotion datasets, such as EMO NET (AbdulMageed and Ungar, 2017), to generate labels for hurricane-specific emotion datasets. We frame this challenge as an unsupervised domain adaptation problem, wherein EMO NET serves as the labeled source domain and the hurricane datasets constitute the unlabeled target domain.

Some people believe that the best way to reduce crime is to give offenders long prison sentences, while others think that alternative solutions should be used to address this issue. I think the justice system should consider other forms of punishment, depending on how serious the crime is.

To begin with, keeping people in prison for many years essentially removes the problem from society until their sentence is over. For example, when someone commits a serious crime like murder, a long prison term is justified because of the severity of the act and the potential danger the person poses to the public. However, for less serious crimes, such as theft, lighter punishments or community service could be more appropriate.

On the other hand, intervening before individuals commit serious crimes can be a smart approach. In Switzerland, for instance, social workers focus on helping young people find apprenticeships, keeping them occupied and generally happier. Long prison sentences can disconnect people from society, making it harder for them to reintegrate later. Allowing offenders to remain in the community, possibly with electronic monitoring, could be a better solution. This benefits the government and society by reducing costs compared to imprisonment, and it allows the individual to continue their life with fewer disruptions.

In conclusion, while there are cases where imprisonment is necessary, I believe that alternative punishments should always be considered, depending on the seriousness of the crime.

In all cases we sample half of the available data for training (if applicable), holding out the other half for validation. Sampling data for local RSA. When computing RSA scores it is common practice in neuroscience research to use the whole upper triangular part of the matrices containing pairwise similarity scores between stimuli, presumably because the number of stimuli is typically small in that setting. In our case the number of stimuli is very large, which makes using all the pairwise similarities computationally taxing. More importantly, when each stimulus is used for computing multiple similarity scores, these scores are not independent, and score distribution changes with the number of stimuli. We therefore use an alternative procedure where each stimulus is sampled without replacement and used only in a single similarity calculation.

**3.3 Answer Generation**

For each bridging anaphor $a$, accompanied by its corresponding question $q_a$ and context $c_a$, we construct a candidate answer set $A$ comprising all antecedents of $a$ present within $c_a$. Furthermore, for every noun phrase (NP) antecedent $n$ identified in $A$, we augment the answer set with semantic variations of $n$ to capture its core meaning. These variations include:
*   The head noun of $n$ (e.g., "earthquake" from "last week's earthquake");
*   A modified form $n'$ derived by removing all post-modifiers from $n$ (e.g., "the preliminary conclusion" from "the preliminary conclusion from a survey of 200 downtown highrises");
*   A modified form $n''$ derived by removing both post-modifiers and the determiner from $n$ (e.g., "total potential claims" from "the total potential claims from the disaster").

It should be noted that if the context $c_a$ contains no antecedent for the bridging anaphor $a$—a scenario that may arise when utilizing a restricted context window—the label "no answer" is included in the set $A$.

**3.4 Inference**

Unlike standard SQuAD-style question answering, which imposes no constraints on the position of the predicted span relative to the query, bridging anaphora resolution requires that the antecedent precede the anaphor. Consequently, during the inference phase, the position of the bridging anaphor $a$ within its context $c_a$ is first identified. The model is then constrained to predict only text spans occurring prior to $a$. Subsequently, the candidate list is pruned to retain only the top $k$ spans (where $k=20$) that contain no more than $l$ words (where $l=5$). Additionally, predictions consisting solely of function words (e.g., "a," "an," "the," "this," "that") are excluded.

**3.5 Training**

The training procedure initiates with the initialization of the BARQA model using SpanBERT (Joshi et al., 2019), selected for its demonstrated performance improvements over standard BERT embeddings on the SQuAD 1.1 dataset. Following initialization, the model undergoes further training employing various pretraining and fine-tuning strategies. Individual relative shares of commits, line changes, stories, and points serve as indicators of the magnitude of each student's contribution to their respective team. Accordingly, potential correlations between these metrics and contribution ratings were examined. Spearman's rank-order correlation was employed, with the critical value for statistical significance adjusted via the Bonferroni correction to account for the increased probability of Type I errors due to multiple comparisons. The analysis revealed a moderately positive correlation between relative commit shares ($M=1$, $SD=0.95$) and contribution ratings ($M=4.14$, $SD=0.59$). I believe that successful people are those who are willing to try new things, rather than just sticking to what they already know. I agree that learning new skills and taking risks are essential for staying one step ahead of others. New experiences help you test yourself, understand your strengths and weaknesses, and give you a chance to prove your own worth.

When I think of a successful person, the first name that comes to mind is Christopher Columbus, the explorer who discovered America. At a time when almost no one believed in his plan to reach India by a different route, he decided to take the risk. Despite widespread skepticism, he approached the king to address doubts about his project. After a long period of facing strong opposition and public criticism, he finally secured the ships and funding for his journey.

Columbus showed how important it is to be confident and how taking risks can truly pay off. The fact that he didn‚Äôt actually reach India doesn‚Äôt diminish his achievement, because the real success was in proving to Europe the value of his idea. His courage made him one of the most important figures of his century and beyond.

On the other hand, people who never try anything new don‚Äôt really discover who they are. Personally, I see this as hiding behind what they‚Äôve already achieved. It might be a way to get by, but it doesn‚Äôt lead to much in the long run.

Some people believe that gaining new experiences and taking risks are essential for success, while others argue that we should simply do our best within our areas of expertise, avoiding unfamiliar experiences and risks. In my view, it is important to seek out new experiences and take risks, as this can contribute to technological progress by offering fresh perspectives and can also enhance our performance by developing our cognitive abilities and ways of thinking.

Firstly, trying new things can open us up to new perspectives, which in turn can lead to innovation by improving on existing ideas. For instance, if an engineer experiments with a new machine while performing his usual tasks, he could make a meaningful contribution to technological advancement and benefit society as a whole. Achieving this kind of progress inevitably involves taking risks. The engineer, for example, may face stress due to potential loss of time and money. Nevertheless, the chance of success and the potential benefits of these new experiences make taking such risks worthwhile.

Secondly, people‚Äôs performance can improve over time as they challenge their cognitive abilities with unfamiliar tasks and manage stress. Thinking is a fundamental human process, and it often functions more effectively in new situations, especially under pressure. Dealing with the challenges that come with new experiences can create additional stress, but this stress can actually result in even higher levels of performance. On the other hand, if someone believes there is no need to improve, they may never strive for greater achievement in their work or responsibilities.

Based on these points, I believe that we should continually try new things and take risks if we want to achieve greater and greater success.

The evaluation of the five projects reveals a distinct correlation between scoring criteria and functional complexity. Projects awarded one point for the "User Interface" (UI) dimension responded exclusively to green flag events, whereas those receiving two points demonstrated responsiveness to both key presses and mouse clicks. Across the four primary dimensions, the majority of projects achieved scores ranging from 4 to 8. However, significant variance was observed in the remaining dimensions.

Regarding synchronization ("SY"), six projects received a score of zero due to the absence of inter-sprite messaging. The remaining projects utilized wait blocks (1 point), broadcast mechanisms (2 points), or more advanced messaging blocks (3 points). The parallelism ("PA") metric was determined by the presence of concurrent script execution under specific conditions, with higher scores reflecting greater conditional complexity. Similarly, the logic ("LO") score was assigned based on the sophistication of conditional structures: the inclusion of an "if" block (1 point), an "if-else" block (2 points), or complex logic operations (3 points).

Analysis of the logic dimension indicates that seven projects lacked conditional statements entirely, nine utilized only basic "if" blocks, and a single project employed an "if-else" structure. These low scores corroborate the assessment that the projects are fundamentally simple, characterized by minimal branching. This simplicity may account for the comparatively high scores observed in synchronization and parallelism, suggesting that participants found it more intuitive to manage program flow through user interaction and messaging rather than complex conditional logic.

While the overall initiative is deemed successful, three critical lessons have been identified to inform the future development of computational thinking (CT) training programs for preteen homeschoolers. First, science-related activities serve as an effective catalyst for fostering interest in CT within this demographic. Second, given that parents act as primary role models in homeschooling environments, establishing a relationship of mutual trust is essential. Although the target group was already familiar with information technologies, initial parental anxiety regarding the alignment of the program with established learning plans was evident. These findings align with and provide further empirical support for the assertions made by Ehsan et al., which emphasize that early exposure to STEM materials provides necessary context for computational thinking.

By improving these issues before students complete baccalaureate computing degrees, companies will have talent pools that better understand the importance and necessity of DEI and also work to ensure they help foster more diverse, equitable, and inclusive environments and technologies. In addition, more students from marginalized groups will retained in the major through degree completion. We study programming process data from two introductory programming courses. Between the course contexts, the programming languages differ, the teaching approaches differ, and the spoken languages differ. In both courses, students’ keystroke data – timestamps and the pressed keys – are recorded as students work on programming assignments. We study how the keystroke data differs between the contexts, and whether research on predicting course outcomes using keystroke latencies generalizes to other contexts. Our results show that there are differences between the contexts in terms of frequently used keys, which can be partially explained by the differences between the spoken languages and the programming languages. Further, our results suggest that programming process data that can be collected nonintrusive insitu can be used for predicting course outcomes in multiple contexts. The predictive power, however, varies between contexts possibly because the frequently used keys differ between programming languages and spoken languages. Thus, contextspecific finetuning of predictive models may be needed. Learning and teaching happens in various contexts across the world. Classrooms with chalkboards may be the primary context associated with learning in some places, while in some other locations, learning activities may primarily be organized in small groups or through apprenticeships without classrooms. The way teaching is organized influences learning outcomes . The situation is similar in learning and teaching programming: programming is taught all around the world, with numerous spoken languages, in numerous programming languages, with numerous teaching approaches, and with varying learning outcomes . In addition to learning outcomes, contextual differences influence how research results transfer from one context to another . A result from one context may be applicable to another context immediately, but it can also be the case that an approach has limited value in another context . This could be due to the data and the context where the data is produced, the methodology, or even the application of the methodology or used libraries . Recently, analysis of student keystrokes has received attention within the computing education research community .

We compute the interannotator agreement by calculating Cohen’s kappa between individual annotator’s assignments and the aggregated majority options. The average Cohen’s kappa (weighted by the number of annotations for every annotator) is 0.736 (i.e. substantial agreement). The results reveal that CoQA baseline does the worst in terms of option e . The main reason for that is because most of the responses generated from this baseline are exact answer spans. Therefore, we observe that it does very well in option b (i.e. correct answer but not a completesentence). The QuAC baseline can correctly select spanbased informative response ∼ 42% of the time. Other times, however, it often selects a span from the passage which is related to the topic but doesn’t contain the correct answer i.e. (option c ). Another problem with this baseline is that it is restricted by the input passage and many not always be able to find a valid span that answers the questions. Our STs+BERT baseline does better in terms of option e compared to the other baselines but it is limited by the STs and the parser errors. As mentioned earlier, ∼ 20% of the time this baseline directly copies the answerphrase in the response which explains the high percentage of option b . Almost all models perform better when trained with SS+ data showing that the additional data from Natural Questions and HarvestingQA is helping. Except for the PGN model trained on SS data, all other variants perform better than STs+BERT baseline in terms of option e . The GPT2 model trained on SS data from scratch does not perform very well because of the small size of training data. The pretraining with OpenSubtitiles questions boosts its performance (option e % for GPT2Pre model variants > option e % for GPT2 model variants). The best model however is DGPT when finetuned with SS+ dataset. While retaining the correct answer, it makes less grammatical errors (lower % in option c and d compared to other models). Furthermore with oracle answers it performs even better (last row in Table 3 ). This shows that DGPT can generate better quality responses with accurate answers.

The experimental evaluation utilizes datasets comprising samples from two distinct domains: Restaurant and Laptop. The proposed method is benchmarked against the following baseline architectures:

*   **LSTM:** Following the approach of Wang et al., input sentences are processed by a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to propagate contextual information among constituent words. The sentence representation is derived by averaging all hidden states from the LSTM, which is subsequently fed into a softmax classifier. Notably, aspect terms do not participate in the classification process.
*   **TDLSTM:** As proposed by Tang et al., the sequences of words preceding (left context) and succeeding (right context) the target aspect term are processed by two separate LSTMs. The mean hidden outputs of these networks are concatenated and provided to a softmax classifier.
*   **AELSTM:** Based on the work of Wang et al., this model first processes the sentence through an LSTM for context propagation. The resulting hidden states are concatenated with the target aspect representation to compute attention scores. These scores are used to pool the hidden outputs, generating an intermediate aspect representation. The final representation is constructed by summing the affine transformations of this intermediate representation and the final LSTM hidden state, which is then passed to a softmax classifier.
*   **ATAELSTM:** Also following Wang et al., this architecture is identical to AELSTM, with the exception that the LSTM input consists of the concatenation of the aspect term representation and the word representation.
*   **IAN:** Following Ma et al., the target aspect and its surrounding context are processed by two distinct LSTMs. The mean hidden outputs serve as the intermediate aspect and context representations, respectively. Attention scores are generated from the hidden states of both LSTMs to refine the final aspect and context representations. The concatenation of these two vectors is then input to a softmax classifier.

To ensure a comprehensive comparison between the proposed IARM model and the aforementioned baselines, the following experimental protocols were executed:

1.  **Overall Comparison:** The IARM model was evaluated against all baseline methods across both the Restaurant and Laptop domains.
2.  **Single and Multi-Aspect Scenarios:** Samples containing single aspects and those containing multiple aspects were tested independently using the trained models. For the IAN baseline, independent experiments were conducted specifically for this scenario.
3.  **Cross-Domain Evaluation:** Models trained on the Restaurant domain were tested on the Laptop test set, and vice versa. Independent experiments were also conducted for the IAN baseline in this setting.

These findings indicate that among the few who teach accessibility, roughly the same kinds of topics are covered in similar manners. In contrast to Lewthwaite and Sloan , these findings indicate that instructors are learning from one another or that they perceive similar accessibility issues as high priority. In addition, these findings show research in accessibility is limited in addressing what content instructors cover, and how student learning is assessed. The body of literature we analyzed covered a narrow range of similar topics with limited approaches and pedagogies. Thus, there are opportunities to expand research on what topics are covered and how, in particular, examining how to include accessibility in core programming and computing courses, not just frontend, webbased or elective courses. More work should focus on assessing accessibility knowledge. Our findings extend Ko and Ladner’s suggestions for three levels to accessibility integration in computing curricula : modifying a lecture, adding a lecture, or adding a course. Although their approach similarly has three different levels, our findings suggest an intermediary level not previously considered: using accessibility as a theme in a course. Depending on the size of the department, adding a special topics course centered around accessibility might not be possible. In these situations, accessibility as a theme can be used as a way to gain a deeper dive into accessibility, while still fitting the curriculum offered by the institution. Web Design/Web Programming and Human Computer Interaction were common courses where accessibility could easily be offered as a theme. While Ko and Ladner focused on depth when looking at adding accessibility into courses, Kawas et al. proposed ways to introduce accessibility in the context of the course. These categorizations are not in competition with our distinctions. Rather, we find that Kawas’s suggestions provide concrete approaches for someone who is looking to use the addition approach and needs ideas for how the content can be introduced into the curriculum. We found that research has investigated how to include accessibility in a small number of similar types of courses. Though there have been suggestions on how to integrate accessibility throughout the curriculum , little research has focused on diverse ways to do so. Additional research may be needed to cover all the primary learning objectives in core courses. Most core courses in computing curricula lack coverage of accessibility.

**Similarity Computation and Candidate Selection**

The similarity score $S(m, n)$ between an input mention $m$ and the set of candidate synonyms $n \in \mathcal{N}$ is computed via the inner product. The system returns the Concept Unique Identifier (CUI) corresponding to the nearest neighbor. Notably, the identification of nearest neighbors is computationally efficient, provided that dense and sparse representations for all synonyms are precomputed.

**4. Experimental Setup**

**4.1 Implementation Details**

The preprocessing pipeline applies standard normalization techniques, including character lowercasing and punctuation removal, to both mentions and synonyms. To address orthographic errors within mentions from the NCBI Disease dataset, a spelling correction algorithm is employed, consistent with the methodology of D'Souza and Ng (2015). Given the prevalence of abbreviations in biomedical literature and the resulting challenges for normalization, the Ab3P module (Sohn et al., 2008) is utilized to detect local abbreviations and expand them to their full definitions based on contextual cues. Furthermore, composite mentions (e.g., "breast and ovarian cancer") are decomposed into distinct entities (e.g., "breast cancer" and "ovarian cancer") using heuristic rules established by D'Souza and Ng (2015). To enhance dictionary coverage, mentions from the training set are integrated into the reference dictionary, following the approach of D'Souza and Ng (2015).

For sparse representations, character-level unigrams and bigrams are utilized within a TF-IDF framework. The maximum sequence length for the BioBERT model is set to 25; any input exceeding this limit is truncated. The candidate retrieval process is configured with a top-$k$ parameter of 20 and a dense representation ratio ($\alpha$) of 0.5. Optimization parameters include a learning rate of $1 \times 10^{-5}$, a weight decay of $1 \times 10^{-2}$, and a mini-batch size of 16. The trainable scalar $\lambda$ was observed to converge to values ranging between 2 and 4, varying by dataset.

Training was conducted for 10 epochs on the NCBI Disease, BC5CDR Disease, and TAC2017 ADR datasets. Due to the larger dictionary size associated with the BC5CDR Chemical dataset, training was limited to 5 epochs for this specific corpus. With the exception of the number of epochs, all hyperparameters were held constant across all datasets and experimental conditions.

Consider the example illustrated in Figure 1(a): in the absence of contextual cues, it is difficult to determine whether the term "Jordan" refers to a footwear brand or an individual. While the majority of existing methodologies prioritize modeling intermodal interactions to derive word-aware visual representations, the final word representations within their hidden layers remain predominantly grounded in textual context, rendering them insensitive to visual information. Intuitively, associated imagery often provides critical context for resolving polysemous entities and should consequently inform the final word representations; for instance, as depicted in Figure 1(b), visual data can guide the representations of "Kian" and "David" toward the semantic category of persons rather than animals.

Furthermore, most prior approaches largely overlook the inherent bias introduced by incorporating visual information. In the context of social media posts, associated images typically highlight only one or two entities mentioned in the text, omitting others. Consequently, the direct integration of visual features may enhance the recognition of image-highlighted entities while simultaneously impairing the identification of non-highlighted ones (e.g., "Oracle Arena" and "King of the Jungle" in Figure 1).

To address these limitations, we leverage existing pretrained contextualized word representations and propose a unified multimodal architecture based on the Transformer framework (Vaswani et al., 2017). This architecture is designed to effectively capture intermodal interactions while mitigating visual bias. Specifically, our approach comprises three key components:

1.  **Sentence Encoding:** We employ a recently pretrained contextualized representation model (Devlin et al., 2018) as the sentence encoder. Its multi-head self-attention mechanism enables each word to capture semantic and syntactic dependencies relative to its context.
2.  **Multimodal Interaction (MMI):** To better capture implicit alignments between text and images, we introduce an MMI module. This module couples a standard Transformer layer with a cross-modal attention mechanism to generate both image-aware word representations and word-aware visual representations for each input token.
3.  **Bias Mitigation via Auxiliary Task:** To substantially eliminate visual context bias, we incorporate text-based entity span detection as an auxiliary task within a unified Transformer-based neural architecture. A conversion matrix is designed to establish correspondence between the auxiliary and primary tasks, thereby ensuring that entity span information is fully utilized to guide final Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER) predictions.

In a more challenging comparison, we compare SDiCoh with ASeq and EAGrid as the baseline models that use DA information. Our SDiCoh even surpasses these models for all problemdomain s on DailyDialog . However, on SwitchBoard , SDiCoh achieves lower accuracy than these models for all problemdomain s except UI. This observation shows that when dialogue utterances are short (like those in SwitchBoard in comparison with those in DailyDialog ), DAs are more crucial for coherence assessment. It is worth noting that unlike EAGrid and ASeq , SDiCoh is completely agnostic to DA information. When we employ DAP as an auxiliary task to train the DiCoh model in our MTL setup, we observe that MDiCoh substantially outperforms the Random , CoSim , and SDiCoh models (which do not use DAs) for all problemdomain s on both DailyDialog and SwitchBoard . It concludes that our proposed MTL approach effectively leverages the DAP task to learn informative utterance vectors for dialogue coherence assessment. Compared with the ASeq and EAGrid models, which explicitly use gold DA labels during evaluations, our MDiCoh achieves the highest accuracy for all problemdomain s on DailyDialog , showing that our approach for involving DAs yields more informative utterance representations for coherence assessments. However, on SwitchBoard , MDiCoh increases the accuracy of SDiCoh up to those of EAGrid for UO and EUO. Surprisingly, it achieves lower accuracy than what EAGrid achieves for UR. An explanation for why MDiCoh outperforms ASeq and EAGrid on DailyDialog but not on SwitchBoard might be that the ASeq and EAGrid models explicitly use gold DA labels during evaluation but MDiCoh does not; and the DA labels in SwitchBoard are about 10 times higher finegrained than those in DailyDialog (see Table 2 ). This interpretation becomes more concrete by observing a considerable reduction in the performance of ASeq and EAGrid when they are evaluated on DailyDialog compared with when they are evaluated on SwitchBoard . In contrast, our MDiCoh , which uses DAs only during training to obtain better utterance vectors, performs almost evenly on both corpora. Since our model does not need DA labels during evaluations, it is more suitable than the examined models for evaluating dialogue coherence in real scenarios. Finally, to shed some light on which parts of a dialogue receive higher attentions by our MDiCoh model, we analyze the attention weights it assigns to utterance words.

For many years, there has been a belief that adults should encourage teenagers to either work for a company or spend a year traveling abroad before starting university. While gaining work or travel experience before university offers several advantages, it is important to recognize that there are also some disadvantages to consider.

On one hand, working or traveling before beginning university can greatly benefit a young person‚Äôs development. For instance, it can help them better understand their academic interests. When young people take a year-long internship at a company, they are exposed to various job roles, which can help them decide which field of study suits them best. Similarly, traveling to another country before university allows them to experience new cultures. By meeting people from different backgrounds and beliefs, they gain life experiences that cannot be taught in a classroom.

However, despite these benefits, there are also some drawbacks to working or traveling before starting university. One common issue is that students may graduate later than their peers who go straight to university after high school. For example, in Thai culture, it is often believed that those who graduate later may struggle to build a successful career. Additionally, critics argue that working before finishing university may not be worthwhile, as many jobs require a solid academic background to perform well and make fewer mistakes. Therefore, some believe it is better for young people to go directly to university.

In summary, gaining work or travel experience before university can help young people discover their academic interests and learn about new cultures. However, it may also lead to delayed graduation and less effective job performance due to a lack of academic preparation.

The proposed framework is designed to facilitate the application of evolving standards and norms across the diverse application contexts of Natural Language Processing (NLP). A prototypical manifestation of outcome disparity is observed in gender bias within image captioning. Research by Zhao et al. (2017) and Hendricks et al. (2018) demonstrates a systematic divergence in model outcomes ($\hat{Y}$) with respect to gender, even when the source distribution is treated as the ideal target distribution. Formally, this is expressed as $Q(\hat{Y}_{\text{target}} \mid \text{gender}) \not\approx Q(Y_{\text{target}} \mid \text{gender}) \sim Q(Y_{\text{source}} \mid \text{gender})$. Consequently, models tend to overpredict the presence of females in images containing ovens and males in images featuring snowboards.

Error disparity is defined as a condition wherein model predictions exhibit elevated error rates for individuals possessing specific user attributes (or ranges of attributes in the case of continuous variables). Formally, the error of a predicted distribution is denoted as $\epsilon_D = |Y_D - \hat{Y}_D|$. An error disparity exists if this error distribution is not uniform with respect to the attribute $A_D$:

$$Q(\epsilon_D \mid A_D) \not\approx \text{Uniform}$$

In practical terms, this implies that the error rate for one demographic group may systematically differ from that of another (e.g., differing error rates between distinct demographic categories). Under unbiased conditions, such differences would be expected to follow a uniform distribution. This formulation accommodates both discrete cases, which are prevalent in NLP tasks such as Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging, and continuous cases, such as age or income prediction.

We posit that the presence of either outcome disparity or error disparity within a target application constitutes predictive bias. It is crucial to note that predictive bias is a property of a model relative to a specific application, rather than an intrinsic characteristic of the model in isolation. This definition aligns with the concept of predictive bias in standardized testing (Swinton, 1981), which asserts that a predictive model cannot be deemed biased without reference to a specific prediction context; thus, the same instrument may be biased in one application while remaining unbiased in another. A canonical example of error disparity is the "Wall Street Journal Effect," a systematic variation in error rates as a function of demographics, first documented by Hovy and Søgaard (2015).

The proposed methodology employs an actor-critic framework to derive an explicitly stochastic dialogue policy (the actor), ensuring high scalability, while simultaneously utilizing an estimated value function (the critic) to accelerate reinforcement learning (RL) convergence. Furthermore, this architecture facilitates imitation learning, enabling the pretraining of the dialogue policy on human-human interaction data. Given the cooperative yet asymmetric nature of the two agents involved in achieving task success, we introduce a Hybrid Value Network (HVN). This network decomposes the task reward into distinct components to enhance policy learning efficacy. Notably, the approach is entirely data-driven; it requires neither the prior construction of a user simulator nor additional human supervision during the training phase. The following subsections detail the state and action representations for both dialogue policies, describe the reward decomposition mechanism and the HVN architecture, and conclude with an overview of the model optimization strategy.

### 3.1 Dialogue Policy

**System Policy**
The system policy, denoted as $\pi$, determines the system action $a_S$ based on the current system dialogue state $s_S$ to generate an appropriate response to the user agent. Each system action $a_S$ constitutes a subset of the dialogue act set $A$, as a single dialogue turn may encompass multiple intents. A dialogue act serves as an abstract representation of an intention (Stolcke et al., 2000), formally defined as a quadruple comprising the domain, intent, slot type, and slot value (e.g., [restaurant, inform, food, Italian]). In practice, dialogue acts are delexicalized within the policy; slot values are replaced by count placeholders and subsequently populated with actual values retrieved from an external database ($DB$) based on the selected entity. This mechanism enables the system to generalize to unseen values. The system dialogue state $s_S^t$ at turn $t$ is constructed by concatenating the following components: (i) the user action at the current turn ($a_U^t$); (ii) the system action from the preceding turn ($a_S^{t-1}$); (iii) the belief state $b_t$ (Williams et al., 2016), which tracks constraint and request slots provided by the user; and (iv) embedding vectors representing the quantity of query results ($q_t$) returned by the database.

**User Policy**
The user policy, denoted as $\mu$, determines the user action $a_U$ based on the user dialogue state $s_U$. This policy governs the expression of constraints and requests directed toward the system agent.

**Error Analysis**

A manual analysis was conducted on 104 error instances to identify the primary failure modes of the EMT model. The results indicate three distinct categories of challenges:

1.  **Reasoning and Decision-Making Failures:** In 40 instances, the model failed to derive the correct entailment states for individual rule sentences. In an additional 23 cases, while the entailment states were correctly identified, the model was unable to synthesize these states into the correct final decision. These findings suggest that explicitly modeling the logical reasoning process represents a promising avenue for future improvement.
2.  **Information Extraction Limitations:** In 24 cases, the model failed to reach the correct decision due to an inability to infer necessary user information from the provided scenarios. This highlights a significant challenge in extracting relevant context from user inputs.
3.  **Rule Parsing Difficulties:** Parsing rule text into discrete rule sentences proved challenging. As illustrated in Figure 3(b), plain text often contains complex clauses defining rule conditions, which are difficult to disentangle. In 17 instances, a single rule sentence contained multiple conditions, leading to failures in conducting accurate entailment reasoning.

**Question Generation Error Analysis**

Regarding the development set, which comprised 562 question generation examples, the EMT model demonstrated poor performance in locating underspecified spans in 115 cases (defined as a span extraction F1 score ≤ 0.5). A detailed manual review of 52 erroneous cases revealed the following patterns:
*   In 29 cases, the model failed to predict the correct entailment states for rule sentences, resulting in the failure to locate the span within the ground truth rule sentence.
*   In 9 cases, the model correctly identified the relevant rule sentence but extracted an incorrect span.
*   A further challenge arises from the one-to-many nature of sequence generation. When multiple underspecified rule sentences exist, the model may generate a question regarding one valid underspecified sentence that differs from the ground truth annotation. This observation suggests the need for new evaluation metrics that account for such valid alternative outputs.

**4. Related Work**

ShARC Conversational Machine Reading (Saeidi et al., 2018) distinguishes itself from conversational question answering (Choi et al., 2018; Reddy et al., 2019) and conversational question generation (Gao et al., 2019) in two key aspects: (1) the requirement for the system to formulate follow-up questions to bridge information gaps, and (2) the necessity to interpret a set of complex decision rules to derive a conclusion, rather than simply extracting an answer from the text. Consequently, Conversational Machine Reading (CMR) can be conceptualized as a specialized form of task-oriented dialogue systems (Wen et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019) designed to assist users in achieving specific objectives.

**6. Experimental Setup**

In contrast to prior approaches, the methodology proposed herein offers intuitive mechanisms to facilitate the interpretation of results, as detailed in Section 7.4.

We evaluate the proposed method (NN) against the approach introduced by Hamilton et al. (2016b), referred to here as AlignCos and described in Section 4. The AlignCos methodology involves aligning vector spaces via the OP algorithm, followed by ranking words based on the cosine distance between their representations across the two spaces. This baseline was previously demonstrated by Schlechtweg et al. (2019) to outperform alternative methods. While certain extensions—such as filtering proper nouns or jointly learning and aligning spaces (Bamler and Mandt, 2017; Rudolph et al., 2017; Rudolph and Blei, 2018; Yao et al., 2018)—may yield enhanced performance, we adhere to the standard AlignCos configuration. This setting represents the most general and widely adopted framework within this domain, for which an open-source implementation is available.

To demonstrate the efficacy of our approach, we apply it to detect shifts in word usage across various scenarios. The corpora utilized in this study, along with their statistical properties, are summarized in Table 1. Our analysis encompasses three demographic distinctions (age, gender, and occupation), a day-of-week distinction, and short-term (four-year) diachronic distinctions. Furthermore, we compare our results with the long-term (ninety-year) diachronic setup established by Hamilton et al. (2016b), which relies on the Google Books corpus.

**Author Demographics**
The Celebrity Profiling corpus (Wiegmann et al., 2019) comprises tweets from public figures annotated with demographic attributes, including age, gender, and occupation. Based on these labels, we constructed the following splits:
1.  **Age:** Young (birth years 1990–2009) versus Older (birth years 1950–1969).
2.  **Gender:** Male versus Female.
3.  **Occupation:** Pairwise comparisons involving Performers, Athletes, and Creators.

**Day-of-Week Analysis**
We utilize the dataset collected by Yang and Leskovec (2011), which contains 580 million English tweets spanning from June 2009 to February 2010, complete with timestamps. Given the scale of this corpus, our analysis focuses on a single month (November 2009). We established a split based on the day of the week, contrasting weekdays (Tuesday and Wednesday) with weekends (Saturday and Sunday). Duplicate entries were excluded from the dataset, as preliminary experiments indicated that heavily duplicated spam tweets adversely affected the stability of the vector representations.

The text feature distillation process begins by transforming each news article $n_i$ into a dual-vector representation consisting of a "key" vector ($n^K_i$) and a "value" vector ($n^V_i$). Let $N^K_t = \{n^K_i\}_t$ and $N^V_t = \{n^V_i\}_t$ denote the sets of key and value vectors, respectively, for all articles published on day $t$. This dual-vector architecture, which encodes semantic information at distinct granularities, was originally proposed and validated in prior research (Miller et al., 2016; Daniluk et al., 2017). Specifically, the key vector $n^K_i$ captures word-level semantics, while the value vector $n^V_i$ encapsulates contextual-level information.

To determine the relevance of each article $i$ to a specific stock $j$ on day $t$, the system computes an attention score. This score is defined as the inner product between the article's key vector and the stock's embedding vector $s_j$:

$$ \text{score}_{i,j} = n^K_i \cdot s_j $$

While alternative similarity metrics, such as cosine similarity or generalized inner products involving arbitrary functions, are theoretically viable, this study employs the standard dot product to isolate the fundamental capabilities of the stock embedding mechanism.

Subsequently, the attention weight $\alpha^j_i$, which quantifies the contribution of article $i$ to the classification of stock $j$'s price movement (up or down), is derived by applying a softmax function to the attention scores across all articles released on day $t$:

$$ \alpha^j_i = \frac{\exp(\text{score}_{i,j})}{\sum_{i'} \exp(\text{score}_{i',j})} $$

Finally, the market status representation for stock $j$ on day $t$, denoted as $m^j_t$, is computed as the weighted sum of the value vectors from the daily corpus. This aggregated vector serves as the input feature for the downstream classifier:

$$ m^j_t = \sum_{n^V_i \in N^V_t} \alpha^j_i n^V_i \quad (3) $$

Consequently, $m^j_t$ represents a synthesized context of the textual information available on day $t$, modulated by the relevance of each article to the specific stock in question.

While the calibration of classification models has been extensively investigated, spanning from statistical machine learning (Platt et al., 1999; Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 2005) to deep learning (Guo et al., 2017), calibration in structured generation tasks, such as Neural Machine Translation (NMT), remains underexplored. Recent studies by Müller et al. (2019) and Kumar and Sarawagi (2019) examined NMT calibration within the training regime, concluding that models trained with label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016) exhibit good calibration. However, we posit that this training-centric perspective may obscure a fundamental issue in NMT: exposure bias (Ranzato et al., 2015), which arises from the discrepancy between teacher-forced training and autoregressive inference.

To address this limitation, the present work focuses on NMT calibration during inference, a setting that more accurately reflects the generative capabilities of these models. To facilitate this evaluation, we employ the Translation Error Rate (TER) metric (Snover et al., 2006) to automatically annotate the correctness of generated tokens. Experimental results across multiple datasets and language pairs indicate that, despite the application of label smoothing, NMT models continue to exhibit significant miscalibration during inference. As illustrated in Figure 1, while modern neural networks in classification tasks typically demonstrate overconfidence (confidence exceeding accuracy) (Guo et al., 2017), NMT models display a dual pattern: they are overconfident in general but notably underconfident (confidence falling below accuracy) regarding low-confidence predictions.

Furthermore, our findings demonstrate a strong correlation between miscalibrated predictions and specific translation errors. Overestimated predictions are predominantly associated with over-translation and mistranslation errors, whereas underestimated predictions correlate more closely with under-translation errors. These observations underscore the critical necessity of investigating inference-time calibration in NMT. Through an examination of the linguistic properties of miscalibrated tokens in NMT outputs, we identify several key findings:

*   **Frequency:** Low-frequency tokens are generally subject to underestimation.

We attribute this gap to the fact that while SLBERT jointly identifies emotion-cause pairs, it adheres to a pipelined decoding order (emotion $\to$ cause). In contrast, our approach achieves fully joint decoding through interleaved actions across all three tasks, thereby facilitating more effective information interaction. Finally, our model also surpasses MTBERT; this suggests that parameter sharing alone is insufficient to effectively capture the complex interdependence between emotions and causes.

Integrating external knowledge as extra semantic representations and additional inputs to the conversation model effectively improves the quality of generated responses. Nevertheless, some research on discourse development suggests that human conversations are not “still”: People chat around a number of related concepts, and shift their focus from one concept to others. Grosz and Sidner models such concept shift by breaking discourse into several segments, and demonstrating different concepts, such as objects and properties, are needed to interpret different discourse segments. Attentional state is then introduced to represent the concept shift corresponding to each discourse segment. Fang et al. shows that people may switch dialog topics entirely in a conversation. Restricting the utilization of knowledge only to those directly appear in the conversation, effective as they are, does not reach the full potential of knowledge in modeling human conversations. To model the concept shift in human conversations, this work presents ConceptFlow ( Con versation generation with Con cept Flow ), which leverages commonsense knowledge graphs to model the conversation flow in the explicit concept space. For example, as shown in Figure 1 , the concepts of a conversation from Reddit evolves from “ chat ” and “ future ”, to adjacent concept “ talk ”, and also hops to distant concept “ dream ” along the commonsense relations—a typical involvement in natural conversations. To better capture this conversation structure, ConceptFlow explicitly models the conversations as traverses in commonsense knowledge graphs: it starts from the grounded concepts, e.g., “ chat ” and “ future ”, and generates more meaningful conversations by hopping along the commonsense relations to related concepts, e.g., “ talk ” and “ dream ”. The traverses in the concept graph are guided by graph attention mechanisms, which derives from graph neural networks to attend on more appropriate concepts. ConceptFlow learns to model the conversation development along more meaningful relations in the commonsense knowledge graph. As a result, the model is able to “grow” the grounded concepts by hopping from the conversation utterances, along the commonsense relations, to distant but meaningful concepts; this guides the model to generate more informative and ontopic responses.

Furthermore, we evaluate models trained on synthetic code-switched data. Specifically, we fine-tune the highest-performing multilingual model using this synthetic data and demonstrate that, in the majority of cases, its performance surpasses that of the base multilingual model. This finding underscores the potential for further optimizing multilingual models for code-switched environments.

The primary contributions of this work are as follows:

*   **Identification of Data Gaps and Benchmark Proposal:** We address the scarcity of standardized datasets for code-switching by introducing **GLUECoS**, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark designed to assess model performance across various Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks in English-Hindi and English-Spanish code-switched contexts.
*   **Task Expansion:** In the development of this benchmark, we identify critical gaps in existing code-switched NLP research and propose a novel task: **Natural Language Inference (NLI)** for code-switched data.
*   **Comparative Evaluation of Embeddings:** We conduct a systematic evaluation of cross-lingual and pre-trained multilingual embeddings across all proposed tasks. Our results indicate that pre-trained multilingual embeddings significantly outperform cross-lingual embeddings, thereby demonstrating the superior efficacy of generalized language models over traditional cross-lingual word embeddings.
*   **Optimization via Synthetic Data:** We fine-tune pre-trained multilingual models on linguistically motivated synthetic code-switched data. The observed performance improvements in most scenarios confirm that these models can be effectively optimized for code-switched settings.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section 2 situates our contributions within the context of prior research. Section 3 details the tasks and datasets comprising GLUECoS, providing the rationale for our methodological choices. Section 4 outlines the experimental setup, including specifications for the baseline models. Section 5 presents and analyzes the results of evaluating all models on the proposed benchmark. Finally, Section 6 concludes with a summary of key findings and directions for future research.

### 2. Relation to Prior Work

The concept of a generalized benchmark for code-switching is inspired by **GLUE** (Wang et al., 2018), which catalyzed research in English Natural Language Understanding (NLU). The success of GLUE eventually led to the curation of more challenging tasks in **SuperGLUE** (Wang et al., 2019) once models surpassed human baselines on the original benchmark. The core motivation behind GLUE is to evaluate models within a multi-task learning framework, enabling tasks with limited training data to benefit from knowledge transfer across other tasks. While the current iteration of our work does not evaluate models in a multi-task setting, we intend to incorporate this capability in subsequent versions of the benchmark. Historically, shared tasks related to code-switching have been conducted as part of workshops co-located with major NLP conferences.

The analyses presented herein utilize the same variables as those described in Section 3.5, with the exception of unigram confusability. This methodological approach facilitates the determination of whether word confusability exerts an effect on duration independent of contextual sensitivity. Consistent with findings from the Switchboard and Buckeye training sets, greater confusability is significantly associated with increased word duration ($p < 0.001$ for all analyses). Table 1 summarizes the results of these analyses applied to the test sets. The observed effects replicate within the test data and remain qualitatively consistent when continuous variables are rank-transformed. Collectively, these analyses substantiate the association between elevated confusability and prolonged word duration.

In a subsequent set of analyses, we examined whether a context-sensitive measure of confusability is requisite for explaining this effect, or whether an out-of-context measure suffices. To address this, unigram confusability was included as a covariate alongside the previously established variables. Unigram confusability mirrors the target measure of word confusability, differing only in the substitution of the language model with a unigram model. Consequently, this metric calculates a word's confusability based solely on its acoustic properties and phonological similarity to other words, thereby excluding top-down expectations derived from context. After controlling for unigram confusability, contextual confusability remains significantly associated with longer word durations in both the Switchboard and Buckeye training sets ($p < 0.001$ for all analyses). Table 2 presents the corresponding results for the test sets. These effects replicate across both test datasets and are similarly robust in the rank-transformed analyses.

**4.1 Neighborhood Density**

This section reports the results of several exploratory analyses. It should be noted that the confidence intervals and $p$-values presented herein are non-bootstrapped. We evaluated the impact of neighborhood density on word duration within the test sets. Weighted neighborhood density is associated with reduced word duration across all analyses (see Appendix B). These findings indicate that the neighborhood density effects identified in prior literature remain qualitatively similar following adjustments for contextual confusability.

**Pretraining Methodology**

The extracted text-table pairs are converted into pretraining instances utilizing a masked language modeling objective, consistent with the approach established by Devlin et al. (2019). Although an auxiliary objective involving the prediction of table-text relevance was investigated, empirical results indicated no performance improvement on downstream tasks. This finding aligns with observations by Liu et al. (2019) regarding the limited utility of next-sentence prediction tasks in similar contexts.

To ensure computational efficiency, the combined tokenized sequence length of the text and table cells is constrained within a fixed budget (e.g., 128 word pieces in the final experimental configuration). To adhere to this constraint, a random text snippet comprising 8 to 16 word pieces is selected from the associated context. Table content is incorporated by initially including the first word of each column header and cell, followed by the iterative, turnwise addition of subsequent words until the sequence budget is reached. Ten distinct snippets are generated per table using this procedure.

The masking strategy adheres to the BERT framework, employing whole-word masking for textual segments. Furthermore, whole-cell masking is applied to tabular data, wherein all word pieces within a cell are masked if any single piece within that cell is selected for masking. Additionally, data augmentation techniques were explored to generate synthetic question-denotation pairs via a grammar-based approach. However, as this augmentation did not yield significant improvements in downstream task performance, these results are excluded from the final analysis.

**Finetuning Overview**

Table parsing is formally defined within a weakly supervised framework as follows. Given a training set of $N$ examples $\{(x_i, T_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$, where $x_i$ represents an utterance, $T_i$ denotes a table, and $y_i$ corresponds to a set of denotations, the objective is to learn a model that maps a novel utterance $x$ to a program $z$. Upon execution against the corresponding table $T$, this program $z$ must yield the correct denotation $y$. The program $z$ consists of a subset of table cells and, optionally, an aggregation operator.

Although paraphrase datasets are typically well-annotated, they are costly to acquire and consequently remain limited in scale. Recent research has demonstrated that training semantic parsing models for single sentences using a combination of large-scale synthesized data and small amounts of paraphrase data yields strong performance on real-world evaluations (Campagna et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). This paper extends that methodology to the multi-turn dialogue setting. Multi-turn dialogues present greater complexity, as they require the modeling of abstract dialogue states—information not explicitly present in standard target annotations such as domain and slot values. To address this, we extend the synthesis algorithm to operate within a dialogue model framework capable of tracking sufficient context to sustain dialogue continuity. Furthermore, we introduce a novel dialogue model specifically designed for data synthesis.

Dialogue State Tracking (DST) is a well-established field, originating with the first Dialogue State Tracking Challenge (Williams et al., 2014); a comprehensive review of prior work is available in Williams et al. (2016). Historical approaches to DST have varied widely, ranging from methods utilizing handcrafted features to extract utterance information (Henderson et al., 2014; Wang and Lemon, 2013) to the application of Convolutional Neural Networks for learning utterance representations (Mrkšić et al., 2017). However, the latter approach lacks scalability due to the absence of parameter sharing across different slots. Subsequent works by Zhong et al. (2018) and Nouri and Hosseini-Asl (2018) introduced global modules to facilitate knowledge transfer through information sharing, though these methods rely on predefined ontologies. To handle unseen slot values, Xu and Hu (2018) employed a pointer network within a Sequence-to-Sequence architecture. More recently, Lee et al. (2019) utilized a pre-trained BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019) to encode slots and utterances, leveraging multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to identify relevant context for slot value prediction. Additionally, Wu et al. (2019) proposed an encoder-decoder architecture with a copy mechanism that shares parameters across all domains, while Zhou and Small (2019) reformulated multi-domain DST as a question-answering task, employing reading comprehension techniques to generate answers via span or value prediction.

This proposal is grounded in empirical observations presented in Figure 2, which illustrates the correlation between BLEU scores and the norms of the source embedding matrix across training checkpoints of a standard Transformer model on the WMT'14 English–German translation task. Our analysis reveals a strong parallel between the trajectory of the embedding norm, denoted as $m_t$, and the progression of BLEU scores. Specifically, when $m_t$ stabilizes within the range of 15,000 to 20,000—approximately two to three times the initial norm $m_0$—the rate of improvement for both metrics decelerates. This convergence suggests that $m_t$ serves as a robust functional metric for assessing model competence. Consequently, this approach circumvents the computational intractability associated with estimating $\lambda_t$ in Equation 7, allowing for the derivation of the following criterion:

$$ \hat{c}(t) = \min\left(1, \frac{m_t - m_0}{\lambda_m m_0 + c_0^2}\right) \quad (10) $$

Here, $\lambda_m$ represents a task-independent hyperparameter governing the duration of the curriculum. By utilizing this criterion, models can autonomously construct a curriculum based on the embedding norm feature. In the early stages of training, a lower $m_t$ facilitates learning via an easier curriculum. As $m_t$ increases, the curriculum dynamically incorporates progressively more complex data.

### 3.3 Norm-Based Sentence Weighting

In traditional competence-based Curriculum Learning (CL), the model uniformly samples sentences falling below its current competence threshold and assigns equal weight to all selected samples. This methodology often results in the redundant utilization of low-difficulty sentences (e.g., where $\hat{d}(x) < 0.1$), which is counterintuitive and inefficient regarding computational resources. Analogous to human pedagogy, once a learner has mastered foundational concepts (such as basic arithmetic), continued review of these elementary tasks is unnecessary; the learner should instead focus on advanced material while retaining prior competence. Furthermore, complex sentences are frequently composed of simpler substructures. Therefore, the representations of simpler sentences can be effectively refined through the processing of more difficult, composite sentences, rendering the exclusive focus on easy samples suboptimal.
